TL;DR: LLaVA-1.6-7B processes ambiguous bistable stimuli (e.g., duck-rabbit) by activating both aspect representations in its vision tower (72% of cases), but final caption commitment is determined downstream; causal steering at CLIP layer 22 flips captions only for default-dominant stimuli, not force-balanced ones, revealing a gap between visual representation ("seeing") and linguistic commitment ("seeing-as").


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand where in a vision-language model the commitment to one interpretation of an ambiguous image is made, using Wittgenstein's duck-rabbit as a test case.

Method: Created a 3,320-generation behavioral baseline over 83 bistable stimuli, identifying three regimes (default-dominant, force-dominant, force-balanced). Trained a TopK sparse autoencoder on the CLIP layer consumed by LLaVA-1.6-7B (validation EV 0.93). Performed causal steering at CLIP layer 22 to test caption flipping.

Result: 72% (50/69) of bistable stimuli showed simultaneous activation of both aspect feature pools in the vision tower. Causal steering flipped captions only on default-dominant stimuli (33% rabbit-flip rate with fluency guard), but failed on force-balanced young/old stimuli at any tested coefficient. Also found that rank-based statistics on TopK SAE outputs require tie-corrected ranking to avoid silent row-order bias.

Conclusion: The dominance bottleneck lies downstream of the vision tower; the gap between vision-side representation (seeing) and language-side commitment (seeing-as) provides an empirical handle on the seeing/seeing-as distinction.

Abstract: Wittgenstein's duck-rabbit poses a question for vision-language models: when a model captions an ambiguous image, where in the model is the commitment to one aspect made? We address this with a 3,320-generation behavioral baseline over 83 bistable stimuli that surfaces three regimes (default-dominant, force-dominant, force-balanced) under neutral vs forced-choice prompting, then probe the underlying representations using a TopK sparse autoencoder we train on the CLIP layer that LLaVA-1.6-7B actually consumes (validation EV 0.93). Across 69 bistable stimuli with both per-aspect feature pools available, 72% (50/69) show simultaneous activation of both pools at the vision tower, including 12/12 default-dominant duck/rabbit and 7/8 force-balanced young/old. Causal steering at CLIP layer 22 flips captions on default-dominant stimuli (33% rabbit-flip rate under a fluency guard) but cannot flip captions on force-balanced young/old at any tested coefficient, despite their vision-side superposition. The dominance bottleneck lives downstream of the vision tower; the gap between vision-side representation and language-side commitment is an empirical handle on the seeing/seeing-as distinction. We also flag a methodological note: rank-based statistics on TopK SAE outputs require tie-corrected ranking to avoid silent row-order bias.

</details>


### [56] [Balancing Real and Synthetic Data for CNN-based Masonry Crack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08033)
*Mattia Forlesi,Alfonso Esposito,Ivan Zyrianoff,Alessandro Marzani,Marco Di Felice*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores using synthetic crack data combined with real masonry crack images to train deep learning models for crack detection. Results show that a mix of 20% synthetic and 80% real data outperforms training on real data alone, achieving 76% F1-score and 80% mIoU using InceptionV4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crack detection is critical for building health monitoring, but deep learning models require large, diverse datasets that are difficult to collect for complex surfaces like masonry. The authors aim to address data scarcity by generating synthetic crack data.

Method: Real masonry crack images were collected from buildings in Bologna. Synthetic data was generated using a crack overlay tool with controlled orientation and placement. Multiple DL architectures were trained on real data, with InceptionV4 selected as best. Six training scenarios with varying real/synthetic ratios were tested using F1-score and mIoU metrics.

Result: The 20/80 synthetic/real scenario achieved 76% F1-score and 80% mIoU, outperforming the real-only baseline. Training on synthetic data with just 20% real data achieved comparable results to training solely on real data.

Conclusion: Synthetic data can effectively reduce real data collection efforts while enhancing crack detection accuracy, demonstrating its potential as a complementary data source for masonry crack detection.

Abstract: Cracks are a critical indicator of building health, and early stage identification is fundamental to prevent harmful damages. Advances in deep learning (DL), particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs), have enabled scalable solutions for automated crack detection. However, CNN performance strongly depends on the availability of large and diverse datasets, which is particularly challenging for complex surfaces such as masonry. Collecting sufficient real data is time-consuming, while publicly available datasets may not be adequate. To address this limitation, we explored generating synthetic crack data, which complements real data and improves training effectiveness. The real dataset consists of masonry crack images collected from buildings in Bologna and surrounding areas. In contrast, the synthetic dataset was generated using a crack overlay tool that adds cracks to background images in a controlled orientation and placement. The real dataset was used to train several DL architectures, to identify the best-performing model (InceptionV4) employed for experiments with generated data. Six training scenarios were tested in InceptionV4 by varying the ratio of real and synthetic data, with evaluation performed on a test set composed of real images using the F1-score and mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) metrics. Results show that training on synthetic data plus a modest addition of 20% real data achieves results comparable to training on real data only. Moreover, the 20/80 scenario (synthetic/real) achieved an 76% F1-score and 80% mean IoU, outperforming the real-only case. As can be seen, the method demonstrates the potential of synthetic data to reduce collection efforts while enhancing crack detection accuracy.

</details>


### [57] [Sci-Rho: A Multilingual Visually-Grounded Symbolic Benchmark for STEM Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08034)
*Muhammad Falensi Azmi,Ikhlasul Akmal Hanif,Vallerie Alexandra Putra,Adi Yeltay,Abdullah Mubarak,Fajri Koto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sci-Rho is a dynamic, multilingual, visually-grounded STEM benchmark with 42,420 instances across 5 subjects and 7 languages. Evaluation of 17 VLMs reveals a gap between average and worst-case accuracy, cross-lingual performance degradation in smaller models, and significant attention variation across languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing symbolic benchmarks are limited to mathematical reasoning, lack visual grounding, and are primarily in English. This paper aims to address these gaps by creating a more robust, multilingual, and visually-grounded benchmark for STEM reasoning.

Method: The authors created Sci-Rho with 4,242 problem templates (606 per language) across 5 subjects, crafted by domain experts. Each template is executable Python code that generates varied problem instances by modifying numerical values, visual patterns, shapes, colors, and functions, yielding 42,420 instances with reasoning steps and solutions.

Result: A significant gap exists between worst-case and average accuracy across 17 VLMs. Smaller models show noticeable cross-lingual performance degradation, while proprietary/larger models remain robust. Attention head analysis reveals substantial cross-lingual variation in image vs. text token attention allocation.

Conclusion: The work highlights the importance of moving beyond static benchmarks for evaluating VLM robustness, as dynamic variations reveal critical weaknesses not captured by average performance metrics.

Abstract: Symbolic benchmarks have emerged as a key approach to assess model robustness under minor modifications to STEM-related questions. However, existing symbolic benchmarks mostly remain limited to mathematical reasoning, lack visual grounding, and are predominantly in English. In this work, we introduce Sci-Rho (Science Rhobustness), a dynamic benchmark for visually-grounded STEM problems spanning five subjects and seven languages, comprising 4,242 problem templates (606 per language) crafted by domain experts, including Olympiad medalists. Each template is implemented as executable Python code that generates diverse but equivalent problem instances by varying numerical values, visual patterns, geometric shapes, color schemes, and function types, resulting in 42,420 instances in total, each paired with reasoning steps and ground-truth solutions. We evaluated 17 state-of-the-art VLMs and discovered a noticeable gap between worst-case accuracy (defined as the proportion of problem templates that a model answers correctly across every generated variation) and average accuracy. We also discovered that smaller models show noticeable performance degradation across languages, whereas proprietary and larger models remain robust. Step-level evaluation reflects this same trend, revealing a significant gap between average F1 and worst-case F1 scores. Finally, our inspection of attention heads of a VLM reveals substantial cross-lingual variation in the relative attention allocated to image tokens compared to text tokens. Our work highlights the importance of evaluation beyond static benchmarks as a metric to measure the quality of VLMs.

</details>


### [58] [DyCo-RL: Dynamic Cross-Modal Coordination for Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08035)
*Hangui Lin,Yan Shu,Zhengyang Liang,Chi Liu,Xiangrui Liu,Minghao Qin,Teng Long,Zheng Liu,Nicu Sebe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DyCo-RL improves RLVR for MLLMs by modeling dynamic cross-modal coordination during CoT reasoning using Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure attention shifts, outperforming existing methods across 7 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods optimize only for reasoning outcomes, ignoring fine-grained cross-modal coordination during generation, which causally leads to reasoning failures.

Method: Proposes DyCo-RL: (1) uses Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure within-modality attention shifts and assign tokens to visual/text roles; (2) evaluates alignment between token attention and assigned role; (3) uses alignment score for advantage reweighting during policy optimization.

Result: Consistently improves four RLVR algorithms (applied to Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B) across seven benchmarks covering visual-centric and mathematical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Algorithm-agnostic DyCo-RL effectively addresses the overlooked cross-modal coordination problem in RLVR, leading to generalizable improvements across diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a leading paradigm for enhancing visual reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing RLVR methods optimize primarily for the reasoning outcome, fundamentally overlooking the fine-grained cross-modal coordination required during the generation process. Through token-level analyses and controlled interventions, we reveal that during Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, MLLMs frequently fail to dynamically alternate between extracting visual evidence and synthesizing textual context-a coordination breakdown that is causally linked to reasoning failures. Motivated by these findings, we propose DyCo-RL, which integrates dynamic cross-modal coordination into RLVR optimization. Specifically, DyCo-RL uses the Fisher-Rao geodesic distance to measure within-modality attention shifts, assigning tokens to either visually-oriented or text-oriented functional roles. It then evaluates the alignment between a token's actual attention allocation and its assigned role, leveraging this score for alignment-guided advantage reweighting during policy optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the algorithm-agnostic DyCo-RL, when applied to Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, consistently improves four representative RLVR algorithms across seven benchmarks spanning visual-centric and mathematical reasoning.

</details>


### [59] [Robust-U1: Can MLLMs Self-Recover Corrupted Visual Content for Robust Understanding?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08063)
*Jiaqi Tang,Jianmin Chen,Youyang Zhai,Wei Wei,Runtao Liu,Mengjie Zhao,Xiangyu Wu,Qingfa Xiao,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Robust-U1 equips MLLMs with explicit visual self-recovery capability to restore corrupted images before reasoning, achieving SOTA robustness on real-world and adversarial corruption benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs suffer significant performance degradation under real-world visual corruptions, and current robustness methods (black-box feature alignment or white-box text-based reasoning) cannot effectively restore lost pixel-level details.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) supervised fine-tuning for initial reconstruction, (2) reinforcement learning with dual rewards (pixel-level SSIM + semantic-level CLIP similarity) for high-quality visual recovery, (3) multimodal reasoning jointly using corrupted input and recovered image.

Result: Achieves SOTA robustness on real-world corruption benchmark, maintains superior performance under adversarial corruptions on general VQA benchmarks, and shows high-quality visual recovery directly enhances reasoning performance.

Conclusion: Self-recovery is a critical mechanism for robust visual understanding in MLLMs, effectively restoring corrupted visual content to improve downstream reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in visual understanding, yet their performance degrades significantly under real-world visual corruptions. While existing robustness enhancement approaches exist, they are limited: black-box feature alignment lacks interpretability, and white-box text-based reasoning cannot restore lost pixel-level details. This work investigates a fundamental research question: Can MLLMs recover corrupted visual content by themselves? To address this, we propose Robust-U1, a novel framework that equips MLLMs with explicit visual self-recovery capability for robust understanding. The approach comprises three core stages: supervised fine-tuning for initial reconstruction, reinforcement learning with dual rewards (pixel-level SSIM and semantic-level CLIP similarity) for aligning high visual quality, and multimodal reasoning that jointly considers both the corrupted input and the recovered image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Robust-U1 achieves state-of-the-art robustness on the real-world corruption benchmark and maintains superior performance under adversarial corruptions on general VQA benchmarks. Analysis confirms that high-quality visual recovery directly enhances reasoning performance, establishing self-recovery as a critical mechanism for robust visual understanding. The source code is available at https://github.com/jqtangust/Robust-U1.

</details>


### [60] [VideoWeaver: Evaluating and Evolving Skills for Agentic Long Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08091)
*Jianhui Wei,Jie Tan,Hengchuan Zhu,Xiaotian Zhang,Yan Zhang,Ziyi Chen,Daoan Zhang,Wei Xu,Zuozhu Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoWeaver is a benchmark and agent harness for long video generation that evaluates AI agents' ability to compose foundation skills into custom workflows, using an agent-as-judge evaluation method and a skill evolution algorithm, finding that explicit composition skills and skill evolution improve output quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent frameworks (Claude Code, Codex, OpenClaw) are strong at tool use but their ability to handle long-horizon multimodal tasks like long video generation is underexplored. Unlike handcrafted pipelines, these frameworks can build their own workflows, motivating the need for a benchmark to evaluate and improve this capability.

Method: The paper introduces VideoWeaver: (1) a benchmark with 16 task categories and 285 cases spanning text, image, audio, video references; (2) an agent-as-judge that inspects both execution trace and final video with evidence-grounded scoring; (3) a skill evolution algorithm that refines and merges agent skills using feedback from the agent-as-judge.

Result: Explicit composition skills improve generation over foundation skills alone; skill evolution further enhances output quality; performance varies notably across harness and model choices; the agent-as-judge aligns well with human judgments, especially on process metrics.

Conclusion: Composition skills and skill evolution are effective for long video generation tasks. The agent-as-judge provides reliable automated evaluation aligned with human judgment. Code and dataset are open-sourced.

Abstract: Recent agent frameworks such as Claude Code, Codex, and OpenClaw are strong at tool use and orchestration, but whether they can handle long video generation, a long-horizon multimodal task, remains underexplored. Unlike earlier video agents whose pipeline is handcrafted, these frameworks can build and refine their own workflows. We introduce VideoWeaver, an agent harness and benchmark that evaluates and evolves skills for long video generation, where an agent turns a single instruction into a long video by composing foundation skills into its own workflow rather than following a predefined pipeline. The benchmark has 16 task categories and 285 cases, with references spanning text, image, audio, video, and their combinations. Because errors can arise at any stage and not just in the final video, we propose an agent-as-judge that inspects both the execution trace and the final video, grounding its scores in evidence such as metadata and intermediate files. Using this feedback, we further design a skill evolution algorithm that refines and merges the agent's skills. Across multiple frameworks and models, we find that an explicit composition skill improves the generation process over using foundation skills alone, that skill evolution further improves output quality, and that performance varies notably across harness and model choices. The proposed agent-as-judge also aligns well with human judgments, especially on process metrics. Code and dataset is available at https://github.com/JianhuiWei7/VideoWeaver

</details>


### [61] [Trustworthy Visual Predicates for Robust Manipulation Understanding under Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08121)
*Fatemeh Ziaeetabar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a predicate-level reliability framework for manipulation understanding under visual degradation, showing that predicate failures are structured—static spatial predicates remain robust while contact-sensitive and dynamic ones are fragile—and that degraded predicates significantly reduce downstream accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual predicates (contact, support, grasp, release, etc.) are widely used in manipulation understanding models, but their reliability under real-world visual degradation (blur, occlusion, noise, etc.) has never been systematically analyzed.

Method: The paper defines a structured predicate vocabulary with confidence-aware estimation, reliability metrics (preservation, degradation sensitivity, temporal consistency, confidence-weighted stability, downstream impact), and evaluates on controlled degradation videos plus public datasets (VISOR/EPIC-KITCHENS, H2O, ARCTIC).

Result: Static spatial predicates are robust; contact-sensitive and dynamic predicates (grasp, release) are fragile. Detection noise, occlusion, and frame dropping cause strongest losses. Downstream accuracy drops from 0.89 to 0.58 under severe degradation; removing confidence weighting reduces accuracy from 0.74 to 0.64.

Conclusion: Predicate reliability serves as a diagnostic layer between low-level perception and high-level structured reasoning, revealing that failures are structured and not uniform.

Abstract: Manipulation understanding requires reliable relational evidence, such as contact, support, containment, motion coupling, grasp, release, and active-hand involvement. Although these visual predicates are widely used in event-chain, graph-based, and neuro-symbolic models, their reliability under visual degradation is rarely analyzed directly. This paper introduces a predicate-level reliability framework for robust manipulation understanding under blur, occlusion, illumination change, low resolution, frame dropping, and detection noise. The framework defines a structured predicate vocabulary, confidence-aware predicate estimation, and reliability metrics for predicate preservation, degradation sensitivity, temporal consistency, confidence-weighted stability, and downstream impact. Experiments on controlled manipulation videos and public egocentric or bimanual datasets, including VISOR/EPIC-KITCHENS, H2O, and ARCTIC, show that predicate failures are structured rather than uniform. Static spatial predicates remain comparatively robust, whereas contact-sensitive, dynamic, and derived predicates such as grasp and release are more fragile. Under severe degradation, detection noise, occlusion, and frame dropping cause the strongest reliability losses. Downstream analysis shows that degraded predicates reduce manipulation-understanding accuracy from 0.89 to 0.58, while removing confidence weighting under moderate degradation reduces accuracy from 0.74 to 0.64. These results show that predicate reliability provides a diagnostic layer between visual perception and structured manipulation reasoning.

</details>


### [62] [Human-Centered Benchmarking of Driver Monitoring Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08123)
*Ruben Dario Florez-Zela*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces HCBF, a multi-dimensional evaluation framework (accuracy, explainability, efficiency, robustness) for driver monitoring systems. Applied to four lightweight models on eye-state classification, it shows models indistinguishable on accuracy dominate different dimensions, with ShuffleNetV2 ranking first overall but failing critically under noise, while the transformer remains robust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current driver monitoring systems are compared solely on classification accuracy, which is insufficient to determine real-world deployment fitness.

Method: Proposes Human-Centered Benchmarking Framework (HCBF) evaluating models across accuracy, explainability, efficiency, and robustness. Applied to MobileNetV3, ShuffleNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and DeiT-Tiny on MRL Eye Dataset.

Result: All four models lie on the Pareto frontier; ShuffleNetV2 wins Human-Centered Score across all weighting scenarios, yet fails critically under sensor noise (closed eyes classified as open), while the transformer remains robust.

Conclusion: Aggregate ranking can mask dimension-specific vulnerabilities that are operationally decisive, underscoring the need for multi-dimensional, human-centered evaluation.

Abstract: Vision-based driver monitoring systems are increasingly deployed in safety-critical intelligent transportation settings, yet they are almost always compared on classification accuracy alone. This paper argues that accuracy is insufficient to characterize a model's fitness for real-world deployment, and proposes the Human-Centered Benchmarking Framework (HCBF), which evaluates models across four dimensions: accuracy, explainability, efficiency, and robustness. The framework is applied to four representative lightweight architectures, MobileNetV3, ShuffleNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and DeiT-Tiny, on the MRL Eye Dataset for eye-state classification. While the models are nearly indistinguishable on clean-set accuracy, each leads in exactly one dimension, and all four lie on the Pareto frontier. A Human-Centered Score computed under three deployment-oriented weighting scenarios ranks ShuffleNetV2 first throughout. However, this aggregate winner retains less than half of its performance under sensor noise and fails by classifying closed eyes as open, whereas the transformer remains robust. These findings show that aggregate ranking can mask dimension-specific vulnerabilities that are operationally decisive, underscoring the value of multi-dimensional, human-centered evaluation.

</details>


### [63] [One Stone, Three Birds: Self-adaptive Optimal Transport for Multi-VLM Selection, Adaptation, and Ensembling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08126)
*Qiyu Xu,Zhanxuan Hu,Yu Duan,Yonghang Tai,Huafeng Li,Quanxue Gao,Xiangyong Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OSTB is a training-free framework that uses self-adaptive optimal transport to jointly handle model selection, target adaptation, and prediction integration across multiple VLMs without requiring target labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM deployment assumes a single pre-selected model is compatible with the target domain, but in realistic cross-domain scenarios, multiple VLMs may be plausible and no instance-level labels exist to identify reliable ones. A coupled solution for model selection, adaptation, and ensembling is needed.

Method: OSTB estimates a consensus sample-to-class transport plan from a pool of frozen VLMs using self-adaptive optimal transport. This transport structure is reused for: (1) model selection via combined semantic-visual reliability ranking, (2) target adaptation via transport-conditioned visual classifiers, and (3) reliability-aware probabilistic ensemble integration.

Result: Experiments on natural-image, remote-sensing, and medical-pathology benchmarks show OSTB improves model ranking, adaptation stability, and ensemble robustness under heterogeneous candidate pools.

Conclusion: The key insight is that model selection, adaptation, and ensembling all depend on the same latent sample-class structure, which can be jointly estimated via optimal transport from multiple VLMs without training.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) enable visual recognition from semantic class descriptions, which makes them attractive when target annotations are scarce or unavailable. Most deployment pipelines, however, first choose a single VLM and then adapt that model to the unlabeled target set. This single-backbone paradigm hides a critical assumption: the selected VLM is already compatible with the target domain. In realistic cross-domain deployment, several general-purpose and domain-specialized VLMs may be plausible, yet no instance-level target labels are available to identify the reliable ones. Deployment therefore requires a coupled solution for model selection, target adaptation, and prediction integration. We revisit this problem from a system-level multi-VLM perspective. Our central observation is that the three decisions above depend on the same latent object: a trustworthy sample-class structure in the target set. Different VLMs may encode different transfer biases and produce conflicting predictions, but their outputs can still provide complementary evidence for estimating this structure. We propose One Stone, Three Birds, a training-free framework based on self-adaptive optimal transport. Given a pool of frozen candidate VLMs, OSTB estimates a consensus sample-to-class transport plan without updating VLM parameters. The learned transport structure is then reused for all deployment objectives: model selection is performed by ranking the combined semantic and visual reliability induced by the consensus plan; target adaptation is obtained by fitting transport-conditioned visual classifiers; and ensembling is implemented through reliability-aware probabilistic integration. Extensive experiments on natural-image, remote-sensing, and medical-pathology benchmarks show that OSTB improves model ranking, adaptation stability, and ensemble robustness under heterogeneous candidate pools.

</details>


### [64] [Phase Marginalization for Patch-Grid Instability in Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08132)
*Oğuzhan Ercan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision Transformers suffer from phase-dependent instability due to fixed patch grids; the authors propose Phase Marginalization, a training-free post-hoc method that evaluates multiple patch-grid phases, inverse-aligns outputs, and aggregates them. Using K=4 achieves modest gains over standard test-time augmentation for dense prediction tasks like segmentation and depth estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers using fixed patch grids introduce phase-dependent instability for dense prediction—changing the patch partition alters token evidence available to a pixel, especially near boundaries. The authors formalize this as a nuisance variable to address this overlooked issue.

Method: Phase Marginalization: a post-hoc method that evaluates structured patch-grid phases, inverse-aligns dense outputs, and aggregates them in the original image coordinate system. The main variant uses K=4 uniform phases and is training-free.

Result: Uniform Phase Marginalization (K=4) improves over the canonical K=1 baseline across segmentation, depth, and local matching. On Cityscapes, it outperforms generic shift-based four-forward TTA by +0.31 mIoU. K=4 is optimal—K=8 shows no change, K=16 adds little accuracy at much higher latency.

Conclusion: Patch-grid phase is a measurable nuisance variable in ViTs. Phase Marginalization provides a simple, effective post-hoc diagnostic and marginalization baseline for dense ViT prediction.

Abstract: Vision Transformers operate on fixed patch grids, which can introduce phase-dependent instability for dense prediction: changing the patch partition can change the token evidence available to a pixel, especially near boundaries. We formalize patch-grid phase as a nuisance variable and propose Phase Marginalization, a post-hoc marginalization method that evaluates structured patch-grid phases, inverse-aligns dense outputs, and aggregates them in the original image coordinate system. The central variant, Uniform Phase Marginalization with K = 4, is training-free and improves over the canonical K = 1 baseline across measured segmentation, depth, and local matching settings. In a controlled Cityscapes experiment, Uniform Phase Marginalization provides a modest compute-matched advantage over generic shift-based four-forward test-time augmentation (TTA) (+0.31 mean Intersection-over-Union over the strongest tested generic row). A scaling study further shows that K = 4 is a practical cost-accuracy trade-off: K = 8 is essentially unchanged and K = 16 adds little accuracy at much higher latency. These results position patch-grid phase as a measurable nuisance variable and Phase Marginalization as a simple diagnostic and post-hoc marginalization baseline for dense ViT prediction.

</details>


### [65] [Gravity-guided Contact Dynamics Estimation from 3D Human Motions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08133)
*Cuong Le,Urs Waldmann,Bastian Wandt,Mårten Wadenbäck*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GraCE estimates ground contact forces and pressure distribution from motion capture data using center of gravity and mass distribution, outperforming prior methods on GroundLink and MOYO datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior methods for ground contact dynamics either require restrictive force plates/pressure mats or only provide rough estimates from vertical distance, failing to capture complex pressure distribution across contact points.

Method: GraCE uses center of gravity relative distance to estimate contact locations, and computes applied force per contact as the product of predicted contact probabilities and total exterior force derived from center of mass trajectory.

Result: Outperforms related work on GroundLink dataset for ground reaction force estimation and on MOYO dataset for detailed contact pressure prediction.

Conclusion: Gravity-guided approach enables scalable, accurate full-body contact dynamics estimation from motion data without specialized hardware, with code to be released.

Abstract: Ground contact forces acting on the human body, are crucial for biomechanics studies or sport performance analysis. Prior methods rely on force plates or pressure mats to collect ground contact dynamics, limiting their applicability to carefully controlled settings. A more scalable solution is to estimate the dynamics directly from motion capture data. Recent approaches only roughly estimate the ground contact dynamics from the vertical distance between the body and the ground plane, which cannot capture the complex pressure distribution of all contact points. To this end, we propose GraCE -- Gravity-guided Contact Dynamics Estimation, a novel full-body contact dynamics model for human motions using a realistic influence of body mass distribution and gravity. We use the human's center of gravity to estimate the ground contacts based on its relative distance to the human body. The applied force on each contact is estimated via the product of predicted contact probabilities and the total exterior force computed from the center of mass trajectory. We outperform related work on the GroundLink dataset for ground reaction force estimation, and on the MOYO dataset for detailed contact pressure prediction. The code is published upon acceptance.

</details>


### [66] [IMAGINE: Adaptive Schema-Imagery Enhanced Composition for Composed Video Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08144)
*Jiale Huang,Zixu Li,Zhiwei Chen,Zhiheng Fu,Chunxiao Wang,Yupeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IMAGINE is a novel network for Composed Video Retrieval that handles implicit semantic concepts (e.g., "cake" implying "birthday party") by using dynamic multimodal prototypes to materialize schema imagery, achieving state-of-the-art results on both CVR and CIR benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CVR methods fail to handle implicit semantics in modification texts, as they assume modified objects appear directly in videos, neglecting latent associations between textual concepts and visual cues.

Method: IMAGINE uses dynamic multimodal prototypes to capture shared latent concepts (schema imagery), which adaptively modulate visual features to inject implicit guidance into retrieval.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on both Composed Video Retrieval and Composed Image Retrieval across three widely used benchmarks.

Conclusion: Bridging the gap between explicit visual contents and implicit retrieval intentions via schema imagery significantly improves retrieval performance.

Abstract: Composed Video Retrieval (CVR) is designed to retrieve a target video that matches a reference video modified by a modification text. While existing methods explore cross-modal correspondences, they often assume modified objects appear directly in videos. However, modification texts frequently describe concepts not explicitly presented but implicitly expressed through semantically related visual cues (e.g., "cake" implying "birthday party"). Current approaches typically rely on aligning explicit feature representations within the concrete space, neglecting critical latent associations. To address this, we propose an adaptIve scheMa-ImAGery enhanced composItional NEtwork (IMAGINE). Unlike standard explicit matching, IMAGINE materializes implicit semantics (termed schema imagery) via dynamic multimodal prototypes. These prototypes capture shared latent concepts to adaptively modulate visual features, effectively injecting implicit guidance into the retrieval process. By bridging the gap between explicit visual contents and implicit retrieval intentions, IMAGINE achieves state-of-the-art performance in both CVR and Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) across three widely used benchmarks.

</details>


### [67] [Property-Informed Diffusion-Based Text-to-Microstructure Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08150)
*Bingxuan Dai,Hongsong Wang,Jie Gui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A property-informed diffusion network that generates 3D metamaterial microstructures from textual descriptions, using dual alignment (contrastive text-structure and reward-guided) to ensure semantic and physical consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional inverse design of microstructures requires domain expertise, iterative simulations, and manual tuning; existing methods suffer from limited design diversity and challenges in ensuring physical feasibility.

Method: A property-informed diffusion-based network that leverages semantic and physical property guidance from text inputs, with a dual alignment strategy (contrastive text-structure alignment + test-time reward-guided alignment) to enforce consistency between generated structures and target prompts.

Result: The model generates semantically meaningful and physically plausible structures across a wide range of material categories.

Conclusion: This approach enables interactive microstructure design and opens new directions for combining language-based interfaces with inverse material discovery.

Abstract: Designing 3D metamaterial microstructures that meet the intended functions remains a major challenge, as it typically requires domain expertise, iterative simulations, and extensive manual tuning. Existing work on inverse design that automatically generates microstructures based on desired target properties often suffers from limited design diversity and faces challenges in ensuring the physical feasibility of the generated structures. To address this issue, a property-informed diffusion-based network is proposed that enables the generation of 3D microstructures directly from textual descriptions. Unlike traditional property conditioning methods, our approach leverages rich guidance in terms of semantics and physical properties in the text input to support diverse structure synthesis. To enforce consistency between the generated structures and the target textual prompts, a dual alignment strategy is adopted, including contrastive text-structure alignment and test-time reward-guided alignment. Experimental results show that the model is capable of generating semantically meaningful and physically plausible structures across a wide range of material categories. Our approach has good potential for interactive microstructure design and opens up new directions for combining language-based interfaces with inverse material discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/hongsong-wang/PropDiff-TMG

</details>


### [68] [RAPID: Layer-Wise Redundancy-Aware Pruning and Importance-Driven Token Merging for Efficient ViT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08156)
*Kyumin Choi,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAPID is a depth-aware token reduction framework for Vision Transformers that uses different strategies across layers: pruning redundant tokens in shallow layers and merging semantically important tokens in deeper layers, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to existing methods like ToMe and ToFu.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers suffer from high computational costs due to quadratic self-attention complexity. Existing token reduction techniques (pruning/merging) overlook how token representations evolve across network depth, treating all layers uniformly.

Method: RAPID employs a bifurcated depth-aware strategy: (1) In shallow-to-middle layers, a redundancy-similarity pruning metric eliminates over-represented local patterns. (2) In deeper layers, it shifts to importance-similarity aware merging using CLS token attention weights to protect critical tokens while fusing less important neighbors.

Result: On ImageNet-1K with ViT and DeiT, RAPID establishes a superior accuracy-compression Pareto frontier, achieving up to 4.29% higher accuracy than ToMe at extreme reduction rates.

Conclusion: RAPID provides a training-free template for optimizing vision models by aligning reduction strategies with hierarchical feature evolution across layers.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve strong performance but suffer from high computational costs due to quadratic self-attention complexity. Although token reduction techniques such as pruning and merging mitigate this, they typically overlook how representations evolve across network depth. We propose RAPID, a depth-aware token reduction framework that adapts reduction strategies to the layer-wise characteristics of token representations. The primary methodological contribution is a bifurcated strategy: in shallow-to-middle layers, RAPID employs a redundancy-similarity aware pruning metric to eliminate over-represented local patterns. As features transition to global semantic concepts in deeper layers, the framework shifts to an importance-similarity aware merging mechanism. This stage leverages classification (CLS) token attention weights to protect semantically critical tokens while fusing less important but similar neighbors. Empirical validation on ImageNet-1K using ViT and DeiT architectures demonstrates that RAPID establishes a superior accuracy-compression Pareto frontier compared to plug-and-play baselines such as ToMe and ToFu. RAPID is particularly robust in aggressive compression regimes, achieving up to 4.29% higher accuracy than ToMe at extreme reduction rates. Our framework provides a training-free template for optimizing vision models by aligning reduction strategies with hierarchical feature evolution.

</details>


### [69] [How Much MRI Preprocessing Is Enough? A Cost-Utility Study for Brain MRI Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08164)
*Jiangshuan Pang,Wangyang Tang,Jing Yan,Zhixuan Cheng,Youzhe He,Zhenkun Zhuang,Tao Zhou,Shiping Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates the impact of MRI preprocessing levels (P0-P7) on self-supervised 3D brain MRI pretraining, finding that beyond a minimal feasible level (P2), stronger preprocessing yields only marginal gains (3.4% for MAE, 1.8% for JEPA) and that most benefits can be recovered by applying preprocessing downstream rather than during pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MRI preprocessing is typically treated as routine data cleaning rather than a modeling choice, and its cost-utility trade-off for self-supervised pretraining has not been systematically studied.

Method: The authors compare a graded P0-P7 preprocessing spectrum for masked autoencoding (MAE) and joint-embedding predictive learning (JEPA) on 20,000 heterogeneous brain MRI volumes, keeping corpus, 3D ViT backbone, masking protocol, and downstream evaluations fixed.

Result: P0/P1 are numerically unstable; P2 is the lowest-cost feasible level. Beyond P2, best preprocessing improves aggregate utility by only 3.4% (MAE) and 1.8% (JEPA). IDH improves modestly, AGE and GLI/PED are near or best at P2, and MCI shows the clearest P7 gain. Cross-level transfer shows P7 advantage can be recovered via downstream preprocessing.

Conclusion: MRI preprocessing should be viewed as a downstream-aware cost-utility decision rather than a default escalation pipeline.

Abstract: MRI preprocessing defines the input distribution seen by brain MRI foundation models, yet it is usually treated as routine data cleaning rather than a modeling choice. We ask how much preprocessing is worth its computational cost for self-supervised 3D MRI pretraining. Keeping the corpus, 3D ViT backbone, masking protocol, and downstream evaluations fixed, we compare a graded P0-P7 preprocessing spectrum for masked autoencoding (MAE) and joint-embedding predictive learning (JEPA) on 20,000 heterogeneous brain MRI volumes, then transfer the encoders to IDH prediction, MCI classification, brain age regression, and GLI/PED tumor segmentation. The results do not support a simple "more is better" rule. P0/P1 are numerically unstable, making P2 the lowest-cost feasible level; beyond P2, choosing the best feasible preprocessing level improves aggregate utility by only 3.4 percentage points for MAE and 1.8 percentage points for JEPA, with most paired gains statistically unresolved. Stronger preprocessing is beneficial only in selected regimes: IDH improves modestly, AGE and GLI/PED are often near or best at P2, and MCI shows the clearest empirical P7 gain. Cross-level MCI transfer further shows that much of the P7 advantage can be recovered by applying stronger preprocessing downstream, without requiring P7 throughout pretraining. These findings recast MRI preprocessing as a downstream-aware cost-utility decision rather than a default escalation pipeline. Code is available at https://github.com/PangJiangShuan/PreBrain.

</details>


### [70] [Empowering Feed-Forward Reconstruction Models with Metric Scale via Satellite Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08205)
*Xianghui Ze,Yongjian Luo,Mengjun Chao,Zhenbo Song,Jianfeng Lu,Yujiao Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A satellite-guided framework that resolves scale ambiguity in feed-forward 3D reconstruction by using satellite imagery as a global metric reference, achieving metric-scale geometry without costly annotations or calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models suffer from unknown global scale ambiguity, limiting metric understanding. Existing metric methods rely on costly metric annotations or accurate camera calibration, which are unreliable in many real-world settings.

Method: Uses readily available satellite imagery as a global metric reference. Given a coarse camera pose, retrieves a local satellite patch and integrates it with a feed-forward reconstruction backbone via bidirectional cross-view interaction, enforcing consistency to infer absolute scale, refine geometry, and estimate camera pose in metric coordinates.

Result: Consistent improvements on KITTI, nuScenes, and Oxford RobotCar in metric depth estimation, multi-view point-cloud reconstruction, and cross-view camera localization, with strong cross-dataset and cross-region generalization.

Conclusion: Satellite imagery is an effective, readily available global reference for resolving scale ambiguity in feed-forward 3D reconstruction, removing the need for metric annotations or camera calibration.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models have recently shown strong generalization across diverse scenes, yet most of them recover geometry only up to an unknown global scale. This scale ambiguity limits their use in applications that require metric understanding of the environment. Existing metric reconstruction methods commonly rely on large-scale metric annotations or accurate camera calibration, both of which are costly or unreliable in many real-world settings. We propose a satellite-guided framework for resolving scale ambiguity in feed-forward 3D reconstruction. The key idea is to use readily available satellite imagery as a global metric reference. Given a coarse camera pose, our method retrieves a local satellite patch and integrates it with a feed-forward reconstruction backbone through bidirectional cross-view interaction. By enforcing consistency between the reconstructed scene and the satellite reference, the model infers absolute scale, refines scene geometry, and estimates camera pose in a metric coordinate frame. Experiments on KITTI, nuScenes, and Oxford RobotCar show consistent improvements in metric depth estimation, multi-view point-cloud reconstruction, and cross-view camera localization, while preserving strong generalization across datasets and geographic regions.

</details>


### [71] [SegmentAnyTreeV2: Scaling Transformer-Based Tree Instance Segmentation Across Sensors, Platforms, and Forests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08206)
*Maciej Wielgosz,Stefano Puliti,Rasmus Astrup*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegmentAnyTreeV2 is a sensor-agnostic deep learning framework for segmenting individual trees in 3D forest point clouds, combining Point Transformer v3 with a specialized mask decoder and achieving state-of-the-art results across diverse forest types and LiDAR platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for tree instance segmentation from point clouds struggle with dense, structurally complex forest stands and lack cross-domain generalization across different sensors, biomes, and LiDAR platforms.

Method: Combines a serialization-based Point Transformer v3 backbone with a lightweight semantic head and a tree-focused cross-attention mask decoder; semantic predictions restrict instance decoding to tree-class voxels; uses instance-aware query initialization, one-to-many seed supervision, and asymmetric mask scoring.

Result: On FOR-instanceV2 test set: 90.5% precision, 80.2% recall, 85.0% F1, 90.7% coverage, 87.6% semantic mIoU. Outperforms previous learning-based methods in instance detection and mask completeness. Strong zero-shot cross-domain generalization demonstrated.

Conclusion: SegmentAnyTreeV2 is a robust, sensor- and platform-agnostic framework for forest point cloud segmentation, with strong performance and generalization. The expanded FOR-instance v3 benchmark provides a valuable resource for future research.

Abstract: We present SegmentAnyTreeV2, a sensor- and platform-agnostic framework for semantic and instance segmentation of forest point clouds. The model combines a serialization-based Point Transformer v3 backbone with a lightweight semantic head and a tree-focused cross-attention mask decoder. Semantic predictions restrict instance decoding to tree-class voxels, while instance-aware query initialization, one-to-many seed supervision, and asymmetric mask scoring improve separation in dense and structurally complex stands. We further introduce FOR-instance v3, an expanded benchmark comprising 427 scenes and 26,496 annotated trees across diverse biomes, forest structures, and LiDAR platforms. On the FOR-instanceV2 test split, SegmentAnyTreeV2 achieves 90.5% precision, 80.2% recall, 85.0% F1, 90.7% coverage, and 87.6% semantic mIoU, outperforming previous learning-based methods in both instance detection and mask completeness. Zero-shot evaluation on independent sites further demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization.

</details>


### [72] [Test-Time Scaling in Multimodal Foundation Models: A Comprehensive Survey of Generation and Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08231)
*Cong Wan,Ying He,Zhongzhan Huang,Hefeng Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents the first comprehensive survey on Test-time Scaling (TTS) for Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs), proposing a unified taxonomy of sampling-based, feedback-based, and search-based approaches, along with applications, benchmarks, and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field of multimodal TTS lacks a systematic survey and unified theoretical framework despite rapid progress, creating a need to delineate the developmental landscape and bridge the gap between fragmented research efforts.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive review and propose a unified taxonomic framework categorizing multimodal TTS methodologies into sampling-based, feedback-based, and search-based approaches, while also summarizing representative applications and benchmarks.

Result: A systematic categorization of existing TTS methods for MFMs into three strategies, along with a summary of key applications and benchmarks for evaluating multimodal TTS in generation and reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: This survey provides a systematic roadmap for future research in multimodal TTS, discussing open challenges and outlining promising research directions in this rapidly evolving field.

Abstract: Test-time Scaling (TTS) has emerged as a pivotal research direction for enhancing model performance by dynamically allocating computational resources during inference. Recent advancements have adapted this paradigm to Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs), unlocking their potential in multimodal reasoning and generation. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks a systematic survey and unified theoretical framework to delineate the developmental landscape of multimodal TTS. To bridge this gap, we present the first comprehensive review of TTS research for MFMs, proposing a unified taxonomic framework that categorizes existing methodologies into three distinct strategies: sampling-based, feedback-based, and search-based approaches. We further summarize representative applications and benchmarks commonly utilized to evaluate multimodal TTS capabilities in generation and reasoning tasks. Finally, this survey discusses open challenges and outlines future research directions, providing a systematic roadmap for subsequent studies in this rapidly evolving field.

</details>


### [73] [Light-WAM: Efficient World Action Models with State-Fusion Action Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08242)
*Ziang Li,Dongzhou Cheng,Yibin Wang,Shiyue Wang,Xiaoyang Xu,Lingxuan Weng,Juan Wang,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Light-WAM is a lightweight World Action Model for robot manipulation that uses a compact video backbone and latent-space supervision to achieve efficient training and inference, with only 0.44B parameters, 72ms latency, and strong performance on LIBERO and RoboTwin benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing World Action Models rely on large-scale generative architectures with high training costs and inference latency, making them impractical for efficient closed-loop policy deployment.

Method: Light-WAM uses a compact video backbone with downsampled latent-space future-video supervision, plus a StateFusionActionExpert that fuses multi-layer states via learned-query pooling for direct action chunk prediction in one forward pass.

Result: Maintains strong performance on LIBERO, achieves usable multi-task performance on RoboTwin 2.0, with only 0.44B trainable parameters, 72.03ms inference latency, 4.1GiB peak GPU memory, and improved training throughput.

Conclusion: Light-WAM demonstrates that efficient, lightweight World Action Models can match or approach the performance of much larger architectures while being far more practical for real-time robot deployment.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) extend robot policy learning by incorporating future prediction as an additional training objective, encouraging the policy to encode task-relevant temporal structure in its representations. Current WAMs often rely on large-scale generative architectures that incur high training costs and inference latency, making them difficult to deploy as efficient closed-loop policies. We propose Light-WAM, a lightweight World Action Model for efficient robot manipulation. Specifically, it is built with a compact video backbone and performs future-video supervision in a downsampled latent space, reducing the cost of video co-training while retaining its benefits for representation learning. For action prediction, Light-WAM introduces the StateFusionActionExpert, which reads adapted states from multiple backbone layers, fuses them through learned-query pooling, and directly predicts action chunks in a single forward pass. This design provides an efficient interface between video backbone representations and robot actions, avoiding the need for heavy generative action experts. Experiments demonstrate that Light-WAM maintains strong performance on LIBERO and achieves usable multi-task performance on RoboTwin 2.0, while using only 0.44B trainable parameters. It also achieves 72.03ms inference latency with 4.1GiB peak GPU memory and improved training throughput.

</details>


### [74] [TIDE: Task-Isolated Diffusion for Unified Video Editing and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08260)
*Qi Liu,Gang Yue,Mingyu Yin,Lisai Zhang,Yidi Wu,Yaole Wang,Yaohui Wang,Chang Yao,Jingyuan Chen,Lin Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TIDE is a unified framework for video generation and editing that uses per-token task embeddings, a dual-path conditioning scheme (VLM + VAE), and multi-task progressive training to handle instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, and multi-reference generation in a single model, achieving SOTA across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video generation/editing capabilities are handled by separate, task-specific models. Unified attempts either require dedicated auxiliary encoders or lack mechanisms to distinguish heterogeneous conditioning tokens, struggling when the number and type of visual conditions vary across tasks.

Method: (1) Per-token task embeddings that assign each input token a task-specific identifier to disambiguate target, source, and reference tokens. (2) Dual-path conditioning scheme coupling a vision-language model (VLM) with a VAE latent path for complementary high-level semantic and fine-grained structural signals. (3) Multi-task progressive training strategy that incrementally introduces tasks of increasing complexity.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple video editing and generation benchmarks for all evaluated tasks (instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, multi-reference generation).

Conclusion: The proposed unified framework effectively harmonizes diverse video tasks, enabling smooth generalization across heterogeneous task distributions without requiring task-specific architectures.

Abstract: Recent advances in Diffusion Transformers have driven rapid progress in video generation and editing, yet these capabilities are still handled by separate, task-specific models. Building a unified framework that supports diverse video tasks remains an open challenge: existing unified attempts either require dedicated auxiliary encoders or lack explicit mechanisms to distinguish heterogeneous conditioning tokens, struggling when the number and type of visual conditions vary across tasks. We propose TIDE, a unified framework that integrates instruction-based editing, reference-guided editing, and multi-reference generation. At its core, we introduce per-token task embeddings that assign each input token a task-specific identifier, enabling the model to explicitly disambiguate target, source, and reference tokens. To simultaneously capture high-level semantic understanding and fine-grained structural fidelity, we design a dual-path conditioning scheme that couples a vision-language model with a VAE latent path for complementary signals. We further devise a multi-task progressive training strategy that incrementally introduces tasks of increasing complexity, effectively harmonizing diverse objectives and enabling smooth generalization across heterogeneous task distributions. Extensive experiments on multiple video editing and generation benchmarks demonstrate that TIDE achieves state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated tasks. Our project page is available at https://LittleWork123.github.io/tide.

</details>


### [75] [Remember with Confidence: Uncertainty Quantification for Spatio-temporal Memory with Probabilistic Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08277)
*Harry Zhang,Nicolas Gorlo,Luca Carlone*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces UQ-DAAAM, a spatial-semantic memory system that uses object-level semantic uncertainty scores to detect and actively refine unreliable VLM captions in multi-view settings, improving embodied 4D memory reliability and question answering performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM-based memory systems treat stored captions as ground truth with no mechanism to detect unreliable, noisy, or viewpoint-inconsistent descriptions.

Method: Proposes object-level semantic uncertainty score measuring cross-view semantic scatter, integrates it into UQ-DAAAM system which actively refines uncertain objects under a fixed query budget by selecting high-quality views and fusing multi-view captions.

Result: On OC-NaVQA benchmark, UQ-DAAAM achieves substantially larger uncertainty reduction and better spatio-temporal question answering than baselines.

Conclusion: Uncertainty quantification can make embodied 4D memory systems more reliable and effective for long-horizon robot operation.

Abstract: Long-horizon robot operation requires spatio-temporal memory to record the environment state and recall it for downstream reasoning. Scene graphs and retrieval-augmented systems ground VLM descriptions to persistent 3D entities with rich semantic descriptions. However, VLM captions are noisy and viewpoint-inconsistent, and existing systems treat them as an oracle with no mechanism to detect unreliable stored descriptions. We introduce object-level semantic uncertainty for multi-view VLM memory: a score that measures object-centric cross-view semantic scatter of captions and identifies semantically unresolved objects. Then, we include our uncertainty scores in an advanced spatial-semantic memory system, that we dub UQ-DAAAM. UQ-DAAAM uses this score to actively refine uncertain objects under a fixed query budget by selecting high-quality views and fusing the resulting multi-view captions into a single object description. We also derive probabilistic guarantees showing that higher-quality candidate views (as selected by our approach) are more likely to reduce uncertainty. Our experiments show that uncertainty quantification can make embodied 4D memory systems more reliable and more effective. In particular, on the OC-NaVQA benchmark, UQ-DAAAM achieves substantially larger uncertainty reduction and better spatio-temporal question answering performance than baselines.

</details>


### [76] [G2G: Exploiting Intra-Group Geometry for Inter-Group Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08284)
*Yufei Wei,Shuhao Ye,Chenxiao Hu,Yiyuan Pan,Dongyu Feng,Rong Xiong,Yue Wang,Yanmei Jiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: G2G-Bridge introduces three lightweight trainable modules on top of a frozen multi-view foundation model to recover relative 6-DoF pose between two image groups (cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry), achieving SOTA with only 32M trainable parameters and relative pose supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-view models treat all views as an unstructured set and lack explicit cross-group reasoning for relative pose estimation between two image groups, which is needed for cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry.

Method: Keeps a pre-trained multi-view foundation model frozen; adds three lightweight trainable modules: (1) a perceiver resampler, (2) a cross-group bridge with merged self-attention, and (3) a multi-frame pose head. Total trainable parameters ~32M (<6% of full model), supervised only by relative poses.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on both cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry across four datasets (indoor/outdoor simulation, real-world cross-season, zero-shot sim-to-real), outperforming baselines retrained with full supervision.

Conclusion: Efficiently bridges cross-group reasoning for relative pose estimation by augmenting a frozen foundation model with minimal trainable modules, demonstrating strong generalization and SOTA performance.

Abstract: Recovering the relative 6-DoF pose between two image groups underlies cross-sequence relocalization and multi-camera rig odometry. Each group carries known intra-group geometry from visual odometry or rig calibration, and pretrained multi-view backbones already fuse such geometry into visual features. Yet current models treat all views as an unstructured set, leaving cross-group reasoning as the missing piece. We introduce \ours{}, which keeps the foundation model entirely frozen and adds three lightweight trainable modules to bridge the two groups: a perceiver resampler, a cross-group bridge with merged self-attention, and a multi-frame pose head. The trainable footprint totals about 32M parameters, under 6\% of the full model, and is supervised only by relative poses. Across four datasets that span indoor and outdoor simulation, real-world cross-season capture, and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, \ours{} attains state-of-the-art accuracy on both tasks, while every baseline is retrained with its full original supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/WeiYuFei0217/G2G.

</details>


### [77] [HACK++: Towards More Effective Head-Aware Key-Value Compression for Efficient Visual Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08302)
*Ziran Qin,Yuchen Jiang,Mingbao Lin,Youru Lv,Hang Guo,Wen Fei,Weiyao Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HACK++ is a training-free KV cache compression framework for VAR models that classifies attention heads into Contextual (semantic) and Structural (spatial) types, then applies adaptive, head-specific compression strategies, achieving near-lossless generation with only 30% attention budget and 10% cache budget on Infinity-2B/8B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VAR models suffer from significant attention complexity and severe memory overhead due to KV cache accumulation across scales, and current one-size-fits-all compression methods perform poorly because VAR attention heads can be stably divided into two functionally distinct types (Contextual vs. Structural) with different dependencies on historical scales.

Method: HACK++ performs one-time offline calibration to classify head types and derive head-specific priors. At inference, it decouples attention from cache compression under independent budgets, using pattern-specific strategies and reliance-aware budget allocation to compress accumulated cache aggressively while bounding current-scale attention cost.

Result: On Infinity-2B/8B, HACK++ maintains near-lossless generation with only 30% attention budget and 10% cache budget, and remains robust even under 1% cache budget. Validated across text-to-image, class-conditional, and unified understanding-and-generation tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed head-aware, adaptive compression framework effectively addresses the unique challenges of KV cache accumulation in VAR models, enabling efficient inference without sacrificing generation quality.

Abstract: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models adopt a next-scale prediction paradigm, offering high-quality generation with substantially fewer decoding steps. However, existing VAR models suffer from significant attention complexity and severe memory overhead due to the accumulation of key-value (KV) caches across scales. In this paper, we tackle this challenge by introducing KV cache compression into the next-scale paradigm. We begin with an in-depth analysis of VAR attention and observe that attention heads can be stably divided into two functionally distinct categories: Contextual Heads focus on maintaining semantic consistency, while Structural Heads preserve spatial coherence. Their functional divergence makes existing one-size-fits-all compression methods perform poorly on VAR models. We further find that the two head types differ markedly in their reliance on historical scales, and that this reliance shifts across layers and generation steps, arguing for an adaptive cache budget allocation. To address these challenges, we propose HACK++, a training-free Head-Aware key-value Compression frameworK for VAR models. From a one-time offline calibration, HACK++ classifies head types and derives head-specific priors. At inference, it decouples attention from cache compression under independent budgets, bounding the current-scale attention cost while compressing the accumulated cache far more aggressively, via pattern-specific strategies and a reliance-aware budget allocation. Extensive experiments on multiple VAR models across text-to-image, class-conditional, and unified understanding-and-generation tasks validate the effectiveness and generalizability of HACK++. For example, on Infinity-2B/8B, HACK++ maintains near-lossless generation with only a 30% attention budget and a 10% cache budget, and remains robust even under a 1% cache budget.

</details>


### [78] [Set-Based Transformer for Atmospheric Compensation in Standoff LWIR Hyperspectral Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08324)
*Fabian Perez,Nicolas Quintero,Jeferson Acevedo,Hoover Rueda-Chacon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight set-based deep learning framework that jointly estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and downwelling spectrum from multiple LWIR hyperspectral measurements taken at different standoff ranges, using a sparse autoencoder with geographically coherent latent features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Atmospheric compensation in passive LWIR hyperspectral imaging under standoff geometry is essential but has been largely overlooked due to practical and modeling difficulties.

Method: A lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements at different standoff ranges as input, jointly estimating transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and a shared downwelling spectrum. Uses a sparse autoencoder for latent feature analysis.

Result: Demonstrates low spectral distortion across all estimated products on a MODTRAN-generated standoff LWIR dataset. Latent features activate on geographically coherent subsets without location supervision.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively performs atmospheric compensation for standoff LWIR hyperspectral imaging, with publicly available dataset and code.

Abstract: Passive long-wave infrared (LWIR) hyperspectral imaging under a standoff geometry depends on atmospheric absorption and emission, as well as reflected radiance, thus making atmospheric compensation essential to get knowledge of a target of interest. Despite its importance, this compensation has been largely overlooked due to its practical and modeling difficulty. In this paper, we present a lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements, collected at different standoff ranges, as input and jointly estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and a shared downwelling spectrum. We analyze the learned representation with a sparse autoencoder and observe that several latent features do activate on geographically coherent subsets of the test data despite the absence of location supervision. Experiments on a MODTRAN generated standoff LWIR dataset demonstrate low spectral distortion across all estimated products. The dataset and code is publicly available at: https://factral.co/SAE-LWIR/

</details>


### [79] [SMI: Efficient Self-Supervised Learning via Mutual-Information-Inspired Dependency Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08332)
*Pritam Mishra,Coloma Ballester,Dimosthenis Karatzas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SMI is a lightweight self-supervised learning method that uses nonlinear dependency optimization on sample-level correlations instead of high-dimensional feature correlation matrices, achieving competitive performance with lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SSL methods rely on large batch sizes, memory banks, momentum encoders, or global synchronization, leading to high computational cost and training complexity.

Method: Proposes Semantic Mutual Information (SMI), a mutual-information-inspired objective under Gaussian assumptions that optimizes a sample-level dependency matrix via nonlinear transformation of pairwise correlations.

Result: Competitive linear evaluation on ImageNet with ResNet-50; improved transfer performance on low-resource and fine-grained benchmarks over Barlow Twins; better alignment-redundancy balance, feature diversity, and spatially localized semantics.

Conclusion: Nonlinear dependency optimization is an effective and computationally efficient alternative to conventional correlation-based SSL objectives.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable representation learning performance, but many existing methods rely on large batch sizes, memory banks, momentum encoders, or global synchronization mechanisms that substantially increase computational cost and training complexity. In this work, we propose Semantic Mutual Information (SMI), a lightweight self-supervised objective derived from a mutual-information-inspired dependency formulation under Gaussian assumptions. Unlike conventional correlation matching objectives that operate on high-dimensional feature correlation matrices, SMI performs optimization on a sample-level dependency matrix through a nonlinear transformation of pairwise correlations. This formulation induces distinct optimization dynamics that emphasize strongly dependent semantic pairs while maintaining representation diversity. Experimental results on ImageNet using a ResNet-50 backbone demonstrate that SMI achieves competitive linear evaluation performance relative to state-of-the-art SSL approaches while substantially reducing computational complexity. Across multiple low-resource benchmarks, SMI consistently improves transfer performance over Barlow Twins, particularly on fine-grained datasets. Furthermore, analyses of optimization dynamics and representation geometry suggest improved alignment--redundancy balance, greater feature diversity, and more spatially localized semantic representations. These results indicate that nonlinear dependency optimization provides an effective and computationally efficient alternative to conventional correlation-based self-supervised learning objectives.

</details>


### [80] [Beyond Raw Signals: Undecoded Generative Latents as Privileged Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08336)
*Cristian Sbrolli,Nicolas Michel,Matteo Matteucci,Toshihiko Yamasaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Direct Latent Augmentation (DLA) to avoid the inefficient decode-encode loop in multimodal learning, and Multilayer Explicit Simulated Synesthesia (MESSy) to transfer privileged latent knowledge to unimodal students via predictive objectives, achieving better accuracy and alignment with physical properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal models face prohibitive inference costs and require scarce paired datasets. Existing generative augmentation methods introduce a "Decode-Encode Loop" where rich latents are decoded to noisy signals and then re-encoded by the classifier, wasting capacity and information.

Method: 1) Direct Latent Augmentation (DLA): directly uses undecoded generative latents as privileged information to bypass the decode-encode bottleneck. 2) Multilayer Explicit Simulated Synesthesia (MESSy): transfers dense latent knowledge to a visual student using a predictive objective instead of rigid representation matching, preserving the student's native visual features.

Result: The proposed framework significantly outperforms raw data augmentation and traditional distillation methods in empirical evaluations.

Conclusion: The approach produces highly accurate unimodal students with "synesthetic" latent structures inherently aligned with unobserved physical properties, demonstrating effective cross-modal knowledge transfer without paired data at inference.

Abstract: While multimodal integration significantly improves computer vision models, deploying them incurs prohibitive inference costs and requires scarce, perfectly paired datasets. Recent methods address this data bottleneck by synthesizing missing modalities via generative AI, yet they introduce a severe inefficiency: the Decode-Encode Loop. Specifically, information-rich generative latents are decoded into noisy raw signals, forcing the downstream classifier to waste capacity re-encoding them. To bypass this bottleneck, we propose Direct Latent Augmentation (DLA), utilizing undecoded generative latents directly as privileged information. Furthermore, to transfer this dense knowledge to a purely visual student, we introduce Multilayer Explicit Simulated Synesthesia (MESSy). Instead of enforcing rigid representation matching, which forces the student to distort its native visual features to accommodate complex multimodal topologies, MESSy uses a predictive objective to safely internalize these physical priors. Empirical results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms raw data augmentation and traditional distillation. Ultimately, our approach yields highly accurate unimodal students with ``synesthetic'' latent structures that are inherently aligned with physical properties they have never directly observed.

</details>


### [81] [Self-Supervised Vision Transformers for CBCT-Based Detection of Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08364)
*Shradhdha Trivedi,Vrundan Sojitra,Mariela Padilla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper studies how DINO-family self-supervised ViTs transfer to CBCT for TMJ OA detection, finding that partially unfreezing the last two transformer blocks boosts performance from 0.671 to 0.902 AUC, outperforming other backbones, and showing adaptation strategy matters more than backbone choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TMJ OA is a prevalent condition with subtle osseous changes on CBCT that are hard to detect automatically. The authors aim to understand how well DINO-family self-supervised ViTs transfer to this low-data medical imaging domain.

Method: A slice-based pipeline using frozen or partially adapted ViT backbones (DINOv1, DINOv2, DINOv2+reg, RAD-DINO) with per-slice encoding and attention-based MIL aggregation for patient-level binary classification, plus systematic ablation of unfreezing strategies.

Result: Partial unfreezing of the final two transformer blocks was decisive, improving AUC from 0.671 to 0.902, outperforming DINOv1 (0.867), DINOv2+reg (0.774), supervised ImageNet baseline (0.843), and adaptation strategy is a stronger performance driver than backbone choice.

Conclusion: Provides practical guidance for adapting DINO-family foundation models in low-data medical imaging: adaptation strategy (partial unfreezing) matters more than backbone selection.

Abstract: Temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJ OA) is a prevalent degenerative condition whose osseous changes are often subtle on cone-beam CT (CBCT), making automated detection challenging. We study how well the DINO family of self-supervised vision transformers -- DINOv1, DINOv2, DINOv2+reg, and RAD-DINO (a radiology-pretrained variant) -- transfers to CBCT, asking how much backbone adaptation is needed and of what kind. We propose a simple slice-based pipeline using Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones: axial CBCT slices are encoded per-slice by a frozen or partially adapted ViT and aggregated via attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) for patient-level binary OA/Normal classification. Through systematic ablation across unfreezing strategies and aggregation designs on a multi-source CBCT dataset, we find that partial unfreezing of the final two transformer blocks is the decisive factor, improving AUC from 0.671 (fully frozen DINOv2) to 0.902. This outperforms DINOv1 (0.867), DINOv2+reg (0.774), and a supervised ImageNet ViT-B/16 baseline (0.843).
