### [134] [Rethinking 3D Shape Generation: Diffusion over Superquadrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08957)
*Zhiyang Liu,Wanze Li,Yuwei Wu,Chengran Yuan,Jiawei Sun,Rui Zheng,Marcelo H Ang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes moving 3D diffusion models from dense geometry representations (voxels, point clouds, meshes) to compact superquadric primitives, reducing dimensionality from millions of values to just 7KB parameters per shape. This enables faster generation (0.6s per shape), resolution-free decoding, part-level editing, and constraint-based design while maintaining competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D diffusion models operate on high-cardinality spaces (voxel/SDF grids, meshes, point clouds), which are computationally and memory intensive, limiting scalability in resolution and controllability.

Method: Represent each 3D shape as a small set of superquadrics (7KB parameters including pose, size, shape) instead of dense geometric representations. Diffusion operates on these compact primitives rather than thousands/millions of values.

Result: Achieves competitive surface-fidelity and distributional performance on standard benchmarks after point-cloud decoding, while enabling efficient generation within 0.6s per shape and supporting resolution-free decoding, part-level editing, and constraint-based design.

Conclusion: Moving diffusion from dense geometry to compact superquadric primitives significantly improves scalability, efficiency, and controllability of 3D shape generation without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Diffusion models have advanced 3D shape generation, yet most methods still denoise in high-cardinality spaces (e.g., voxel/SDF grids, meshes, or point clouds), which is computationally and memory intensive and makes it difficult to scale in terms of both higher resolution and stronger controllability. We rethink the diffusion representation and propose to move diffusion from dense geometry to compact geometric primitives, representing each shape as a small set of superquadrics. Instead of operating on thousands to millions of geometric representation values, we leverage 7KB superquadric parameters (pose, size, and shape), drastically reducing diffusion-state dimensionality and per-step compute/memory. Our diffusion-over-superquadrics improves scalability by supporting broader capabilities (e.g., resolution-free point-cloud decoding, part-level editing, and constraint-based design) and achieving competitive surface-fidelity and distributional performance on standard benchmarks after point-cloud decoding, while enabling efficient generation within 0.6s per shape for most conditions.

</details>


### [135] [ChinaHeritaQA: A Culturally-Grounded Visual Question Answering Dataset for World Heritage Sites in China](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08959)
*Yi Zhang,Bolei Ma,Yong Cao,Chengyan Wu,Daniel Hershcovich,Anna-Carolina Haensch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ChinaHeritaQA is a multimodal benchmark with 2,279 images and 14,133 bilingual QA pairs about UNESCO Chinese heritage sites. It tests VLMs across seven cognitive dimensions. Top models surpass humans on average but struggle with cultural reasoning, despite strong visual recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate and benchmark the cultural reasoning abilities of vision-language models on Chinese UNESCO World Heritage sites, addressing the gap where VLMs excel at visual recognition but lack culturally grounded understanding.

Method: Created a dataset of 2,279 in-the-wild images with 14,133 bilingual (Chinese/English) multiple-choice QA pairs spanning seven cognitive dimensions, guided by a UNESCO-aligned heritage ontology with rigorous human annotation.

Result: Top VLMs exceed human average performance but show substantial task-level variation—strong at visual recognition, weak at cultural reasoning. Performance varies by dynasty and region.

Conclusion: Strong visual retrieval does not extend to cultural and historical understanding; the dataset supports future research on culturally aware multimodal learning.

Abstract: We introduce ChinaHeritaQA, a multimodal benchmark dataset for evaluating the cultural reasoning abilities of vision-language models (VLMs) on UNESCO World Heritage sites in China. The dataset comprises 2,279 in-the-wild images paired with 14,133 bilingual (Chinese/English) multiple-choice QA pairs spanning seven cognitive dimensions, from basic identity recognition to historical periodization and architectural analysis. Guided by a UNESCO-aligned heritage ontology and verified through rigorous human annotation, the dataset ensures linguistic quality and factual consistency. Evaluations of state-of-the-art VLMs reveal that while top models exceed human performance on average, substantial task-level variation emerges: models excel at visual recognition but struggle with culturally grounded reasoning. Performance also varies by dynasty and region. ChinaHeritaQA reveals that strong visual retrieval does not extend to cultural and historical understanding. We release the dataset to support future research on culturally aware multimodal learning.

</details>


### [136] [EPS3D: End-to-End Feed-Forward 3D Panoptic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08980)
*Runsong Zhu,Jiaxin Guo,Xiaoyang Guo,Zhengzhe Liu,Ka-Hei Hui,Wei Yin,Kai Chen,Wei Chen,Weiqiang Ren,Yunhui Liu,Pheng-Ann Heng,Chi-Wing Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EPS3D is an end-to-end feed-forward framework for open-vocabulary 3D panoptic segmentation that uses multi-view images and distillation-based training to achieve fast, consistent 3D scene understanding, outperforming SOTA by +13% mIoU on Replica.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods rely on additional preprocessing steps, leading to error accumulation and lack of 3D consistency in open-vocabulary 3D panoptic segmentation.

Method: An end-to-end architecture with distillation-based training on diverse 3D scenes to predict 3D-aware semantic and instance features from multi-view images, plus a mutual enhancement module (Ins2Sem and Sem2Ins) for semantic-instance consistency.

Result: Outperforms SOTA baselines with +13% mIoU on Replica, processes each scene in ~1 second, and supports robotic manipulation and 3D scene editing.

Conclusion: EPS3D provides an efficient, accurate, and coherent solution for open-vocabulary 3D panoptic segmentation with practical downstream task applicability.

Abstract: This paper introduces EPS3D, a new end-to-end feed-forward framework for open-vocabulary 3D panoptic segmentation. Unlike existing methods relying on additional preprocessing, we design an end-to-end architecture, with a distillation-based training strategy on diverse 3D scenes to predict 3D-aware semantic and instance features from multi-view images, improving 3D consistency and avoiding error accumulation. We further propose a mutual enhancement module to enforce inherent semantic-instance consistency. By aligning semantics within instances (Ins2Sem) and refining instance features with semantic guidance (Sem2Ins), we achieve more coherent 3D scene understanding. Ultimately, EPS3D outperforms SOTA baselines on two benchmarks (e.g., +13% mIoU for semantics on Replica) with high efficiency (e.g., 1s per scene), supporting tasks like robotic manipulation and 3D scene editing.

</details>


### [137] [Scaling by Diversified Experience for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09009)
*Leiyu Wang,Zhaofengnian Wang,Xueqi Li,Luoyi Fan,Cewu Lu,Nanyang Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SyVLA introduces Intention Decoupling to separate reasoning from control, and similar-sample guided RL to stabilize policy learning, achieving superior robotic task performance and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models struggle in real-world deployment because high-level reasoning is entangled with low-level control, and policy optimization is unstable.

Method: (1) Intention Decoupling algorithm to isolate control-relevant features from reasoning contexts; (2) Similar-sample guided RL pipeline to stabilize policy updates and mitigate distribution shift.

Result: Superior task success rates and stronger out-of-distribution generalization on real-world robotic tasks and multi-modal benchmarks, while preserving core vision-language capabilities.

Conclusion: SyVLA effectively decouples reasoning and control with diversified experience training, enabling robust and generalizable VLA models for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models face significant challenges in real-world deployment due to the entanglement of high-level reasoning with low-level control, and the instability of policy optimization. In this paper, we introduce SyVLA, a robust VLA model trained with diversified experiences. We propose an Intention Decoupling algorithm to isolate control-relevant features from reasoning contexts and a similar-sample guided RL pipeline to stabilize policy updates and mitigate distribution shift. Extensive experiments on real-world robotic tasks and multi-modal benchmarks demonstrate that SyVLA achieves superior task success rates and stronger out-of-distribution generalization compared to existing methods, while effectively preserving core vision-language capabilities. Codes and Datasets is released on \href{https://sy-vla.github.io/}{project page}.

</details>


### [138] [Frequency Decoupled Framework for Screen Content Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09029)
*Xufei Wang,Qicheng Zhang,Qi Wu,Ziyang Gu,Shizhuang Weng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FDF is a frequency decoupled framework for Screen Content Image Super-Resolution that separates amplitude (periodic patterns) and phase (coherent context) via APFN, then integrates them using OAIF-Net with implicit neural representations, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing implicit neural representation methods for SCISR overlook inherent frequency characteristics of screen content images, leading to suboptimal performance in recovering regular textures and global configuration.

Method: Proposes FDF with two components: (1) APFN separates images into amplitude and phase streams—ACM organizes high-energy amplitude into prototypes for periodic patterns, PCSA progressively reinforces configuration via consistency propagation; (2) OAIF-Net integrates periodic and coherent implicit representations for joint exploitation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art SCISR performance at multiple scales across four public SCI datasets. Ablation studies confirm effectiveness of each component.

Conclusion: Frequency decoupling from a phasor perspective, combined with bespoke implicit representations, effectively recovers regular textures and global configuration in screen content images.

Abstract: Methods based on implicit neural representations have demonstrated superior performance in Screen Content Image Super-Resolution (SCISR) . However, they overlooked the inherent frequency characteristics, leading to suboptimal performance. We propose a frequency decoupled framework (FDF) that rethinks SCISR from a phasor perspective by capturing structured energy in amplitude and relational continuity in phase, and jointly exploiting them with bespoke implicit representations to faithfully recover the regular textures and global configuration of Screen Content Image (SCI).
  Amplitude-Phase Factorization Network (APFN) first separates images into amplitude and phase streams, where Amplitude Clustering Module (ACM) organizes sparse yet high-energy amplitude responses into representative prototypes for periodic pattern extraction, while Phase Consistency Self-Attention (PCSA) progressively reinforces configuration through continuous consistency propagation.
  And Oscillation-Anharmonic Implicit Fitting Network (OAIF-Net) integrates periodic and coherent implicit representations for efficient exploitation of the periodic patterns and coherent context embedded in SCI.
  Experimental results show FDF achieves state-of-the-art SCISR performance at multiple scales across four public SCI datasets. Ablation experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of each component in extracting and exploiting periodic patterns and coherent context.

</details>


### [139] [CRANE: Knowledge Editing for Reasoning MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09033)
*Han Huang,Hao Wang,Mengqi Zhang,Shu Wu,Qiang Liu,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies three failure modes in knowledge editing for reasoning multimodal LLMs (Structural Collapse, Cognitive Dissonance, Shallow Internalization), proposes a CoT-aware evaluation protocol (ReasonEdit-Bench), and introduces CRANE—a retrieval-augmented framework achieving 96.9% Grounded Success without per-edit parameter modification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods appear successful under traditional metrics (up to 100% teacher-forcing accuracy) but fail catastrophically when the model's chain-of-thought reasoning process is examined (Grounded Success as low as 0%). The three identified failure modes (Structural Collapse, Cognitive Dissonance, Shallow Internalization) were previously unrecognized, and no suitable benchmark existed for evaluating knowledge editing on reasoning MLLMs.

Method: The authors propose (1) ReasonEdit-Bench, a CoT-aware evaluation protocol with conflict stratification, multi-level probes, and multi-hop portability tests; and (2) CRANE, a retrieval-augmented framework combining a modality-aware dual-library retrieval system with two-phase training (SFT for structural initialization, then GRPO with Cognitive Routing Reward to arbitrate between visual priors and edit facts).

Result: On ReasonEdit-Bench: 96.9% Grounded Success on conflict scenarios, 96.9% intermediate entity usage in multi-hop chains, 97.6% text-locality and 68.1% image-locality Edit Independence. On MMEVOKE benchmark: 87.0% under gold retrieval.

Conclusion: Knowledge editing for reasoning MLLMs requires CoT-aware evaluation; retrieval-augmented approaches like CRANE (without parameter modification) outperform weight-modifying methods that cause structural collapse or shallow internalization.

Abstract: The emergence of reasoning multimodal large language models (MLLMs), which generate explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning before producing answers, has introduced a new challenge for knowledge editing: methods that appear successful under traditional metrics (teacher-forcing accuracy up to 100%) can fail severely when the model's reasoning process is examined (Grounded Success as low as 0%). We identify three failure modes: (1) Structural Collapse, where weight-modifying methods destroy the CoT format; (2) Cognitive Dissonance, where the model's reasoning chain actively rejects the injected edit fact based on visual evidence; and (3) Shallow Internalization, where methods succeed on exact queries but fail on rephrase or multi-hop variants. On reasoning MLLMs, these modes interact: methods that generalize (FT, LoRA) trigger format collapse, while methods without deep modification cannot generalize. To expose these failures, we propose a CoT-aware evaluation protocol and construct ReasonEdit-Bench, with conflict stratification, multi-level probes, and multi-hop portability tests.
  We propose CRANE, a retrieval-augmented framework that requires no per-edit parameter modification. CRANE combines a modality-aware dual-library retrieval system with a two-phase training strategy: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for structural initialization, followed by GRPO with a Cognitive Routing Reward that trains the model to arbitrate between visual priors and injected edit facts. On ReasonEdit-Bench, CRANE achieves 96.9% Grounded Success on conflict scenarios and 96.9% intermediate entity usage in multi-hop chains, with 97.6% text-locality and 68.1% image-locality Edit Independence. On the out-of-distribution MMEVOKE benchmark, CRANE reaches 87.0% under gold retrieval.

</details>


### [140] [Leveraging NeRF-Rendered Images for 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09034)
*Mizuki Morikawa,Yuta Shimizu,Chunyu Li,Yusuke Monno,Masatoshi Okutomi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes using NeRF-rendered images to train 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for street scenes, combining NeRF's higher quality with 3DGS's faster rendering by removing transient objects and adding bird's-eye views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NeRF and 3DGS show complementary strengths (NeRF: higher quality, 3DGS: faster speed). The paper aims to combine these advantages for street scene novel view synthesis.

Method: Uses a pre-trained street-specific NeRF to generate training images for 3DGS. NeRF-rendered images remove transient objects from street views and create bird's-eye views as additional training data. A diffusion-based image enhancement further improves additional view quality.

Result: Experiments on 1 synthetic and 2 real datasets show improved street-scene rendering while maintaining 3DGS speed and NeRF quality.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully bridges NeRF and 3DGS, achieving both high rendering quality and fast speed for street scenes.

Abstract: Neural radiance field (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) are two mainstream approaches for novel view synthesis. They often show complementary performance, i.e., 3DGS demonstrating faster rendering speed and NeRF demonstrating higher rendering quality. Motivated by this, we propose leveraging NeRF-rendered images for 3DGS. Specifically, we target street scenes and utilize a pre-trained street-specific NeRF method to produce training images for a target 3DGS method. In our 3DGS training, NeRF-rendered images are used to remove transient objects in street-level input views and to generate bird's-eye views as additional views, inheriting the higher-quality rendering of NeRF into 3DGS. We further incorporate a diffusion-based image enhancement to improve the image quality of the additional views. Experimental results on one synthetic and two real datasets demonstrate that our proposed method improves street-scene rendering while preserving the speed of 3DGS and the quality of NeRF.

</details>


### [141] [MilliVid: Hierarchical Latents for Long-Range Consistency in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09056)
*Ishaan Preetam Chandratreya,David Charatan,Basile Van Hoorick,Sergey Zakharov,Vitor Guizilini,Phillip Isola,Vincent Sitzmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a coarse-to-fine rollout strategy for video generation using multi-scale token hierarchies, where coarser tokens capture scene layout and semantics while finer tokens handle appearance details, enabling long-range consistency with reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-range temporal consistency in video generation is challenging due to impractical transformer sequence lengths required for even a few dozen frames. The authors aim to mitigate this issue.

Method: (1) Pre-train an autoencoder that compresses each frame into a hierarchical token representation (coarse to fine). (2) Train a video diffusion model to generate tokens using coarse-to-fine rollout, controlling detail levels at each step to prioritize long-range consistency for perceptually important content.

Result: On a custom long Minecraft video dataset, the proposed approach produces substantially more consistent rollouts compared to existing baselines.

Conclusion: Coarse-to-fine multi-scale token generation effectively preserves long-range consistency (geometry, object permanence) while reducing computational cost on less perceptually relevant details.

Abstract: Video generative models have become increasingly powerful, but long-range consistency remains challenging to achieve because even a few dozen frames require impractically long transformer sequence lengths. We show that this issue can be mitigated by generating video using coarse-to-fine rollout within a multi-scale token space. Our approach is simple: first, we pre-train an autoencoder that compresses each frame into a hierarchy of tokens, with levels ranging from the typical latent resolution to only a handful of tokens per frame. The coarsest levels capture the most consequential information, such as scene layout and semantics, while finer levels add high-frequency appearance and texture. Then, we train a video diffusion model to generate these tokens using coarse-to-fine rollout. By carefully controlling the level of detail at which frames are generated and used as context during each rollout step, we are able to preserve long-range consistency in geometry and object permanence while spending less compute on the long-range consistency of less perceptually relevant details. We validate this approach using a custom dataset of long Minecraft videos, where it produces substantially more consistent rollouts compared to existing baselines.

</details>


### [142] [See More, Think Deeper: Query-Expanded Visual Evidence and Answer-Clue Guided Reflection for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09064)
*Shuning Wang,Zhiheng Wu,YiNuo Lu,Naiming Liu,Chen Jia,Bowen Liu,Shuo Nie,Weijie Zhu,Yumeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoVER is a framework for Video-LLMs that improves long-video understanding by dynamically gathering query-expanded visual evidence (See More) and verifying answers through answer-specific visual feedback (Think Deeper), achieving state-of-the-art performance even at 7B scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Video-LLMs for long-video understanding suffer from two key limitations: (1) evidence acquisition relying on a single search intent, and (2) answer generation lacking effective visual feedback mechanisms.

Method: CoVER introduces two complementary mechanisms: (1) "See More" - dynamic query-expanded visual evidence gathering, and (2) "Think Deeper" - verifying draft answers with answer-specific visual feedback, shifting from answer-centric to evidence-centric, visually verifiable reasoning.

Result: CoVER-7B substantially outperforms same-scale models and surpasses state-of-the-art closed-source models on certain metrics.

Conclusion: The evidence-centric and visually verifiable reasoning approach effectively addresses key limitations in long-video understanding, demonstrating that small models can outperform larger closed-source counterparts with the right framework.

Abstract: Recent advances in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have enabled performance on long-video understanding tasks. However, existing methods still face two key limitations: evidence acquisition often relies on a single search intent, and answer generation lacks an effective visual feedback mechanism. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{CoVER}, a Comprehensive Visual Evidence and Reflection framework for long-video understanding. CoVER enables Video-LLMs to \textbf{See More} by dynamically gathering query-expanded visual evidence, and \textbf{Think Deeper} by verifying draft answers with effective answer-specific visual feedback. Together, these mechanisms shift long-video understanding from answer-centric generation to evidence-centric and visually verifiable reasoning. Experimental results show that CoVER-7B substantially outperforms models with the same parameter scale and even surpasses state-of-the-art closed-source models on certain metrics.

</details>


### [143] [REFINE: Super-efficient 3D Gaussian Splatting Pruning via Rendering-Free Primitive Importance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09074)
*Zhang Chen,Shuai Wan,Mengting Yu,Fuzheng Yang,Junhui Hou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: REFINE is a fast 3DGS pruning method using a rendering-free Hessian-based importance metric that achieves 3000x complexity reduction while maintaining competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS pruning methods suffer from either severe quality degradation or prohibitive computational overhead, motivating the need for an efficient yet high-fidelity pruning approach.

Method: REFINE introduces a rendering-free primitive importance metric via an analytically approximated Hessian field that models visibility, projection geometry, and adaptive hyperparameters, bypassing costly forward rendering passes.

Result: Achieves 3,000x reduction in pruning-related computational complexity compared to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining highly competitive rendering quality across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: REFINE offers a practical solution for efficient 3DGS pruning that balances quality and computational efficiency at an unprecedented scale.

Abstract: Existing pruning methods for 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) suffer from either severe quality degradation or prohibitive computational overhead. In this paper, we propose REFINE, a highly accelerated 3DGS pruning framework centered on a novel rendering-free primitive importance metric. Our approach leverages an analytically approximated, rendering-aware Hessian field to quantify the expected perceptual error induced by the removal of individual primitives. By modeling the joint modulation of visibility, projection geometry and the content adaptive hyperparameter, we entirely bypass costly forward rendering passes and derive an anisotropic perceptual weight field that serves as a high-fidelity proxy for primitive importance. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that REFINE maintains highly competitive rendering quality while achieving an unprecedented $3,000\times$ reduction in pruning-related computational complexity compared to state-of-the-art pruning methods.

</details>


### [144] [Beyond Scalar Rewards by Internalizing Reasoning into Score Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09076)
*Xin Jin,Huanqia Cai,Zhen Li,Zechao Zhan,Dengyang Jiang,Aiming Hao,Yuming Jiang,Chunle Guo,Peng Gao,Ming-Ming Cheng,Steven C. H. Hoi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Z-Reward proposes a teacher-student framework for text-to-image reward modeling, where a large VLM teacher uses reasoning to predict score distributions, and a compact student internalizes these distributions without explicit reasoning, achieving high preference accuracy and serving as a differentiable optimization signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual preference is inherently subjective and should be modeled as a distribution over rubric scores rather than a deterministic scalar. Existing reward models (scalar, score-token, pairwise) over-compress uncertainty, while reasoning-based generative rewards provide better judgments but are costly and hard to use directly for optimization.

Method: Z-Reward uses a teacher-student framework: (1) A large VLM teacher trained via Group-wise Direct Score Optimization (GDSO), combining policy-gradient rewards from distribution expectations with pointwise and pairwise supervision on score distributions and gaps. (2) A compact VLM student trained via Reasoning-Internalized Score Distillation (RISD), transferring the teacher's reasoning-conditioned distribution without requiring explicit reasoning at inference.

Result: The 27B GDSO teacher achieves 89.6% human preference accuracy (outperforming SFT, RewardDance, GRPO). The 9B RISD student reaches 88.6% (outperforming OPD, closely matching the teacher). Z-Reward as a differentiable reward signal yields 41.3% net human-preference improvement over SFT baseline.

Conclusion: Z-Reward effectively decouples reasoning-heavy judgment from efficient deployment, providing strong reward signals for text-to-image post-training while maintaining practicality for downstream optimization.

Abstract: Reward models are central to text-to-image post-training, but visual preference is subjective and better represented as a distribution over rubric scores than as a deterministic scalar. Existing scalar, score-token, and pairwise reward models over-compress uncertainty and fine-grained score differences, while reasoning-based generative rewards provide stronger judgments but are costly to deploy and difficult to use as direct optimization signals. We propose Z-Reward, a teacher-student reward modeling framework that decouples reasoning-heavy judgment from efficient reward deployment. The teacher is a large VLM that uses reasoning to infer rubric-aligned score distributions, and is trained with Group-wise Direct Score Optimization (GDSO), which combines policy-gradient rewards from distribution expectations with direct pointwise and pairwise supervision on score distributions and score gaps. The student is trained with Reasoning-Internalized Score Distillation (RISD), which transfers the teacher's reasoning-conditioned score distribution into a compact VLM without requiring explicit reasoning chains at inference time. On our internally annotated evaluation set, the 27B GDSO teacher reaches 89.6% human preference accuracy, outperforming SFT, RewardDance, and GRPO, while the 9B RISD student reaches 88.6%, outperforming the OPD baseline and closely matching the larger teacher. We further show that Z-Reward can serve as a differentiable reward signal for text-to-image optimization, yielding a 41.3% net human-preference improvement over the SFT baseline.

</details>


### [145] [Edge-Constrained UAV Small-Object Detection with P2 Enhancement and Quantum-Inspired Lightweight Structure Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09081)
*Wuming Lei,Yanbin Gao,Mingyan Sun,Xiaobin Li,Xuechen Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper enhances YOLOX-Nano for UAV object detection by adding a P2 high-resolution detection branch and using a quantum-inspired evolutionary algorithm (QIEA) for lightweight structure screening, achieving significant improvements in small-object detection accuracy on VisDrone dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV object detection requires compact detectors that preserve small-object details under onboard constraints, but repeated downsampling in lightweight networks weakens shallow spatial information, and manually adding modules may increase cost without stable gains.

Method: Combines a P2 high-resolution detection branch with a quantum-inspired evolutionary algorithm (QIEA) for lightweight structure screening; search space defined by lightweight priority and task specificity; evaluation jointly considers accuracy, FLOPs, latency, memory, and recall.

Result: On VisDrone, P2 branch increases AP_small by 31.10% over YOLOX-Nano baseline. YOLOX-Nano+-P2 improves AP_50:95 by 17.5% and AP_small by 44.9% vs NanoDet-Plus. QIEA-selected candidate achieves highest Recall_50, but +P2 remains best AP-oriented variant after full training.

Conclusion: Supports P2 as the main small-object enhancement path and QIEA as a lightweight tool for candidate screening and accuracy-cost analysis; proxy rankings from evolutionary search do not necessarily transfer to final AP scores after full training.

Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) object detection requires compact detectors that retain small-object details under onboard computation and memory constraints. Repeated downsampling inlightweight networks weakens shallow spatial information, while manually adding attention orfusion modules may increase cost without stable gains. This study analyzes YOLOX-Nano underedge-deployment constraints by combining a P2 high-resolution detection branch with a quantum-inspired evolutionary algorithm (QIEA) for lightweight structure screening. The search space isdefined by lightweight priority and task specificity, and the evaluation jointly considers accuracy,floating-point operations (FLOPs), latency, memory consumption, and recall. On VisDrone, theP2 branch increases APamall by 31.10% over the YOLOX-Nano baseline. Compared with NanoDet-Plus with similar model size, YOLOX-Nano+-P2 improves APs0.ss by 17.5% and APamal by 44.9%.The QIEA-selected candidate obtains the highest Recallso, but +P2 remains the strongest AP-oriented variant after full training. Full 100-epoch verification of Random-best, GA-best, andSA/QUBO-best candidates further shows that proxy rankings do not necessarily transfer to finalAPse9s. These results support using P2 as the main small-object enhancement path and QIEA as alightweight tool for candidate screening and accuracy-cost analysis. The source code, configurationfiles, diagnostic scripts, and summarized results are available at https://github.com/Ming23233/UAV-QIEA-Edge-Detection

</details>


### [146] [Driving Video Retrieval for Complex Queries with Structured Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09109)
*Manyi Yao,Sparsh Garg,Christian Shelton,Amit Roy-Chowdhury,Abhishek Aich*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STRIVE-D is a data-calibrated retrieval framework for driving videos that combines rule-based, vision-language, and keyword-based signals, using weakly labeled in-domain data to calibrate rules, achieving up to 84% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video retrieval methods (vision-language, keyword-based, rule-based) fail to accurately capture dynamic driving events (e.g., cut-ins, hard braking) because motion is often not explicitly described in text, and rule-based approaches are brittle when assumptions mismatch real data.

Method: STRIVE-D uses weakly labeled in-domain videos to estimate query rule reliability, adapts rules that mismatch observed data, and fuses calibrated rule scores with vision-language and keyword-based retrieval signals.

Result: Across three driving benchmarks, including newly released human-annotated event data on DrivingDojo, STRIVE-D delivers up to 84% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Data-calibrated rule fusion significantly improves driving video retrieval, especially for dynamic events not well captured by text-based or brittle rule-based methods alone.

Abstract: Video retrieval at scale is central to data curation and safety validation in autonomous driving, where users want to find not only scenes but also dynamic events such as cut-ins and hard braking. Existing vision-language and keyword-based retrieval methods often miss these events because the relevant motion may not be explicitly described in text or captured by lexical overlap. Rule-based retrieval can encode such events more directly, but it is brittle: generated or hand-written rules often fail when their assumptions do not match real driving data. We propose STRIVE-D, a data-calibrated retrieval framework for driving videos. It uses weakly labeled in-domain videos to estimate when a query rule is reliable, adapt rules that mismatch observed data, and fuse calibrated rule scores with vision-language and keyword-based retrieval signals. Across three driving benchmarks, including newly released human-annotated event data on DrivingDojo, STRIVE-D delivers up to 84% relative improvement in top-1 accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [147] [HDRAgent: An Agentic Framework for Multi-Exposure HDR Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09110)
*Weiyu Zhou,Tao Hu,Yijian Wang,Xiaogang Xu,Ruixing Wang,Qingsen Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HDRAgent is the first agent-driven multi-exposure HDR imaging framework that adaptively selects reconstruction strategies based on scene conditions, using MLLM-based scene perception, contextual knowledge matching, and feedback mechanisms to reduce ghosting artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-exposure HDR methods follow a fixed feed-forward paradigm, making them prone to ghosting artifacts in complex dynamic scenes.

Method: HDRAgent consists of three key components: (1) Fine-grained Contextual Knowledge Matching (FCM) module using MLLM-derived scene perception to retrieve relevant cases/tools; (2) Perception-Distortion feedback mechanism for post-execution quality assessment; (3) Agent-guided generative alignment strategy for handling extreme motion via dynamic-region parsing.

Result: HDRAgent effectively reduces ghosting and local artifacts while achieving competitive or superior objective performance and visual quality.

Conclusion: The agent-driven adaptive framework successfully addresses ghosting artifacts in dynamic HDR imaging, outperforming fixed reconstruction paradigms.

Abstract: Most existing multi-exposure HDR methods follow a fixed feed-forward reconstruction paradigm, making them prone to ghosting artifacts in complex dynamic scenes. To address this issue, we propose HDRAgent, the first agent-driven framework for HDR imaging, which adaptively selects reconstruction strategies according to the current scene conditions. Specifically, to provide scene-specific prior knowledge, we introduce a fine-grained contextual knowledge matching (FCM) module. This module leverages multimodal large language model (MLLM)-derived scene perception to retrieve relevant historical cases and tool knowledge, organizing them into structured evidence for MLLM-based adaptive tool scheduling. In addition, we propose a perception--distortion feedback mechanism that transforms post-execution quality assessment and artifact diagnosis into structured feedback, which is accumulated in historical memory to help subsequent contextual knowledge refinement and strategy selection. Furthermore, considering that extreme motion can invalidate alignment methods, we design an agent-guided generative alignment strategy that uses MLLM-based dynamic-region parsing to reconstruct unreliable contents in non-reference frames under reference-frame guidance. Experiments demonstrate that HDRAgent effectively reduces ghosting and local artifacts while achieving competitive or superior objective performance and visual quality.

</details>


### [148] [Illumination-Invariant Anomaly Detection for Sub-Canopy UAV Multispectral Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09111)
*Likun Chen,Yanfeng Gu,Xian Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A prior-free anomaly detection framework for UAV multispectral point clouds that handles illumination heterogeneity by coupling solar angle estimation with ray tracing for shadow extraction and using illumination-consistent sparse representation to disentangle reflectance from lighting variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV multispectral point clouds suffer from severe illumination heterogeneity caused by vegetation shadows, which compromises sub-canopy target detection. Existing methods require flight metadata or fail to distinguish dark objects from true shadows.

Method: (1) Solar angle estimation formulated as inverse optimization, coupled with spectral indices and ray-tracing for prior-free shadow extraction. (2) Illumination-consistent sparse representation using a background dictionary from neighbors sharing the same illumination state.

Result: Significantly improves separability between anomalies and background in complex forest environments, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: The framework is effective for camouflaged military target detection, fallen tree trunk mapping, and archaeological ruin discovery under dense foliage.

Abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) multispectral point clouds (MPC) provide high-dimensional spatial-spectral data for sub-canopy target detection; however, their efficacy is significantly compromised by severe illumination heterogeneity caused by vegetation shadows. To address this, we propose a prior-free anomaly detection framework capable of robustly handling lighting variations. First, we formulate solar angle estimation as an inverse optimization problem. By coupling spectral indices with a ray-tracing model, this strategy achieves Prior-Free Shadow Extraction without relying on flight metadata, effectively distinguishing dark objects from true shadows. Second, to mitigate spectral distortions, we introduce an Illumination-Consistent Sparse Representation mechanism. Unlike standard reconstruction methods, we construct a background dictionary strictly from neighbors sharing the same illumination state. This constraint effectively disentangles spectral reflectance from lighting variations, ensuring that targets are represented solely by physically consistent background points. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method significantly improves the separability between anomalies and background in complex forest environments, demonstrating superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines. This framework is particularly suited for identifying camouflaged military targets, mapping fallen tree trunks, and uncovering archaeological ruins hidden beneath dense foliage.

</details>


### [149] [An Enhanced Geometric-Spectral Feature Learning Framework for Airborne Multispectral Point Cloud Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09123)
*Xian Li,Yanfeng Gu,Aleksandra Pižurica*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes an attention-based geometric-spectral feature learning framework for airborne multispectral point cloud classification, featuring two-stream fusion, residual attention block, and a joint loss function to handle high dimensionality, unbalanced samples, and inter-class similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multispectral point cloud classification is hindered by high-dimensional heterogeneous data, unbalanced sample distribution, and inter-class spectral similarity, limiting model representation power.

Method: A two-stream attention-based fusion framework: Stream 1 extracts position-encoded global spectral features via self-attention; Stream 2 extracts spectral-guided geometric features via multikernel point convolution and feature aggregation attention. A residual attention fusion block integrates both streams, and a joint loss function handles unbalanced/inter-class similar samples.

Result: Experiments on two airborne MPC datasets show the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in classification accuracy.

Conclusion: The attention-based geometric-spectral feature learning framework effectively addresses key MPC classification challenges, and the datasets/code will be publicly released.

Abstract: Multispectral point cloud (MPC) is composed of 3D spatial-spectral information, which holds tremendous potential for accurate land-cover classification. However, the representation power of classification models is limited by inherent high-dimensional and heterogeneous spatial-spectral information, unbalanced sample distribution, and inter-class spectral similarity of airborne MPCs. We build two MPC datasets and propose an enhanced geometric-spectral feature learning framework based on attentions for airborne MPC classification. A key component in our model is a two-stream feature fusion method with attention mechanisms, which enhances the representation capability of spatial-spectral features from high-dimensional heterogeneous MPCs. The first stream aims to extract position-encoded global spectral features with fusion self-attention, and the second stream comprises a multikernel point convolution and feature aggregation attention to extract spectral-guided geometric features. We then develop a residual attention fusion block to integrate the most informative geometric-spectral features from the two parallel streams. Another important contribution of this work is a joint loss function to improve the learning ability on unbalanced and interclass similar samples. Experimental results on two airborne MPC datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with the state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, the codes and datasets used in this paper will be made available freely at https://github.com/HITlixian/TGRS_GSFF.

</details>


### [150] [A Geometric Framework for Absolute Pose and Velocity Estimation with Event Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09139)
*Zibin Liu,Shunkun Liang,Banglei Guan,Yang Shang,Qifeng Yu,Ji Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a geometric framework using 3D line-event correspondences to simultaneously estimate absolute 6-DoF pose and velocity from event streams, with linear and polynomial solvers achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current event-based geometric methods focus mainly on velocity estimation, while absolute pose estimation remains underexplored. Simultaneously recovering both from event streams is an open challenge.

Method: Leverages 3D lines and their triggered events, using two geometric constraints (orthogonality between 3D line and event plane normal; collinearity of event with 2D line projection). Proposes linear and polynomial solvers for pose, and linear/optimization-based solvers for velocity, requiring minimum three event-line correspondences.

Result: Extensive simulation and real-world experiments show state-of-the-art performance with significant improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency over existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively fills the gap in absolute pose estimation from event data, enabling simultaneous 6-DoF pose and velocity recovery with high accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Despite the rapid advancements in event-based motion estimation, current geometric methods primarily focus on velocity estimation. However, absolute pose estimation, which is equally crucial for key applications such as robotic navigation and augmented reality, remains relatively underexplored. Consequently, the simultaneous recovery of absolute pose and velocity from event streams remains an open and challenging problem. To address this gap, we propose a geometric framework for absolute pose and velocity estimation by leveraging 3D lines in the scene and the events they trigger. At the core of the framework lie two key geometric constraints: the orthogonality between a 3D line and the normal vector of its corresponding event plane, and the collinearity of an event with the 2D projection of its associated line. Based on these constraints, we present both linear and polynomial solvers for absolute pose estimation. The former enables efficient computation, while the latter provides a globally optimal solution for rotation. For velocity estimation, we develop an efficient linear solver and a more accurate optimization-based solver to recover both angular and linear velocities. Notably, our methods require a minimum of three event-line correspondences to determine the 6-DoF absolute pose or velocities independently. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real-world datasets demonstrate that our methods achieve state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency compared to existing methods. The demo code is publicly available at https://github.com/Zibin6/EventPoseVelocity.

</details>


### [151] [DiffSight-Former: Modeling Structural Differences and Temporal Dynamics for Glaucoma Progression Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09140)
*Yi Huang,Lei Bi,Jinman Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffSight-Former is a Transformer-based framework for predicting glaucoma progression from sequential fundus images. It uses a fundus-specific foundation model for feature extraction, models structural changes in the optic disc/cup and retinal vasculature, and employs a time-aware Transformer with temporal interval embeddings. It achieves high AUC (91.54%) and sensitivity (92.16%) on SIGF dataset and 87.48% accuracy on GRAPE dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning methods for glaucoma detection rely on single time-point images, failing to capture longitudinal structural and vascular changes. Current sequential models struggle to detect subtle early progression signals and depend on fixed-length inputs or already glaucomatous images, limiting early prediction utility.

Method: The framework (1) uses a fundus-specific foundation model for time-variant feature extraction; (2) introduces a multi-structure difference modeling module to quantify progression-related changes in optic disc/cup region and retinal vasculature; (3) integrates these with temporal interval embeddings; (4) processes them via a time-aware Transformer to model progression and predict future glaucoma onset.

Result: On SIGF (405 sequences): AUC 91.54%, sensitivity 92.16%. On GRAPE (263 sequences): average accuracy 87.48% across three clinical visual-field progression criteria. Outperforms existing approaches and shows robustness across different temporal settings.

Conclusion: DiffSight-Former demonstrates strong performance and robustness for longitudinal glaucoma monitoring and early risk prediction, highlighting its potential for clinical application in early detection from sequential fundus images.

Abstract: Glaucoma is a leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, and early detection from fundus images is critical for effective disease management. While deep learning has achieved promising performance in fundus image analysis, most existing methods rely on single time-point images and fail to capture longitudinal structural and vascular changes associated with disease progression. Sequential fundus images acquired during clinical follow-up provide valuable temporal information; however, current sequential models often struggle to detect subtle early progression signals and commonly depend on fixed-length inputs or diagnostic cues from already glaucomatous images, limiting their clinical utility for early prediction. To address these limitations, we propose DiffSight-Former, a framework for glaucoma progression prediction from sequential fundus images. It incorporates a time-variant feature extraction module based on a fundus-specific foundation model to obtain robust anatomical representations. A multi-structure difference modeling module is introduced to quantify progression-related changes in the optic disc/cup region and retinal vasculature. These representations are integrated with temporal interval embeddings and processed by a time-aware Transformer to model disease progression and estimate the probability of future glaucoma onset. Experiments were conducted on two longitudinal datasets, SIGF (405 sequences) and GRAPE (263 sequences). On SIGF, DiffSight-Former achieved an AUC of 91.54% and a sensitivity of 92.16% for progression prediction. On GRAPE, it achieved an average accuracy of 87.48% across three clinical visual-field progression criteria. Compared with existing approaches, DiffSight-Former demonstrates strong performance and robustness across different temporal settings, highlighting its potential for longitudinal glaucoma monitoring and early risk prediction.

</details>


### [152] [Decoding Pedestrian Crossing Intention from Egocentric Vision via Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09142)
*Danya Li,Xiang Su,Yan Feng,Rico Krueger*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper studies pedestrian crossing intention prediction from egocentric video using VLMs. Zero-shot VLMs show moderate gains, but fine-tuned models (especially Qwen3-VL-2B with eye gaze and ego motion cues) achieve a 14.5% accuracy improvement over a transformer baseline, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Egocentric vision's potential for traffic-safety prediction is underexplored. The authors aim to decode pedestrian crossing intentions from short egocentric video clips to improve traffic safety.

Method: The task is formulated as a closed-ended VQA problem using VLMs. The authors benchmark three families of VLMs in zero-shot settings, then apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning. They also incorporate contextual cues: ego motion, vehicle motion, and eye gaze.

Result: Fine-tuned VLMs outperform zero-shot counterparts by a large margin and achieve a 9% accuracy improvement over a specialized transformer baseline. The best model (fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-2B with eye gaze and ego motion) achieves a 14.5% accuracy improvement, setting a new state of the art.

Conclusion: VLMs, when fine-tuned with parameter-efficient methods and augmented with contextual cues like ego motion and eye gaze, are highly effective for egocentric pedestrian intent decoding, outperforming specialized baselines.

Abstract: Egocentric vision offers a first-person view of human perception and decision making, yet its potential for traffic-safety prediction remains underexplored. In this work, we study the decoding of pedestrian crossing intentions from short egocentric video clips. We approach this by formulating the task as a closed-ended visual question answering (VQA) problem and leveraging vision language models (VLMs) to predict the pedestrians' intent. We first benchmark three families of state-of-the-art VLMs in a zero-shot setting, finding that they achieve moderate gains over random guessing but exhibit limited higher-level traffic reasoning. Motivated by these findings, we further adapt VLMs to the target task using parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Our results show that the fine-tuned models substantially outperform their zero-shot counterparts and achieve a 9\% accuracy improvement over a specialized transformer-based baseline. Finally, we demonstrate that incorporating additional contextual cues, including ego motion, vehicle motion, and eye gaze, further improves predictive performance. In particular, the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-2B model guided by eye gaze and ego motion achieves a 14.5% accuracy improvement over the transformer baseline, establishing a new state of the art for egocentric pedestrian intent decoding.

</details>


### [153] [CAMF-Det: Closure-Aware Multimodal Fusion for LiDAR-Camera 3D Object Detection on UAV Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09143)
*Yanze Jiang,Yanfeng Gu,Xian Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAMF-Det is a closure-aware multimodal fusion framework for LiDAR-camera 3D object detection on UAV platforms that models ground-object occlusion (e.g., tree canopies) via a Beer-Lambert-inspired formulation, predicts occlusion intensity online, and embeds these priors throughout the pipeline, achieving significant performance gains on two self-built UAV datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal 3D object detection has been well-studied for ground vehicles but unexplored for UAV platforms. In top-down UAV scenes, frequent occlusion by tree canopies causes spatially varying and modality-dependent information degradation, which existing fusion frameworks do not explicitly model.

Method: (1) Dual-modal closure modeling module: constructs occlusion intensity GT for both modalities offline using a Beer-Lambert-inspired formulation and building-mask correction. (2) Dual-modal prediction network: converts offline GT into online occlusion intensity predictions under single-frame inference. (3) Injects both GT and predicted occlusion intensity into data augmentation, feature encoding, multimodal fusion, and detection head.

Result: On two self-built UAV datasets (SI3D-DI, SI3D-DII), CAMF-Det achieves best performance across all difficulty levels, with hard-level mAP_BEV improvements of 9.43% and 4.88% over the best competing methods.

Conclusion: Explicit occlusion prior modeling and exploitation is effective for robust multimodal 3D detection in occluded UAV scenes.

Abstract: Multimodal 3D object detection based on LiDAR and cameras has demonstrated excellent performance in ground-vehicle scenarios, but has not been explored for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) platforms. In UAV top-down scenes, frequent groundobject occlusion dominated by tree canopies causes spatially varying and modality-dependent information degradation. Existing multimodal fusion frameworks neither explicitly model such ground-object occlusion nor embed occlusion awareness into the detection pipeline, limiting their performance in occluded UAV scenes. To address these challenges, we propose CAMF-Det, a closure-aware multimodal fusion framework for LiDAR-camera 3D object detection on UAV platforms, which derives dual-modal occlusion intensity through physics-inspired modeling and embeds them as priors throughout the detection pipeline. First, a dual-modal closure modeling module explicitly constructs occlusion intensity ground truth for both modalities offline via a Beer-Lambert-inspired formulation and building-mask correction. Second, using these ground-truth maps as supervision, a dual-modal prediction network converts the offline modeling results into online occlusion intensity predictions under single-frame inference. Third, both ground-truth and predicted occlusion intensity are injected into data augmentation, feature encoding, multimodal fusion, and detection head, enabling adaptive detection under spatially varying and modality-dependent information degradation. Experiments on two self-built UAV-based multimodal datasets, SI3D-DI and SI3D-DII, demonstrate that CAMF-Det achieves the best performance across all difficulty levels, with hard-level mAP$_{\mathrm{BEV}}$ improvements of 9.43% and 4.88% over the best competing methods, respectively. These results confirm the effectiveness of explicit occlusion prior modeling and exploitation for robust multimodal 3D detection in UAV scenes.

</details>


### [154] [Ultra Flash: Scaling Real-Time Streaming Video Generation to High Resolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09150)
*Luxury,Jie Huang,Zihao Fan,Xiaoxiao Ma,Yuming Li,Jun-hao Zhuang,Zeyue Xue,Siming Fu,Haoran Li,Mingchen Zhong,Guohui Zhang,Shichen Ma,Yijun Liu,Jiaqi Shi,Yanwen Ma,Yaofeng Su,Haoyu Wang,Yaowei Li,Songchun Zhang,Weiyang Jin,Yuxuan Bian,Shiyi Zhang,Haojun Xu,Shuai Lu,Xin Han,Wei Tang,Haoyang Huang,Nan Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ultra Flash is a cascaded streaming framework for real-time high-resolution video generation, achieving ~30 FPS at 1K and ~18 FPS at 2K on a single GPU via architecture-preserving super-resolution training, causal streaming latent upsampling, and cascade optimization with preference learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autoregressive video diffusion models are limited to low resolutions (e.g., 480P), lacking efficient, scalable, real-time high-resolution video generation capabilities.

Method: (1) Architecture-preserving T2V-to-TV2V super-resolution training with AIGC-oriented data degradation; (2) Causal streaming latent upsampler with high-resolution decoder; (3) Cascade optimization: hybrid-reward sparse causalization, single-step distillation, and streaming self-forcing preference optimization with dynamic cache management.

Result: ~30 FPS at 1K resolution and ~18 FPS at 2K resolution on a single GPU, with state-of-the-art visual quality and superior efficiency.

Conclusion: Ultra Flash reliably produces ultra-high-resolution streaming video while maintaining state-of-the-art visual quality and superior efficiency, bridging the gap between low-resolution generative models and real-time high-resolution video generation.

Abstract: While recent autoregressive video diffusion models achieve remarkable streaming quality, they remain confined to low resolutions (e.g., 480P), leaving efficient, scalable, real-time high-resolution video generation a fundamental open challenge. To bridge this gap, we present Ultra Flash, a cascaded streaming framework capable of real-time high-resolution video generation. Ultra Flash achieves ~30 FPS at 1K resolution and ~18 FPS at 2K resolution on a single GPU through three key contributions: (1) an architecture-preserving T2V-to-TV2V super-resolution training paradigm coupled with an AIGC-oriented data degradation pipeline that effectively preserves the generative capability of the base model, enabling enhanced high-resolution detail when cascaded after mainstream low-resolution generative models; (2) a causal streaming latent upsampler paired with a high-resolution decoder, which enhances spatiotemporal coherence while enabling efficient latent spatial scaling and precise high-resolution decoding with negligible computational overhead; and (3) a cascade high-resolution streaming video generation optimization scheme that first performs hybrid-reward-enhanced sparse causalization and single-step distillation of the super-resolution model, then introduces cascaded streaming self-forcing preference optimization with dynamic cache management, jointly enhancing overall coherence, improving quality, and enabling real-time high-resolution streaming video generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Ultra Flash reliably produces ultra-high-resolution streaming video while maintaining state-of-the-art visual quality and superior efficiency.

</details>


### [155] [OmniGen-AR: AutoRegressive Any-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09156)
*Junke Wang,Xun Wang,Qiushan Guo,Peize Sun,Weilin Huang,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniGen-AR is a unified autoregressive framework that handles multiple visual generation tasks (text-to-image, segmentation/depth-to-image, image editing, frame prediction, text-to-video) within a single model using a disentangled causal attention mechanism, achieving SOTA results on GenEval (0.63) and VBench (80.02).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autoregressive visual generation models are limited to single-modality conditions (e.g., text only), restricting their applicability in real-world scenarios that require image synthesis from diverse control signals such as spatial maps, visual context, and video.

Method: (1) Discretizes various visual conditions through a shared visual tokenizer and text prompts with a text tokenizer to unify diverse inputs. (2) Proposes Disentangled Causal Attention (DCA) that separates the full-sequence causal mask into condition causal attention and content causal attention to prevent information leakage, acting as a training-time regularizer without affecting inference.

Result: Achieves 0.63 on GenEval and 80.02 on VBench, with new state-of-the-art or competitive results across multiple benchmarks covering text-to-image, depth-to-image, segmentation-to-image, image editing, frame prediction, and text-to-video generation.

Conclusion: OmniGen-AR demonstrates that a unified autoregressive framework with disentangled causal attention can effectively handle any-to-image generation tasks, offering flexible and high-fidelity visual generation across diverse conditional inputs.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) models have demonstrated strong potential in visual generation, offering superior performance with simple architectures and optimization objectives. However, existing methods are typically limited to single-modality conditions, e.g., text, restricting their applicability in real-world scenarios that demand image synthesis from diverse controls. In this work, we present OmniGen-AR, a unified autoregressive framework for Any-to-Image generation. By discretizing various visual conditions through a shared visual tokenizer and text prompts with a text tokenizer, OmniGen-AR supports a broad spectrum of conditional inputs within a single model, including text (text-to-image generation), spatial signals (segmentation-to-image and depth-to-image), and visual context (image editing, frame prediction, and text-to-video generation). To mitigate the risk of information leakage from condition tokens to content tokens, we introduce Disentangled Causal Attention (DCA), which separates the full-sequence causal mask into condition causal attention and content causal attention. It serves as a training-time regularizer without affecting the standard next-token prediction during inference. With this design, OmniGen-AR achieves new state-of-the-art or at least competitive results across a range of benchmark, e.g., 0.63 on GenEval and 80.02 on VBench, demonstrating its effectiveness in flexible and high-fidelity visual generation.

</details>


### [156] [Zero-Parameter Geometric Gating for Temporally Stable Low-Altitude UAV Video Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09162)
*Jingpu Yang,Fengxian Ji,Zhengzhao Lai,Juanfan Wu,Mingxuan Cui,Yufeng Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A zero-parameter geometric gate selects between homography (rigid) and optical flow (non-rigid) warping per 16×16 grid region using RANSAC inlier ratios, then fuses via Semantic Similarity Propagation, improving mIoU by +4.24–4.91% on synthetic UAVid with only 211K added parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense optical flow introduces spatially structured noise in planar regions dominating aerial imagery, degrading temporal consistency in low-altitude UAV video semantic segmentation.

Method: Zero-parameter geometric gate: RANSAC homography inlier ratios on a 16×16 grid → median-threshold binary decision routes each region to homography or optical flow warp → Semantic Similarity Propagation (SSP) fusion.

Result: +4.24–4.91% mIoU on synthetic UAVid across SegFormer-b2 and Hiera-S+UPerNet; Moran's I=0.32 (p<0.001) for flow residuals in planar regions; Spearman ρ=0.66 predicting boundary instability; rigidification recovers temporal consistency from 62% to 92% (+29.5pp) in homography-valid regions.

Conclusion: The geometric gate effectively mitigates flow-induced noise in planar regions, improving temporal consistency with minimal overhead and no additional learned parameters for the routing mechanism.

Abstract: Video semantic segmentation for low-altitude UAVs requires temporal consistency, yet dense optical flow introduces spatially structured noise in the planar regions that dominate aerial imagery. We propose a zero-parameter geometric gate that uses RANSAC homography inlier ratios on a $16\times16$ spatial grid to route each region to either homography or optical flow warp before fusion via Semantic Similarity Propagation. The gate requires no learned parameters -- only a median-threshold binary decision on RANSAC statistics -- adding only 211K trainable parameters (the SSP fusion layer) to a frozen backbone. On synthetic UAVid, the method achieves +4.24--4.91\% mIoU improvement over base models across two architectures (SegFormer-b2 and Hiera-S+UPerNet). Mechanism diagnostics reveal that flow residuals in planar regions are spatially autocorrelated (Moran's I = 0.32, $p < 0.001$), predict boundary instability (Spearman $ρ= 0.66$), and that rigidification recovers temporal consistency from 62\% to 92\% (+29.5pp) in homography-valid regions.

</details>


### [157] [Vision-Language Guided Hyperspectral Object Tracking via Semantics Fusion and Contextual Template Updating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09167)
*Rui Yao,Yuhong Zhang,Kunyang Sun,Hancheng Zhu,Jiaqi Zhao,Zhiwen Shao,Abdulmotaleb El Saddik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLHTrack is a hyperspectral object tracking framework that uses vision-language joint learning with a Language-Guided Band Selection Module (LBSM) to address spectral redundancy and a Dynamic Template Update with Mamba (DTUM) module to handle target deformation, achieving SOTA results on HOT2023 and HOT2024 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two key challenges in hyperspectral object tracking: (1) efficiently extracting and exploiting spectral information from redundant spectral bands, and (2) handling drastic appearance variations caused by occlusion, illumination changes, and large deformations in dynamic scenes.

Method: Proposes VLHTrack with three key components: (1) Language-Guided Band Selection Module (LBSM) using LLM descriptions for semantic-to-spectral mapping to reduce redundancy; (2) Multi-Modal Vision-Language Fusion Module for integrating visual and linguistic embeddings; (3) Dynamic Template Update with Mamba (DTUM) module using selective state space modeling for learning inter-frame dependencies and updating template features.

Result: VLHTrack outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on the HOT2023 and HOT2024 hyperspectral object tracking benchmarks.

Conclusion: The vision-language joint tracking framework effectively addresses spectral redundancy and target deformation challenges, demonstrating that incorporating language priors and dynamic temporal modeling significantly improves hyperspectral object tracking performance.

Abstract: Hyperspectral object tracking (HOT) leverages the rich spectral information provided by hyperspectral videos (HSVs), offering substantial potential for object tracking. However, efficiently extracting and exploiting spectral information from redundant spectral bands remains a fundamental challenge, which severely limits model generalization and tracking performance. Moreover, in dynamic scenes, targets often experience drastic appearance variations due to factors such as occlusion and illumination changes. These variations lead to large deformations between the current frame and the template. Such discrepancies pose major challenges for existing temporal modeling approaches. In this work, we propose VLHTrack, a novel hyperspectral vision-language (VL) joint tracking framework. Specifically, we incorporate language priors to address the fundamental challenge of spectral redundancy by designing a Language-Guided Band Selection Module (LBSM). By leveraging Large Language Model (LLM) descriptions, LBSM establishes a semantic-to-spectral mapping that mitigates redundancy and accentuates discriminative spectral features. A Multi-Modal Vision-Language Fusion Module is then employed to seamlessly integrate visual and linguistic embeddings, harnessing their complementary advantages to learn coherent cross-modal representations. To address target deformation in long-term sequences, we propose a dynamic update template feature strategy implemented via the Dynamic Template Update with Mamba (DTUM) module. By leveraging selective state space modeling, DTUM learns inter-frame dependencies to update template feature, ensuring efficient template feature evolution guided by temporal context. Experiments on HOT2023 and HOT2024 demonstrate that VLHTrack outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

</details>


### [158] [Claude Code-Driving Scenario Mining for the Argoverse 2 Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09180)
*Wei Deng,Caoshengzhe Xue,Shuaikun Liu,Zhaohong Liu,Mengshi Qi,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A four-stage pipeline for the CVPR 2026 Argoverse 2 Scenario Mining Challenge using Claude Code agents and Qwen3-VL verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve high-performance scenario mining for autonomous driving by combining code generation, data curation, review, and verification.

Method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) autonomous code generation with Claude Code/GLM 5.1, (2) iterative screening using Timestamp Balanced Accuracy ≥0.8, (3) semantic code review, (4) Qwen3-VL scene-level false positive filtering.

Result: Results reported on Argoverse 2 test set (specific metrics not provided in abstract).

Conclusion: The multi-stage approach combining LLM-based code generation with verification shows promise for autonomous driving scenario mining.

Abstract: We present our submission to the CVPR 2026 Argoverse 2 Scenario Mining Challenge. Our system uses a four-stage pipeline: (1) autonomous code generation via a Claude Code agent powered by GLM~5.1, (2) iterative training set screening with Timestamp Balanced Accuracy threshold 0.8 to curate few-shot examples, (3) semantic code review by a separate Claude Code session, and (4) Qwen3-VL scene-level verification to filter false positives. We report results on the Argoverse 2 test set.

</details>


### [159] [Counterfactual Reasoning for Fine-Grained Evidence Disentanglement in VideoQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09181)
*Zhou Du,Hamid Krim,Xiao Wu,Zhaoquan Yuan,Liangwei Li,Keisuke Fujii*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CREDiT proposes a counterfactual reasoning framework for VideoQA that disentangles causal visual evidence from spurious correlations using a structural causal model, feature-level interventions, and counterfactual inputs, achieving improved accuracy and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VideoQA models rely on spurious statistical correlations rather than causal evidence, leading to unfaithful and brittle reasoning. Prior methods fail to explicitly disentangle causal cues from confounders and provide only coarse-grained time-interval localization.

Method: CREDiT uses a structural causal model to decompose cross-modality representations into causal and non-causal components under independence and minimality constraints, with feature-level causal interventions and counterfactual inputs to approximate causal effects.

Result: Experiments on NExT-GQA, SportsQA, and SPORTU-video show consistent improvements in answer accuracy and reasoning reliability across generic and complex sports scenarios.

Conclusion: CREDiT enables more trustworthy VideoQA systems by explicitly disentangling causal evidence from confounders, leading to faithful and robust reasoning.

Abstract: Recent advances in video multimodal models have significantly improved VideoQA performance. However, these systems often rely on spurious statistical correlations rather than answer-relevant causal evidence, resulting in unfaithful and brittle reasoning, especially in complex real-world scenarios. Existing methods either rely on cross-modality correlations, costly curated training resources, or insufficient causal assumptions and constraints, and typically operate at the time-interval level. As a result, they fail to explicitly disentangle causal visual cues from confounders and provide limited fine-grained evidence localization. To address this issue, we propose a Counterfactual Reasoning framework for fine-grained Evidence Disentanglement (CREDiT). CREDiT formulates the VideoQA process using a structural causal model and learns cross-modality representations that are explicitly decomposed into causal and non-causal components under independence and minimality constraints. To facilitate faithful disentanglement, we introduce feature-level causal interventions and construct counterfactual inputs that approximate causal effects while suppressing non-causal correlations. Extensive experiments on NExT-GQA, SportsQA, and SPORTU-video demonstrate that CREDiT consistently improves answer accuracy and reasoning reliability across both generic and complex sports scenarios, leading to more trustworthy VideoQA systems.

</details>


### [160] [CP4D: Compositional Physics-aware 4D Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09187)
*Hanxin Zhu,Cong Wang,Tianyu He,Long Chen,Xin Jin,Chen Gao,Zhibo Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CP4D is a novel 4D generation paradigm that decomposes dynamic 3D scenes into static environments and physically grounded dynamic objects, using a three-stage pipeline combining pre-trained expert models, hybrid motion synthesis (physics simulators + video diffusion priors), and automated composition to produce physically consistent, photorealistic 4D scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 4D generation methods fail to capture underlying physical principles, producing physically inconsistent and visually implausible results. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by incorporating physical dynamics into 4D scene synthesis.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: 1) Pre-trained expert models generate high-fidelity 3D representations of static environment and foreground objects separately. 2) Hybrid motion synthesis strategy integrating physics simulator priors with video diffusion model common sense for plausible trajectories and interactions. 3) Automated composition mechanism fusing static environment and dynamic objects into coherent 4D scenes.

Result: Generates explorable and interactive 4D scenes with high visual fidelity, strong physical plausibility, and fine-grained controllability, significantly outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: CP4D effectively addresses physical inconsistency in 4D generation by explicitly modeling static environments and physically grounded dynamic objects, achieving superior performance in visual quality, physical plausibility, and controllability.

Abstract: 4D generation (\textit{i.e.}, dynamic 3D generation) has recently emerged as a rapidly growing research frontier due to its powerful spatiotemporal modeling capabilities. However, despite notable advances, existing approaches typically fail to capture the underlying physical principles, producing results that are both physically inconsistent and visually implausible. To overcome this limitation, we present CP4D, a novel paradigm for photorealistic 4D scene synthesis with faithful adherence to complex physical dynamics. Drawing inspiration from the compositional nature of real-world scenes, where immutable static backgrounds coexist with dynamic, physically plausible foregrounds, CP4D reformulates 4D generation as the integration of a static 3D environment with physically grounded dynamic objects. On this basis, our framework follows a three-stage pipeline: \textbf{1)} Firstly, we leverage pre-trained expert models to generate high-fidelity 3D representations of the environment and foreground objects respectively. \textbf{2)} Subsequently, to produce physically plausible trajectories and realistic interactions for these objects, we propose a hybrid motion synthesis strategy that integrates priors from physical simulators with the common sense embedded in video diffusion models. \textbf{3)} Finally, we develop an automated composition mechanism that seamlessly fuses the static environment and dynamic objects into coherent, physically consistent 4D scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CP4D can generate explorable and interactive 4D scenes with high visual fidelity, strong physical plausibility, and fine-grained controllability, significantly outperforming existing methods. The project page: https://anonymous.4open.science/w/CP4D/.

</details>


### [161] [Event-driven dynamic trajectories reconstruction and measurement of mechanical parameters for fragments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09208)
*Haoyang Li,Banglei Guan,Muxi Zha,Yifei Bian,Minzu Liang,Yang Shang,Qifeng Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes an event-driven method using multiple event cameras to reconstruct fragment trajectories and measure mechanical parameters (velocity, kinetic energy) under high-speed, high-occlusion, and strong flash interference conditions during warhead detonation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-intensity flash, smoke, and mutual occlusion during warhead detonation severely hinder accurate acquisition of fragment mechanical parameters, which are critical for lethality assessment.

Method: A multi-event-camera vision system is built, employing three geometric constraints: time-correlated epipolar constraint, trifocal tensor line constraint, and local homography constraint. An entropy-weighted probability model filters mismatches, and 3D trajectories are reconstructed via spatial line-line intersection and nonlinear optimization.

Result: The method successfully reconstructs 3D fragment trajectories and calculates velocity and kinetic energy, overcoming flash and smoke interference.
