To address these limitations, we systematically evaluate a wide range of spherical functions in the context of scene reconstruction. Some of them are introduced to graphics and computer vision for the first time in this paper. Based on the insights from the experiment, we develop a novel spherical formulation, the Normalized Anisotropic Spherical Gabor function that enables efficient modeling and learning of high-frequency appearance effects while maintaining compact representation. Compared to existing approaches, our function achieves higher-quality reconstruction of view-dependent phenomena such as glints, while being up to five times more memory-efficient and more efficient to evaluate. We validate its performance in radiance-field reconstruction tasks.

</details>


### [214] [Echo-Memory: A Controlled Study of Memory in Action World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09803)
*Wayne King,Zeyue Xue,Yuxuan Bian,Jie Huang,Haoran Li,Yaowei Li,Yaofeng Su,Yuming Li,Haoyu Wang,Shiyi Zhang,Songchun Zhang,Yuwei Niu,Sihan Xu,Junhao Zhuang,Haoyang Huang,Nan Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Echo-Memory presents a controlled study isolating memory mechanisms in action-conditioned world models, finding that raw context is strong, compression sacrifices evidence, and state-space recurrence is best for open-domain return.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory designs in action-conditioned world models are hard to compare due to entangled differences in backbone, training, retrieval, and evaluation. The central failure of these models is often memory rather than local image synthesis—when the camera leaves and returns, scenes or objects may silently change.

Method: Echo-Memory fixes the action-to-video interface and varies only how history is stored and read by the generator under a shared video diffusion backbone, optimizer, camera-action representation, sampler, and evaluation pipeline. It compares raw context, compression-based memory, spatial summaries with different read-out paths, and state-space recurrence across four axes: capacity, compression, read-out, and recurrence. A three-branch evaluation protocol (replay quality, in-domain loop revisit, open-domain return) is used.

Result: Raw context is a strong capacity baseline improving open-domain return far more than replay metrics. Compactness is not a free substitute for capacity—aggressive compression loses salient evidence. Block-wise state-space recurrence is the strongest open-domain return mechanism. The three evaluation branches routinely disagree, showing replay fidelity is insufficient for assessing world memory.

Conclusion: Memory in action-conditioned world models should be evaluated beyond isolated replay metrics. The structure of implicit memory matters as much as the decision to use it, and state-space recurrence offers the best open-domain performance.

Abstract: We present \textbf{Echo-Memory}, a controlled study of memory mechanisms in action-conditioned world models. These models generate multi-segment videos from a first frame, text prompt, and camera-action sequence, but their central failure is often memory rather than local image synthesis: after the camera leaves and returns, the scene or salient object may silently change. Existing memory designs are hard to compare because gains are entangled with backbone, training, retrieval, and evaluation differences. Echo-Memory fixes the action-to-video interface and varies only how history is stored and read by the generator. Under a shared video diffusion backbone, optimizer, camera-action representation, sampler, and evaluation pipeline, we compare raw context, compression-based memory, spatial summaries with different read-out paths, and state-space recurrence. This matched matrix separates four otherwise conflated axes: \emph{capacity}, \emph{compression}, \emph{read-out}, and \emph{recurrence}. We also evaluate memory through a three-branch protocol: replay quality, in-domain loop revisit, and open-domain return probes. The branches routinely disagree, showing that replay fidelity is not a sufficient proxy for remembering a world. Three findings follow. Raw context is a strong capacity baseline and improves open-domain return far more than it improves replay metrics. Compactness is not a free substitute for capacity: aggressive spatial and hybrid-compression memories lose the salient evidence needed for return. Finally, block-wise state-space recurrence is the strongest open-domain return mechanism in our matrix, showing that the structure of implicit memory matters as much as the decision to use it. These results provide a compact protocol for studying memory in action world models beyond isolated replay metrics.

</details>


### [215] [PTL-Diffusion: Manifold-Aware Diffusion with Periodic Terminal Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09816)
*Danqi Zhuang,Jisui Huang,Xiaoyue Xi,Andrew Kiggins,Xiaojie Wang,Ke Chen,Yue Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PTL-Diffusion replaces the standard single Gaussian terminal distribution in diffusion models with a periodic family of Gaussian terminal laws, embedding phase structure directly into the forward noising process, and shows improved manifold-level distributional matching on torus, cylinder, and face datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard diffusion models use a single unstructured Gaussian terminal distribution, which provides little explicit structure for data concentrated near low-dimensional manifolds where different regions correspond to distinct local geometric or semantic factors.

Method: PTL-Diffusion uses a periodically forced Ornstein-Uhlenbeck forward process that converges to a periodic family of Gaussian terminal laws instead of a single invariant law. It derives closed-form forward marginals, the limiting periodic Gaussian family, and explicit Gaussian reverse posteriors. An invariant-average regularization term couples phase-conditioned reverse dynamics through the averaged periodic reference law.

Result: Experiments on torus and cylinder point-cloud benchmarks and the Olivetti face dataset show that PTL-Diffusion improves manifold-level distributional matching over matched DDPM baselines, reducing phase-conditioned errors, feature-space covariance errors, and nearest-neighbour manifold distances.

Conclusion: Structured terminal reference laws are a promising direction for diffusion models, motivating more expressive phase constructions and larger-scale evaluations.

Abstract: Standard diffusion models typically use a single time-homogeneous Gaussian terminal distribution as the reference law for generation. While this choice is analytically convenient and empirically powerful, it provides little explicit structure for data concentrated near low-dimensional manifolds, where different regions of the data distribution may correspond to distinct local geometric or semantic factors. As a result, the reverse model must recover manifold-level structure almost entirely from an unstructured terminal reference distribution.
  We propose PTL-Diffusion, a proof-of-concept diffusion framework whose forward noising process converges to a nonconstant periodic family of Gaussian terminal laws rather than to a single invariant law. Unlike a phase-conditioned DDPM, where phase information only enters the denoising network while the forward process remains unchanged, PTL-Diffusion embeds phase structure directly into the forward noising dynamics.
  The proposed construction remains close to standard denoising diffusion models: for a periodically forced Ornstein--Uhlenbeck-type forward process, we derive closed-form forward marginals, the limiting periodic Gaussian terminal family, and explicit Gaussian reverse posteriors, enabling standard noise-prediction training. We also introduce an invariant-average regularization term coupling the phase-conditioned reverse dynamics through the averaged periodic reference law. Experiments on torus and cylinder point-cloud benchmarks and the Olivetti face dataset show that PTL-Diffusion improves manifold-level distributional matching over matched DDPM baselines, reducing phase-conditioned errors, feature-space covariance errors, and nearest-neighbour manifold distances. These results suggest structured terminal reference laws as a promising direction, while motivating more expressive phase constructions and larger-scale evaluations.

</details>


### [216] [OmniGameArena: A Unified UE5 Benchmark for VLM Game Agents with Improvement Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09826)
*Mingxian Lin,Shengju Qian,Yuqi Liu,Yi-Hua Huang,Yiyu Wang,Wei Huang,Yitang Li,Fan Zhang,Zeyu Hu,Lingting Zhu,Xin Wang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniGameArena introduces a real-time benchmark with 12 Unreal Engine 5 games (Solo, PvP, Coop) and the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC) to evaluate VLM agents beyond cold-start scores, measuring skill improvement across reflection rounds and generalization to held-out tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing game benchmarks for VLM agents only report single first-attempt scores, focus on single-agent Solo play, and lack unified protocols to compare heterogeneous agents (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, specialized game policies) fairly.

Method: Built 12 Unreal Engine 5 games with unified action interfaces across Solo, PvP, and Coop modes. Introduced IDC, where a reflector LLM autonomously refines a skill prompt across multiple rounds to measure score evolution and generalization.

Result: Report cold-start leaderboard scores for 12 VLM agents and additional IDC observables (score evolution across reflection rounds, held-out task performance) for 4 top agents.

Conclusion: The framework provides richer evaluation of VLM agents in games by capturing learning dynamics and generalization beyond static single-attempt scores.

Abstract: Vision-language model (VLM) agents are increasingly deployed in interactive game environments. Yet game benchmarks for VLM agents typically report a single first-attempt score per (agent, game) pair, focus on single-agent Solo play, and lack unified protocols for evaluating heterogeneous agent classes (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, and specialized game policies) on the same footing. We address these gaps with OmniGameArena, a real-time benchmark of twelve newly built Unreal Engine 5 games spanning Solo (7), PvP (3), and Coop (2) with unified action interfaces, and the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC), an agentic-reflection harness in which a tool-using reflector LLM autonomously refines a bounded skill prompt across multiple rounds. Beyond cold-start leaderboard scores, IDC exposes two additional observables for each (agent, game) pair: how the score evolves across reflection rounds, and how the learned skill behaves on held-out task variants. We report these observables for twelve VLM agents on the cold-start leaderboard and four top agents under IDC.

</details>


### [217] [Latent Spatial Memory for Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09828)
*Weijie Wang,Haoyu Zhao,Yifan Yang,Feng Chen,Zeyu Zhang,Yefei He,Zicheng Duan,Donny Y. Chen,Yuqing Yang,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mirage introduces a latent spatial memory for video world models that stores 3D scene information directly in diffusion latent space, avoiding expensive pixel-space reconstruction. It achieves up to 10.57× faster generation and 55× memory reduction while maintaining state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video world models rely on explicit point cloud memory in RGB space, which is computationally expensive (repeated rendering/VAE encoding) and inherently lossy due to the round trip through pixel space discarding rich latent features.

Method: Mirage constructs a persistent 3D cache directly in diffusion latent space. It lifts latent tokens into 3D via depth-guided back-projection and queries the memory by synthesizing novel views through direct latent-space warping, eliminating pixel-space reconstruction entirely.

Result: Latent spatial memory achieves up to 10.57× faster end-to-end video generation and 55× reduction in memory footprint relative to explicit 3D baselines. State-of-the-art on WorldScore and strong reconstruction quality on RealEstate10K.

Conclusion: Working in latent space for 3D spatial memory is both faster and more memory-efficient than pixel-space approaches, while preserving richer feature information and leveraging the geometric prior of diffusion models for superior performance.

Abstract: Video world models that maintain 3D spatial consistency across generated frames typically rely on explicit point cloud memory constructed in RGB space. This design is both computationally expensive, requiring repeated rendering and VAE encoding, and inherently lossy, as the round trip through pixel space discards rich features of the learned latent representation. In this paper, we introduce \emph{latent spatial memory} for video world models, a persistent 3D cache that stores scene information directly in the diffusion latent space, avoiding pixel-space reconstruction. Building on this, we propose Mirage, a latent-space spatial memory framework that constructs the memory by lifting latent tokens into 3D via depth-guided back-projection and queries it by synthesizing novel views through direct latent-space warping. This unified formulation eliminates both the information loss of pixel-space reconstruction and the computational burden of repeated encoding and rendering. Experiments show that latent spatial memory achieves up to \textbf{10.57}$\times$ faster end-to-end video generation and \textbf{55}$\times$ reduction in memory footprint relative to explicit 3D baselines. Leveraging the geometric prior of the diffusion model, Mirage attains state-of-the-art performance on WorldScore and strong reconstruction quality on RealEstate10K.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [218] [The Need for Neural ISP in the Small-Pixel Era: How Shrinking Pixels Push Optics to the Limit and Neural Restoration Pushes Back](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07675)
*Jingxi Li,Neerja Aggarwal,Laurent Gudemann,Shivansh Rao,Vishal Vinod,Tom E. Bishop,Ziv Attar*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Neural ISPs can overcome the "telephoto physics wall" by learning to correct optical aberrations, achieving 2.5-3x resolution improvement over traditional ISPs with sub-0.5 micron pixels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smartphone telephoto cameras face diminishing returns due to geometric aberrations as pixels shrink below 0.5 microns. Traditional ISPs cannot correct these because they lack an explicit PSF model.

Method: Controlled simulation of a telephoto module across five pixel pitch configurations (0.35-0.75μm), comparing a learning-based Neural ISP against a traditional stage-wise ISP, including a multi-frame low-SNR extension.

Result: Neural ISP achieves 745 cycles/mm MTF50 at 0.35μm (2.5-3x improvement over traditional ISP), LPIPS improves from 0.244 to 0.151. Multi-frame Neural ISP recovers bright-light performance in low-SNR conditions, while traditional ISP shows no improvement.

Conclusion: Neural ISPs can replace complex optics by computationally correcting aberrations, enabling high-resolution telephoto cameras with smaller pixels.

Abstract: Smartphone telephoto cameras are approaching a "telephoto physics wall": as pixel pitches shrink toward sub-0.5 micron, the optics remain limited by geometric aberrations, leading to diminishing returns on resolution. Traditional Image Signal Processors (ISPs) cannot eliminate these aberrations, because they operate through local, stage-wise processing with no explicit model of the underlying point spread function (PSF). We demonstrate how a learning-based Neural ISP for image restoration, trained on the underlying degradations, inverts what stage-wise pipelines cannot, turning small-pixel designs into a net advantage.
  We investigate this through a controlled simulation of a representative telephoto module, evaluating five configurations (0.35--0.75 micron pixel pitch). The aperture is scaled proportionally to keep per-pixel SNR and diffraction spot size fixed, thereby isolating geometric aberration and spatial sampling. While the traditional ISP improves only modestly with smaller pixels, the Neural ISP scales substantially: at 0.35 micron} it reaches 745 cycles/mm MTF50 (vertical), a 2.5--3x resolution improvement over the traditional ISP, and LPIPS improves significantly from 0.244 to 0.151 while traditional results stay comparatively flat. In a low-SNR extension (15 dB per-frame bursts at 0.35 micron), a multi-frame Neural ISP recovers performance close to the bright-light single-frame baseline, whereas a multi-frame traditional ISP shows no meaningful improvement -- indicating that traditional pipelines at small pixels are bottlenecked by uncorrected PSF blur rather than by noise. These results point to a design philosophy in which Neural ISPs enable high-resolution telephoto modules by correcting residual optical aberrations rather than requiring increasingly complex optics.

</details>


### [219] [Multi-planar 2D-U-Net Segmentation of 3D-CT Abdominal Organs augmented by Spatial Occurrence Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07717)
*Daria Kern,Negar Chabi,Souraj Adhikary,Andre Mastmeyer*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A lightweight 2D-U-Net framework uses coarse-to-fine segmentation, multi-planar predictions, and fuzzy 3D spatial maps to segment five abdominal organs in CT scans, achieving up to 4% Dice improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve segmentation accuracy of multiple abdominal organs in large 3D CT scans by incorporating spatial occurrence maps into a multi-planar 2D-U-Net framework, while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) Coarse detection of the abdominal volume of interest via axial 2D-U-Net scanning to determine organ extents; (2) Refined multi-planar 2D-U-Net segmentation augmented with fuzzy 3D spatial occurrence maps providing anatomical location cues.

Result: Evaluated on 80 CT scans from public sources; shows up to ~4% Dice improvement compared to the same model without spatial occurrence maps.

Conclusion: Adding spatial occurrence maps to multi-planar 2D-U-Net segmentation effectively boosts accuracy for abdominal organ segmentation in large field-of-view CT scans.

Abstract: This work proposes a lightweight 2D-U-Net-based framework for segmenting five abdominal organs in large field-of-view 3D CT scans. The method combines coarse-to-fine segmentation, predictions from multiple anatomical planes, and additional fuzzy 3D spatial maps that provide anatomical location cues to improve segmentation accuracy. We combine multi-planar 2D-U-Net models augmented by a spatial occurrence map. The approach involves two main stages. First, the abdominal volume of interest region is detected by traversing the whole scan axially with a 2D-U-Net and determining the x-y-z-minimum and -maximum extents of the 5 abdominal organs of interest. Second, we use spatial occurrence maps to enhance our multi-planar 2D-U-net architecture inside the bounds from the former stage. The method is evaluated on 80 CT scans from various public sources. The results show Dice improvements of about 4% at maximum compared to the same model trained without spatial occurrence maps.

</details>


### [220] [Programmable Silicon Retina on Pixel Processor Array](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08370)
*Maciej Lewandowski,Prince Philip,Alexandre Marcireau,Chetan Singh Thakur,André van Schaik,Piotr Dudek*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-stage Silicon Retina model on the SCAMP-5 Pixel Processor Array, combining spatial filtering and gain control with standard DVS processing. The bio-inspired model reduces saliency prediction loss by 13% and event rate by 47% compared to standard DVS, albeit with lower intensity reconstruction quality, suggesting benefits for bandwidth-constrained edge applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether adding biologically inspired processing stages (spatial filtering and gain control) to standard DVS sensors can offer advantages for downstream tasks like saliency prediction.

Method: Implemented a multi-stage Silicon Retina model on the SCAMP-5 Pixel Processor Array with a GPU-based simulation framework. Used a lightweight ~100k-parameter FireNet-style network adapted from event-based reconstruction to saliency prediction.

Result: 13% reduction in saliency prediction loss and ~47% reduction in event rate compared to standard DVS, though less effective at absolute intensity frame reconstruction.

Conclusion: The silicon retina's "information distillation" mechanism enables more efficient representation for downstream neural networks, especially beneficial for bandwidth-constrained edge applications.

Abstract: Standard dynamic vision sensors approximate retinal processing by detecting temporal contrast changes, offering high speed and high dynamic range. In this work, we explore whether incorporating additional biologically inspired processing stages - specifically spatial filtering and gain control - can offer advantages for certain downstream tasks such as saliency prediction. We present the first implementation of a multi-stage Silicon Retina model on the SCAMP-5 Pixel Processor Array, along with a GPU-based simulation framework. We evaluate the performance of our model on Video Intensity Reconstruction and Video Saliency Prediction. While the bio-inspired model is less effective at reconstructing absolute intensity frames, it achieves a 13\% reduction in saliency prediction loss in comparison to standard DVS event representation, while reducing the event rate by approximately 47\%. These experiments are obtained using a lightweight $\approx 100$k-parameter FireNet-style network, adapted from event-based reconstruction to saliency prediction. These results suggest that the silicon retina's "information distillation" mechanism can achieve a more efficient representation for downstream neural networks, particularly in bandwidth-constrained edge applications.

</details>


### [221] [X-Palm: Paired Multispectral-to-Smartphone Dataset for Cross-Domain Palmprint Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08437)
*Jamal Seyedmohammadi,Pai Chet Ng,Angelo Genovese,Zhixiang Chi,Jeannie Lee,Konstantinos N. Plataniotis*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: X-Palm is a new cross-domain palmprint dataset with 6,006 images from 103 individuals, pairing controlled multispectral enrollment with unconstrained smartphone captures to bridge the domain gap. Extensive benchmarking shows existing models fail on cross-domain data, while models trained on X-Palm achieve robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing palmprint datasets are largely restricted to controlled setups and fail to capture the compound variability of real-world environments, hindering deployment of palmprint biometrics in unconstrained authentication scenarios.

Method: X-Palm is the first dataset providing paired-identity acquisition across two domains: (1) controlled multispectral palmprint using a custom scanner, and (2) unconstrained smartphone palmprint with simultaneous variations in hardware, pose, illumination, background, distance, perspective, and palm surface conditions.

Result: Benchmarking 12 SOTA models shows severe performance collapse on X-Palm when models are trained on controlled data. Conversely, models trained on X-Palm exhibit consistent robustness across domains.

Conclusion: X-Palm is a valuable resource for training models towards real-world, cross-domain generalization in palmprint recognition.

Abstract: Palmprint modality offers a privacy-preserving biometric solution, yet its deployment is hindered by the domain gap between controlled enrollment and unconstrained authentication. Existing datasets are largely restricted to controlled setups and fail to capture the compound variability of real-world environments. In this paper, we introduce X-Palm, a cross-domain dataset comprising 6,006 palm images from 103 individuals (206 hands). To the best of our knowledge, X-Palm is the first palmprint dataset providing novel paired-identity acquisition specifically designed to bridge the gap between reliably controlled multispectral enrollment and unconstrained mobile authentication while encompassing a broad spectrum of in-the-wild variability. Unlike existing datasets that focus on single to a few variations, X-Palm addresses the massive modality and environmental shifts encountered in practical deployments by capturing paired data for identities across two distinct domains: (1) a controlled Multispectral Palmprint setting using our custom-developed scanner, and (2) an unconstrained smartphone palmprint setting that is participant-driven, incorporating simultaneous variations in hardware, hand pose, illumination, background, camera-to-hand distance, perspective, and palm surface conditions (e.g., moisture and occlusions). Our extensive benchmarks of 12 SOTA models reveal that while existing methods achieve high performance on controlled data, they experience severe performance collapse on X-Palm. Conversely, models trained on X-Palm demonstrate consistent robustness across domains, positioning X-Palm as a valuable resource for training a model towards real-world, cross-domain generalization. Data access instructions and the related benchmarking codes are publicly available at: https://github.com/X-Palm/X-Palm-2026

</details>


### [222] [Active Source-free Domain Adaptation in Open-set Medical Image Segmentation via Decomposed Uncertainty and Prototype Discrepancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08749)
*Jin Yang,Yichi Zhang,Peijie Qiu,Xiaobing Yu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Proposes ASFOSDA, the first active source-free open-set domain adaptation method for medical image segmentation, using CDU and CPD for sample selection and target-refined self-training for semi-supervised learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing active domain adaptation methods assume closed-set segmentation (same classes) and require source data access, both impractical in clinical settings where target domains have private classes (new structures/pathologies) and data privacy restricts source data usage.

Method: (1) Active open-set query strategy with Class-aware Decomposed Uncertainty (CDU) estimating aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty via test-time augmentation, and Class-agnostic Prototype Discrepancy (CPD) measuring cross-domain and self-domain discrepancy. (2) Target-refined Self-training for generating high-quality pseudo labels on unselected samples, enabling semi-supervised training.

Result: Outperformed state-of-the-art adaptation methods on cross-domain open-set volumetric medical image segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed ASFOSDA effectively addresses open-set domain adaptation without source data access, advancing practical clinical deployment of DL segmentation models.

Abstract: Deep learning (DL) methods are challenged to demonstrate robust performance across different segmentation datasets due to domain shifts, but active domain adaptation techniques enhance their generalization performance by querying a few samples from target domains for adaptation training. However in clinical practice, target domains often include private classes of new anatomical structures or pathologies that are not presented in the source data, and existing methods implement closed-set segmentation where source and target domains have the same segmentation classes. Additionally, source data are often inaccessible during adaptation due to strict data privacy regulations. To address these limitations, we propose an Active Source-free Open-set Domain Adaptation (ASFOSDA) method which is the first work to implement active learning for adapting DL models in open-set medical image segmentation without the access to source data. This method employs an active open-set query strategy to select the most informative target samples for training models based on Class-aware Decomposed Uncertainty (CDU) and Class-agnostic Prototype Discrepancy (CPD). CDU measures sample aleatoric uncertainty and model epistemic uncertainty by employing test time augmentation in stochastic processes. CPD measures cross-domain and self-domain discrepancy for selecting diverse samples. Subsequently, to boost the adaptation performance by enhancing training samples, a Target-refined Self-training strategy is proposed to generate high-quality pseudo labels for unselected samples, thus combining them with labeled samples for a semi-supervised training. We evaluated our method on cross-domain open-set volumetric medical image segmentation tasks, and it outperformed state-of-the-art adaptation methods.

</details>


### [223] [Dynamic XR Rendering Offloading Based on Feature-Based Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09330)
*Sige Liu,Zhe Wang,Lavish Kamal Kumar,Yansha Deng*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents an edge-aided XR rendering testbed that uses a contextual bandit learning controller to dynamically offload rendering workloads between client and edge server, along with a perceptual evaluation metric robust to spatial/temporal misalignments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XR applications demand intensive computation and low latency for real-time rendering, but existing solutions lack adaptive offloading that accounts for both perceptual quality and network conditions.

Method: The testbed integrates HoloLens 2, GPU-enabled edge server, and HOLO Stream SDK; proposes a deep feature embedding + cosine similarity perceptual metric; and designs a contextual bandit learning controller for real-time rendering placement decisions.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate feasibility and performance, validating effectiveness in delivering high-quality and interactive XR experiences.

Conclusion: The proposed edge-aided testbed with adaptive offloading and perceptual-aware learning controller can successfully balance quality and latency for real-time XR rendering.

Abstract: Extended Reality (XR) applications demand intensive computation and low latency, especially for real-time rendering tasks. In this letter, we present an edge-aided XR rendering testbed that dynamically offloads rendering workloads between the XR client and the edge server built upon network conditions and latency constraints. The testbed integrates a Microsoft HoloLens 2 headset, a GPU-enabled edge server, and a customized remote rendering toolkit based on the HOLO Stream SDK, enabling seamless switching between local and edge rendering modes in real time. To overcome the limitations of pixel-level quality metrics under head movements and asynchronous frame arrivals, we propose a perceptual evaluation metric based on deep feature embeddings and cosine similarity, which remains robust to spatial and temporal misalignments. Furthermore, we design a contextual bandit learning controller to adapt rendering placement decisions in real time by jointly optimizing perceptual quality and latency. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility and performance of our testbed, validating its effectiveness in delivering high-quality and interactive XR experiences.

</details>


### [224] [Vendor-agnostic 4D Phase Contrast MRI: a complete open-source pipeline for velocities, displacement, and strain analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09444)
*Marta B. Maggioni,Sabine M. Räuber,Katarina Puš,Bostjan Šimunič,Xeni Deligianni,Regina M. M. Schlaeger,Francesco Santini*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: An open-source 4D flow PC-MRI pipeline integrating compressed sensing acceleration, BART-based reconstruction, and strain analysis was developed and validated across two Siemens MRI systems for forearm and thigh muscles, reducing scan times by ~7x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Phase contrast MRI enables quantitative tissue motion/strain assessment but lacks standardized, vendor-agnostic open-source pipelines for accelerated acquisitions.

Method: The pipeline combines: (1) a compressed sensing-accelerated sequence in PyPulseq, (2) BART-based reconstruction, (3) strain analysis, and (4) a gradient probing sequence for velocity sign correction. Validated on two Siemens 3T systems for forearm (Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, n=9) and thigh (Vastus Lateralis, n=10) during NMES-induced contractions.

Result: CS reduced acquisition from 35→5 min (arm) and 80→11 min (leg). Vastus Lateralis strains were ~10x higher than Flexor Digitorum Superficialis (peak strain 0.49 vs 0.063). Sigmoid-fitted strain curves quantified peak strain, mean strain, and buildup rate.

Conclusion: The pipeline demonstrates multi-platform compatibility and provides a reproducible, open framework for quantitative muscle imaging.

Abstract: Phase contrast MRI (PC MRI) enables quantitative assessment of tissue motion and strain. Although it is increasingly used, standardized, vendor-agnostic pipelines for accelerated acquisitions remain scarce. We present a fully open-source 4D flow PC-MRI pipeline integrating a compressed sensing-accelerated sequence implemented in PyPulseq, BART-based reconstruction, and strain analysis. Additionally, a gradient probing sequence was developed to ensure correct velocity sign assignment across scanner orientations and vendors. The pipeline was validated across two Siemens MRI systems (3T MAGNETOM Prisma and 3T Vida Fit) in two anatomical applications: forearm (Flexor Digitorum Superficialis, n=9) and thigh (Vastus Lateralis, n=10) during Neuromuscular Electrical Stimulation (NMES)-induced contractions. Compressed sensing reduced acquisition times from 35 and 80 minutes to 5 and 11 minutes for the arm and leg acquisitions, respectively. Muscle strain maps and sigmoid-fitted strain curves enabled extraction of peak strain, mean strain, and buildup rate. Strains in the Vastus Lateralis were approximately one order of magnitude higher than in the Flexor Digitorum Superficialis (median peak strain 0.49 vs. 0.063, mean strain 0.31 vs. 0.031). The pipeline demonstrates multi-platform compatibility and provides a reproducible, open framework for quantitative muscle imaging.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [225] [FADRW: A Feature-Aware Modulated and Dynamically Reweighted Loss for Few-Shot Linguistic Steganalysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07655)
*Shuo Liu,Xianghong Lin,Yukun Wei,Zhongliang Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes FADRW, a novel loss function framework that addresses class imbalance and feature marginalization in few-shot linguistic steganalysis, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on real-world social media datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Social media platforms enable malicious linguistic steganography, posing security risks. Detection is hindered by extreme class imbalance (<1% steganographic samples) and feature marginalization where faint steganographic signals are overwhelmed by benign text features.

Method: FADRW (Feature-Aware Modulated and Dynamically Reweighted Loss) framework with two components: (1) Dynamic Reweighting to progressively counteract decision bias, and (2) Feature-Aware Modulation module to structurally reshape the feature space and enhance separability of subtle steganographic features.

Result: Extensive experiments on datasets from three real-world social platforms show FADRW significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, especially in few-shot steganographic sample scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed loss function framework effectively addresses optimization-level challenges (class imbalance and feature marginalization) in few-shot steganalysis, providing a robust solution for detecting hidden malicious content in social media text.

Abstract: The ubiquity of social media platforms facilitates malicious linguistic steganography, posing significant security risks. However, detection is severely hampered by two fundamental issues during model training. Firstly, extreme class imbalance (less than 1% steganographic samples) induces a strong decision bias. Secondly, the invisibility of generative steganography means its features are nearly indistinguishable from benign text; this similarity, compounded by their extreme rarity, leads to severe feature marginalization, where faint steganographic signals are completely overwhelmed. To directly address these optimization-level challenges, we propose FADRW (Feature-Aware Modulated and Dynamically Reweighted Loss), a novel loss function framework engineered for few-shot steganalysis. FADRW employs Dynamic Reweighting to progressively counteract decision bias, and a Feature-Aware Modulation module to structurally reshape the feature space, preventing feature marginalization by enhancing the separability of these subtle features. Extensive experiments on datasets from three real-world social platforms demonstrate that FADRW significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly in the challenging few-shot steganographic sample scenario.

</details>


### [226] [Hessian-matching Based Weighting for Attitude Determination Using Short-Range DoA Measurements with IMU Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07719)
*Chenxin Tu,Hengchuan Zhang,Xiaowei Cui,Gang Liu,Mingquan Lu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes total least squares (TLS)-consistent attitude determination methods using short-range wireless arrays, with Hessian-matching weighting strategies and gravity augmentation for improved accuracy in GNSS-denied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate attitude determination is critical for unmanned vehicles in GNSS-denied environments. Short-range wireless arrays introduce non-negligible anisotropic position errors in navigation-frame direction vectors, which existing methods do not properly model.

Method: The authors develop a TLS-consistent formulation treating total DV error, solve the covariance-weighted orthogonal Procrustes problem via manifold Gauss-Newton, and propose Hessian-matching scalar weighting strategies (full-attitude and direction-of-interest) that approximate the TLS Hessian within the efficient Wahba framework. IMU-derived gravity is incorporated as an additional DV pair for static initialization.

Result: Simulations show Hessian-matching weighting improves accuracy and robustness over baselines; gravity-DV augmentation further reduces attitude errors and improves solution availability under limited anchor availability.

Conclusion: The TLS-consistent formulation with Hessian-matching weighting and gravity augmentation provides a principled, accurate, and robust approach for short-range AD in GNSS-denied conditions.

Abstract: Accurate and reliable attitude determination (AD) is essential for unmanned vehicles operating in Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-denied environments. Short-range wireless arrays can provide direction-of-arrival (DoA) measurements from multiple anchors, enabling AD by aligning corresponding direction vectors (DVs) expressed in the body and navigation frames. In short-range scenarios, navigation-frame DVs inherit non-negligible uncertainty induced by anchor/vehicle position errors in addition to DoA-induced errors in body-frame DVs. Moreover, due to projection and unit-norm normalization, the DV errors are generally anisotropic, which motivates a total least squares (TLS) viewpoint. This paper identifies the key modeling distinction in short-range AD, develops a TLS-consistent formulation based on the total DV error and solves the resulting covariance-weighted orthogonal Procrustes problem via a manifold Gauss--Newton method. To retain the efficiency and numerical robustness of the closed-form weighted Wahba solution, we further propose Hessian-matching based scalar weighting strategies that approximate the Hessian of Wahba formulation to the TLS formulation, including a full-attitude strategy for overall accuracy and a direction-of-interest (DOI) strategy for prioritizing a selected attitude component. Finally, we incorporate IMU-derived gravity as an additional DV pair for static initialization, leading to extended Wahba and extended TLS formulations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed Hessian-matching weighting improves accuracy and robustness compared with existing baselines, and that gravity-DV augmentation further reduces attitude errors and improves solution availability under limited anchor availability.

</details>


### [227] [Optimal Wiener-Filter Solutions for Denoising of Graph Signals on Directed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07876)
*Chun Hei Michael Chan,Alexandre Cionca,Dimitri Van De Ville*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Wiener-filter solution for denoising graph signals on directed graphs, demonstrating optimal solutions under various stationarity and noise conditions, with validation on temperature graph data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend graph signal denoising to directed graphs using Wiener-filter solutions under different stationarity and noise assumptions.

Method: The authors propose a Wiener-filter approach for graph signals on directed graphs, considering various stationarity assumptions with both uncorrelated and correlated noise conditions to derive optimal denoising solutions.

Result: Optimal Wiener-filter solutions were derived under different stationarity and noise assumptions, with successful proof-of-concept on temperature graph data.

Conclusion: Wiener-filter solutions are effective for denoising signals on directed graphs, opening new possibilities for graph signal processing applications.

Abstract: Graph signal processing has opened new avenues to the canonical denoising problem in interesting settings. Specifically, here we propose a Wiener-filter solution for graph signals on directed graphs. Under various stationarity assumptions combining uncorrelated and correlated noise conditions, we show optimal solutions, including a successful proof-of-concept for temperature graph.

</details>


### [228] [A Double Proportionate Sparse Adaptive Filter for Impulsive Noise Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08225)
*Mohammad Salman,Falah Ibrahim Al Hassan,Hadi Zayyani,Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo,Hasan Abu Hilal*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: DP-SAF is a sparse adaptive filter with O(M) complexity that combines proportionate step sizing and zero-attraction with a sign-error update for impulsive noise robustness, outperforming competitors in steady-state MSD and convergence speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse adaptive filters and impulsive noise robust algorithms have been developed separately, leaving a gap when both properties are needed simultaneously. DP-SAF aims to close this gap.

Method: Introduces two diagonal gain matrices: one scales adaptation proportionately to coefficient magnitudes, the other applies magnitude-dependent zero-attraction for inactive taps. Uses sign-error update for robustness. Derives both matrices from minimum-norm optimization. Complexity is O(M).

Result: Under Bernoulli impulsive noise model, DP-SAF achieves better steady-state MSD than competing algorithms while matching or exceeding their convergence speeds.

Conclusion: The proposed DP-SAF effectively combines sparse adaptation and impulsive noise robustness in a single efficient O(M) update, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Sparse adaptive filters and impulsive noise robust algorithms have largely been developed along separate tracks, leaving a gap when both properties are needed simultaneously. This letter proposes the double proportionate sparse adaptive filter (DP-SAF), which closes this gap within a single $\mathcal{O}(M)$ update. Two independent diagonal gain matrices are introduced; one scales the adaptation step proportionately to coefficient magnitudes, and the other applies a magnitude-dependent zero-attraction that is strongest for inactive taps. A sign-error update provides robustness against impulsive corruptions. Both gain matrices are derived from a minimum-norm optimization framework. Simulations under a Bernoulli impulsive noise model show that DP-SAF consistently achieves a better steady-state MSD than the competing algorithms while matching or exceeding their convergence speeds.

</details>


### [229] [CG-MambaNet: A spatiotemporal framework for cross-patient epileptic seizure prediction using CNN-GCN-Mamba-BiLSTM with event-level clinical evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08226)
*Mufeng Chen,Qi Wu,Bingchao Huang,Xiwen Lai,Zekai Chen,Xinge Ouyang,Tingyao Zhang,Xufang Yang,Quansheng Ren*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CG-MambaNet is a spatiotemporal seizure prediction framework using depthwise separable CNNs for multi-scale spectro-temporal features, graph convolutional networks for inter-channel synchrony, and bidirectional Mamba+LSTM for temporal dynamics, achieving SOTA cross-patient results on CHB-MIT (AUC 0.8152) and SIENA (AUC 0.7104) with only 0.32 false alarms/hour.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep-learning methods have two architectural limitations (modeling EEG channels independently and lacking frequency decomposition) and a methodological limitation (data splits permitting patient-level information leakage, leading to overoptimistic estimates).

Method: A depthwise separable CNN extracts multi-scale spectro-temporal features from each EEG patch; a two-layer GCN with learnable adjacency matrix captures inter-channel synchrony; bidirectional Mamba + bidirectional LSTM models long/short-range temporal dynamics; two-layer MLP outputs seizure probability. Hierarchy: frequency decomposition → spatial mixing → temporal integration.

Result: Under strict leave-one-patient-out CV with 5 seeds: CHB-MIT AUC-ROC 0.8152±0.0176, SIENA AUC-ROC 0.7104±0.0261, surpassing all published cross-patient methods. Event-level evaluation yields 0.32 false alarms/hour on CHB-MIT.

Conclusion: CG-MambaNet effectively addresses architectural and methodological limitations in seizure prediction, achieving clinically meaningful performance with low false alarm rates under rigorous cross-patient evaluation.

Abstract: Epileptic seizure prediction from scalp EEG is critical for closed-loop neurostimulation therapy. Existing deep-learning methods share two architectural limitations: they model EEG channels independently, neglecting inter-channel spatial synchrony, and process raw time-domain samples without frequency decomposition. A methodological limitation also affects the field: most studies use data splits that permit patient-level information leakage, yielding optimistic estimates that do not generalise to unseen patients. We present CG-MambaNet, a spatiotemporal seizure prediction framework addressing all three limitations. A depthwise separable CNN front-end decomposes each EEG patch into multi-scale spectro-temporal features, capturing delta-to-gamma band dynamics before sequence modelling. A two-layer graph convolutional network with a learnable adjacency matrix captures inter-channel functional synchrony without montage-specific coordinates, applicable to bipolar (CHB-MIT) and referential (SIENA) montages. A bidirectional Mamba encoder followed by a bidirectional LSTM models long- and short-range temporal dynamics, and a two-layer MLP produces the final seizure probability. This serial hierarchy ensures frequency decomposition precedes spatial mixing, which precedes temporal integration. Under strict leave-one-patient-out cross-validation with five independent random seeds, CG-MambaNet achieves AUC-ROC of 0.8152+/-0.0176 on CHB-MIT (n=22) and 0.7104+/-0.0261 on SIENA (n=6), surpassing all published cross-patient methods without domain adaptation. An event-level evaluation framework merging consecutive alarmed windows via a persistence filter reduces false predictions to 0.32 alarms/hour on CHB-MIT, demonstrating clinically meaningful alarm burden.

</details>


### [230] [A dual-system approach for epilepsy diagnosis: integrating mamba-Bi-LSTM architecture with SHAP-based verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08240)
*Mufeng Chen,Jia Xie,Fuchang Luo,Quansheng Ren*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study proposes a dual-system AI diagnostic framework for epilepsy that combines Mamba + Bi-LSTM for high-accuracy discrimination (98.7% accuracy, 0.992 F1 score) and SHAP-based verification for explainability, using cross-modal EEG and clinical text data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Epilepsy diagnosis is hindered by sudden, unpredictable seizures and reliance on manual, time-consuming, and experience-dependent EEG interpretation by doctors.

Method: A dual-system framework: (1) a main discrimination system using Mamba + Bi-LSTM deep learning model for heterogeneous data fusion (EEG + clinical text); (2) a verification system using SHAP for explainable diagnosis, with diagnostic consistency/confidence-based output and automatic feedback for model optimization.

Result: Accuracy improved from 92.6% to 98.7%, F1 score from 0.895 to 0.992, surpassing existing methods. Verification feedback integration averages 220 ms, improving overall diagnostic accuracy by 5.1%.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-system framework significantly enhances epilepsy diagnostic accuracy and explainability, with the verification system adding minimal latency while boosting performance.

Abstract: This study develops a medical AI-assisted diagnosis system based on deep learning, which provides intelligent diagnostic solutions for epilepsy, a disease that seriously threatens the life and health of patients. Epilepsy has sudden and unpredictable seizures. Traditional diagnostic methods mainly rely on doctors' manual interpretation of EEG, which is time-consuming and dependent by experience. In response to the above challenges, this study designed a dual-system intelligent diagnosis framework, which includes two core components: the main discrimination system and the verification system. The main discrimination system uses a deep learning model that combines the innovative Mamba architecture with the Bi-LSTM structure to integrate and analyze heterogeneous data to achieve extremely high diagnostic accuracy; the verification system provides an explainable diagnostic basis through the SHAP method to enhance the credibility of the results. This system establishes a cross-modal database to realize intelligent analysis of multi-source heterogeneous data-fusion EEG signals and clinical text data for epilepsy. The system outputs results based on diagnostic consistency and confidence levels, and high-confidence predictions can also be used as automatic feedback sources to optimize the model. The experimental results show that the accuracy of the main discriminant model of the intelligent diagnosis system for epilepsy has increased from 92.6% to 98.7% and the F1 score has increased from 0.895 to 0.992, all of which have exceeded the existing optimal methods; the average processing time for verification system feedback integration is only 220 ms, which increases the overall diagnostic accuracy by 5.1%.

</details>


### [231] [Spatio-Sequential Recurrent Network for 3-D Tunnel Propagation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08246)
*Jiahao Li,Jingxin Xue,Keqi Ni,Kunyu Wu,Hao Qin,Xinyue Zhang,Xingqi Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes UG-SSRNN, a deep learning model for joint 3-D super-resolution of tunnel electromagnetic fields from coarse PWE simulations, achieving high accuracy and significant speedup over fine-mesh simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-mesh PWE simulations are accurate but slow, limiting real-time tunnel propagation analysis. Existing ML models only provide 1-D or 2-D refinement, lacking joint 3-D enhancement.

Method: UG-SSRNN is a U-shaped gated spatio-sequential recurrent neural network using sliding-window context encoding, a K-layer convolutional recurrent backbone with shared propagation-context state and diagonal feedback, and a prediction-aware upsampling head.

Result: Experiments on four tunnel cross sections, unseen-material/frequency tests, and Massif Central tunnel validation show close agreement with fine-mesh PWE references, with significantly reduced computation time.

Conclusion: UG-SSRNN effectively enables real-time, high-fidelity 3-D tunnel electromagnetic modeling, filling the gap in joint spatio-sequential reconstruction.

Abstract: Fine-mesh parabolic wave equation (PWE) simulations are high-fidelity but time-consuming, which limits real-time tunnel propagation analysis and motivates coarse-to-fine reconstruction. Existing machine learning (ML)-assisted tunnel models typically provide only one-dimensional (1-D) longitudinal refinement or two-dimensional (2-D) cross-sectional refinement, rather than joint 3-D enhancement. Motivated by this gap, this letter proposes a U-shaped gated spatio-sequential recurrent neural network (UG-SSRNN), a spatio-sequential reconstruction model for tunnel electromagnetic fields. UG-SSRNN jointly super-resolves transverse slices and models longitudinal evolution. It uses sliding-window context encoding and a K-layer convolutional recurrent backbone with a shared propagation-context state and diagonal feedback. A prediction-aware upsampling head leverages the previous prediction to improve slice-to-slice consistency. Experiments on four tunnel cross sections, unseen-material and unseen-frequency tests, and validation in the Massif Central tunnel show close agreement with fine-mesh PWE references. The proposed approach significantly reduces tunnel electromagnetic modeling time.

</details>


### [232] [Feedforward Nonlinear Equalizer for Short- to Medium-Reach Wireline Links](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08313)
*Kunmo Kim,Paul Kwon,Elad Alon,Ali Niknejad*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a feedforward nonlinear equalizer (FFNE) that avoids the feedback timing bottleneck of decision-feedback equalizers (DFEs) while approaching their noise-margin advantages in wireline links, with demonstrated extensions to PAM-4 and multi-tap scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To remove the feedback-timing bottleneck inherent in decision-feedback equalizers (DFEs) for short- to medium-reach wireline links, while maintaining a comparable noise-margin advantage.

Method: Proposes a feedforward nonlinear equalizer (FFNE) that reduces short-window maximum-likelihood sequence estimation to a compact binary decision rule. Includes a pattern-detection-based FFNE (PD-FFNE) extension for PAM-4 and joint precursor/postcursor equalization.

Result: The FFNE approaches the noise-margin advantage of an ideal 1-tap DFE without feedback timing penalties. The PD-FFNE outperforms conventional FFE+DFE baselines under representative channel conditions.

Conclusion: The FFNE framework offers a practical feedforward alternative to DFE-based equalization, balancing performance and complexity for short- to medium-reach wireline applications.

Abstract: This paper presents a feedforward nonlinear equalizer (FFNE) framework for short- to medium-reach wireline links that removes the feedback-timing bottleneck of decision-feedback equalizers (DFEs) while approaching the noise-margin advantage within a characterized operating region. The proposed FFNE reduces short-window maximum-likelihood sequence estimation to a compact binary decision rule, enabling a low-complexity feedforward realization without transmitter-side encoding. For the single-postcursor NRZ case, the mathematical foundation, hardware implementation, tap adaptation, statistical analysis, and equalization limit relative to an ideal 1-tap DFE are established. A window-length-3 FFNE quantifies the performance-complexity tradeoff of longer sequence windows. The framework is further extended to PAM-4 modulation and simultaneous precursor/postcursor equalization through a pattern-detection-based FFNE (PD-FFNE), which outperforms conventional FFE+DFE baselines under representative channel conditions.

</details>


### [233] [Enhanced Wide-Angle Steering with Multi-Mode Multi-Port Aperture Antenna Arrays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08320)
*Tim Hahn,Dirk Manteuffel*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A new antenna concept using multi-mode multi-port radiators enables wide-angle beam scanning up to ±77° with only 3dB scan loss and no grating lobes, verified through theory, algorithm development, and measurement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve wide-angle beam steering beyond conventional phased array limits while avoiding grating lobes and minimizing scan loss.

Method: Develops theory of multi-mode multi-port aperture radiators, designs an antenna array using these radiators, and implements an advanced beamforming algorithm that exploits higher degrees of freedom.

Result: Achieved wide-angle steering up to ±77° from broadside with 3dB scan loss in both horizontal and vertical planes, with no visible grating lobes.

Conclusion: Multi-mode multi-port antennas combined with appropriate beamforming can significantly extend the scanning range of antenna arrays beyond conventional limits.

Abstract: A novel concept for wide-angle scanning is proposed based on multi-mode multi-port antennas. The theory of multi-mode multi-port antennas based on aperture radiators is developed and applied towards the design of an antenna array consisting of multi-mode aperture radiators. An advanced beamforming algorithm is developed and implemented, making use of the higher degrees of freedom available to multi-mode multi-port antennas. The manufactured antenna array is measured and compared to the expected performance. Wide-angle steering up to $\pm77^\circ$ from broadside with respect to a scan loss of $3\,\mathrm{dB}$ is achieved in both the horizontal and vertical plane with no visible grating lobe.

</details>


### [234] [A Switching Beamformer for Highly Non-Stationary Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08385)
*Manan Mittal,Ryan M. Corey,John R. Buck,Andrew C. Singer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The Universal Switching Beamformer (USB) integrates competitive sequential prediction to dynamically adjust memory length for adaptive beamforming, achieving both rapid tracking and stable interference rejection without heuristic tuning, with theoretical regret bounds and validation on SwellEx-96 data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional adaptive beamformers suffer from a fundamental memory trade-off in non-stationary environments: short windows enable fast tracking but high variance, while long windows provide stable rejection but fail to adapt to sudden interference changes. Existing methods lack a principled solution to this dilemma.

Method: The USB integrates competitive sequential prediction into beamforming using a linear transition diagram, implicitly maintaining an exponential family of candidate covariance histories and dynamically re-weighting them based on cumulative output power, enabling automatic memory length adjustment without change detection or parameter tuning.

Result: A theoretical regret bound is proven relative to an omniscient oracle. Simulations and SwellEx-96 experiments show USB achieves both the agility of short-window estimators and the precision of long-term integration in highly non-stationary scenes.

Conclusion: The USB provides a principled, theoretically grounded solution for adaptive beamforming under rapidly changing interference, eliminating the need for heuristic parameter tuning while outperforming conventional fixed-window approaches.

Abstract: Adaptive beamforming is a cornerstone of array signal processing, yet its performance often collapses in the face of complex, rapidly changing interference. When interferers appear or move unpredictably, conventional estimators encounter a fundamental memory trade-off: short windows enable rapid tracking but suffer from high estimation variance, while long windows provide stable rejection but fail to adapt to shifts. This challenge is resolved by introducing the Universal Switching Beamformer (USB), which integrates competitive sequential prediction into the beamforming architecture. By employing a linear transition diagram, the USB implicitly maintains an exponentially large family of candidate covariance histories and dynamically re-weights them based on their cumulative output power. This mechanism allows the beamformer to automatically vary its effective memory length without explicit change detection or heuristic parameter tuning. A theoretical upper bound is proven on the regret relative to an omniscient oracle that selects the best piecewise-stationary covariance model in hindsight. Extensive simulations and experiments on the SwellEx-96 dataset demonstrate that the USB achieves the agility of short-window estimators and the precision of long-term integration, providing a principled solution for tracking highly non-stationary scenes.

</details>


### [235] [Mixture-of-Experts Transformer for Automatic Modulation Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09085)
*Jiale Wang,Wupeng Xie,Yaxin Mu,Xin Liu,Zhilong Zhao,Jingwei Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: MoEformer is an adaptive Multi-Scale Mixture-of-Experts Transformer network for Automatic Modulation Recognition that uses temporal resampling, dynamic expert fusion, and Rotary Position Embeddings to achieve state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks while maintaining efficient model complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning-based AMR methods rely on static multi-scale fusion strategies that lack flexibility to adapt to highly dynamic temporal variations of modulation signals.

Method: MoEformer constructs multi-scale expert views through temporal resampling, uses an input-dependent gating mechanism for dynamic expert fusion, and integrates Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) within Transformer encoders to capture both local and global temporal dependencies, processing I/Q signals directly.

Result: Outperforms competitive baselines on RadioML2016.10a (63.74%), RadioML2016.10b (66.24%), and RadioML2018.01A (64.22%), while achieving optimal trade-off between recognition performance and model complexity.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive multi-scale fusion approach via Mixture-of-Experts effectively addresses the limitation of static fusion strategies in AMR, delivering superior and efficient modulation recognition for next-generation wireless systems.

Abstract: Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) is a key enabling technology for cognitive radio and intelligent spectrum management in next-generation wireless systems. However, current deep learning-based AMR methods predominantly rely on static multi-scale fusion strategies, which lack the flexibility to adapt to the highly dynamic temporal variations of modulation signals. To address this limitation, we propose MoEformer, an adaptive Multi-Scale Mixture-of-Experts Transformer network that directly processes I/Q signals to preserve their temporal and phase structures. Specifically, MoEformer constructs multi scale expert views through temporal resampling, employs an input-dependent gating mechanism for dynamic expert fusion, and integrates Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) within Transformer encoders to capture both local and global tem poral dependencies. Comprehensive evaluations on three widely adopted benchmarks (RadioML2016.10a, RadioML2016.10b, and RadioML2018.01A) demonstrate that MoEformer outperforms the competitive baselines, achieving superior average recognition accuracies of 63.74%, 66.24%, and 64.22%, respectively. In addition, the proposed method strikes an optimal trade-off between recognition performance and model complexity.

</details>


### [236] [RSMA Technique for Multi-User Downlink Single-Waveguide Multi-Pinching Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09095)
*Harsh Raj,Mallena Vardhan,Keshav Singh,Sudip Biswas*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes integrating RSMA with pinching antennas in a SISO single-waveguide architecture to enable multi-user communication, achieving improved sum-rate, fairness, and BER with low hardware complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable low-complexity, cost-effective multi-user communication using reconfigurable pinching antennas while effectively managing multi-user interference.

Method: Integrates rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) into a SISO single-waveguide system with multiple pinching antennas activated along a shared waveguide.

Result: Improved system sum-rate, enhanced user rate fairness, and lower bit error rate (BER) compared to baselines.

Conclusion: RSMA-enhanced pinching antennas offer a low-cost, scalable solution for near-field LoS multi-user communications.

Abstract: Pinching antennas have recently emerged as a promising technology for reconfigurable wireless systems due to their ability to dynamically radiate signals from flexible positions along a waveguide. This letter investigates a multi-user communication framework by integrating rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) into a single-input single-output (SISO) single-waveguide architecture equipped with multiple pinching antennas. Multiple antennas are activated along a shared waveguide to radiate a common guided signal toward distributed users, enabling strong near-field line-of-sight (LoS) links with low hardware complexity and a single radiofrequency (RF) chain. To manage multi-user interference, RSMA is employed within the proposed architecture. Simulation results show that the proposed framework improves system sum-rate, enhances user rate fairness, and achieves lower bit error rate (BER) while preserving the low-cost and scalable characteristics of pinching antenna systems (PASS).

</details>


### [237] [Towards Intelligent Wireless Networks: The Synergy of Generative AI and Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09113)
*Afan Ali,Ali Arshad Nasir,Naveed Iqbal,Daniel Benevides da Costa*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A proactive GenAI-enabled digital twin framework for 6G wireless optimization achieves ~69.2% energy savings by anticipating network issues before they occur, validated on UAV-assisted non-terrestrial networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-assisted DT approaches are reactive, only adjusting network parameters after performance degradation, or restrict GenAI to isolated signal-level tasks. This work aims to achieve proactive, system-level wireless optimization.

Method: A GenAI-enabled DT framework that continuously synchronizes channel states, mobility dynamics, traffic conditions, and energy information in real-time, enabling anticipation of congestion, interference, and energy demand before they occur.

Result: Approximately 69.2% energy savings over reactive baselines while maintaining reliable QoS under dense and mobility-intensive conditions in a UAV-assisted NTN scenario.

Conclusion: The framework offers a scalable foundation for broader AI-native 6G applications including aerial platforms, autonomous systems, XR, industrial automation, and SAGS integrated infrastructures.

Abstract: This paper proposes a generative AI (GenAI)-enabled digital twin (DT) framework for proactive and energy-aware wireless optimization in future 6G ecosystems. Most existing AI-assisted DT approaches remain fundamentally reactive, adjusting network parameters only after performance degradation occurs or restricting GenAI to isolated signal-level tasks such as channel estimation. This work adopts a proactive approach. Instead of responding to problems after they appear, the proposed framework continuously synchronizes channel states, mobility dynamics, traffic conditions, and energy information within a real-time DT environment, enabling the system to anticipate congestion, interference, and energy demand before they materialize. The result is a closed-loop proactive architecture that operates at the system level, jointly managing communication, mobility, and resource dynamics for autonomous wireless control. Evaluations on a UAV-assisted non-terrestrial network (NTN) scenario show approximately 69.2\% energy savings over reactive baselines while maintaining reliable quality-of-service (QoS) under dense and mobility-intensive conditions. Beyond this specific scenario, the framework offers a scalable foundation for broader AI-native 6G applications, including aerial platforms, autonomous systems, extended reality (XR), industrial automation, and space-air-ground-sea (SAGS) integrated infrastructures.

</details>


### [238] [Block-Term Decomposition Approach to Blind Multi-trial Functional Ultrasound Unmixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09264)
*Sofia-Eirini Kotti,Eleftherios Kofidis,Borbála Hunyadi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a tensor decomposition-based model for functional ultrasound data that jointly separates latent neuronal sources, estimates spatiotemporal maps, and deconvolves hemodynamic response functions using constrained optimization with alternating projected gradient descent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fUS signals provide only indirect observations of neuronal activity via neurovascular coupling, requiring blind separation of latent neuronal sources and deconvolution of hemodynamic responses.

Method: A convolutive block-term tensor decomposition model with low-rank spatial maps, piecewise-constant neuronal activation signals, and trial/source-dependent HRFs, computed via alternating projected gradient descent.

Result: Accurate recovery of spatial maps and reliable activation temporal profile estimation across various noise levels; HRF estimation remains the most challenging part.

Conclusion: The proposed tensor decomposition approach is effective for fUS source separation and activation estimation, though HRF deconvolution remains difficult.

Abstract: Functional ultrasound (fUS) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging modality due to its high resolution in both space and time, low cost and potential portability. Nevertheless, fUS signals provide only indirect observations of neuronal activity through the neurovascular coupling, and hence require the blind separation of latent neuronal sources while also deconvolving their hemodynamic responses. In this work, we propose a data-driven convolutive block-term tensor decomposition-based model for multi-trial fUS measurements, where each source has a spatiotemporal representation comprising a low-rank spatial map and a piecewise-constant neuronal activation signal convolved with a trial- and source-dependent hemodynamic response function (HRF) with a physiologically plausible shape. We propose a constrained optimization framework for the model computation, which consists of alternating projected gradient descent iterations. Simulation results are reported that demonstrate accurate recovery of spatial maps and reliable estimation of activation temporal profiles across various noise levels, while confirming that HRF estimation remains the most challenging part of the problem.

</details>


### [239] [Wearable Single-Lead ECG Detects Fine-Grained Structural Heart Disease Through Echo-Report Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09332)
*Chenyang He,Qinghao Zhao,Shun Huang,Jun Li,Gongzheng Tang,Hao Zhang,Tong Liu,Zhengkai Xue,Jian Liu,Kangyin Chen,Cheng Ding,Shenda Hong*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: AnyECG-Echo uses contrastive pre-training between single-lead ECG and echocardiography reports to detect 13 subtypes of structural heart disease, validated across 25,222 patients (including external cohort), achieving high AUROC (0.836-0.931) and demonstrating physiologically interpretable outputs that function as digital biomarkers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection of structural heart disease (SHD) is critical but limited by echocardiography accessibility. Single-lead ECG is ubiquitous via wearables, but existing AI models require 12-lead inputs, lack generalizability, or need massive labeled datasets.

Method: Contrastive pre-training framework between single-lead ECG and echocardiography reports, evaluated on n=25,222 patients including geographically independent external cohort (n=16,621), covering 13 fine-grained SHD subtypes with dual-axis mechanistic interpretability (Shapley attribution + quantitative measurement correlations).

Result: High AUROC for key subtypes: reduced LV systolic function (0.866-0.924), global heart enlargement (0.877-0.931), mitral stenosis (0.836-0.906). Model outputs align with established physiological traits and function as digital biomarkers tracking LVEF and myocardial wall thickness.

Conclusion: Wearable single-lead ECGs can effectively detect fine-grained structural heart disease, enabling practical population-scale screening for SHD.

Abstract: Structural heart disease (SHD) is a primary driver of heart failure and cardiovascular mortality, yet early detection remains constrained by the limited accessibility of echocardiography. While single-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) is ubiquitous through wearables, existing AI screening models often depend on 12-lead inputs, generalize poorly across institutions, or require massive, condition-specific labeled datasets. Recent work has demonstrated the feasibility of contrastive pre-training between single-lead ECGs and echocardiography reports within a single health system. Here, we present AnyECG-Echo, a framework that advance this paradigm toward clinical translation through three key developments: (1) evaluation in a geographically independent external cohort (n = 16,621); (2) diagnostic coverage of 13 fine-grained SHD subtypes spanning myocardial, chamber, valvular, and great-vessel pathologies; and (3) dual-axis mechanistic interpretability combining electrophysiology-grounded Shapley attribution with emergent correlations to quantitative measurements. Across validation cohorts totaling n = 25,222, the model demonstrated high AUROC for high-impact subtypes, including reduced left ventricular systolic function (AUROC 0.866-0.924), global heart enlargement (0.877-0.931), and mitral stenosis (0.836-0.906). Furthermore, we successfully validated the alignment of model outputs with established medical physiological traits, thereby enhancing interpretability. Notably, we discovered that AnyECG-Echo's outputs function as physiologically grounded digital biomarkers that accurately track objective metrics such as LVEF and myocardial wall thickness. These findings prove that wearable single-lead ECGs can effectively detect fine-grained structural heart disease, offering a practical solution for population-scale screening.

</details>


### [240] [Orbital Plane Geometry and Information Conditioning for Doppler-Only LEO Positioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09496)
*Charles E Thornton*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a geometric information model for Doppler-only positioning using LEO satellites, showing that each satellite contributes information within its orbital plane and deriving closed-form eigenvalue expressions for multi-satellite positioning accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental role of constellation geometry in Doppler-only positioning, motivated by the observation that Doppler measurements provide information primarily along the satellite's orbital plane.

Method: Models each satellite's Doppler information as a weighted projection operator onto its orbital plane; derives closed-form expressions for eigenvalues, condition number, and worst-case CRLB; validates with Monte Carlo simulations of pass-integrated Fisher information matrices.

Result: For two satellites, positioning conditioning depends on the dihedral angle between orbital planes and relative information strengths; the surrogate model captures dominant conditioning trends from orbital-plane diversity.

Conclusion: Provides a simple geometric framework for understanding constellation geometry's role in Doppler-only positioning accuracy.

Abstract: We study an idealized information model for Doppler-only positioning with low earth orbit (LEO) signals of opportunity from a stationary receiver. Motivated by the observation that Doppler measurements from a satellite pass provide information primarily within the associated orbital plane, we model each satellite contribution as a weighted projection onto that plane. Under this model, the combined information matrix from multiple satellites is a sum of orbital-plane projection operators. Closed-form expressions are derived for the eigenvalues, condition number, and worst-case Cramer-Rao lower bound. For two satellites, the conditioning is governed by the dihedral angle between orbital planes and the relative information strengths of the two links. Monte Carlo evaluation of pass-integrated Doppler Fisher information matrices demonstrates that the proposed surrogate captures the dominant conditioning trends associated with orbital-plane diversity. The results provide a simple geometric framework for understanding the role of constellation geometry in Doppler-only positioning systems.

</details>


### [241] [Hierarchical Federated Learning for Unsupervised Waveform Classification over Tactical MANETs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09504)
*Charles E. Thornton,Daniel J. Jakubisin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical federated learning framework for unsupervised waveform classification over tactical MANETs, using denoising convolutional autoencoders and connectivity-based relay aggregation, showing that moderate link loss can actually improve unsupervised representation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional federated learning assumes ideal links or supervised training, which don't hold for tactical MANETs with no persistent backhaul, no ground truth labels, and unreliable communication.

Method: Each node trains a local denoising convolutional autoencoder on raw IQ observations via self-supervised reconstruction. A two-stage aggregation protocol uses OLSR-consistent relay aggregators to compress cluster updates before forwarding to a mobile server proxy.

Result: In-network aggregation reduces transmission bits by ~12% at equivalent performance. Stochastic channel-driven subsampling under non-IID data acts as implicit regularizer, with MANET conditions matching or exceeding ideal federated averaging.

Conclusion: Moderate link loss can partially compensate for client drift in heterogeneous networks, suggesting robustness of the unsupervised approach in contested environments.

Abstract: Distributed radio frequency sensing in contested tactical environments demands collaborative learning across mobile nodes. In ad-hoc networks, learning must occur without persistent backhaul, ground truth labels, or reliable communication links. Traditional federated learning approaches assume either ideal link conditions or supervised training objectives, neither of which holds in practice for deployed MANET platforms.
