This paper presents a hierarchical federated learning framework for unsupervised waveform classification over tactical MANETs subject to Rayleigh fading, random waypoint mobility, and multi-hop routing loss. Each node trains a local denoising convolutional autoencoder on raw IQ observations without label exchange, learning compact representations through a self-supervised reconstruction objective. A two-stage aggregation protocol elects connectivity-based relay aggregators consistent with OLSR multipoint relay selection, compressing cluster-level model updates before forwarding to a mobile server proxy. Simulation results demonstrate that in-network aggregation reduces attempted transmission bits relative to relay-forward federated averaging by around 12% at equivalent classification performance. Notably, stochastic channel-driven subsampling under non-IID data acts as an implicit regularizer, with both MANET conditions matching or exceeding ideal federated averaging on unsupervised representation quality. This suggests that moderate link loss can partially compensate for client drift in heterogeneous networks. Performance is assessed on analysis of the learned latent embeddings using KMeans normalized mutual information and linear probe accuracy.

</details>


### [242] [Bernoulli Filtering for Multi-Sensor Tracking with Thresholded Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09573)
*Gustav Zetterqvist,Fredrik Gustafsson,Gustaf Hendeby*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper extends a missed detection framework using a Bernoulli filter to handle recursive multi-sensor tracking with state-dependent detection probabilities, achieving 62.4% reduction in GOSPA error compared to conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Target tracking is challenging when sensor detection thresholds cause state-dependent missed detections, especially in multi-sensor scenarios with clutter and uncertain target existence. Existing frameworks are limited to individual measurements and do not address recursive tracking.

Method: The authors extend the missed detection framework using a Bernoulli filter formulation, implementing a Bernoulli particle filter evaluated in simulated 2D multi-sensor tracking with nonlinear measurements, clutter, and detection uncertainty.

Result: Incorporating accurate detection threshold knowledge reduces the GOSPA metric by 62.4% compared to a conventional Bernoulli filter with fixed detection probability, with better balance between missed detections and false alarms.

Conclusion: The proposed Bernoulli filter with state-dependent detection modeling significantly outperforms conventional fixed-probability approaches in challenging multi-sensor tracking scenarios.

Abstract: Target tracking is challenging when sensor detection thresholds cause state-dependent missed detections, particularly in multi-sensor scenarios with clutter and uncertain target existence. A recently developed missed detection framework models detection probability as a function of target state, sensor characteristics, and detection threshold, but it is limited to individual measurements and does not address the recursive tracking problem. This work extends the framework using a Bernoulli filter formulation to jointly handle recursive target tracking, clutter, and target existence uncertainty. A Bernoulli particle filter is evaluated in a simulated 2D multi-sensor tracking scenario with nonlinear measurements, clutter, and detection uncertainty. Incorporating accurate detection threshold knowledge reduces the generalized optimal subpattern assignment (GOSPA) metric by 62.4% compared to a conventional Bernoulli filter with fixed detection probability, while better balancing missed detections and false alarms.

</details>


### [243] [Throughput Analysis for Near-Field Mobile Communications: Beamfocusing or Caustic Beamforming?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09652)
*Jiannan Wang,Xianghao Yu,Robert Schober*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analytically compares caustic beamforming (CB) and beamfocusing (BF) for THz communications with mobile users, showing that CB eliminates beam tracking overhead and becomes the preferred paradigm at extremely high frequencies due to its invariance to user speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The combination of extremely narrow beams in THz/ELAA systems and user mobility causes high switching overhead for conventional beamfocusing, degrading throughput. Caustic beamforming offers a potential alternative, but a rigorous comparative framework was missing.

Method: The authors develop an analytical framework using Airy beam as a canonical model for CB. They derive closed-form throughput expressions for both BF (with defocusing penalty and optimal dwell time) and CB (function of SNR and trajectory geometry), and establish a switching overhead threshold for paradigm selection.

Result: BF throughput depends on user speed and switching overhead with an optimal dwell time. CB throughput is determined by SNR and trajectory geometry but invariant to user speed. A crossover threshold for switching overhead is analytically established, which vanishes at extremely high frequencies.

Conclusion: At extremely high frequencies, the switching overhead threshold asymptotically vanishes, positioning continuous caustic beamforming as the preferred design paradigm for high-mobility THz communications.

Abstract: The migration to the Terahertz (THz) band and the deployment of extremely large antenna arrays (ELAAs) are transitioning wireless communications into the radiative near-field regime, fundamentally evolving conventional angular beam steering to beamfocusing (BF). However, the combination of the extremely narrow beamwidth and the mobility of the users necessitates frequent beamfocusing reconfigurations, incurring a significant switching overhead that degrades the system achievable throughput. In this regard, caustic beamforming (CB) is a promising alternative based on the synthesis of a continuous curved beam, which eliminates the need for beam tracking at the expense of a distributed beamforming gain. By leveraging the Airy beam as a canonical model, this paper develops an analytical framework to compare the throughputs achieved by CB and BF. Our main results include closed-form throughput expressions for both beamforming strategies and a performance boundary for paradigm selection. First, we derive the BF throughput by modeling a defocusing penalty induced by continuous user movement. The optimal beam dwell time that maximizes the throughput is analytically determined, and the impact of user speed and switching overhead on the throughput is quantified. For the CB scheme, we demonstrate that its throughput is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and the geometry of the trajectory of the user, yet invariant to the user speed. Finally, we analytically establish a threshold for the switching overhead to define the crossover point of the achievable throughput of both beamformers. Crucially, this threshold asymptotically vanishes at extremely high frequencies, positioning the continuous CB scheme as the preferred beam design paradigm for high-mobility THz communications.

</details>


### [244] [Jamming-Resilient Sparse Delay-Doppler NOMA: Unitary Precoding, Randomized Active Sets, and Superincreasing Power Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09753)
*Michel Kulhandjian,Hovannes Kulhandjian,Theodoros A. Tsiftsis*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A sparse delay-Doppler NOMA scheme is proposed that resists jamming by using random subset activation of delay-Doppler bins with unitary precoding, achieving no jammer-induced BER floor and robustness even when the shared seed is compromised.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design a NOMA scheme that is resilient to intentional jamming, overcoming the partial-band floor limitation of conventional OTFS-NOMA.

Method: Users transmit on random small subsets of delay-Doppler bins, spread via unitary precoders (Hadamard, DFT, Haar-random), with active subsets redrawn per frame from a shared pseudo-random seed. Receiver discards jammed bins, recovers sparse signals via least squares, and decodes via SIC. For >2 users uses Merkle-Hellman knapsack power allocation; for >4 users partitions into pairs on disjoint bin subsets.

Result: All precoders yield similar BER due to Marchenko-Pastur conditioning. No jammer-induced BER floor. Even compromised seed preserves performance. Superincreasing power allocation enables low-complexity SIC matching ML detection. 40 dB BER-ratio improvement against pattern-aware jammers, ~24 dB cumulative gain over conventional OTFS-NOMA.

Conclusion: The proposed sparse delay-Doppler NOMA is provably resilient to jamming, with closed-form BER showing no floor, and extends naturally to Rician fading. Monte Carlo simulations validate analytical results within 3 dB.

Abstract: We propose a sparse delay-Doppler NOMA scheme resilient to intentional jamming. The transmitter places user data on a small random subset of delay-Doppler bins, spreads the result through a unitary precoder, and re-draws the active subset per frame from a pseudo-random seed
  shared with the receiver. The receiver detects and discards jammed bins, recovers the sparse signal by least squares, and decodes per bin via SIC. Hadamard, DFT, and Haar-random precoders all yield essentially the same BER, because a Marchenko-Pastur conditioning argument
  controls any random unitary submatrix. The closed-form BER has no jammer-induced floor, unlike the well-known partial-band floor of conventional OTFS-NOMA. The same argument shows that compromising the shared seed does not break the system: random unitary submatrices remain
  well-conditioned, so BER stays within the unjammed envelope. For more than two users we use a superincreasing power allocation (Merkle-Hellman knapsack) and prove the resulting low-complexity SIC matches maximum-likelihood detection exactly, removing the usual SIC propagation
  ceiling. For more than four users we partition them into pairs assigned to disjoint bin subsets; this OMA-friendly NOMA rule reaches floor BER at eight users by SNR around 20 dB. We extend the framework to Rician fading and show the jammer-independence property holds for
  arbitrary Rician K-factor. Monte Carlo simulations track the analytical predictions within 3 dB and show at least a 40 dB BER-ratio improvement against pattern-aware jammers, with about 24 dB of cumulative gain over conventional OTFS-NOMA under oracle jamming.

</details>


### [245] [Adaptive Derivative Estimation via Stein's Unbiased Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09829)
*Yonathan Murin,Ali Ozer Ercan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: SURDE is a causal FIR derivative filter that uses Stein's Unbiased Risk Estimator (SURE) to dynamically balance noise amplification and smoothing bias by soft-combining filters of different lengths, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating derivatives from noisy sampled data is fundamental to many fields. Causal FIR derivative filters face a tradeoff: short filters amplify noise while long filters introduce smoothing bias. Existing methods fail to optimally address this tradeoff at each time step.

Method: SURDE evaluates a data-driven cost derived from SURE across a bank of candidate filter lengths and soft-combines their outputs via exponential weighting. The weighting temperature is derived in closed form using a minimax-optimal oracle inequality.

Result: SURDE outperforms ICI and AWVE for first-derivative estimation in numerical simulations. It is robust to noise-variance misspecification (9% degradation over a 4× range) and superior on the EuRoC MAV real dataset.

Conclusion: SURDE is causal, computationally light, requires only a rough noise variance estimate, and offers theoretically grounded adaptive derivative estimation with strong practical performance.

Abstract: Estimating derivatives from noisy sampled data is fundamental to control, human--computer interaction, and biomedical engineering. Causal FIR derivative filters offer a natural approach for this challenge, yet their performance depend on their length. While short filters amplify noise, long filters introduce smoothing bias. We present SURDE (SURE Derivative Estimator), which addresses this tradeoff at each time step by evaluating a data-driven cost derived from Stein's Unbiased Risk Estimator (SURE) across a bank of candidate lengths and soft-combining their outputs via exponential weighting. We prove a minimax-optimal oracle inequality for the soft-combined estimator and use it to derive the optimal weighting temperature in closed form. Thus, the only tuning parameter for SURDE is the noise variance. Via numerical simulations we show that SURDE consistently outperforms alternative adaptive methods (the Intersection of Confidence Intervals (ICI) rule and the Adaptive Windowing Velocity Estimator (AWVE)) for first-derivative estimation. We further show that \surede{} is robust to noise-variance misspecification (9\% degradation over a $4\times$ range), and that it is superior to ICI and AWVE also over real data scenarios (the EuRoC MAV dataset). SURDE is causal, computationally light, and requires only a rough estimate of the noise variance.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [246] [Offline Reinforcement Learning for Plasma Control in Nuclear Fusion: Codebase and Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07550)
*Yang Fu,Haomin Bao,Rohit Sonker,Xiaoyan Hu,Aravind Venugopal,Jeff Schneider,Jiayu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL4F is a new offline RL benchmark for plasma control in nuclear fusion, built from real DIII-D tokamak data. It evaluates multiple methods across four plasma profile tracking tasks, finding model-based RL performs best on average but no single method dominates all tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL is promising for plasma controller development using historical tokamak data, avoiding costly online trial-and-error. However, progress is hindered by the lack of a standardized benchmark for realistic multi-actuator, long-horizon plasma control problems.

Method: The authors built closed-loop evaluation environments from historical DIII-D tokamak discharge data, covering four full-profile tracking tasks (rotation, density, temperature, pressure). They evaluated multiple imitation learning and offline RL baselines under a unified protocol.

Result: Offline model-based RL methods achieved the best average performance on most objectives, though no single method dominated all tasks, highlighting the importance of dynamics modeling in complex, long-horizon plasma control.

Conclusion: RL4F provides an open-source benchmark (codebase, datasets, evaluation framework) useful for both the fusion community and offline RL algorithm development.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) offers a promising route for developing plasma controllers from historical tokamak data, since online trial-and-error on real devices is costly and risky. However, progress in this direction remains difficult to measure due to the lack of a standardized offline RL benchmark for realistic multi-actuator, long-horizon plasma control problems in nuclear fusion. We introduce RL4F, an Offline Reinforcement Learning Benchmark for Plasma Control in Nuclear Fusion, providing closed-loop evaluation environments and baseline comparisons across four full-profile tracking tasks: rotation, density, temperature, and pressure. The dynamics function underlying the evaluation environment is built from historical discharge data from DIII-D, a real-world Tokamak. We evaluate a broad set of imitation learning and offline RL baselines under a unified protocol. We find that offline model-based RL methods obtain the best average performance on most objectives, although no single method dominates all tasks, highlighting the importance of dynamics modeling in complex, long-horizon plasma control tasks. To foster further research, we open-source the codebase, datasets, and evaluation framework, providing a benchmark not only for the fusion community but also for algorithm development in offline RL.

</details>


### [247] [MedicalRec: Medical recommender system for image classification without retraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07553)
*Roghayeh Taghavi,Aysa Hasanazde Bashkandi,Amir Ali Bengari,Mohammad Amin Raji,Mohammad Salahi Ardekani,Parisa Mardukhian,Parvaneh Rezaei,Ramin Mousa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes MedicalRec, a transformer-based recommender system for medical image classification models, along with MedicalRec-Bench, a dataset of 5,000+ records from 3,000 articles. The model recommends optimal classification models to reduce trial-and-error energy consumption, achieving 75.5% HitRate@100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML/DL models in healthcare consume significant computing power and energy. Researchers often use trial-and-error to select classification models, leading to unnecessary energy waste and carbon emissions. This paper aims to reduce that waste by recommending suitable models.

Method: The authors collected 5,000+ records from 3,000 medical image classification articles (MedicalRec-Bench). The dataset has four variants with different feature counts (5, 9, 11, 18 features). They developed a transformer-based recommender system (MedicalRec) to suggest optimal models for classification tasks.

Result: MedicalRec achieved a maximum HitRate@100 of 75.5% on the benchmark dataset and was evaluated against 12 base models. The dataset contains significant missing values due to non-reporting by authors.

Conclusion: The transformer-based recommender system can effectively recommend medical image classification models, reducing the need for energy-intensive trial-and-error experimentation. Dataset and code are publicly available on GitHub.

Abstract: The emergence of machine learning and deep learning has revolutionized the efficiency of diagnostic, therapeutic, and administrative systems in healthcare. However, this rapid adoption has come at the cost of requiring significant computing power and energy consumption, as well as e-waste disposal and carbon emissions. One of the challenges of these models is choosing the right model for classification tasks. To this end, researchers attempt to identify the optimal model using their data through trial and error, which involves energy consumption and waste. The goal of this study is to develop a model-based recommender system for medical image classification. For this purpose, a data set was collected from 3,000 articles in the field of medical image classification. This dataset, publicly available under the name MedicalRec-Bench, contains over 5,000 records of models tested in various tasks, including Skin Cancer Classification, Tumour Classification, Wound Classification, Breast Cancer, and MRI classification. The dataset was evaluated in four different modes, depending on the number of features: MedicalRec I (5 features), MedicalRec II (9 features), MedicalRec III (11 features), and MedicalRec IV (18 features). Collecting all values for the features is challenging due to non-reporting by the authors; hence, the dataset contains significant amounts of missing values. The Medical Recommender System (MedicalRec) is a transformer-based model used for item recommendations in this study. This model achieved remarkable results in the evaluation on the dataset and in the evaluation with 12 base models. This model achieved a maximum HitRate@100 of 75.5%. The dataset and implementations are available through the GitHub link: https://github.com/Ramin1Mousa/MedicalRec

</details>


### [248] [SPIN: Decentralized Swarm Control via Tensorized Policy Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07557)
*Zhaowen Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPIN framework enables computationally efficient decentralized multi-agent swarm coordination on edge devices by factorizing joint policy tensors into Matrix Product State chains (reducing exponential to linear complexity) and using a hybrid neuro-symbolic pipeline with offline-trained encoders and zero-shot runtime adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent swarm coordination on resource-constrained edge platforms is bottlenecked by exponential scaling of joint action spaces and high-latency communication overhead.

Method: (1) Models swarm topologies as compressed tensor networks, factorizing joint policy tensors into MPS chains (complexity: O(m·n·χ²) vs O(n^m)). (2) Uses a decoupled hybrid neuro-symbolic pipeline: offline-trained neural encoders map geometric descriptors to targets; runtime uses Radon-Nikodým derivative as zero-shot importance-reweighting filter for instantaneous adaptation.

Result: Validated in discrete-time multi-agent simulations (tracking, dispersion/coverage, multi-goal coordination). Achieves stable target-directed motion, anti-collapse spatial spreading, and structured subgroup formation across multiple targets.

Conclusion: SPIN provides a mathematically grounded route to tractable, low-power edge swarm intelligence by bypassing exponential complexity and avoiding power-intensive online training.

Abstract: Decentralized multi-agent swarm coordination on resource-constrained edge platforms remains fundamentally bottlenecked by the exponential scaling of joint action spaces and high-latency communication overhead. This paper introduces the Swarm Policy Interference Network (SPIN) framework, an architectural paradigm that bypasses these limitations by modeling swarm topologies as a compressed tensor network. We factorize the joint policy tensors of local multi-agent cliques into Matrix Product State (MPS) chains, reducing the computational complexity of evaluation from an exponential $O(n^m)$ wall to a strictly linear $O(m \cdot n \cdot χ^2)$ constraint. To bridge local continuous spatial geometry with this discrete algebraic backend without requiring power-intensive online training loops, we introduce a decoupled, hybrid neuro-symbolic control pipeline. Local multi-layered neural networks operate as structural coordination encoders, pre-trained offline to nonlinearly map hand-engineered geometric descriptors into abstract environmental target measures. At runtime, edge agents execute instantaneous behavioral adaptations by applying the Radon-Nikodým derivative directly as a zero-shot importance-reweighting filter. We validate the framework within a discrete-time multi-agent simulation sandbox spanning tracking, decentralized dispersion/area coverage, and multi-goal coordination regimes. Qualitative telemetry demonstrates that the integrated pipeline achieves stable target-directed motion, anti-collapse spatial spreading under decentralized constraints, and structured subgroup formation across multiple targets, providing a mathematically grounded route to tractable, low-power edge swarm intelligence.

</details>


### [249] [Boundary Variance Inflation Causes Acquisition Bias in Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07561)
*Maria Bånkestad,Sanna Jarl,Jens Sjölund*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stationary kernel Gaussian processes on bounded domains cause boundary-inflated variance and acquisition bias due to kernel neighborhood truncation, with effects varying across acquisition strategies and worsening in higher dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and diagnose the underexplored causes and effects of boundary-induced acquisition bias in Gaussian processes on bounded domains, which leads to over-exploration in Bayesian optimization.

Method: Tracing root cause to truncation of kernel correlation neighborhood at boundaries; analyzing how this geometric distortion manifests across three acquisition classes (variance maximization, negative integrated posterior variance, expected predictive information gain); introducing a function-free selection-profile diagnostic.

Result: Boundary distortion worsens with dimensionality; variance maximization concentrates at corners; other acquisition classes shift selections to interior shells; acquisition behavior can be dominated by kernel geometry rather than task-specific uncertainty.

Conclusion: A function-free diagnostic can quantify boundary-induced bias for arbitrary acquisitions, kernels, and bounded-domain geometries, enabling detection when acquisition behavior is geometry-dominated rather than objective-driven.

Abstract: Gaussian processes with stationary kernels on bounded domains exhibit inflated posterior variance near the boundary. Despite being a long-recognized artifact in geostatistics and a source of over-exploration in Bayesian optimization, the causes and effects of boundary-induced acquisition bias are underexplored. We trace the root cause to a simple geometric mechanism: the truncation of the kernel correlation neighborhood at the domain boundary creates an observation-independent distortion that worsens with dimensionality. We show how this distortion manifests across three acquisition classes: variance maximization concentrates selections at the corners, whereas negative integrated posterior variance and expected predictive information gain move selections inward to axis-aligned interior shells. These patterns arise without reference to any objective function, meaning that acquisition behavior can be dominated by kernel geometry rather than the desired task-specific uncertainty. To quantify this, we introduce a function-free selection-profile diagnostic for arbitrary acquisitions, kernels, and bounded-domain geometries.

</details>


### [250] [Emergence via Phase Transitions: Mechanism Landscapes and Universal Convergence Across Complex Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07563)
*Truong Xuan Khanh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Hierarchical Emergence Framework (HEF), a mathematical framework modeling emergence as a phase transition in mechanism landscapes. It proves convergence to unique fixed-point representations and empirically validates the framework on 111 grokking experiments, finding systematic weight norm peaks before grokking and convergence to consistent accuracy levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why independently evolving systems across machine learning, biology, and physics converge toward similar high-level structures despite different microscopic details, and to provide a unified mathematical framework for understanding such convergence phenomena.

Method: HEF models emergence as a phase transition in a mechanism landscape constrained by thermodynamic and information-theoretic laws, with a critical energy threshold Ec separating exploration and convergence regimes. The framework proves physical feasibility, metric contraction, and convergence to a unique fixed point. Empirically tested via 111 grokking experiments on modular arithmetic transformers.

Result: Weight norm peaks systematically before grokking in 92% of runs; normalized accuracy curves collapse onto a tanh kink (R^2=0.93); all grokked models converge to 0.9745±0.014 regardless of initialization, weight decay, or training fraction (ANOVA p>0.13).

Conclusion: HEF provides a falsifiable mathematical scaffold for studying convergence phenomena across complex systems, validated by reproducible empirical evidence from grokking experiments showing phase-transition-like behavior consistent with Landau-Ginzburg universality class.

Abstract: Across machine learning, biology, and physics, independently evolving systems often converge toward strikingly similar high-level structures despite radically different microscopic details. Grokking circuits converge across random seeds, evolutionary lineages rediscover similar metabolic solutions, and renormalization flows approach common fixed points. We propose the Hierarchical Emergence Framework (HEF) as a candidate universality framework for such convergence phenomena. HEF models emergence as a phase transition in a mechanism landscape constrained by thermodynamic and information-theoretic laws. The framework introduces a critical energy threshold Ec separating an exploration regime with competing mechanisms from a convergence regime governed by a unique minimum-cost mechanism. Under structural assumptions, we prove physical feasibility, derive strict metric contraction, and establish convergence toward a unique fixed-point representation independent of initial conditions. We further connect this convergence structure to causal emergence through Effective Information and mechanism competition entropy. To test the framework, we study delayed generalization ("grokking") in modular arithmetic transformers across 111 experiments. We identify a reproducible empirical fingerprint of the Ec transition: the weight norm peaks systematically before grokking in 92% of runs. Normalized accuracy curves collapse onto a tanh kink (R^2=0.93) consistent with a Landau-Ginzburg universality class, and all grokked models converge to 0.9745+/-0.014 regardless of initialization, weight decay, or training fraction (ANOVA p>0.13). HEF is not presented as a universal theory of emergence, but as a falsifiable mathematical scaffold for studying convergence phenomena across complex systems.

</details>


### [251] [STARIXNet: Multivariate and Multi-attribute Deep Learning Approach to Real-Time Resource Allocation in Cloud Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07565)
*Ahmed Abdulaal,Maruf Aytekin,Thilaga kumaran Srinivasan,Tomer Lancewicki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STARIXNet is a lightweight neural network for intelligent microservice scaling that uses multivariate spatio-temporal analysis across system metrics to prioritize service stability and cost-efficiency over pure forecasting accuracy, achieving 10-50% cost savings at Walmart.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current microservice scaling solutions are limited to univariate analysis (e.g., only CPU usage), treat scaling as a pure forecasting task ignoring underestimation risks, or are computationally too complex for real-time deployment.

Method: STARIXNet is a lightweight neural network that captures spatio-temporal relationships among multiple system metrics, modeling Seasonal, Temporal, Auto-Regressive Integrated, and eXogenous (STARIX) patterns, with an aggregation policy prioritizing service stability > cost-efficiency > forecast accuracy.

Result: Benchmarked against existing solutions in real-world settings and deployed at Walmart for critical production microservices, achieving 10% to 50% tangible cost savings with intangible benefits in service stability and customer experience.

Conclusion: STARIXNet effectively addresses multivariate scaling in cloud platforms, balancing cost savings with service stability, and proves practical for large-scale real-time deployment.

Abstract: Intelligent scaling of microservices in cloud platforms is crucial for mitigating escalating compute costs while avoiding service disruptions. Current solutions are limited to the univariate space, typically focusing on CPU usage alone to drive scaling decisions. Moreover, they address the problem as a purely forecasting task, focusing on prediction precision while neglecting the greater risks of underestimation and delays in system responsiveness. Alternative solutions are computationally complex, making them impractical for large-scale, real-time deployments. To address these challenges, we present STARIXNet, a lightweight neural network that guides resource allocation decisions in the multivariate space by capturing spatio-temporal relationships among multiple system metrics. STARIXNet models multiple quasi-dependent attributes, in particular the (S)easonal, (T)emporal, (A)uto-(R)egressive (I)ntegrated, and e(X)ogenous patterns, then implements an aggregation policy to finalize scaling decisions, prioritizing service stability, followed by cost-efficiency, over raw forecast accuracy. We empirically demonstrate the performance of STARIXNet by benchmarking against existing solutions in real-world settings. STARIXNet is deployed for critical production microservices at Walmart achieving tangible savings ranging from 10\% to 50\%, in addition to intangible benefits through improved service stability and customer experience.

</details>


### [252] [TriHead-GAN: A Generative Adversarial Network with Triple-Head Discriminator for Carbon Emission Time Series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07569)
*Zesen Wang,Lijuan Lan,Yonggang Li,Chunhua Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriHead-GAN is a Transformer-based adversarial framework with a triple-head discriminator that jointly supervises distributional authenticity, cross-variable dependencies, and temporal smoothness for generating realistic city-level carbon emission time series data, outperforming mainstream baselines on multiple datasets and improving downstream forecasting in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: City-level high-frequency carbon emission monitoring data is extremely scarce, limiting deep learning models. Time series generation is a natural remedy, but existing GAN and diffusion models fail to preserve cross-variable correlations (e.g., CO₂ with pollutants and meteorological factors) and realistic step-wise variability, often producing overly smooth sequences.

Method: TriHead-GAN uses a Transformer-based generator with global self-attention, local temporal convolution, per-step noise injection, and anti-smoothing loss. Its triple-head discriminator jointly supervises: (1) distributional authenticity via Wasserstein critic, (2) cross-variable dependency via leakage-free regression of the target variable, and (3) step-wise temporal smoothness via adjacent-difference prediction.

Result: Experiments on the self-collected Changsha Carbon dataset, two public carbon datasets (China, US), and ETTh1 benchmark show TriHead-GAN achieves favorable performance over mainstream baselines on the vast majority of settings, and synthetic windows improve downstream forecasting accuracy in low-resource carbon monitoring scenarios.

Conclusion: TriHead-GAN effectively addresses the scarcity of city-level carbon emission data by generating high-fidelity synthetic time series that preserve cross-variable dependencies and realistic temporal dynamics, with demonstrated utility for improving downstream forecasting tasks.

Abstract: Accurate carbon emission monitoring is critical for climate policy and emerging regulatory mechanisms such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, yet city-level high-frequency monitoring data remain extremely scarce, severely limiting data-hungry deep learning models. Time series generation is a natural remedy, but existing GAN and diffusion-based generators often provide limited explicit supervision for the domain structure of carbon emission data: they may match marginal distributional statistics while insufficiently preserving cross-variable correlations between CO$_2$ and co-emitted pollutants and meteorological factors, and tend to collapse the first-difference statistics of atmospheric measurements, producing sequences that are smooth on average but lack the realistic step-wise variability of the underlying signals. We propose TriHead-GAN, a Transformer-based adversarial framework whose triple-head discriminator jointly supervises three complementary aspects of the joint distribution: distributional authenticity via a Wasserstein critic, cross-variable dependency via leakage-free regression of the target variable, and step-wise temporal smoothness via adjacent-difference prediction. The generator combines global self-attention with local temporal convolution, per-step noise injection, and an anti-smoothing loss that matches first-difference statistics. Experiments on the self-collected Changsha Carbon dataset, two public carbon datasets (China, US), and the ETTh1 benchmark show that TriHead-GAN achieves favorable performance over mainstream baselines on the vast majority of settings, and that the resulting synthetic windows improve downstream forecasting accuracy in low-resource carbon monitoring scenarios.

</details>


### [253] [Enabling KV Caching of Shared Prefix for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07571)
*Younghun Go,Jaehoon Han,Changyong Shin,Chuk Yoo,Gyeongsik Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Existing KV caching for shared prefixes fails in diffusion language models (DLMs) due to bidirectional attention causing accuracy collapse. This paper proposes BiCache, the first bidirectional prefix caching technique that dynamically identifies safe shallow layers for reusing shared prefix KVs, improving throughput by 36.3%-98.3% without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caching for shared prefixes is essential for high-throughput LLM serving, but existing LLM caching techniques fail in DLMs because bidirectional attention means updating any token alters all KVs, causing model accuracy to collapse to near zero.

Method: BiCache is based on the observation that shared prefix KVs remain stable in shallow layers, where the depth of safe layers depends on the fraction of shared prefix tokens. It dynamically identifies the safe layer depth for reusing shared prefix KVs and eliminates redundant computation.

Result: BiCache improves serving throughput by 36.3%-98.3% compared to existing techniques with only 0-1.8% accuracy difference (no collapse).

Conclusion: BiCache is the first effective KV caching technique for shared prefixes in DLMs, enabling high-throughput DLM serving without accuracy degradation.

Abstract: Key-value (KV) caching for shared prefixes is essential for high-throughput large language model (LLM) serving, but it faces critical challenges in emerging diffusion language models (DLMs). In DLMs, bidirectional attention means that updating any token dynamically alters the entire context and its corresponding KVs. Thus, existing caching techniques developed for LLMs, which assume that KVs remain invariant once computed, corrupt the shared prefix KVs. Our experiments show that applying these techniques to DLMs causes model accuracy to collapse to near zero.
  To unlock high-throughput DLM serving, we propose bidirectional prefix caching, bicache, the first KV caching technique for shared prefixes in DLMs. bicache is designed based on key observations from our comprehensive analysis: shared prefix KVs remain stable and reusable in shallow layers, while the depth of shallow layers depends on the fraction of shared prefix tokens in each request. Thus, bicache dynamically identifies a safe layer depth for reusing shared prefix KVs and eliminates redundant computation. Evaluations demonstrate that bicache significantly improves serving throughput by 36.3%-98.3% compared to existing techniques without accuracy collapse (only 0-1.8% difference).

</details>


### [254] [When Should an AI Scientist Stop? Verifiable Experiment Steering and Refusal for Autonomous Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07576)
*Neel Tushar Shah,Manglam Kartik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CARTOGRAPH is a verification layer for AI scientists that uses unresolved-subspace steering, ambiguity closure, and residual-based library detection to improve experimental design and detect when models are inadequate, outperforming baselines and successfully flagging questionable claims in real scientific systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem that AI scientists lack rigorous verification mechanisms—specifically, they cannot detect when their model library is inadequate for the data, nor properly resolve ambiguity in experimental design.

Method: CARTOGRAPH operates via three principles: (1) Select — unresolved-subspace experiment steering using isotropic Fisher-information trace; (2) Resolve — explicit ambiguity closure via CARTOGRAPH-A (exact A-optimal rule); (3) Refuse — residual-based library inadequacy detection. It works under a local linear-Gaussian bridge.

Result: CARTOGRAPH-A beats raw projection 129W/0T/15L at d=8 (p<10⁻²¹) across five testbeds; identifies then revokes 3 out-of-library pharmacokinetic mechanisms while keeping 1 in-library control; in audits of A-Lab, flags all 4 inconclusive claims while passing 32/36 confirmed claims.

Conclusion: CARTOGRAPH provides a principled verification layer that significantly improves AI scientist reliability by detecting model inadequacy and steering experiments more effectively, with demonstrated success in both synthetic and real-world scientific discovery systems.

Abstract: We present CARTOGRAPH, a verification layer for AI scientists that couples unresolved-subspace experiment steering (select), explicit ambiguity closure (resolve), and residual-based library inadequacy detection (refuse). Under a local linear-Gaussian bridge, raw unresolved projection is the isotropic unresolved Fisher-information trace, while CARTOGRAPH-A is the exact unresolved A-optimal rule; closed-form EIG and Box-Hill arise as local comparators rather than global equivalents. Across five testbeds, CARTOGRAPH-A beats raw projection 129W/0T/15L at d = 8 (p < 10^-21) in a replicated structured cascade. More distinctively, the framework tentatively identifies three out-of-library pharmacokinetic mechanisms and then revokes those identifications as residuals expose structural misfit, while one perturbed in-library control stays identified throughout. In low-dimensional pharmacokinetic and filtered EPA settings, near-ties against disagreement are predicted by theory and observed. Finally, in a retrospective audit of 40 positive claims from the published A-Lab autonomous materials system, the refuse guard flags all 4 claims later marked inconclusive under manual reanalysis while passing 32/36 confirmed claims. Code is available at https://github.com/ai4science-boed/cartograph.git

</details>


### [255] [MST-Direct at Scale: Multivariate and Conditional Geostatistical Simulation via Sinkhorn Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07578)
*Tcharlies Bachmann Schmitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends MST-Direct (a matching-via-Sinkhorn-transport method) from bivariate/unconditional/small-grid to multivariate/conditional/large-grid settings, solving scalability, multi-variable extension, and hard-data conditioning issues. It preserves the exact joint distribution and outperforms PPMT in validation tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original MST-Direct had three key limitations: (1) poor scalability beyond a few thousand nodes, (2) restriction to bivariate cases, and (3) inability to condition on hard data. This work aims to address these shortcomings.

Method: (i) Sparse, candidate-restricted Sinkhorn matcher with O(nC) memory complexity for scalability; (ii) Extension to multiple variables via matching target value tuples onto an independent FFT-MA Gaussian backbone that reproduces a prescribed variogram; (iii) Hard-data conditioning by fixing observed data tuples at spatial locations while conditioning the backbone via kriging.

Result: Validated on six-variate, heteroscedastic, nonlinear reference distribution under unconditional (200x200) and conditional (100x100, 200 hard-data samples) scenarios. MST-Direct achieves zero histogram error, exactly honors hard data, and accurately reproduces spatial correlation, while PPMT remains an approximation.

Conclusion: The extended MST-Direct successfully overcomes the original limitations and delivers exact multivariate joint distribution preservation with perfect conditioning, outperforming the benchmark PPMT method.

Abstract: This paper extends MST-Direct, a Matching-via-Sinkhorn-Transport approach for multivariate geostatistical simulation, from the original bivariate, unconditional, small-grid formulation to multivariate, conditional, and large-grid settings. We address the three main limitations identified in the original work: (i) scalability beyond a few thousand nodes through a sparse, candidate-restricted Sinkhorn matcher with O(nC) memory complexity; (ii) extension to multiple variables by matching target value tuples onto an independent FFT-MA Gaussian backbone that reproduces a prescribed variogram; and (iii) hard-data conditioning by fixing observed data tuples at their spatial locations while conditioning the backbone through kriging. Because the transport plan remains a permutation of the target tuples, the multivariate joint distribution is preserved exactly.
  The method is validated using the same six-variate, heteroscedastic, strongly nonlinear reference distribution employed in Direct Multivariate Simulation (DMS), under both unconditional (200x200) and conditional (100x100, 200 hard-data samples) scenarios, and is benchmarked against the Projection Pursuit Multivariate Transform (PPMT). Results show that MST-Direct reproduces the joint distribution with zero histogram error, exactly honours hard data, and accurately reproduces the prescribed spatial correlation structure, whereas PPMT remains an approximation.
  Index Terms-Optimal transport, Sinkhorn algorithm, geostatistical simulation, multivariate simulation.

</details>


### [256] [Training-Inference Kernel Contracts: Bounding Divergence in Post-Training and Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07581)
*Bruce Changlong Xu,Lan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes "kernel contracts" as a framework to specify and manage acceptable divergence between training and inference kernels in post-training pipelines, with mathematical bounds connecting numerical drift to policy-gradient bias in RL settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern post-training pipelines use different kernels for training (autograd-optimized) and inference (low-precision, fused, dynamically batched), which can produce different distributions at identical weights in finite precision. This gap is often concentrated on underrepresented slices that benchmarks miss.

Method: A contract-first framework (C = N, S, R, O, Pi) combining numerical, statistical, runtime, and observability clauses with escalation policies. A chain of bounds from logit drift → TV distance → bounded reward drift, specialized to RL post-training with per-token importance-ratio drift analysis under explicit support and norm assumptions.

Result: Derived bounds connecting kernel divergence to policy-gradient bias; described a four-stage promotion pipeline, online routing loop, and minimal YAML DSL for contract artifacts. No production-scale empirical validation is reported.

Conclusion: This is a framework and vocabulary paper establishing theoretical foundations for managing training-inference kernel divergence via contracts, laying groundwork for future empirical validation.

Abstract: A modern post-training pipeline often writes one symbol for its policy, pi_theta, while evaluating it through two different programs: a training kernel optimized for autograd and an inference kernel optimized for low-precision, fused, dynamically batched serving. In finite precision, these kernels can induce different distributions at identical weights, with the gap concentrated on slices that aggregate benchmarks under-represent. This paper proposes kernel contracts: a contract-first framework for specifying acceptable divergence between K_train and K_inf. A contract C = (N, S, R, O, Pi) combines numerical, statistical, runtime, and observability clauses with an escalation policy from violations to routing actions. We derive a chain of bounds from logit drift to total-variation distance to bounded reward drift, and specialize it to RL post-training, where per-token importance-ratio drift yields a bound on policy-gradient bias under explicit support and norm assumptions. We also describe a four-stage promotion pipeline, online routing loop, and minimal YAML DSL for contract artifacts. This is a framework and vocabulary paper; we do not report production-scale empirical validation.

</details>


### [257] [Customer Churn Prediction on Structured Data Using FT-Transformer and Stacking Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07582)
*Joyjit Roy,Samaresh Kumar Singh,Laxmi Shaw*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid model combining FT-Transformer and XGBoost via calibration-aware stacking for customer churn prediction, achieving 62.10% F1 and 0.861 AUC-ROC on a bank churn dataset, outperforming MLP baselines with robust statistical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Customer churn prediction is critical for cost-effective customer retention in data-driven industries, but remains challenging due to class imbalance, nonlinear feature interactions, and heterogeneous feature types in structured datasets. Prior research lacks statistical validation, probability calibration, and reproducibility.

Method: The authors propose a hybrid architecture integrating FT-Transformer (for higher-order feature interactions via self-attention) with XGBoost (for gradient-boosted decision boundaries). Class imbalance is handled via class-weighted loss functions. Models are ensembled through out-of-fold stacking with a logistic regression meta-learner for calibration-aware combination.

Result: On a public bank churn dataset, the hybrid model achieves 62.10% F1, 0.861 AUC-ROC, and 0.647 PR-AUC, outperforming MLP baseline by 3.37 F1 points and 0.027 AUC under 5x5 cross-validation with 95% confidence intervals. Ablation studies confirm both transformer and stacking components contribute materially.

Conclusion: The proposed methodology offers a reproducible, extensible reference architecture for churn prediction on structured tabular data, addressing gaps in statistical rigor and calibration found in prior work.

Abstract: Customer churn prediction is essential across data-driven industries such as insurance, digital banking, eCommerce, and subscription platforms, where retaining existing customers is typically more cost-effective than acquiring new ones. Predicting churn on structured datasets remains challenging due to class imbalance, nonlinear feature interactions, and heterogeneous feature types. Tree-based ensemble methods consistently demonstrate strong performance in these contexts, often outperforming conventional neural networks. This study introduces a validated hybrid architecture that integrates feature-tokenized transformers (FT-Transformer) with gradient-boosted trees through calibration-aware stacking. The proposed framework addresses persistent gaps in statistical validation, probability calibration, and reproducibility found in prior research. The FT-Transformer captures higher-order feature interactions using self-attention, while XGBoost captures gradient-boosted decision boundaries with complementary inductive biases. Class imbalance is handled using class-weighted loss functions, thereby avoiding synthetic oversampling and preserving minority-class distributions. The models are ensembled using out-of-fold (OOF) stacking with a logistic regression meta-learner, which recalibrates overconfident base model outputs and learns optimal combination weights. On a public bank churn dataset, the hybrid model achieves 62.10% F1, 0.861 AUC-ROC, and 0.647 PR-AUC, outperforming the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) baseline by 3.37 F1 points and 0.027 AUC under 5x5 cross-validation with 95% confidence intervals reported. Ablation studies demonstrate that both the transformer component and stacking strategy contribute materially to performance. The proposed methodology offers a reproducible and extensible reference architecture for contemporary churn prediction on structured tabular data.

</details>


### [258] [Outage Detection in Self-Healing Smart Grids Using Reinforcement Learning with Spectral Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07583)
*Lihui Liu,Mucun Sun,Caisheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a spectral graph reinforcement learning framework for outage management in smart grids that uses frequency-domain information to achieve near-optimal, real-time power restoration across various outage scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ML methods for outage mitigation are too slow and computationally expensive for smart grids, and conventional GNNs operating in spatial domain fail to capture important frequency-domain relationships (global structural patterns and system-wide interactions) in power networks.

Method: A spectral graph reinforcement learning framework that learns optimal power restoration policy using a spectral graph neural network, operating in the frequency domain rather than the spatial domain.

Result: Evaluated on IEEE 13-bus, 34-bus, and 123-bus test systems; achieves near-optimal performance in real time and generalizes well across a wide range of outage scenarios.

Conclusion: Spectral graph RL is effective for real-time outage management in distribution networks, offering better performance than spatial-domain GNN approaches by leveraging frequency-domain information.

Abstract: Self-healing smart grids can quickly adjust their network configuration during outages to minimize power disruptions. During an outage, several actions can be taken, such as network reconfiguration through switching operations and emergency load shedding. However, traditional machine learning methods for outage mitigation are not well suited for smart grids due to their slow response time and high computational cost. To address these challenges, recent studies have explored reinforcement learning to automatically perform network reconfiguration. In these approaches, the control policy is typically modeled using a graph neural network (GNN). However, conventional GNNs operate in the spatial domain and may fail to capture important relationships in the frequency domain. Frequency-domain information is particularly useful for modeling global structural patterns and system-wide interactions in power networks. In this paper, we propose a spectral graph reinforcement learning framework for outage management in distribution networks to enhance system resilience. Our model learns the optimal power restoration policy using a spectral graph neural network. We evaluate the proposed method on three modified IEEE test systems: the 13-bus, 34-bus, and 123-bus networks. Experimental results show that our approach achieves near-optimal performance in real time and generalizes well across a wide range of outage scenarios.

</details>


### [259] [From Human Guidance to Autonomy: Agent Skill System for End-to-End LLM Deployment on Spatial NPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07586)
*Jiajie Li,Erwei Wang,Zhiru Zhang,Samuel Bayliss*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage methodology for deploying LLMs on AMD XDNA 2 NPUs, progressing from human-guided development to fully autonomous agent deployment, achieving 2.2x prefill and 4.0x decode speedups, and autonomously deploying eight previously unsupported LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing efforts for deploying LLMs on spatial NPUs are labor-intensive and focus on single-kernel optimization rather than end-to-end deployment on resource-constrained hardware.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) human-guided agent development of a reference Llama-3.2-1B deployment with structured documentation, (2) distillation of documentation into an eight-phase agent skill system for autonomous deployment, with numerical correctness gates enforced at each phase.

Result: Autonomous deployment of eight LLMs (Llama-3.2-3B, SmolLM2-1.7B, Qwen2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,3B}, Qwen3-{0.6B,1.7B,4B}) on AMD XDNA 2 NPU in 0.5-4 hours with no prior support via open-source software, three matching/exceeding the reference deployment performance.

Conclusion: The agent skill system achieves functional generalization to unseen LLMs and competitive performance without model-specific human engineering, demonstrating a viable path for automating end-to-end LLM deployment on spatial NPUs.

Abstract: Spatial neural processing units (NPUs) provide an energy-efficient platform for edge LLM inference, but efficiently deploying an LLM end-to-end on such hardware remains labor-intensive. Although AI coding agents have begun to lower this cost, existing studies have largely focused on single-kernel optimization rather than end-to-end LLM deployment on resource-constrained spatial NPUs.
  We present a two-stage methodology, instantiated on the AMD XDNA 2 NPU, that progresses from human-guided development to agent autonomy. In the first stage, we develop a reference deployment of Llama-3.2-1B through human-guided agent assistance. The resulting implementation achieves a speedup of 2.2x on prefill and 4.0x on decode over the hand-optimized baseline, with the optimization trajectory and its lessons recorded as structured documentation throughout. In the second stage, we distill the documentation into an agent skill system consisting of eight phases, orchestrating the optimization and debugging skill sets, with numerical correctness strictly enforced at each phase.
  Using our agent skill system, we autonomously deploy eight additional decoder-only LLMs (Llama-3.2-3B, SmolLM2-1.7B, Qwen2.5-{0.5B, 1.5B, 3B}, Qwen3-{0.6B, 1.7B, 4B}) end-to-end on the AMD XDNA 2 NPU using the open-source compiler stack. To our knowledge, these models have not previously been deployed on AMD NPUs via any open-source software stack. Each deployment completes in 0.5-4 hours of agent wall time with almost no human guidance, and passes the numerical-correctness gates, demonstrating functional generalization to previously unencountered LLMs. Three of the eight match or exceed the sustained performance of our Llama-3.2-1B reference deployment, suggesting that the resulting implementations can be competitive without additional model-specific human engineering.

</details>


### [260] [The Routing Plateau: Understanding and Breaking the Accuracy Limits of LLM Routers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07587)
*Yifan Lu,Qiyue Zhang,Shenrun Zhang,Zhibo Yu,Zhuang Wang,Hanjie Chen,Jiarong Xing*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a "routing plateau" phenomenon where 21 LLM routing methods perform similarly and far below oracle levels, caused by a predictability bottleneck where routers learn global trends rather than query-specific signals, and proposes that larger datasets, stronger encoders, and end-to-end fine-tuning can help move beyond this plateau.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically investigate why diverse LLM routing methods (kNN, classifiers, ranking, confidence-based) all converge to similar performance levels well below the theoretical upper bound (oracle).

Method: Conducted an extensive study evaluating 21 different routing methods across 5 benchmarks, analyzing their accuracy patterns and diagnosing the underlying causes of performance convergence through predictability bottleneck analysis.

Result: All 21 methods converge to a narrow performance range (the "routing plateau"), far below the oracle. The root cause is that routers learn global averaged model-performance trends instead of fine-grained query-specific routing signals, causing them to all solve easy queries but collectively fail on hard queries requiring instance-specific decisions.

Conclusion: Moving beyond the plateau requires larger training datasets, stronger encoders, and end-to-end fine-tuning rather than relying on current routing paradigms.

Abstract: LLM routing has become a popular approach to improve the cost-quality trade-off of LLM services by dynamically selecting a model for each query. Recent work has explored a broad range of routing methods, including clustering-based routers, learned classifiers, pairwise ranking, and confidence-based approaches. Our extensive study of 21 routing methods across five benchmarks reveals a consistent phenomenon that we call the routing plateau: many methods, including kNN, achieve very similar accuracy and converge to a narrow performance range that remains far below the oracle router. Our investigation shows that the plateau is largely caused by a predictability bottleneck: current routers mainly learn global averaged model-performance trends rather than fine-grained query-specific routing signals. As a result, they solve overlapping easy queries but collectively fail on hard queries that require instance-specific routing decisions. We further study how to move beyond the plateau and find that larger training datasets, stronger encoders, and end-to-end fine-tuning can further improve routing accuracy. These findings characterize the common limits of current routing methods and provide insights and actionable directions for the community to build more effective routing systems.

</details>


### [261] [Optimality of Sequential Filtering Under Independent Cost and Selectivity Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07589)
*Hrishikesh Paranjape,Abhishek Mandal,Xian Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes sequential filtering pipelines, proving that ordering filters by increasing cost-to-rejection-probability ratio minimizes expected total cost under independence assumptions, and validates this optimal ordering dominates common heuristics through Monte Carlo simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sequential filtering pipelines are widely used in ranking systems, cascaded ML inference, and fraud detection, yet filter ordering is typically determined by heuristics without formal guarantees.

Method: The paper formalizes sequential filtering under an expected-cost objective, proves optimal ordering (increasing cost/rejection ratio) under an independence model, and validates via extensive Monte Carlo simulations.

Result: The optimal ordering strictly dominates common heuristics across all Monte Carlo runs, both in expectation and across the full distribution of outcomes.

Conclusion: Ordering filters by increasing cost-to-rejection-probability ratio is provably optimal under independence assumptions, offering a principled alternative to heuristic-based filter ordering.

Abstract: Sequential filtering pipelines are a common design pattern in large-scale systems, where a large population of items is progressively reduced by a sequence of stages that each incur cost. Despite their prevalence in ranking systems, cascaded machine learning inference, and fraud detection, filter ordering is often determined by heuristics without formal guarantees. We formalize sequential filtering under an expected-cost objective and prove that, under an independence model, ordering filters by increasing ratio of cost to rejection probability minimizes expected total cost. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations show that the optimal ordering strictly dominates common heuristics across all runs, both in expectation and across the full distribution of outcomes.

</details>


### [262] [ResearchClawBench: A Benchmark for End-to-End Autonomous Scientific Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07591)
*Wanghan Xu,Shuo Li,Tianlin Ye,Qinglong Cao,Yixin Chen,Hengjian Gao,Yiheng Wang,Qi Li,Kun Li,Sheng Xu,Shengdu Chai,Fangchen Yu,Xiangyu Zhao,Zhangrui Zhao,Weijie Ma,Zijie Guo,Haoyu Zhou,Haoxiang Yin,Lixue Cheng,Chaofan Hu,Haoxuan Li,Lu Mi,Xuxuan Xie,Yifan Zhou,Ruizhe Chen,Zhiwang Zhou,Xingjian Guo,Yuhao Zhou,Xuming He,Shengyuan Xu,Xinyu Gu,Jiamin Wu,Mianxin Liu,Chunfeng Song,Fenghua Ling,Dongzhan Zhou,Shixiang Tang,Yuqiang Li,Mao Su,Peng Ye,Siqi Sun,Bin Wang,Xue Yang,Zhenfei Yin,Tianfan Fu,Guangtao Zhai,Wanli Ouyang,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai,Wenlong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ResearchClawBench is a benchmark with 40 tasks across 10 scientific domains for evaluating autonomous AI research agents, finding that current systems still fall far short of reliably re-discovering published scientific findings (best score ~21.5 out of ~100).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI coding agents are increasingly used in science, but their end-to-end autonomous research capability remains difficult to verify due to lack of standardized evaluation benchmarks.

Method: Created 40 tasks from 10 scientific domains, each grounded in a real published paper with related literature and raw data. Used expert-curated multimodal rubrics with weighted criteria for evaluation. Tested seven autonomous agents and seventeen LLMs under a unified protocol (ResearchHarness).

Result: Best autonomous agent (Claude Code) scored 21.5; best ResearchHarness LLM (Claude-Opus-4.7) scored 20.7; LLM frontier mean was only 26.5 — far from reliable re-discovery. Failures concentrated in experimental protocol mismatch, evidence mismatch, and missing scientific core.

Conclusion: Current AI systems are far from capable of reliable autonomous scientific research; ResearchClawBench provides a reproducible frontier for measuring progress in this direction.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly used for scientific work, but their end-to-end autonomous research capability remains difficult to verify. We present ResearchClawBench, a benchmark for evaluating autonomous scientific research across 40 tasks from 10 scientific domains. Each task is grounded in a real published paper, provides related literature and raw data, and hides the target paper during evaluation. Expert-curated multimodal rubrics decompose the target scientific artifacts into weighted criteria, enabling evaluation of target-paper-level re-discovery while leaving room for new discovery. We evaluate seven autonomous research (auto-research) agents under a unified protocol and seventeen native LLMs through the lightweight ResearchHarness. Current systems remain far from reliable re-discovery: the strongest autonomous agent, Claude Code, averages 21.5, and the strongest ResearchHarness LLM, Claude-Opus-4.7, averages 20.7, with an LLM frontier mean of only 26.5. Error analysis shows that failures concentrate in experimental protocol mismatch, evidence mismatch, and missing scientific core. ResearchClawBench provides a reproducible evaluation frontier for measuring progress toward autonomous scientific research.

</details>


### [263] [UNIQ: Conformal Calibration for Adaptive Conservatism in Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07592)
*Aditya Upadhyay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UNIQ introduces state-adaptive conservatism to offline RL via conformal prediction, improving IQL with minimal memory overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing offline RL methods apply a fixed conservatism penalty uniformly across all states, ignoring local data coverage variations.

Method: Built on IQL, UNIQ trains a multi-expectile value ensemble, uses split conformal prediction for uncertainty estimation, and maps this to state-dependent expectiles.

Result: Outperforms IQL on D4RL MuJoCo (especially Walker2d and replay tasks), with ~250 MB peak VRAM — 10x less than EDAC.

Conclusion: UNIQ improves the performance-efficiency trade-off in offline RL without pursuing state-of-the-art performance.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning requires careful conservatism to mitigate distribution shift, yet most existing methods apply a fixed penalty uniformly across all states regardless of local data coverage. We present UNIQ (Uncertainty-Informed Quantile), an offline RL method that introduces state-adaptive conservatism through conformally calibrated uncertainty estimation. Built on the Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) backbone, UNIQ trains a multi-expectile value ensemble, computes distribution-free uncertainty estimates using split conformal prediction, and maps the resulting signal to a state-dependent expectile that relaxes conservatism in well-covered regions while strengthening it in uncertain regions near the data frontier. On D4RL MuJoCo benchmarks, UNIQ consistently improves over IQL, with the largest gains observed on Walker2d and replay-heavy tasks. At the same time, UNIQ operates at near-IQL memory cost (approximately 250 MB peak VRAM), providing roughly a 10x reduction compared to EDAC. Rather than pursuing overall state-of-the-art performance, we position UNIQ as a practical mechanism contribution that improves the performance-efficiency trade-off in offline reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [264] [Shortcuts in the Tail: Debiasing via Post-Hoc Spectral Compression of Fine-Tuning Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07596)
*Edward Sun,Dmitrii Troitskii*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Truncating the tail of SVD of ΔW (fine-tuned minus base weights) reduces spurious correlations in fine-tuned models without retraining, group labels, or counterfactual data, achieving up to 5× reduction in group gaps with <2pp accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning introduces spurious correlations that cause systematic failures on underrepresented groups. Existing mitigation methods require retraining, group labels, or curated counterfactual data—all expensive or impractical.

Method: Apply top-k truncated SVD to ΔW = W_ft - W_base, removing the tail singular components. Tested on 3 instruction-tuned models (0.5B–7B) across 4 classification benchmarks, with controlled comparisons (bottom-/random-k truncation, matched-rank LoRA).

Result: Top-k truncation reduces spurious-group gap on every benchmark cell with <2pp accuracy loss, up to 5× on CivilComments. Controls rule out generic low-rank approximation or rank-constrained training as explanations.

Conclusion: The shortcut/spurious response resides in the tail of the singular ordering of ΔW, suggesting the singular basis of ΔW is a useful coordinate system for studying what fine-tuning has learned.

Abstract: Fine-tuning often introduces spurious correlations alongside task knowledge, causing systematic failures on underrepresented groups. Existing mitigations require retraining, group labels, or curated counterfactual data. We show a simple post-hoc intervention reduces shortcut reliance without any of these: truncating the tail of the SVD of $ΔW = W_\mathrm{ft} - W_\mathrm{base}$ reduces the spurious-group gap while preserving task accuracy. Across three instruction-tuned models ($0.5$B--$7$B) and four classification benchmarks, top-$k$ truncation reduces the gap on every cell at $<2$ pp accuracy loss, by up to $5\times$ on CivilComments. We propose this works because the shortcut response sits in the tail of the singular ordering of $ΔW$, a claim about how truncation behaves rather than about the raw singular values, which are broadly distributed and look the same across all four datasets. A controlled boundary case in which fine-tuning has only a shortcut to learn shows the predicted FT-to-base collapse, and bottom-/random-$k$ and matched-rank LoRA controls rule out generic low-rank approximation and rank-constrained training as the explanation. We read this as preliminary evidence that the singular basis of $ΔW$ is a useful coordinate system for studying what fine-tuning has learned.

</details>


### [265] [A spectral audit framework reveals task-dependent aperiodic reliance across EEG and ECG deep learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08583)
*Jasmeet Singh Bindra,Siddharth Panwar,Shubhajit Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a spectral audit framework to assess how much deep learning models rely on aperiodic (1/f-like) signals in physiological time series (EEG, ECG). Results show significant aperiodic reliance across architectures and tasks, particularly for sleep-wake classification and clinical abnormality detection, persisting even after demographic controls. The authors recommend aperiodic controls become standard for interpretable deep learning on physiological signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning on physiological time series (e.g., EEG, ECG) typically focuses on domain-specific rhythmic features (oscillations, ECG morphology), but these signals sit atop a broadband aperiodic 1/f-like envelope that covaries with arousal, age, and pathology. The paper aims to audit how much neural models actually rely on this aperiodic component vs. periodic features, which is critical for proper interpretability.

Method: The authors propose a spectral audit framework combining: (1) aperiodic/periodic decomposition of power spectra, (2) phase-preserving Fourier interventions to selectively manipulate the aperiodic component, (3) sham controls, and (4) simulation validation. They test six neural architectures across multiple tasks (sleep-wake classification, clinical abnormality detection, motor imagery) and seven EEG foundation models, plus extend the audit to PTB-XL ECG data with demographic matching controls.

Result: Aperiodic reliance was task-dependent and architecture-general. Flattening drops exceeded 0.42 balanced-accuracy points for sleep-wake classification, 0.07-0.13 for clinical abnormality detection, and minimal for motor imagery. Six of seven EEG foundation models showed FDR-significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG; age/sex and recording-era controls reduced but did not eliminate the effect. ECG audit revealed neural drops of 0.32-0.36 persisting after demographic matching, confirming the confound extends beyond EEG.

Conclusion: Aperiodic controls should become standard for interpretable physiological time-series deep learning. The aperiodic 1/f-like signal is a widespread confound class affecting model interpretation across both EEG and ECG modalities, and existing demographic controls are insufficient to fully mitigate its influence.

Abstract: Deep learning on physiological time series is interpreted through domain-specific features -- oscillatory rhythms in EEG, morphological complexes in ECG -- yet these signals sit atop a broadband aperiodic 1/f-like envelope that covaries with arousal, age, and pathology. We introduce a spectral audit framework combining aperiodic/periodic decomposition, phase-preserving Fourier interventions, sham controls, and simulation validation. Aperiodic reliance was task-dependent and architecture-general: across six neural architectures, flattening drops exceeded 0.42 balanced-accuracy points for sleep-wake classification, reached 0.07-0.13 for clinical abnormality detection, and remained minimal for motor imagery. Six of seven EEG foundation models showed FDR-significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG; age/sex and recording-era controls reduced but did not eliminate the effect. Applying the audit to PTB-XL ECG revealed neural drops of 0.32--0.36 persisting after demographic matching, confirming this confound class extends beyond EEG. Aperiodic controls should become standard for interpretable physiological time-series deep learning.

</details>


### [266] [Repetition Mismatch: Why Data Mixture Experiments Don't Scale and How to Fix Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07597)
*Kevin Zhou,Lisa Alazraki,Kris Cao,Marek Rei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that pre-training data mixture extrapolation fails due to repetition mismatch—where high-quality small datasets get repeated at different rates across training budgets—and proposes a repetition-controlled subsampling method that dramatically improves mixture optimization with far fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-training data mixtures tuned via small-scale experiments often fail to generalize to larger training budgets, especially when high-quality data is scarce and must be repeated. The source of this failure had not been isolated prior to this work.

Method: The authors identify repetition mismatch as the primary cause and propose a repetition-controlled subsampling procedure that matches the target repetition rate. They conduct controlled experiments with two and three data sources at various scales (e.g., 757M parameter model), using 1/16 of target tokens.

Result: With repetition control, a single experiment using 1/16 target tokens recovers mixture within 0.05 of optimum (vs. 0.75 error without control). Without it, 3-4 horizons consuming 44-94% target budget are needed. For three sources, two repetition-controlled horizons suffice.

Conclusion: Repetition dynamics—not scale alone—determine whether small-scale mixtures generalize. Data repetition should be treated as a first-class variable in mixture optimization.

Abstract: Pre-training data mixtures are commonly tuned by running small-scale experiments and extrapolating to the target training budget. When high-quality data is scarce and must be repeated, this extrapolation frequently fails, but the source of the failure has not been isolated. We show that a primary culprit is a repetition mismatch: because high-quality datasets are small, their repetition rate changes as the training budget grows, shifting the optimal mixture in ways that small-scale proxy experiments do not anticipate. A subsampling procedure that matches the target repetition rate controls for this effect. In a two-source setting combining limited high-quality data with web crawl, a single repetition-controlled experiment using only 1/16 of the target tokens recovers a mixture within 0.05 of the optimum for a 757M parameter model, compared to an error of 0.75 without repetition control. Achieving comparable accuracy without repetition control requires three to four horizons, consuming 44 to 94% of the target token budget. With three data sources, the larger mixture space requires more than a single experiment to constrain, but the approach remains effective: at the 757M scale, just two repetition-controlled horizons recover the optimal mixture, outperforming baselines that instead require the full two-source experiments to construct. Our results reveal that repetition dynamics, not scale alone, shape whether small-scale mixture experiments generalize. More broadly, they suggest that data repetition deserves treatment as a first-class variable in mixture optimization, rather than an inconvenient side effect of limited data.

</details>


### [267] [How Much Capacity Does EEG Denoising Need? Ultra-Compact Networks reveal Benchmark Saturation and Metric-Utility Gap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08594)
*Jasmeet Singh Bindra,Siddharth Panwar,Shubhajit Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEG denoising models saturate reconstruction performance at just 3-6.5K parameters (far below current large models), and reconstruction metrics fail to predict downstream BCI task utility—in fact, denoising can significantly degrade classification accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No prior study has isolated model capacity as a controlled variable in EEG denoising, nor tested whether reconstruction quality translates to downstream neural-signal utility for BCI tasks.

Method: Fixed architecture, loss, data split, and training recipe; swept only channel width (1.05K to 40.26K parameters) in a minimal depthwise-separable convolutional U-Net. Evaluated on EEGDenoiseNet, cross-dataset BCI transfer, controlled retraining, and downstream motor-imagery classification with five decoders across all BCI Competition IV-2a subjects.

Result: Reconstruction saturated by 3-6.5K parameters (tiny). A 200x larger baseline (8.46M params) matched the compact model on EOG.
