Conclusion: DOG-DPO offers an efficient, principled geometric approach to safety data selection that outperforms scalar-based methods, demonstrating that directional preference geometry can effectively compress alignment datasets without sacrificing safety performance.

Abstract: Safety alignment for large language models relies on preference data, but current pipelines often train on large, redundant datasets. Existing data selection methods typically score each preference pair independently, collapsing directional preference information into scalar quality or diversity scores. This sample-centric view is especially limiting in multi-dataset settings, where shared safety directions coexist with dataset-specific residual risks. We propose DOG-DPO, a training-free data selection framework that treats preference pairs as structured geometric signals. DOG-DPO first represents each preference pair as a direction in model representation space. It then decomposes multi-dataset preference geometry into a global anchor subspace and dataset-specific residual subspaces. Finally, it selects subsets by maximizing diversity-based coverage, encouraging broad, non-redundant coverage of alignment directions before DPO training. Across six safety benchmarks and two model backbones, DOG-DPO achieves a strong utility-robustness trade-off using only 11% of the preference pairs. It recovers most of the safety gains of full-data training while remaining entirely teacher-free, training-free, and substantially faster than representative selection baselines.

</details>


### [296] [Semantic Cache Distillation: Efficient State Transfer via Reuse and Selective Patching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07684)
*Qianli Ma,Zhiqing Tang,Hanshuai Cui,Zhi Yao,Weijia Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCD replaces raw KV cache transmission with compact semantic codes in disaggregated LLM serving, achieving up to 2.65× TTFT speedup while maintaining generation quality within 5% F1 of oracle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disaggregated serving creates severe communication bottlenecks from transmitting high-dimensional KV caches, and cache reuse across heterogeneous models causes semantic misalignment that degrades generation quality.

Method: SCD is a loss-constrained framework with two mechanisms: (1) Reuse - reconstructs most layers from low-rank subspaces to minimize transfer cost, and (2) Patch - predicts normalized inputs at sparse transition layers to truncate error propagation.

Result: Up to 2.65× TTFT speedup over oracle consumer prefill; dominates quantization and selective recomputation baselines on quality-latency Pareto frontier; maintains generation quality within 5% F1 of oracle.

Conclusion: SCD effectively resolves the communication bottleneck in disaggregated LLM serving while preserving generation quality, outperforming existing baselines in bandwidth-constrained regimes.

Abstract: Disaggregated serving alleviates memory bottlenecks in Large Language Model (LLM) inference but creates a severe communication bottleneck: transmitting high-dimensional Key-Value (KV) caches often dominates time-to-first-token (TTFT). Moreover, reusing caches across heterogeneous models (e.g., base and fine-tuned variants) causes semantic misalignment that accumulates over layers, degrading generation quality. We propose Semantic Cache Distillation (SCD), a loss-constrained framework that replaces raw KV transmission with compact semantic codes. SCD addresses these challenges via two mechanisms: (1) Reuse, which reconstructs most layers from low-rank subspaces to minimize transfer cost, and (2) Patch, which predicts normalized inputs at sparse transition layers to truncate error propagation. Empirically, SCD delivers up to 2.65 $\times$ TTFT speedup over the oracle consumer prefill and dominates quantization and selective recomputation baselines on the quality--latency Pareto frontier in bandwidth-constrained regimes, while keeping generation quality within 5\% F1 of the oracle.

</details>


### [297] [Test-Time Adaptive Composition for Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) in IoT Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07685)
*Deepak Kanneganti,Sajib Mistry,Sheik Mohammad Mostakim Fattah,Aneesh Krishna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Test-Time Adaptive (TTA) composition framework for MLaaS in IoT that adjusts individual services during inference without re-composition, reducing computational time while maintaining compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic IoT environments degrade long-term effectiveness of MLaaS compositions, and existing adaptive methods (service replacement/re-composition) are difficult and time-consuming for identifying substitutes.

Method: (1) TTA-aware composability model to check adapted service compatibility, (2) Service-level adaptation model to adjust individual services during inference while preserving composition performance.

Result: The proposed framework reduces computational time more effectively than traditional adaptive approaches.

Conclusion: TTA-based adaptation is a more efficient alternative to service replacement or re-composition for maintaining MLaaS in dynamic IoT environments.

Abstract: The dynamic nature of Internet of Things (IoT) environments affects the long-term effectiveness of Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) compositions. Existing adaptive composition methods are mainly based on service replacement or re-composition, where identifying suitable substitutes is difficult and time-consuming. To address this, we propose a novel Test-Time Adaptive (TTA) composition framework for MLaaS in IoT environments. First, we introduce a TTA-aware composability model to determine whether adapted services remain compatible with the existing composition. Next, we design a service-level adaptation model to adjust individual services during inference while preserving composition performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces computational time more effectively than traditional adaptive approaches.

</details>


### [298] [Knowledge-Inclusive Adaptive Physics-Informed Neural Network for Microbial Interaction Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07686)
*Ravisha Rupasinghe,Rajith Vidanaarachchi,Asela Hevapathige,Sachith Seneviratne,Sen-Lin Tang,Saman Halgamuge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a knowledge-inclusive PINN framework that enriches parameter discovery by incorporating auxiliary knowledge sources (text from literature and network structure) beyond just experimental data, applied to microbial community modeling with gLV equations. It achieves up to 53% improvement over state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional PINNs rely solely on experimental measurements for parameter discovery. However, knowledge exists in other forms (text, network structure) that could enrich modeling. The authors aim to incorporate these auxiliary knowledge sources to improve microbial community modeling.

Method: A PINN framework that integrates (1) text knowledge from peer-reviewed metagenomics literature to provide biological context on external influences, (2) network-based structural knowledge by explicitly modeling microbial interactions, and (3) experimental measurements of microbial abundance via data-driven integration. Applied to generalized Lotka-Volterra (gLV) for microbial communities.

Result: Improves over state-of-the-art by up to 53% even without knowledge. Knowledge addition yields up to 23% improvement in Bray-Curtis Dissimilarity-based accuracy and 47% in R². Validated on real and simulated datasets from human- and plant-associated microbial communities.

Conclusion: Incorporating auxiliary knowledge sources (text and network structure) into PINN frameworks significantly improves microbial community modeling and reveals ecological insights validated against documented literature.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) is a way of including knowledge in the form of equations in Machine Learning methods. Beyond equations, knowledge exists in other forms, such as text and network structure. While existing PINN-based approaches discover equation parameters from data, they rely solely on experimental measurements. We propose a new PINN framework that enriches parameter discovery by incorporating auxiliary knowledge sources. We instantiate our framework for microbiology, where generalised Lotka-Volterra (gLV) serves as a biological foundation for modelling microbial communities. We demonstrate that incorporating knowledge improves microbial community modelling. Our framework enriches the gLV parameters using peer-reviewed metagenomics literature, as text provides biological context on external influences that gLV alone cannot capture. We combine this knowledge with experimental measurements of microbial abundance using a data-driven integration approach. We integrate network-based structural knowledge by explicitly modelling microbial interactions. Our knowledge-inclusive framework infers microbial networks, revealing ecological insights. We validate these findings against ecological roles documented in the literature. We evaluate on real and simulated datasets spanning human- and plant-associated microbial communities. Our framework improves over the state-of-the-art by up to 53%, even without knowledge. Knowledge addition yields gains of up to 23% in Bray-Curtis Dissimilarity-based accuracy and 47% in $\mathrm{R}^2$.

</details>


### [299] [HARP: Efficient Data Selection for Finetuning Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07690)
*Ning Wang,Zhengxin Zhang,Maosen Tang,Yitang Gao,Claire Cardie,Sainyam Galhotra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARP is an efficient train-based data selection method that uses hierarchical pruning and empirical Bayes to reduce finetuning cost while maintaining downstream performance, outperforming baselines by up to +8.9 points with 7× fewer training examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing train-free selectors (e.g., embedding similarity) are scalable but misaligned with target objectives, while train-based selectors (e.g., gradient signals, Shapley) reflect downstream utility better but require costly repeated finetuning. The goal is to design a selector that preserves alignment without prohibitive cost.

Method: HARP organizes the training pool into a node-leaf hierarchy, evaluates only representative leaves, infers unmeasured utilities via empirical Bayes posteriors, and selects data using two envelopes: HARP-C (conservative redundancy control) and HARP-E (additive complementary region rewards).

Result: HARP variants achieve the best results, outperforming the strongest baseline by up to +8.9 points while using roughly 7× fewer training examples.

Conclusion: Under local smoothness and bounded estimation error, HARP theoretically controls selection error while reducing train-evaluate cost, offering an efficient bridge between alignment and scalability.

Abstract: Finetuning data selection requires balancing two competing goals: selecting examples that improve the downstream objective, and doing so without repeatedly finetuning models. Train-free selectors are scalable but rely on proxies such as embedding similarity or clustering, which may not match the target objective. Train-based selectors better reflect downstream utility through gradient signals, subset evaluation, or Shapley attribution, but require many costly train--evaluate iterations. We propose Hierarchical Active Region Pruning (HARP), an efficient train-based selector that preserves downstream alignment while reducing selection cost. HARP organizes the training pool into a node--leaf hierarchy, evaluates only representative leaves, and infers unmeasured utilities with empirical Bayes posteriors. It then selects data using two complementary envelopes: HARP-C, which conservatively controls redundancy, and HARP-E, which additively rewards complementary regions. We theoretically show that, under local smoothness and bounded estimation error, HARP controls selection error while reducing train--evaluate cost. We further validate that HARP variants achieve the best result and outperform the strongest baseline by up to $+8.9$ points, while using roughly $7\times$ fewer training examples.

</details>


### [300] [BCG-FM: A Foundation Model for Ambient Cardiac Health Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07692)
*Magnus Ruud Kjaer,Haejun Han,Ashish Neupane,David Q. Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BCG-FM is the first foundation model for ambient mechanical biosignals, pretrained on 2.75M hours of ballistocardiography data from 145,985 individuals, achieving state-of-the-art biological-age estimation (3.26-year MAE) and clinically relevant health discrimination across 15 conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundation models for biosignals rely on modalities requiring deliberate user action (wearing devices or visiting sleep labs). The authors aim to develop a model using truly ambient, contactless biosignals that require zero user effort.

Method: Uses a piezoelectric bed sensor to record BCG signals nightly. Pretrains BCG-FM using participant-level contrastive learning on 2.75M hours of data from 145,985 individuals. Evaluates via frozen embeddings on biological-age estimation and health condition discrimination.

Result: Achieves 3.26-year MAE on biological-age estimation (lowest for ambient/contactless modality). Provides clinically relevant discrimination across 15 health conditions and 3 external cohorts. With only 500 labeled participants, outperforms supervised baseline trained on 3,372 participants. Representation quality scales log-linearly with contrastive batch size.

Conclusion: Ambient, longitudinal mechanical biosignals are a viable modality for health foundation models, enabling effortless large-scale health monitoring.

Abstract: Foundation models for wearable biosignals have matched or exceeded supervised specialists across a range of clinical tasks, yet all rely on modalities that require deliberate user action--wearing a device or visiting a sleep lab. We introduce BCG-FM, the first foundation model for ambient mechanical biosignals. A piezoelectric sensor embedded in the bed surface records ballistocardiography (BCG) each night without user effort; we pretrain BCG-FM with participant-level contrastive learning and using a total of 2.75 million hours of nightly recordings from 145,985 individuals, the largest raw-waveform biosignal pretraining corpus to date. Frozen BCG-FM embeddings achieve 3.26-year MAE on biological-age estimation (the lowest reported for any ambient, contactless modality) and yield clinically relevant discrimination across 15 self-reported health conditions and three independent external cohorts. Pretrained representations from only 500 labeled participants outperform a fully supervised baseline trained on 3,372, and representation quality scales log-linearly with contrastive batch size. These results establish ambient, longitudinal mechanical biosignals as a viable modality for health foundation models.

</details>


### [301] [Vessel Traffic Flow Prediction on Sparse Data via Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks with a Learnable Tweedie Head](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07694)
*Kyeongjun Lee,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a plug-and-play Tweedie output head for ST-GNNs to improve vessel traffic flow prediction under sparse, bursty conditions, outperforming conventional methods on real-world AIS data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maritime traffic flow data is highly sparse with intermittent bursts, causing conventional ST-GNNs to produce conservative near-zero predictions and fail to capture non-zero activity.

Method: A model-agnostic learnable Tweedie head is proposed as a plug-and-play output module for any ST-GNN backbone, optimized via closed-form Tweedie unit deviance with node-level variance power learning.

Result: Experiments on real AIS data from Port of LA and Long Beach show consistent RMSE improvement across multiple ST-GNN backbones, especially on non-zero events.

Conclusion: The Tweedie head enables more reliable forecasts for practical maritime traffic control under sparse and bursty conditions.

Abstract: Accurate vessel traffic flow prediction is crucial for smart port operations and navigational safety. However, maritime traffic flow data are often highly sparse with intermittent bursts, making robust forecasting challenging. Under such conditions, conventional spatio-temporal graph neural networks (ST-GNNs) can degrade toward conservative near-zero predictions and fail to capture non-zero activity. Although zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) models partially address excess zeros, their two-part formulation can still remain conservative around abrupt transitions. To address these issues, we propose a model-agnostic learnable Tweedie head that can be attached as a plug-and-play output module to arbitrary ST-GNN backbones. Instead of likelihood-based Tweedie training, which typically requires surrogate objectives, our approach optimizes the closed-form Tweedie unit deviance and predicts the mean for point forecasting while learning a node-level variance power to capture heterogeneous variability across port areas. Experiments on a maritime traffic graph constructed from real-world AIS data in the Port of Los Angeles and Long Beach show that the proposed head consistently improves RMSE across multiple ST-GNN backbones, especially on non-zero events, leading to more reliable forecasts for practical maritime traffic control.

</details>


### [302] [DSFNet: Learning Dual-Domain Spectral Operators for Multi-Modality Spatio-Temporal Forecasting in Urban Transportation Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07695)
*Yongchao Li,Yang Li,Zhuoxuan Li,Jun Chen,Chu Zhang,Jinde Cao,Leszek Rutkowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSFNet is a dual-domain spectral filtering network for multi-modality spatio-temporal forecasting that explicitly models cross-variable couplings and heterogeneous spatial patterns, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines with 3.21%-10.16% MAE reduction on real-world traffic datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MoSTF approaches fail to explicitly model coupling relationships between different modality variables, and struggle with temporal dynamic heterogeneity under exogenous influences and heterogeneous spatial dependencies with complex cross-variable couplings.

Method: DSFNet uses dual-domain spectral filtering with feature-domain and spatial-domain spectral operators to factorize space-modality interactions, plus an external gating mechanism to adaptively regulate temporal dynamics under external influences.

Result: On five real-world traffic datasets, DSFNet reduces MAE by 3.21%-10.16% compared to the second-best baselines, demonstrating superior accuracy, efficiency, and robustness.

Conclusion: DSFNet significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines in multi-modality spatio-temporal forecasting by explicitly modeling cross-modality couplings and heterogeneous dependencies through dual-domain spectral filtering.

Abstract: Multi-Modality Spatio-Temporal Forecasting (MoSTF) extends traditional spatio-temporal forecasting by incorporating diverse traffic modalities. Despite significant recent strides in spatio-temporal modeling, existing approaches often fail to explicitly model the coupling relationships between different modality variables. Accurate MoSTF is challenging, as it requires modeling (1) temporal dynamic heterogeneity under exogenous influences and (2) heterogeneous spatial dependencies alongside complex cross-variable couplings. To address these challenges, we propose the Dual-Domain Spectral Filtering Network (DSFNet). Our framework employs dual-domain spectral filtering to capture heterogeneous spatial patterns and explicitly model the relationships between variables. Unlike graph-based message passing or dense attention over node-modality pairs, DSFNet factorizes space-modality interactions into feature-domain and spatial-domain spectral operators, enabling scalable modeling of nonlocal dependencies and cross-modality couplings. Furthermore, we introduce an external gating mechanism to adaptively regulate temporal dynamics under external influences. We validate our method through extensive experiments on five representative real-world traffic datasets. Compared with the second-best baselines, DSFNet reduces MAE by 3.21%-10.16% across these datasets. The results demonstrate that DSFNet significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines in accuracy while exhibiting efficiency and robustness.

</details>


### [303] [Adversarial Robustness of Activation Steering in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07696)
*Kien Le,Thai Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation steering is highly brittle under adversarial input perturbations, with robustness dropping up to 64%, confidence collapsing, and optimal layers shifting by up to 17 positions—suggesting structural rather than method-specific vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While activation steering is a popular method for controlling LLM behavior, its robustness under realistic adversarial input perturbations has never been systematically evaluated.

Method: Systematic evaluation across 4 extraction methods, 3 attack strategies, 6 personas, and 5 models (1.5B–30B), measuring directional robustness, confidence, steering strength, and layer selection stability under adversarial text perturbations.

Result: Attacks succeed broadly: robustness drops up to 64%, post-attack confidence near/below 0.25, optimal layer shifts up to 17 positions. Extracting vectors from perturbed inputs partially recovers steerability for PCA/MD on mid-to-large models but fails to locate improved optimal layers.

Conclusion: Activation steering brittleness is structural rather than method-specific; current layer selection strategies are not robust enough for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Activation steering has become a popular training-free method to control LLM behavior by injecting precomputed direction vectors into the model's residual stream at inference time. Yet its robustness to realistic input variation remains unstudied. We present the first systematic evaluation of activation steering robustness under adversarial text perturbations on the inputs, covering four extraction methods, three attack strategies, six personas from Anthropic Model-Written Evaluation Dataset, and five models ranging from 1.5B to 30B parameters. Attacks succeed broadly across all settings: directional robustness drops by up to 64%, post-attack confidence collapses near or below 0.25 across all methods and models, and steering strength degrades on nearly every steerable input. Layer selection is equally fragile, with the optimal layer identified by an automated method on clean inputs shifting by up to 17 positions under perturbation, a failure that compounds the vector-level breakdown. Extracting vectors from adversarially perturbed inputs partially recovers steerability for PCA and MD on mid-to-large models, but they consistently fail to locate the improved optimal layer, limiting the practical benefit of this mitigation. Together, these findings reveal that the brittleness of activation steering is structural rather than method-specific, and that current layer selection strategies are not robust enough for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [304] [Pharmacogenomic Knowledge Graph Augmentation for Graph Neural Network-Based Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07698)
*Juergen Dietrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that augmenting GNN-based DDI prediction with pharmacogenomic knowledge (CYP enzyme annotations from PharmGKB) partially overcomes the Information Ceiling—improving DDI type classification under pair-level splits and CYP2C9-mediated predictions, though binary detection and drug-level generalization remain bounded.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work established that DDI prediction performance is bounded by an Information Ceiling derived from training label structure, which architectural improvements cannot surpass. The motivation is to test whether incorporating complementary pharmacogenomic prior knowledge (CYP enzyme data) can partially close this ceiling.

Method: CYP substrate/inhibitor/inducer annotations for CYP2D6, CYP3A4, CYP2C19, CYP2C9 (12-dim feature vector) are concatenated to molecular embeddings from SMILES-derived graphs. Experiments use pair-level and drug-level splits; evaluated on DDI type classification, binary interaction detection, and Tox21 toxicity benchmark.

Result: KG augmentation improves DDI type classification under pair-level splits (F1-macro 0.532 vs 0.241 baseline). Binary detection and drug-level generalization remain bounded. CYP2C9-mediated prediction improves significantly (probabilities 0.560-0.586 vs 0.033-0.117 baseline). Tox21 results confirm dependency on annotation coverage.

Conclusion: Pharmacogenomic knowledge augmentation partially closes the Information Ceiling for certain tasks (DDI type classification, CYP-specific predictions), but cannot overcome it for binary detection and drug-level generalization. This motivates a multimodal framework for the next study.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) applied to drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction rely exclusively on molecular structure encoded as SMILES-derived graphs. Prior work in this series demonstrated that model performance is bounded by the structural information content of training labels -- an Information Ceiling -- that architectural refinements alone cannot overcome. The present study investigates whether pharmacogenomic prior knowledge from the PharmGKB database partially closes this ceiling by providing metabolic pathway context that is independent of, and complementary to, molecular structure. Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme substrate, inhibitor, and inducer annotations for four clinically relevant isoforms (CYP2D6, CYP3A4, CYP2C19, CYP2C9) are extracted and incorporated as a 12-dimensional feature vector concatenated to the molecular embedding prior to interaction prediction. Experiments are conducted under both pair-level and drug-level data splits to quantify generalization to unseen drugs. Results indicate that knowledge graph (KG) augmentation substantially improves DDI type classification under pair-level split conditions (F1-macro: 0.532 vs. 0.241 baseline), while binary interaction detection and drug-level generalization remain bounded by the Information Ceiling (AUC inflation: 0.224 vs. 0.250 baseline). Mechanistic validation on strictly held-out compounds confirms that augmentation preferentially improves CYP2C9-mediated interaction prediction, with probabilities increasing from 0.033-0.117 (baseline) to 0.560-0.586 (KG-augmented). An extension to single-molecule toxicity prediction on the Tox21 benchmark confirms that the effect is contingent on pharmacogenomic annotation coverage. These findings motivate the multimodal framework proposed for the subsequent study in this series.

</details>


### [305] [EssentialGIN: a new approach for gene essentiality prediction based on graph isomorphism neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07700)
*Sahar Mansouri-Rad,Zahra Narimani,Parvin Razzaghi,Nazanin Hosseinkhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a modified graph isomorphism network that integrates biological data (gene expression, orthology, subcellular localization) with PPI network topology for essential gene prediction, outperforming centrality-based methods, Node2Vec, MLP, and GAT, especially in complex organisms like H. sapiens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Essential gene prediction via wet-lab experiments is costly and time-consuming. Traditional centrality-based computational methods suffer from high false positive rates, motivating the need for more accurate computational approaches that integrate biological information and complex models like deep learning.

Method: The authors modify graph isomorphism networks to embed proteins as nodes in a PPI network, preserving topological features while integrating biological data (gene expression, orthology, subcellular localization). This creates a deep architecture for essential gene prediction.

Result: The proposed method outperforms centrality-based methods, Node2Vec, MLP, and graph attention networks (GAT). In simpler organisms (E. coli, D. melanogaster), MLP with Node2Vec also performs well, but in H. sapiens, the proposed architecture significantly outperforms all other methods.

Conclusion: Graph isomorphism networks that integrate biological data as node attributes while preserving network topology significantly improve essential gene prediction accuracy, particularly for complex organisms like humans.

Abstract: Background: Prediction of essential genes (proteins), is a basic and challenging problem but at the same time very costly and time-consuming in wet-lab experiments. Predicting essential genes, only based on computational methods (to introduce wet-lab candidates) using centrality measures are not accurate and result in large number of false positives; therefore, more complex models such as deep learning and also integration of biological information are used in recent research to identify essential genes.
  Methods: In this work we focus on graph isomorphism networks, in order to embed proteins as a node in PPI network to conserve topological features of PPI network, and also integrate biological data such as gene expression data, gene orthology information and gene subcellular localization information, and introduced a deep architecture for predicting essential genes. Graph isomorphism network architecture is modified in this work for embedding node information.
  Results: Our experiments proved that the proposed method outperforms baseline centrality-based methods and also machine learning based methods such as Node2Vec, MLP, and also graph attention networks (GAT).
  Conclusion: In this paper we observed that using graph isomorphism networks that integrate biological data (as node attributes) and preserve network topology can significantly improve the essential gene prediction accuracy. In simpler organisms such as E. coli and D. melanogaster, methods such as multi-layer perceptron using Node2Vec embedding also performs very good, but in H. sapiens the introduced architecture significantly outperforms deep learning and other graph neural network solutions.
  Keywords: Essential gene prediction, graph neural network, graph isomorphism network, PPI network, node embedding

</details>


### [306] [EvoCSFL: Surrogate-Assisted Evolutionary Client Selection for Efficient and Robust Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07702)
*Lin Qiang,Sun Xiaoyan,Hu Yao,Fang Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a surrogate-assisted evolutionary client selection framework for federated learning that uses candidate selection strategies, a multi-metric optimization function, and a surrogate-guided evolutionary algorithm to achieve faster convergence, lower energy consumption, and improved robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneity in client data and systems causes poor convergence and robustness in federated learning with random client selection.

Method: (1) Uses typical client selection strategies to generate candidate sets; (2) Develops a metric function integrating model performance, latency, and energy consumption; (3) Constructs a surrogate model to approximate subset performance; (4) Employs an evolutionary algorithm guided by the surrogate model to search the combinatorial selection space.

Result: Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR10, CINIC10, and TinyImageNet show faster convergence, lower energy consumption, and improved robustness over existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed surrogate-assisted evolutionary selection framework effectively addresses client heterogeneity challenges in federated learning.

Abstract: The heterogeneity of client data and systems makes it difficult to achieve satisfactory convergence speed and robustness in federated learning with random client selection. To address this issue, this paper proposes a surrogate-assisted client evolutionary selection framework for federated learning. In this framework, some typical client selection strategies are first used to generate candidate sets, and a metric function that integrates model performance, communication latency, and energy consumption is developed to formulate the client selection problem as a combinatorial optimization one. Subsequently, a surrogate model is constructed using the candidate selections and metric to efficiently approximate the performance of selected client subsets. An evolutionary algorithm is employed to search the combinatorial space of client selections, guided by the surrogate model to accelerate convergence. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR10, CINIC10, and TinyImageNet demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves faster convergence, lower energy consumption, and improved robustness compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [307] [How Much Dense Attention is Necessary? Oracle-Guided Sparse Prefill for Full/GQA Layers in Hybrid Long-Context Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07703)
*Hongxing Wang,Harenome Razanajato,Zhen Zhang,Yujie Yuan,Hongsheng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how much dense attention is needed in long-context LLMs by introducing an attention-mass top-k oracle for GQA models. It shows oracle-guided sparse attention preserves task performance within ~1 point, then derives a head-collapsed auxiliary indexer via KL distillation that achieves ~1-2 point gaps while enabling 1.7-1.9x speedups on NPU/GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context prefill remains expensive because full/GQA layers still score the entire historical sequence, even in hybrid models with local, sparse, linear, or recurrent components. The paper aims to study how much dense attention is actually needed to preserve task-level behavior under explicit support granularity and top-k budgets.

Method: 1) Introduces an attention-mass top-k oracle for GQA checkpoints that computes dense attention, selects head-averaged token support, and recomputes attention only on that support. 2) Derives a head-collapsed auxiliary indexer trained by KL distillation from dense attention-mass distributions while keeping the backbone frozen. Distilled separate indexers for Qwen3.5-0.8B and Qwen3.5-9B.

Result: Oracle: Longest per-query rows stay within 1 point of dense; Qwen3.5-9B RULER sweep 4K-100K within 0.48 points. Distilled indexer: 16K/32K validation macro gaps of +2.04 (0.8B) and +1.13 (9B) points. Speedups: 1.71x (0.8B NPU) and 1.93x (9B GPU) vs FlashAttention-2; random-init stress rows reach 3.44x.

Conclusion: Oracle feasibility demonstrates sparse-budget viability with minimal quality loss. Distilled indexers show reasonable quality preservation with tangible speedups, but fused selection-block-shared support can introduce larger realization gaps. The paper concludes by separating oracle feasibility, distilled-indexer quality, and runtime headroom, leaving a fully matched quality-latency frontier to future work.

Abstract: Long-context prefill remains expensive because full/GQA layers still score the historical sequence, even in hybrid models with local, sparse, linear, or recurrent components. We study how much dense attention is needed to preserve task-level behavior under explicit support granularity and top-k budgets.
  We introduce an attention-mass top-k oracle for existing GQA checkpoints: for each layer and query position, it computes dense attention, selects head-averaged token support, and recomputes attention only on that support. The oracle is a diagnostic reference, not a deployable accelerator, and separates sparse-budget feasibility from indexer error and runtime realization effects.
  On Qwen-family retrieval-heavy evaluations, the longest per-query oracle rows stay within 1 point of dense, and a Qwen3.5-9B RULER-style sweep from 4K to 100K stays within 0.48 points. Guided by the oracle, we derive a head-collapsed auxiliary indexer trained by KL distillation from dense attention-mass distributions while keeping the backbone frozen. With separately distilled Qwen3.5-0.8B and Qwen3.5-9B indexers, the reported 16K/32K validation macro gaps are +2.04 and +1.13 points, treated as quality preservation rather than improvement; fused selection-block-shared support can introduce a larger realization gap.
  Preliminary single-card TTFT measurements show distilled-indexer sparse serving speedups of 1.71x for Qwen3.5-0.8B on NPU and 1.93x for Qwen3.5-9B on GPU against its dense FlashAttention-2 baseline. Additional random-init stress rows reach 3.44x, indicating sparse-runtime headroom but not validated output quality. This first release separates oracle feasibility, distilled-indexer quality, and runtime headroom, leaving a fully matched quality-latency frontier to future work.

</details>


### [308] [FunctionEvolve: Structure-Guided Symbolic Regression with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07704)
*Zeyu Xia,Jun Zhu,Dong Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FunctionEvolve uses expression trees with LLM-guided evolutionary search for symbolic regression, achieving 82.9% exact recovery on 129 tasks, 4.5x better than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-driven symbolic regression systems are structure-blind: they select among opaque candidates, lack explicit local mutation mechanisms, and rely on brittle coefficient fitting that undervalues correct symbolic skeletons.

Method: An evolutionary framework using expression trees: structural summaries for diverse parent selection, local tree edits to preserve useful subexpressions, and structure-aware fitting that decomposes, constrains, and simplifies coefficients.

Result: On 129-task LLM-SRBench synthetic subset, FunctionEvolve with Claude Opus 4.6 recovers 107 exact forms (82.9% SA@50, 4.5x above baselines; 55.8% SA@1, 3.6x above prior best).

Conclusion: Structure-visible search is central to reliable symbolic recovery; LLM-guided refinements and structure-aware coefficient optimization serve as essential proposal and scoring mechanisms. Benchmark auditing reveals collinearity issues in materials-science subset.

Abstract: Symbolic regression aims to uncover explicit scientific laws from data. Recent methods use LLMs to guide mutation from background text, which is more directed than random genetic programming. However, exact symbolic recovery requires both semantic guidance and explicit structure, so that domain-informed search are carried out through valid symbolic representation. Current LLM-driven systems remain structure-blind: they select among opaque candidates, lack explicit mechanisms for local mutation, and rely on brittle coefficient fitting that can undervalue correct skeletons. We propose FunctionEvolve, an evolutionary framework using expression trees to organize the whole search: structural summaries promote diverse parent selection, local tree edits preserve useful subexpressions, and structure-aware fitting decomposes, constrains, and simplifies coefficients for more reliable scoring. It uses only elementary function families, without additional domain-specific rules limiting generalization. On the 129-task synthetic subset of LLM-SRBench, FunctionEvolve with \emph{Claude Opus 4.6} recovers 107 exact forms, reaching 82.9% SA@50, 4.5x above same-backbone baselines, and 55.8% SA@1, 3.6x above the strongest previously published top-1 result. Ablations show that structure-visible search is central to reliable recovery, with LLM-guided refinements and structure-aware coefficient optimization serving as essential proposal and scoring mechanisms. We also audit the benchmark and show that collinearity in its materials-science subset creates identifiability issues.

</details>


### [309] [SAW: Stage-Aware Dynamic Weighting for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07705)
*Yuchen He,Baolong Bi,Shenghua Liu,Huaming Liao,Yuyao Ge,Bolin Wan,Siqian Tong,Juan Chen,Jiafeng Guo,Xueqi Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAW is a lightweight dynamic weighting method for multi-objective RL that uses coefficient of variation to rebalance rewards across asynchronously learned dimensions, improving LLM alignment efficiency and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward learning is asynchronous across objectives: well-learned dimensions produce homogeneous, low-variance signals whose noise contaminates aggregated rewards or occupies fixed advantage budget, interfering with high-value signals from under-learned dimensions.

Method: Stage-Aware Dynamic Weighting (SAW) uses coefficient of variation (CV) as a scale-invariant proxy for real-time informativeness to reweight each dimension's reward/advantage contribution, relying solely on batch-level statistics with negligible overhead.

Result: Experiments on tool-calling and text summarization tasks show SAW consistently improves training efficiency and final performance under both GRPO and GDPO frameworks.

Conclusion: SAW is a general-purpose plug-in for multi-reward LLM alignment that effectively addresses reward asynchrony with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Although multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) is central to aligning large language models with complex human preferences, the prevailing practice of static weighted summation overlooks a more fundamental phenomenon: reward learning is markedly asynchronous across objectives. Well-learned dimensions quickly produce homogeneous, low-variance signals whose residual noise contaminates the aggregated reward (in GRPO) or occupies a fixed share of the advantage budget (in GDPO), interfering with the scarce yet high-value signals carried by under-learned dimensions. To address this asynchrony, we propose Stage-Aware Dynamic Weighting (SAW), a lightweight, algorithm-agnostic dynamic weighting mechanism. SAW utilizes the coefficient of variation (CV) as a scale-invariant proxy for real-time informativeness, reweighting each dimension's reward or advantage contribution by its relative informativeness within the batch. Unlike gradient-based methods that require multiple forward and backward passes, SAW relies solely on batch-level statistics, introducing nearly negligible computational overhead. Experiments on tool-calling and text summarization tasks demonstrate that SAW consistently improves both training efficiency and final performance under both GRPO and GDPO frameworks, confirming it as a general-purpose plug-in for multi-reward LLM alignment. Our code is available at https://github.com/Zhaolutuan/SAW

</details>


### [310] [Decoding Naturalistic Emotion Dynamics from the Brain: An LLM-Enhanced Regression Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07707)
*Lemei Zhang,Peng Liu,Hans Dahle Kvadsheim,August Sætre Aasvær,Shuer Ye,Reza Bonyadi,Maryam Ziaei,Jon Atle Gulla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study reframes emotion decoding from neural signals as a continuous, multi-target regression problem rather than discrete classification. Using LLMs to extract fine-grained sentiment profiles from a naturalistic auditory narrative (Alice in Wonderland), they train machine learning models on Dynamic Functional Connectivity (DFC) from fMRI data. DFC-based models outperform static ROI amplitude representations in tracking continuous emotional trajectories, and graph-theoretical XAI reveals interpretable emotion-specific network configurations, supporting constructionist frameworks over locationist accounts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional emotion decoding from neural signals is oversimplified as discrete, single-label classification based on stable stimuli, failing to capture the continuous, fluid, and co-occurring nature of human affect.

Method: (1) LLM-based extraction of continuous sentiment profiles from Alice in Wonderland as scalable proxy labels. (2) fMRI-based Dynamic Functional Connectivity (DFC) and static ROI amplitude representations. (3) Regularized and kernel-based machine learning algorithms as continuous estimators. (4) Graph-theoretical Explainable AI (XAI) for feature interpretation.

Result: Models trained on DFC temporal snapshots significantly outperform static ROI-based models in tracking continuous emotional trajectories under rapidly fluctuating narrative input.

Conclusion: LLM-automated annotation is useful in affective neuroscience; dynamic, distributed network interactions offer superior explanatory power over locationist accounts, supporting psychological constructionist frameworks.

Abstract: Decoding emotional states from neural signals has been typically framed as a discrete, single-label classification task based on emotionally stable stimuli, a formulation that oversimplifies the continuous, fluid, and co-occurring nature of human affect. This study reconceptualizes emotion decoding by adopting a multi-target regression framework to track multiple overlapping emotional dimensions as continuous trajectories over time. Leveraging the robust generalization capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), we extracted fine-grained, continuous sentiment profiles from a naturalistic auditory narrative, Alice in Wonderland, to serve as scalable proxies for subjective affect from human fMRI dataset. Departing from standard classification paradigms or mass-univariate subtractive contrasts that filter out network dynamics, we leverage regularized and kernel-based machine learning algorithms as continuous estimators to track the magnitude of macroscale neural state variations. We demonstrate that models trained on temporal snapshots of Dynamic Functional Connectivity (DFC) significantly outperform static region-of-interest (ROI) amplitude representations, effectively capturing continuous emotional trajectories under rapidly fluctuating narrative input. Furthermore, by implementing graph-theoretical Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, we deconstruct the underlying predictive features to reveal highly interpretable, emotion-specific topological configurations. Collectively, these results highlight the utility of LLM-automated annotation in affective neuroscience and provide compelling empirical evidence for psychological constructionist frameworks, demonstrating that dynamic, distributed network interactions offer superior explanatory power over strictly locationist accounts of emotion.

</details>


### [311] [WhiFlash: Accelerating Speculative Decoding with Token-Level Cross-Paradigm Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07710)
*Young D. Kwon,Miles Williams,Rui Li,Alexandros Kouris,Stylianos I. Venieris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WhiFlash is the first cross-paradigm speculative decoding method that unifies autoregressive and diffusion-based drafting models under a single token-level controller, using fine-grained routing and cache optimizations to achieve up to 69.6% throughput gains over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The autoregressive bottleneck of LLMs during inference, especially in complex agentic workloads. Existing speculative decoding methods use static drafting paradigms (either autoregressive or diffusion-based), but drafting accuracy fluctuates dramatically within a single sequence, leaving performance unrealized.

Method: WhiFlash introduces a cross-paradigm approach unifying autoregressive and diffusion-based parallel drafting under a single token-level controller. It uses fine-grained routing (either lightweight entropy-based or learned neural policy) to balance expected token gain and latency. Novel cache-management optimizations (Lazy Catch-up and KV-only Prefill) reduce switching overhead below 7%.

Result: Achieves significantly higher acceptance lengths, with category-specific throughput gains up to 69.6% over EAGLE-3 (autoregressive) and 37.3% over DFlash (diffusion-based).

Conclusion: Capitalizing on complementary strengths of fundamentally distinct drafting architectures through dynamic, fine-grained switching yields substantial inference speedups over static or coarse-grained routing approaches.

Abstract: The autoregressive nature of large language models (LLMs) remains a significant bottleneck for inference, particularly in complex agentic workloads. While speculative decoding (SD) accelerates inference, current approaches rely on static drafting paradigms, utilising either autoregressive drafting models for reasoning or diffusion-based parallel drafting models for structured outputs. We empirically find that drafting accuracy fluctuates dramatically within a single sequence, leaving significant performance unrealised by static paradigms and coarse-grained routing. To address this volatility, we introduce WhiFlash, the first cross-paradigm SD method that unifies autoregressive and diffusion-based parallel drafting under a single token-level controller. WhiFlash adopts a fine-grained routing mechanism that employs either a lightweight entropy-based or a learned neural policy, both parametrised to provide a tunable balance between expected token gain and latency. To make high-frequency switching computationally viable, we introduce novel cache-management optimisations, Lazy Catch-up and KV-only Prefill, reducing switching overhead to below 7% of per-round latency. By capitalising on the complementary strengths of fundamentally distinct drafting architectures, WhiFlash achieves significantly higher acceptance lengths, yielding category-specific throughput gains of up to 69.6% over the state-of-the-art autoregressive EAGLE-3 and 37.3% over the diffusion-based DFlash.

</details>


### [312] [Rosetta Memory: Adaptive Memory for Cross-LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07711)
*Hao Yang,Shiqi Shen,Haoxuan Li,Zhipeng Wang,Zhi Gong,Xu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the problem of memory adaptation across different LLMs (e.g., using GPT for writing and Claude for reading memory) by shifting from LLM-centric memory design to memory-centric LLM adaptation, proposing profile-conditioned operators trained with a minimum-gain sampling curriculum and performance-gap reward, achieving consistent improvements on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory systems are designed around a specific LLM backbone, but users frequently switch between different LLMs (e.g., Claude for coding, GPT for writing). Memory written by one model often needs to be consumed by another, yet making upstream memory effectively adapt to and activate downstream LLMs remains an underexplored problem.

Method: The authors shift from LLM-centric memory design to memory-centric LLM adaptation. They design two profile-conditioned operators (write and read) that are jointly trained, use a minimum-gain sampling curriculum to prioritize least-served LLMs during training, and employ a performance-gap reward to measure the operators' actual contribution vs. the LLM's own capability.

Result: Experiments on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue show the model consistently outperforms baselines and remains robust under unseen-model replacement.

Conclusion: The proposed memory-centric LLM adaptation approach effectively bridges the gap between different LLMs' memory usage, enabling robust cross-model memory transfer and task completion.

Abstract: Memory is the key component for transforming a stateless LLM into a persistent, evolving agent through experience accumulation, long-horizon planning, and continual self-improvement. Existing memory systems typically take the LLM as the center and design memory operations tailored to a specific backbone. In practice, however, users frequently switch between LLMs, for example using Claude for coding and GPT for writing across tasks, or routing different steps to different backbones within a single task for cost-effective trade-offs. As a result, memory written by one model often needs to be consumed by another. Making upstream memory effectively adapt to and activate downstream LLMs remains a critical yet underexplored problem. To bridge this gap, we shift the perspective from LLM-centric memory design to \emph{memory-centric LLM adaptation}. Specifically, we approach the above upstream-downstream memory adaptation problem from both the write and read sides, and design two profile-conditioned operators that are jointly trained to optimize how memory is stored and presented for better task completion. To ensure the learned operators generalize across a broad set of LLMs, we propose a minimum-gain sampling curriculum that prioritizes the least-served LLMs during training. To better measure the operators' actual contribution rather than the LLM's own capability, we design a performance-gap reward that compares against a naive memory baseline. Experiments on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms baselines and remains robust under unseen-model replacement.

</details>


### [313] [Attention at the Theoretical Minimum: A Mathematics of Arrays Framework for Memory-Optimal Transformer Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07713)
*Lenore Mullin,Gaetan Hains*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) reformulation of attention that eliminates all intermediate memory buffers (softmax temps, key transpose) via algebraic construction, reducing data movement from O(n²) to O(n·dk + n·dv) with projected 2-100× speedup and 2-50× energy savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention implementations incur quadratic memory traffic in sequence length due to intermediate arrays (key transpose, softmax temps), and DRAM accesses cost 100-1000× more energy than arithmetic, making FLOP-only analysis misleading. The paper aims to address this memory bottleneck algebraically rather than through empirical tuning.

Method: The authors apply Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) to derive a Denotational Normal Form (DNF) of scaled dot-product attention and numerically stable softmax. This eliminates all intermediate arrays (transposed-key buffer, every softmax temporary) through algebraic rewriting. The pipeline goes from Python specification → DNF → Operational Normal Form (ONF) → dimension-lifted hardware mapping.

Result: The DNF achieves O(n·dk + n·dv) data movement vs O(n² + n·dk + n·dv) for standard implementation. Verified numerically against PyTorch at full double precision. Predictive model projects 2-100× speedup and 2-50× energy reduction, with widening advantage at exascale.

Conclusion: MoA provides a unified algebraic framework for array fusion, shape-transformation correctness, and predictive cost models — unlike hardware-specific accelerators (e.g., FlashAttention). Memory minimality is a theorem provable before code execution, enabling formally verified, performance-portable AI kernels for edge (DARPA) and exascale (DOE) deployment.

Abstract: The attention mechanism is the dominant computational bottleneck in modern transformer-based AI. Its standard implementation incurs quadratic memory traffic in the sequence length~$n$, and DRAM accesses cost 100--1000$\times$ more energy than arithmetic operations on contemporary hardware, so any analysis focused solely on FLOP counts fundamentally mischaracterises the bottleneck.
  We present a Mathematics of Arrays (MoA) reformulation of scaled dot-product attention and its numerically stable softmax, deriving a Denotational Normal Form (DNF) that eliminates all intermediate arrays -- including the implicit transposed-key buffer and every softmax temporary -- by algebraic construction rather than empirical tuning. The DNF achieves $O(n_{dk} + n{_{dv}})$ data movement versus $O(n^2 + n_{dk} + n_{dv})$ for the standard implementation, where $n$ is the sequence length, $dk$ is the key dimensionality and $dv$ the value dimensionality, and is verified numerically against PyTorch at full double-precision floating-point on concrete inputs.
  Unlike hardware-specific accelerators or empirical tiling schemes such as FlashAttention, MoA simultaneously provides array fusion, shape-transformation correctness, and predictive cost models from a single algebraic framework. Memory minimality is a theorem established before any code is written. A predictive performance model projects $2$--$100\times$ speedup and $2$--$50\times$ energy reduction, with the advantage widening at exascale. The derivation establishes a formally verified pipeline from Python specification through (ONF) Operational Normal Form, and dimension-lifted hardware mapping, providing performance-portable AI kernels of direct relevance to DARPA edge-deployment and DOE exascale priorities.

</details>


### [314] [Beyond Accuracy: Interpreting Topic Representation in Suicide Ideation Detection Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07714)
*Hamideh Ghanadian,Isar Nejadgholi,Hussein Al Osman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study analyzes how suicide ideation detection models internally represent psychological risk factors, finding that topic-aware data augmentation improves both performance and interpretability by making underrepresented factors like immigration, family issues, and financial crisis more coherent and separable in the model's representation space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Suicide ideation detection models are typically evaluated only on aggregate performance metrics, with little understanding of how they internally encode psychologically meaningful risk factors. For high-stakes mental health applications, transparency and safety require deeper insight into these internal representations.

Method: The authors trained suicide detection models on original and topic-augmented datasets, then analyzed internal representation spaces using visualization and geometric analysis to examine coherence and separability of topic-related features.

Result: Topic-aware augmentation increased the clarity and distinctness of underrepresented psychosocial risk factors such as immigration, family issues, and financial crisis in the model's internal representation space.

Conclusion: Data augmentation not only improves model performance but also leads to more structured and interpretable internal representations, which is critical for responsible deployment in mental health applications.

Abstract: Suicide ideation detection models are typically evaluated using aggregate performance metrics, yet little is known about how they internally represent psychologically meaningful risk factors. In high-stakes mental health applications, understanding these internal representations is essential for safety, transparency, and responsible deployment. In this work, we move beyond accuracy and analyze how suicide detection models trained on original and topic-augmented datasets encode psychological risk factors in their internal representation space. Using visualization and geometric analysis, we examine the coherence and separability of topic-related features. Our results show that topic-aware augmentation increases the clarity and distinctness of underrepresented psychosocial risk factors such as immigration, family issues, and financial crisis. These findings suggest that augmentation not only improves model performance but also leads to more structured and interpretable internal representations.

</details>


### [315] [A Geometry-Aware Triplane Field Network for Vehicle Aerodynamic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07724)
*Kangkang Qi,Huiyu Yang,Keqi Ding,Yunpeng Wang,Yuntian Chen,Yuanwei Bin,Rikui Zhang,Jianchun Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GTF-Net is a machine learning method for CFD that uses triplane features with AFNO spectral mixing and CNN refinement to predict vehicle aerodynamic pressure and wall shear stress more accurately than existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity CFD is costly for early-stage vehicle design; a faster ML-based surface-field predictor that captures both global flow context and local geometry is needed.

Method: GTF-Net constructs triplane features from surface points via MLP and bilinear rasterization, processes them with a dual-stream backbone (AFNO spectral mixing + CNN refinement), and augments queries with directional coordinates, normal-projection features, and voxel curvature proxy.

Result: GTF-Net reduces relative L2 error for pressure from 0.157 to 0.145 and wall shear stress from 0.237 to 0.226, outperforming Transolver, GINO, and TripNet.

Conclusion: Combining structured triplane representation with explicit aerodynamic geometry cues (AFNO + CNN + query-side encoding) improves prediction accuracy, supported by ablation studies.

Abstract: High-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) is crucial to vehicle aerodynamic analysis, but its cost still constrains early-stage design exploration. Machine-learning-based surface-field prediction offers a faster alternative if the model can efficiently capture both global flow context and local geometric detail. This work proposes a machine-learning-based method, named the geometry-aware triplane field network (GTF-Net), for vehicle aerodynamic pressure and wall shear stress prediction. GTF-Net constructs triplane features directly from sampled surface points through a shared multilayer perceptron (MLP) and smooth bilinear rasterization. The planes are then processed by a dual-stream backbone that combines adaptive Fourier neural operator (AFNO) spectral mixing with convolutional neural network (CNN) refinement, so long-range aerodynamic coupling and local geometry-induced variations are modeled in the same representation. At query stage, sampled triplane features are combined with vehicle-aligned directional coordinates, normal-projection features, and a voxel-based curvature proxy. GTF-Net is compared with Transolver, geometry-informed neural operator (GINO), and TripNet, a triplane-based surrogate model. GTF-Net improves the relative L2 error from the strongest baseline value of 0.157 to 0.145 for pressure prediction and from 0.237 to 0.226 for wall shear stress prediction. Ablation results show that AFNO mixing, local CNN refinement, and query-side geometric encoding each contribute to accuracy, supporting the proposed mechanism of combining structured triplane representation with explicit aerodynamic geometry cues.

</details>


### [316] [Cutting LLM Evaluation Costs with SySRs: A Bandit Algorithm that Provably Exploits Model Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07726)
*Zifan Lyu,Chahine Nejma,Tobias Wegel,Fanny Yang,Florian E. Dorner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Synchronized Successive Rejects (SySR), an adaptive best-arm identification algorithm that reduces the cost of benchmarking LLMs by using paired comparisons and leveraging model similarity, achieving better performance than baselines across 15 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarking all LLMs on every test query is wasteful, especially when some models clearly underperform. The authors aim to reduce evaluation costs by adaptively allocating budget and leveraging model similarity.

Method: The proposed Synchronized Successive Rejects (SySR) algorithm extends the classical Successive Rejects method by incorporating paired comparisons between models. It is hyperparameter-free and theoretically guarantees improved performance when models are similar.

Result: SySR outperforms all baselines in average error rate across 15 standard benchmarks and achieves better worst-case budget for reliably identifying the best model.

Conclusion: SySR effectively reduces benchmarking costs while maintaining high accuracy in identifying the best LLM, with theoretical guarantees that improve with model similarity.

Abstract: Large Language Models are typically benchmarked by evaluating every model on every test query. For practitioners seeking the best model to deploy, this is often wasteful: if a model clearly performs worse than others, there is no need to precisely estimate its performance. Best-arm identification algorithms can be naturally applied to drastically reduce costs by adaptively allocating evaluation budget. Further, language models often respond similarly to the same prompt-a property previous work has tried to leverage with mixed success. We propose Synchronized Successive Rejects (SySRs), augmenting the classical Successive Rejects algorithm with paired comparisons. Unlike prior attempts to leverage model similarity in best-model identification, our approach is hyperparameter-free and enjoys performance guarantees that improve with the degree of similarity between evaluated models. Empirically, our method outperforms all baselines in terms of average error rate across 15 standard benchmarks, and in terms of worst-case budget for reliably identifying the best model.

</details>


### [317] [Characterizing the Discrete Geometry of ReLU Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07728)
*Blake B. Gaines,Jinbo Bi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves that ReLU network region connectivity graphs have average degree bounded by 2×input dimension and diameter independent of input dimension, despite exponential growth in region count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how linear regions of ReLU networks connect is fundamental to network behavior, yet little is known about the geometry of these complexes beyond total region counts.

Method: Theoretical analysis of connectivity graphs of linear regions in fully-connected ReLU networks, deriving upper bounds on average degree and diameter, with experimental validation on synthetic and real-world data.

Result: Average degree of the region connectivity graph ≤ 2×input dimension; diameter has an upper bound independent of input dimension even as region count grows exponentially.

Conclusion: ReLU network region connectivity graphs exhibit surprisingly constrained geometry regardless of network width/depth, with degree bounded by input dimension and diameter dimension-independent.

Abstract: It is well established that ReLU networks define continuous piecewise-linear functions, and that their linear regions are polyhedra in the input space. These regions form a complex that fully partitions the input space. The way these regions fit together is fundamental to the behavior of the network, as nonlinearities occur only at the boundaries where these regions connect. However, relatively little is known about the geometry of these complexes beyond bounds on the total number of regions, and calculating the complex exactly is intractable for most networks. In this work, we prove new theoretical results about these complexes that hold for all fully-connected ReLU networks, specifically about their connectivity graphs in which nodes correspond to regions and edges exist between each pair of regions connected by a face. We find that the average degree of this graph is upper bounded by twice the input dimension regardless of the width and depth of the network, and that the diameter of this graph has an upper bound that does not depend on input dimension, despite the number of regions increasing exponentially with input dimension. We corroborate our findings through experiments with networks trained on both synthetic and real-world data, which provide additional insight into the geometry of ReLU networks. Code to reproduce our results can be found at https://github.com/bl-ake/ICLR-2026.

</details>


### [318] [scCBGM: Interpretable Single-Cell Counterfactual Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07760)
*Alma Andersson,Aya Abdelsalam Ismail,Edward De Brouwer,Doron Haviv,Tommaso Biancalani,Kyunghyun Cho,Gabriele Scalia,Aïcha BenTaieb,Hector Corrada Bravo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: scCBGM is a framework for interpretable counterfactual editing of single-cell RNA sequencing data using concept bottleneck architectures with decoder skip connections and cross-covariance penalty, achieving superior combinatorial generalization and counterfactual prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The combinatorial space of cellular conditions makes exhaustive experimental mapping infeasible, creating a need for computational methods to predict cellular responses to perturbations in an interpretable manner.

Method: scCBGM adapts concept bottleneck architectures for single-cell data using decoder skip connections and a cross-covariance penalty for disentanglement, extended to flow matching models for concept-guided editing in both encoding-decoding and generation regimes. A synthetic benchmark with ground-truth counterfactuals was developed for evaluation.

Result: Superior performance in combinatorial generalization and counterfactual prediction across multiple real datasets, validated at cell-level on synthetic data and population-level on real benchmarks.

Conclusion: scCBGM enables interpretable and precise counterfactual editing of individual cells, offering a powerful tool for understanding cellular phenotypes and responses to perturbations in disease biology and therapeutic design.

Abstract: Understanding cellular phenotypes and how they respond to perturbations is critical for disease biology and therapeutic design. Single-cell RNA sequencing enables characterization at cellular resolution, yet the combinatorial space of conditions makes exhaustive experimental mapping infeasible. We introduce single-cell Concept Bottleneck Generative Models (scCBGM), a framework for interpretable and precise counterfactual editing of individual cells. scCBGM adapts concept bottleneck architectures for single-cell data through decoder skip connections and a cross-covariance penalty that promotes disentanglement without dimensional constraints. We extend the framework to flow matching models, enabling concept-guided editing in both encoding-decoding and generation regimes. To enable rigorous evaluation, we develop a synthetic benchmark with ground-truth counterfactuals. Across multiple real datasets, scCBGM demonstrates superior performance in combinatorial generalization and counterfactual prediction, supported by cell-level validation on synthetic data and population-level benchmarks on real datasets.

</details>


### [319] [Contrast encodes inductive bias: separating slow noise from dynamics in predictive representation learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07770)
*Paarth Gulati,Ilya Nemenman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-supervised methods like JEPA confuse slow noise with true dynamics when using cross-trajectory contrastive objectives. Using within-trajectory negatives removes this shortcut, forcing encoders to learn true dynamical variables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and address the failure mode where contrastive predictive objectives preferentially encode slow-varying noise instead of true latent dynamics, degrading downstream performance regardless of data scale.

Method: The authors study the failure in two settings: SimCLR-style JEPA on synthetic moving dots, and DySIB on rigid-body pendulum videos. They replace cross-trajectory negative sampling with within-trajectory negative sampling.

Result: Within-trajectory negative sampling removes the slow noise shortcut, allowing representations to capture true dynamical variables. Longer trajectories improve representations even under strong noise.

Conclusion: Negative sampling strategy is critical for dynamical representation learning; within-trajectory negatives are a principled solution for physical systems with noisy observations.

Abstract: Self-supervised methods that learn representations and predict dynamics fully in the latent space, such as JEPA, have been shown to confuse slowly varying noise with the dynamical signals they aim to capture. Specifically, when noise features remain approximately constant within each trajectory, contrastive predictive objectives preferentially encode these features instead of the true latent variables governing the system. The learned representation then becomes dominated by trajectory-specific noise, so downstream performance degrades with noise strength and does not improve even as the number and duration of training trajectories increase. We argue that this failure is a property of the objective itself, shared by a long line of contrastive predictive objectives that sample negatives across trajectories. To illustrate this generality, we study the failure mode and its remedy in two settings: a standard SimCLR-style JEPA on a synthetic moving-dot dataset, and DySIB, a recently introduced method designed for extracting physically interpretable representations of dynamics, on movies of a rigid-body pendulum. When negatives are instead sampled within a single trajectory, the slow noise can no longer distinguish frames within that trajectory, removing the predictive shortcut. Training one encoder simultaneously on many such trajectories then forces it to encode the variables relevant for the dynamics, with longer trajectories yielding better representations even for strong slow noise. Our results point toward principles for designing contrastive predictive objectives in dynamical representation learning, especially for physical systems with noisy experimental observations.

</details>


### [320] [A Framework for Evaluating and Benchmarking Concept Drift Detection Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07789)
*Vitor Cerqueira,Heitor Murilo Gomes,Marco Heyden,Bernhard Pfahringer,Albert Bifet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a benchmarking framework for concept drift detection, introducing controlled drift injection into real-world datasets, timing-aware evaluation metrics, and a leave-one-dataset-out hyperparameter optimization protocol. Evaluating 14 methods on 7 datasets with 4 drift types reveals strengths/weaknesses and establishes baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field of drift detection suffers from inconsistent evaluation practices—oversimplified synthetic data, incompatible metrics, and opaque hyperparameter choices—making fair comparisons between methods difficult.

Method: (1) Drift simulation via Monte Carlo trials injecting controlled distributional changes into real-world datasets; (2) Timing-aware evaluation protocol with new metrics (F1 detection score, normalized detection time); (3) Leave-one-dataset-out hyperparameter optimization for robust configuration.

Result: Benchmarked 14 drift detection methods on 7 real-world datasets across 4 drift types (class prior, label swap, feature permutation, feature filtering) under both abrupt and gradual transitions, providing baseline performance metrics.

Conclusion: The framework enables fairer, more reproducible drift detection evaluation, revealing insights into method strengths/weaknesses and establishing baselines for future research.

Abstract: Data stream mining is fundamentally challenged by concept drift, where distributional changes can degrade model performance. Despite the proliferation of drift detection methods, progress in the field is hindered by inconsistent evaluation practices: studies rely on oversimplified synthetic data generators, adopt incompatible metrics, and lack transparency in hyperparameter selection, making fair comparisons difficult. We address this gap with a novel benchmarking framework comprising three contributions: (1) a drift simulation method that injects controlled distributional changes into real-world datasets via Monte Carlo trials, enabling supervised evaluation while preserving real-world data complexity; (2) an evaluation protocol for drift detection with timing-aware criteria, including the derivation of new metrics (e.g., F1 detection score, normalized detection time) that are comparable across streams; and (3) we advocate for a leave-one-dataset-out hyperparameter optimization protocol for drift detection methods that promotes configuration robustness across heterogeneous stream dynamics. We benchmark 14 widely used drift detection methods on 7 realworld datasets across 4 drift types (class prior, label swap, feature permutation, feature filtering), each under both abrupt and gradual transitions. Our experimental results provide insights into the strengths and weaknesses of current drift detection approaches while establishing baseline performance metrics for future research in this area. All code and experiments are publicly available.

</details>


### [321] [Byzantine Cheap Talk: Adversarial Resilience and Topology Effects in LLM Coordination Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07790)
*Aya El Mir,Martin Takáč,Salem Lahlou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-agent LLM coordination is vulnerable to Byzantine attacks and topology disclosure even when agents detect betrayal, due to meta-reasoning failures and payoff structure constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the robustness vulnerabilities of multi-agent LLM communication protocols under adversarial and structural constraints, building on known cheap-talk cooperation results.

Method: 4-player Stag Hunt experiment across six model families and 720 trials, testing Byzantine agents (signal cooperation but defect) and restricted/disclosed communication topology.

Result: Byzantine betrayal is detected within one round but collective adaptation fails; restricting topology collapses cooperation only when disclosed; two stable archetypes (Defection-Prone and Cooperation-Persistent) replicate across all models.

Conclusion: Communication channels are adversarial injection vectors; disclosing network topology degrades coordination even without adversaries; meta-reasoning about hidden information causes failure, not information loss itself.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems increasingly rely on communication protocols for coordination, yet their robustness under adversarial and structural constraints remains poorly understood. Building on prior work showing that cheap-talk channels enable cooperation in LLM coordination games, we investigate two vulnerability classes in a 4-player Stag Hunt across six model families and 720 trials. First, when Byzantine agents signal cooperation but defect, non-Byzantine agents detect the betrayal within one round yet fail to adapt collectively: a substantial fraction continue cooperating despite repeated exploitation, unable to recover coordination due to the game's unanimity payoff structure. Second, explicitly restricting communication topology collapses cooperation, while applying identical restrictions silently preserves near-perfect cooperation. This establishes that coordination failure stems from agents' meta-reasoning about hidden information, not information loss itself. We identify two stable behavioral archetypes that replicate across all model cohorts: Defection-Prone models that switch permanently after betrayal, and Cooperation-Persistent models that continue cooperating at significant individual cost.
