These findings reveal concrete security vulnerabilities: communication channels can be exploited as adversarial injection vectors, and disclosing network topology to agents can degrade coordination even without any adversary present.

</details>


### [322] [Mitigating the Contractivity Trap in Diffusion ODEs via Stein Stabilization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07835)
*Shigui Li,Delu Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SteinDiff is a reference-free, inference-time stabilization framework that uses Stein-derived corrections to resolve the contractivity trap in large-step diffusion model inference, improving generative quality without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the contractivity trap in diffusion models: efficient large-step inference conflicts with contraction-based stability guarantees, leading to error accumulation and severe artifacts.

Method: SteinDiff introduces a geometry-aware residual correction mechanism with a closed-form Stein correction coefficient for step-wise solver adjustment, enabling reference-free adaptation to local data geometry at inference time.

Result: The method mitigates severe artifacts and improves generative quality across large-step inference settings, supported by a score-controlled perturbation bound under distributional shifts.

Conclusion: SteinDiff effectively resolves the contractivity trap in diffusion model inference, offering a practical inference-time solution without retraining or reference samples.

Abstract: A fundamental tension exists in the large-step inference of diffusion models via their deterministic probability flow ordinary differential equation (PF-ODE) trajectories, which we identify as the contractivity trap: efficient inference favors large step sizes, while aggressive steps and highly expressive denoisers can undermine contraction-based stability certificates for error suppression. To address this, we propose SteinDiff, a step-wise inference-time stabilization framework that employs Stein-derived corrections without requiring reference samples. Specifically, SteinDiff introduces a geometry-aware residual correction mechanism that regularizes large-step solver updates without retraining. To this end, we derive a closed-form Stein correction coefficient for step-wise solver adjustment, enabling reference-free adaptation to local data geometry. We further establish a score-controlled perturbation bound under distributional shifts and provide a complementary Stein perspective on EDM-style parameterizations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SteinDiff mitigates severe artifacts and improves generative quality across large-step inference settings.

</details>


### [323] [Teacher-Free Self-Training Amplifies but Does Not Compound: A Pass@$K$ Crossover on a Free-Verifier Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07856)
*Igor Lima Strozzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether a language model gains new capabilities through self-training on its own verified outputs or merely amplifies existing ones. Using a trapdoor DSL with a generator, learned critic, and exact verifier, they find that self-training concentrates probability mass rather than expanding reach—this is amplification, not compounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether language models acquire genuinely new capabilities when trained on their own verified outputs, or simply become better at expressing capabilities they already possessed—a fundamental question for understanding self-training dynamics.

Method: A teacher-free "constellation" setup with a generator, learned critic, and exact verifier on a FlashFill-style trapdoor DSL. All experiments run on one 4-bit Qwen3-4B on a single 24 GB GPU. They compare critic-guided selection vs. verifier-filtered best-of-k, and perform per-round STaR self-training across K=4 independent trajectories.

Result: (i) Critic-guided selection outperforms verifier-filtered best-of-k by +9.1 pp (6/6 seeds), with gains on tasks where candidates disagree. (ii) STaR self-training raises the ceiling but never accelerates—gain tracks remaining headroom. (iii) Pass@K crossover shows the trained model wins at pass@8 but the base overtakes at pass@64 on every trajectory, indicating probability mass concentration rather than capability expansion.

Conclusion: Self-training in this setting produces amplification (better exploitation of existing capabilities) rather than compounding (acquisition of new capabilities), as evidenced by the budget-dependent pass@K crossover.

Abstract: When a language model trains on its own verified outputs, does it acquire capability beyond its base, or merely get better at expressing capability the base already had? We make the question decidable with a teacher-free "constellation" -- a generator, a learned critic, and a free exact verifier -- on a FlashFill-style "trapdoor" DSL, where verified (problem, solution) pairs are cheap to synthesize, hard to invert, and free to check exactly. Everything runs on one 4-bit Qwen3-4B on a single 24 GB GPU, with no model in the loop larger than the base. We report three findings. (i) Critic-guided selection beats verifier-filtered best-of-$k$ by $+9.1$ pp ($6/6$ seeds), with the entire gain localized to tasks where candidates disagree on held-out inputs. (ii) Per-round STaR self-training raises the ceiling but never accelerates -- the gain tracks remaining headroom and decelerates across $K=4$ independent training trajectories. (iii) The domain has no clean zero-capability frontier, so the usual "$0\% \to$ climb $=$ emergence" test is invalid here. A measured pass@$K$ crossover settles the diagnosis: the trained model wins at the operating budget (pass@$8$) but the base overtakes it at a large budget (pass@$64$) on every trajectory, so self-training concentrates probability mass rather than expanding reach. This is amplification, not compounding. ($K=4$ is indicative, not yet a robust across-trajectory CI.)

</details>


### [324] [Instrumented data for causal scientific machine learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07865)
*Daniel N. Wilke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes "instrumented data" as a third data paradigm (beyond observational and synthetic) where each datum carries its mechanistic model, uncertainty quantification, and counterfactual capabilities, realized through V&V instrumented image-to-simulation pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific ML is limited not by model size but by data; observational data lacks causal explanation, and synthetic data is tied to fixed templates rather than specific user cases.

Method: Introduces instrumented data via V&V image-to-simulation pipelines: sensor observations become fully specified, solver-backed simulations with editable parameters and propagated aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty, supporting Pearl's do-operator for causal interventions.

Result: Near-term applications in validation, auditing, and surrogate training across computational biology, climate, materials, fluid mechanics, and medical imaging.

Conclusion: Long-term falsifiable implication concerns foundation models for scientific reasoning; the substrate is case-specific, mechanistically supervised, and causally grounded.

Abstract: Scientific machine learning is limited less by model size than by the data it is trained on. Observational data records what happened but not why; template synthetic data has a known generating process but only for the simulator's template, not the case a user faces. We argue a third option is now operationally feasible: instrumented data, in which every datum carries the mechanistic model that produced it, an explicit uncertainty over that model, and an executable family of counterfactuals. Verification-and-validation (V&V) instrumented image-to-simulation pipelines are one realisation: a sensor observation becomes a fully specified, solver-backed simulation with explicit, editable parameters and a propagated aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty. The substrate is case-specific, mechanistically supervised, and supports causal interventions through Pearl's do-operator. Near-term consequences for validation, auditing, and surrogate training span computational biology, climate, materials, fluid mechanics, and medical imaging; a longer-term, falsifiable implication concerns foundation models for scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [325] [Still: Amortized KV Cache Compaction in a Single Forward Pass](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07878)
*Charles O'Neill,Alex Sandomirsky,Harry Partridge,Mudith Jayasekara,Max Kirkby*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Still introduces a lightweight, trainable Perceiver-based KV cache compactor that enables efficient, reusable, and high-quality context compression across extreme ratios (8×–200×) and long contexts (8k–128k), outperforming prior methods on RULER, HELMET, and LongBench benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The KV cache is the memory bottleneck for long-horizon language model deployment, and existing compaction methods (selection vs. synthesis) fail to simultaneously satisfy three requirements: lightweight inference, expressive context preservation, and cross-trajectory reusability.

Method: Still employs a small per-layer Perceiver module trained once on a frozen base model. It produces compact keys and values in a single forward pass, enabling iterative (amortized) compression without per-context optimization.

Result: On Qwen and Gemma models, Still achieves favorable speed-quality trade-offs across 8×–200× compression and 8k–128k context lengths. It outperforms the strongest baseline by 8–22 points on RULER, preserves full-context gains on HELMET, and wins pairwise LongBench summarization against KV-Distill.

Conclusion: Amortization makes long-context cache compaction tractable, and synthesis makes compact states useful even at extreme compression ratios, enabling a long-horizon regime inaccessible to per-context methods.

Abstract: The KV cache is the memory bottleneck of long-horizon language model deployment. Practically, a deployable compactor must be lightweight enough to call during inference, expressive enough to preserve context under constraint, and reusable across a trajectory. Existing compaction methods satisfy only part of this requirement: selection methods are lightweight but subset-bound, while synthesis methods are expressive but rely on per-context optimization. Here we introduce Still, a small per-layer Perceiver trained once against a frozen base model that produces compact keys and values in a single forward pass. On Qwen and Gemma models, Still occupies the favorable side of the speed--quality frontier across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $200\times$ and context lengths from $8$k to $128$k. On the long-context RULER grid, Still exceeds the strongest baseline by 8--22 points. The same compact cache also supports free-form summarization, preserving most of the full-context gain on HELMET and winning a pairwise LongBench summarization comparison against KV-Distill. Because compaction is a forward pass, Still can be applied iteratively, entering a long-horizon regime unavailable to per-context methods. We show that amortization makes long-context cache compaction tractable, and synthesis makes its compact state useful at extreme compression.

</details>


### [326] [Breaking the Bubble: Asynchronous Pipeline Parallel Training with Bounded Weight Inconsistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07881)
*Itay Elam,Eliron Rahimi,Avi Mendelson,Chaim Baskin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACI is a bubble-free asynchronous pipeline parallelism method that bounds forward/backward version drift using local gradient accumulation, achieving up to 1.69x speedup over synchronous baselines without weight stashing or extra memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pipeline schedules trade off throughput, memory, and optimization consistency. Synchronous pipelines preserve weight consistency but have bubbles; asynchronous ones remove bubbles but introduce weight-version mismatch requiring complex mechanisms.

Method: PACI uses local gradient accumulation as a version-control mechanism to slow parameter-version evolution relative to pipeline delay, bounding the number of optimizer updates crossed by any micro-batch without weight stashing, prediction, or global synchronization.

Result: In GPT-style pretraining, PACI matches synchronous 1F1B-flush in stability and perplexity, retains the same peak memory, achieves full pipeline utilization, and improves time-to-accuracy by up to 1.69x.

Conclusion: Forward/backward inconsistency need not be eliminated; when explicitly bounded, it can be safely traded for substantial efficiency gains.

Abstract: Pipeline parallelism is essential for training large neural networks, but existing schedules trade off throughput, memory, and optimization consistency. Synchronous pipelines preserve forward/backward weight consistency but suffer from bubbles; asynchronous pipelines remove bubbles but introduce weight-version mismatch, typically requiring weight stashing, prediction, or correction mechanisms. We introduce PACI (Pipeline Asynchronous training with Controlled Inconsistency), a bubble-free asynchronous pipeline method that bounds forward/backward version drift without weight stashing, prediction, additional parameter copies, or global synchronization. The key idea is to use local gradient accumulation as a version-control mechanism: by slowing parameter-version evolution relative to pipeline delay, PACI limits the number of optimizer updates crossed by any micro-batch while preserving steady-state utilization. In GPT-style language-model pretraining, PACI matches the stability and final perplexity of synchronous 1F1B-flush, retains the same peak memory footprint, achieves fully utilized pipeline throughput, and improves training time-to-accuracy by up to $1.69\times$ over the fastest flush baseline. These results show that forward/backward inconsistency need not be eliminated: when explicitly bounded, it can be safely traded for substantial efficiency gains.

</details>


### [327] [Strained Coherence: A Pre-Failure Signal in Coding Agent Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07889)
*Marut Pandya,Kasey Zhang,Baiqing Lyu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM coding agents sometimes notice problems in their reasoning but proceed anyway ("strained coherence"). We define it, build a judge (Claude Sonnet 4.6) to detect it, and show flagged trajectories fail far more often (94% vs 46%). The detector outputs interpretable span-level evidence of conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and formalize a specific safety-relevant failure mode in LLM-based coding agents: when they acknowledge a flaw in their reasoning but act against it anyway, termed "strained coherence."

Method: Operationally define strained coherence; build a Claude Sonnet 4.6 judge that reads full trajectories and flags spans of interest; evaluate on Terminal-bench-2 trajectories with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B and Gemma4-31B backbones; compare against a lexical discourse-marker baseline.

Result: Flagged trajectories fail 94% vs 46% (47-point gap, p=0.003). At matched selectivity, 94% precision vs 88% baseline. Intersection of methods yields 100% failure rate. Replication on Gemma4-31B shows directionally consistent but non-significant results (20-point gap, p=0.31), attenuated by zero-think trajectories. First flag at median 83-84% of trajectory time.

Conclusion: Strained coherence is a detectable and statistically significant failure mode in LLM agents, with strong predictive power for task failure, and interpretable span-level output enables better understanding of agent behavior.

Abstract: LLM-based coding agents sometimes acknowledge a problem in their own reasoning and then proceed anyway. We call this pattern strained coherence: a safety-relevant failure mode in which an agent has information that should change its behavior, states that information, and still acts against it. The pattern overlaps with verbalized reward hacking, where an agent names a tension between a task proxy and the underlying goal yet optimizes the proxy anyway. We give an operational definition, build a Claude Sonnet 4.6 judge that reads full trajectories and flags spans where the pattern occurs, and evaluate it on 44 Terminal-bench-2 trajectories using a Qwen3.5-35B-A3B backbone. Flagged trajectories fail 94% of the time versus 46% for unflagged trajectories (47-point gap, Fisher's exact p = 0.003; 46 points after excluding three prompt-embedded examples, p = 0.006). At matched selectivity, the detector reaches 94% precision versus 88% for a lexical discourse-marker baseline; the 10-trajectory intersection of the two methods has a 100% failure rate (Clopper-Pearson 95% CI [69%, 100%]). We replicate on Gemma4-31B with 43 trajectories: the overall signal is directionally consistent but not significant (20-point gap, p = 0.31), with attenuation driven largely by 13 trajectories with zero think content, where the detector has no substrate to analyze. In the high-verbosity Gemma tertile, the gap is +30 points; in the mid- and high-verbosity Qwen tertiles, it is +40 points each. The first flag appears at a median of 83-84% of elapsed trajectory time across both models, and the binary flag survives paraphrases that soften explicit conflict markers (8/8 trajectories). Unlike univariate predictors, the detector emits interpretable span-level output -- quoted acknowledgment, quoted action, and typed conflict -- showing what the agent saw and ignored.

</details>


### [328] [Partially Performative Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07890)
*Jaewook Lee,Tijana Zrnic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces partially performative prediction, a framework that models distribution shift as arising from both endogenous (model-induced) and exogenous (time-varying) sources, extending performative prediction to dynamic environments and analyzing learning heuristics like repeated retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world distribution shifts are rarely purely endogenous (caused by the model itself) or purely exogenous (external changes). The authors aim to bridge these two perspectives by proposing a unified framework that captures both sources simultaneously.

Method: The paper generalizes performative prediction by allowing data distributions to evolve due to both model deployment and external time-varying processes. It defines online analogues of performative stability and performative optimality, and analyzes practical heuristics like repeated retraining.

Result: The authors extend central notions (performative stability/optimality) to online settings tracking partially performative environments, and characterize conditions under which repeated retraining successfully adapts to these mixed-source distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Partially performative prediction provides a more realistic modeling framework for distribution shift in consequential domains, and repeated retraining can be effective under certain conditions in these mixed endogenous-exogenous environments.

Abstract: Performative prediction studies feedback loops that arise when predictive models are deployed in consequential domains. In these settings, deploying a model can change the population whose patterns the model aims to predict, inducing a distribution shift that is endogenous to the learning system. This perspective departs from classical treatments of distribution shift, where shifts are typically modeled as exogenous changes in the data-generating process. Yet, in practice, distribution shift is rarely one or the other. Predictive models may influence future data through the decisions they support, while the world itself continues to drift for reasons beyond the learner's control. We study partially performative prediction, a framework that captures both endogenous and exogenous sources of distribution shift. The framework generalizes performative prediction by allowing the data distribution to evolve both in response to the deployed model and according to an external, time-varying process. We extend the central notions of performative stability and performative optimality to this setting by defining their online analogues that track the evolving partially performative environment. We analyze practical learning heuristics, including repeated retraining, and characterize when they successfully adapt to partially performative environments.

</details>


### [329] [Temporal Coverage over Density: Parsimonious Training-Set Design for ML Climate Downscaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07898)
*Karandeep Singh,Stefan Rahimi,Chad W. Thackeray,Stephen Cropper,Alex Hall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study evaluates three strategies for selecting training years for machine-learning downscalers under fixed computational budgets, finding that distributing training years across the full climate trajectory outperforms training on historical or endpoint data alone, and that broad sampling of internal variability is more valuable than temporal continuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution regional climate simulations are computationally expensive, yet critical for impacts assessments. A key challenge is how to best allocate limited high-resolution simulations across a changing climate trajectory to capture both forced climate response and internal variability.

Method: Using CESM2 Large Ensemble over the western US, the authors compare three training-year selection strategies under fixed data budgets: (1) contiguous historical years, (2) years from start and end of simulation, and (3) years distributed throughout the full climate trajectory.

Result: Training on temporally distributed years performs best, successfully reproducing variability in unseen ensemble members while retaining strong performance across climate diagnostics. Models using only one-tenth of available data remain competitive with full-data training.

Conclusion: Broad sampling of climate states is more valuable than temporal continuity under fixed computational budgets, providing practical guidance for regional climate downscaling and large-ensemble projection workflows.

Abstract: High-resolution regional climate simulations provide critical information for climate impacts assessments but remain computationally expensive, motivating the development of machine-learning downscalers and emulators. A key challenge is determining how limited high-resolution simulations should be distributed across a changing climate trajectory to capture both forced climate response and internal variability. Using the CESM2 Large Ensemble over the western United States, we compare three training-year selection strategies under fixed data budgets: a contiguous block of historical years, years drawn from both the beginning and end of the simulation period, and years distributed throughout the full climate trajectory. Including both historical and future years consistently outperforms training on historical years alone, demonstrating the importance of exposing downscaling models to climate states outside the historical record and highlighting limitations of stationarity assumptions common in statistical downscaling. Training on years distributed throughout the full climate trajectory performs best overall, indicating that broad sampling of internal variability provides additional information beyond exposure to the forced climate response alone. Models trained on temporally distributed subsets more successfully reproduce variability in unseen ensemble members while retaining strong performance across a wide range of climate diagnostics. Even when trained on only one-tenth of the available high-resolution years, temporally distributed models remain highly competitive with full-data training. These results suggest that, under fixed computational budgets, broad sampling of climate states is more valuable than temporal continuity when allocating scarce high-resolution simulations. The findings provide practical guidance for regional climate downscaling and large-ensemble projection workflows.

</details>


### [330] [Layer-wise Derivative Controlled Networks Achieve Competitive Accuracy and Gradient Stability Across Data Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07908)
*Rowan Martnishn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CR networks with DREG (derivative-controlled regularization) show strong generalization across tabular (Pima Diabetes) and NLP (SST-5) domains, outperforming baselines with statistically significant accuracy gains and exceptionally stable gradient tail ratios (~1.01-1.02 vs 1.07-1.09).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the generalization properties of derivative-controlled networks (CR) across different data regimes, domains, and representation qualities, and to establish gradient tail ratio as a label-free diagnostic for generalization capability.

Method: CR combines cubic polynomial layers with a forward-mode per-layer Jacobian penalty (DREG). The paper ablates DREG coefficient schedule shapes and evaluates CR on Pima Diabetes (tabular) and SST-5 (NLP) datasets under varying training data sizes (5%-100%), frozen-embedding and BERT fine-tuned regimes.

Result: CR achieves strong low-data performance on Pima Diabetes with consistent accuracy advantage across 5%-100% training data. Gradient tail ratios (~1.01-1.02) are significantly more stable than ReLU networks (1.07-1.09). On SST-5, CR outperforms prior BERT baselines with less training data. All results statistically significant (p<0.05).

Conclusion: Layer-wise derivative control induces a structural inductive bias toward low-frequency, stable representations that generalizes robustly across tabular and NLP domains, data volumes, and representation qualities. Gradient tail ratio is a reliable, label-free diagnostic of generalization.

Abstract: Derivative-controlled networks based on ChainzRule (CR) combine cubic polynomial layers with a lightweight forward-mode per-layer Jacobian penalty (DREG). In this second paper of a multi-part series, we evaluate the generalization properties of CR across data regimes.
  We ablate the shape of the DREG coefficient schedule, demonstrating that the optimal annealing range depends on representation noise. On the Pima Diabetes dataset, CR achieves strong low-data performance and maintains a consistent accuracy advantage over baselines from 5\% to 100\% training data, supported by exceptionally stable gradient tail ratios ($\sim$1.01--1.02 vs. 1.07--1.09 for ReLU networks). Extensions to SST-5 show competitive or superior results in both frozen-embedding and BERT fine-tuned regimes, including outperforming prior BERT baselines despite substantially less training data. These results are statistically significant: CR achieves superior accuracy over the strongest published baselines we could identify on both datasets ($p < 0.05$).
  These results establish that layer-wise derivative control induces a structural inductive bias toward low-frequency, stable representations that generalizes robustly across tabular and NLP domains, data volumes, and representation qualities. The gradient tail ratio serves as a reliable, label-free diagnostic of generalization capability.

</details>


### [331] [CAAL: Contextual Bandits based Online Hand-Craft Active Learning Strategy Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07910)
*Shao-An Yin,Jiacong Li,Tianpei Xie,Cecile Levasseur,Wojciech Kowalinski,Nicola Elia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAAL is a new active learning framework that dynamically selects hand-crafted strategies using reward prediction with external context information, outperforming existing adaptive strategies on public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active learning algorithms struggle with the uncertainty of unlabeled data distributions, making it difficult to choose the best hand-crafted strategy.

Method: CAAL treats each strategy as an "arm" and dynamically selects strategies for labeling batches using reward prediction with external context information, rather than relying solely on labeled data feedback.

Result: CAAL outperforms existing baseline adaptive strategies on public datasets, with consistent results regardless of batch size.

Conclusion: CAAL provides a general framework that can be customized with domain knowledge for more effective reward and context design.

Abstract: The challenge with active learning algorithms is the uncertainty of the statistical distribution of unlabeled data, making it difficult to choose the best hand-crafted strategy. To address this, we introduced Contextual Adaptive Active Learning (CAAL). In CAAL, each "arm" represents a hand-crafted strategy. Unlike existing frameworks that select strategies based only on feedback from labeled data, we dynamically choose strategies for labeling batches of data using reward prediction with external context information. This general framework allows for customization with domain knowledge to design more effective rewards and context candidates. In addition, we experimentally show that CAAL outperforms the existing baseline adaptive strategy on public datasets using our reward and context design. Our results are consistent regardless of batch size in each iteration.

</details>


### [332] [The Easy, the Hard, and the Learnable: Confidence and Difficulty-Adaptive Policy Optimization for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07950)
*Zhanke Zhou,Xiangyu Lu,Chentao Cao,Brando Miranda,Tongliang Liu,Bo Han,Sanmi Koyejo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies three inefficiencies in GRPO-based LLM reasoning training (confidence inflation, advantage contraction, hierarchical convergence) and proposes CoDaPO, a method that reweights and resamples training examples based on rollout confidence and empirical difficulty, improving accuracy across 12 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GRPO-style training treats easy, hard, and learnable questions equally via uniform sampling and weighting, leading to inefficient compute allocation. The authors aim to understand and fix these inefficiencies.

Method: CoDaPO (Confidence and Difficulty-adaptive Policy Optimization) assigns each question a bounded value from rollout confidence and empirical difficulty, then uses this value to reweight policy updates and resample high-value learnable questions within mini-batches.

Result: Across twelve benchmarks, CoDaPO consistently improves accuracy over existing RL methods.

Conclusion: Adaptive reweighting and resampling based on confidence and difficulty is an effective strategy for improving compute allocation in LLM reasoning RL training.

Abstract: RL with verifiable rewards can substantially improve LLM reasoning, yet standard GRPO-style training often treats easy, hard, and learnable questions alike through uniform sampling and weighting, leading to inefficient compute allocation. We study GRPO by tracking token log-probabilities, group-normalized advantages, and the induced token-level update weights. This reveals three recurring dynamics as training proceeds: (1) confidence inflation, (2) advantage contraction, and (3) hierarchical convergence. These findings suggest that the utility of each update depends strongly on both question difficulty and the model's current competence. Motivated by this, we propose Confidence and Difficulty-adaptive Policy Optimization (CoDaPO), which assigns each question a bounded value from rollout confidence and empirical difficulty. CoDaPO then uses this value to reweight policy updates and resample high-value learnable questions within mini-batches, thereby increasing discovery within the learnable band under a fixed compute budget. Across twelve benchmarks, CoDaPO consistently improves accuracy over existing RL methods. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/CoDaPO.

</details>


### [333] [Minibatch Selection via Partition Matroid Constrained Gradient Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07954)
*Prayas Agrawal,Prateek Chanda,Ishita Khatri,Ganesh Ramakrishnan,Bamdev Mishra,Pratik Jawanpuria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PartitionSel proposes a cross-domain minibatch selection method for LLM training that uses a validation-guided gradient-matching utility under partition-matroid constraints, achieving better convergence and reduced gradient conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing minibatch selection methods for heterogeneous LLM training either select samples independently within domains or rely on expensive proxy models, lacking cross-domain coordination to reduce redundancy.

Method: PartitionSel formulates a weakly submodular objective maximizing validation-guided gradient-matching utility under per-domain budget constraints (partition-matroid), solved via orthogonal matching pursuit with provable guarantees.

Result: Outperforms per-domain and domain-agnostic baselines on Qwen2.5/Llama-3 fine-tuning with MetaMathQA and Mol-Instructions; reduces conflicting gradient pairs in batches.

Conclusion: Cross-domain coupling via PartitionSel translates into more compatible training updates and robust gains over existing approaches.

Abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) on heterogeneous data requires selecting minibatches that balance convergence speed with coverage across domains. Existing methods either select samples independently within each domain or rely on computationally expensive proxy models to learn continuous domain weights. We propose PartitionSel, a cross-domain minibatch selection approach that maximizes a validation-guided gradient-matching utility under per-domain budgets encoded as a partition-matroid constraint. By coupling the per-domain budgets through a single utility, PartitionSel is designed to reduce redundancy in selections across domains. The proposed objective is weakly submodular and admits an orthogonal matching pursuit algorithm with provable approximation guarantees. Empirically, we evaluate PartitionSel for minibatch selection during the fine-tuning of Qwen2.5 and Llama-3 on MetaMathQA and Mol-Instructions. PartitionSel achieves robust gains over per-domain and domain-agnostic baselines on both benchmarks. It also reduces the number of conflicting gradient pairs within each batch, indicating that the cross-domain coupling translates into more compatible training updates.

</details>


### [334] [Overcoming the Limits of Finite Difference Method; Physics-Informed Neural Network for Noisy High-Dimensional Heat Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07982)
*Shreesh Bhattarai,Harish Chandra Bhandari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINN achieves ~91% accuracy under 20% boundary noise in 3D heat diffusion (vs. 36% for FDM) and shows a dimensionality-driven efficiency crossover, establishing PINN as the operational standard when noise and dimensionality are both high.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical numerical methods (e.g., FDM) suffer catastrophic accuracy degradation under noisy boundary conditions in high-dimensional heat diffusion, revealing a fundamental limitation that needs a systematic solution.

Method: Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework applied to 1D, 2D, and 3D transient heat diffusion with noisy boundary conditions, compared against FDM across accuracy and computational efficiency.

Result: Under 20% boundary noise in 3D: PINN ~91% accuracy vs. FDM ~36%. In a physical copper system, PINN reduces boundary reconstruction error by 3.3×. PINN requires fewer spacetime nodes than FDM in 3D for superior accuracy.

Conclusion: Solver selection should be jointly determined by noise exposure and dimensionality; PINN should be the operational standard in high-noise, high-dimensional regimes where classical methods are insufficient.

Abstract: High-dimensional transient heat diffusion under noisy boundary conditions exposes a fundamental limitation of classical numerical methods: accuracy degrades catastrophically where physical noise is unavoidable. This paper presents a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework as a systematic solution to this problem across one, two, and three spatial dimensions, establishing clear operational regimes that redefine solver selection in noisy thermal systems. Under 20% boundary noise in 3D, PINN sustains approximately 91% accuracy while Finite Difference Method (FDM) collapses to 36%, a clear decisive advantage. This is further confirmed in a physical copper thermal system, where PINN reduces boundary reconstruction error by 3.3 times under realistic noise conditions. This noise resilience is accompanied by a dimensionality-driven efficiency crossover: PINN requires fewer spacetime nodes than FDM in 3D while achieving superior accuracy, exposing the true cost of classical discretization at scale. These findings reframe solver selection: the decisive axis is not accuracy alone, but noise exposure and dimensionality jointly. When noise and dimensionality are both high, the classical solver paradigm is insufficient; this work provides the foundation to justify PINN as the operational standard in such regimes.

</details>


### [335] [Enhancing AI Interpretability and Safety through Localised Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07998)
*Ian Seet,Jonas Bozenhard,Simon Osterman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that localized hardware machine learning architectures with higher per-node expressivity but lower bandwidth could be more interpretable and computationally efficient than deep neural networks on GPU clusters, especially for smaller datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concerns over interpretability, safety, and sustainability of large, opaque AI models (LLMs, LRMs) running on GPU clusters motivate the search for alternative architectures.

Method: The authors draw an analogy from localized ML models (more interpretable/efficient on small datasets) to localized hardware architectures, then evaluate hardware ML paradigms based on per-node expressivity, energy efficiency, and technological maturity.

Result: Localized hardware architectures with lower bandwidth but higher expressivity per node may be fundamentally more interpretable than deep neural networks on GPUs, while remaining competitive for smaller datasets.

Conclusion: Localized hardware architectures present a promising direction for improving interpretability and efficiency, but their practical maturity needs further assessment.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI, especially powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), raise concerns over the interpretability, safety and sustainability of these large and opaque AI models. The power of such architectures is derived not only from the scalability of deep neural networks, but also massively parallel hardware such as GPU clusters. The diffuse nature of deep neural networks gives them great function-approximation capability when provided with sufficient training data but imposes a cost in interpretability and computational efficiency. Observing that localised machine learning (ML) models tend to be more interpretable and computationally efficient than deep neural networks on small datasets, we reason by analogy that similar advantages may apply to specific localised hardware ML architectures. We argue that localised architectures with lower bandwidth but higher expressivity per node have the potential to be fundamentally more interpretable than deep neural networks running on GPU clusters while remaining competitive for smaller datasets. We then evaluate the suitability of various hardware ML paradigms for implementing such localised architectures and evaluate their per-node expressivity, energy efficiency and practical maturity of the technology required.

</details>


### [336] [Evaluating the Impact of Task Granularity on Catastrophic Forgetting in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08013)
*Emre Alyamac,Himanshu Janmeda,Shashwat Krishna,Yash Vijay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether learning coarse categories before fine-grained classes reduces catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. Experiments on CIFAR-100 compare Coarse-to-Fine, Fine-to-Coarse, and Flat training orders using EWC, hypothesizing that general-first learning creates a stable foundation for knowledge retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting is the central challenge in Continual Learning. The authors aim to understand if the order of learning (coarse-to-fine vs. fine-to-coarse vs. flat) affects how well models retain previously acquired knowledge.

Method: Three approaches are tested on CIFAR-100: (1) Coarse-to-Fine — train on 2 super-classes then expand to 10 sub-classes, (2) Fine-to-Coarse — train on 10 sub-classes then group into 2 super-classes, (3) Flat — train on all 10 classes from start. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) is used to mitigate forgetting during transitions.

Result: Evaluation uses standard metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) plus continual learning metrics like backward transfer and forgetting rates. Results are not explicitly stated in the abstract but are expected to inform optimal learning sequence design.

Conclusion: The work could inform how to design learning sequences for real-world systems that need to learn incrementally, potentially showing that learning general patterns first creates a stable foundation for retaining knowledge.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting, the abrupt loss of previously acquired knowledge upon learning new information, remains the central challenge in Continual Learning. This project investigates whether the order in which a model learns information affects how well it retains knowledge. Specifically, we ask: does learning general categories first (like "animals" vs "vehicles") before learning specific classes (like "dog" vs "cat") reduce forgetting compared to learning all classes at once?
  We test three approaches on CIFAR-100: (1) Coarse-to-Fine: train on 2 super-classes, then expand to 10 specific sub-classes, (2) Fine-to-Coarse: train on 10 sub-classes, then group into 2 super-classes, and (3) Flat: train on all 10 classes from the start. We use Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) to prevent forgetting during transitions. Our hypothesis is that learning general patterns first creates a stable foundation that helps the model retain knowledge when learning more detailed distinctions. We evaluate using standard metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) plus continual learning metrics like backward transfer and forgetting rates. This work could inform how we design learning sequences for real-world systems that need to learn incrementally.

</details>


### [337] [Semantic Quorum Assurance: Collective Certification for Non-Deterministic AI Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08021)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SQA introduces a multi-agent validation framework for LLM-driven cloud operations that reduces unsafe mutation approvals from 18.5% to 0.3% through diverse validator panels and risk-adaptive quorum logic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents executing infrastructure mutations (e.g., IAM changes, security group modifications) can produce syntactically valid but operationally unsafe actions, which classical consensus protocols fail to detect since they only ensure deterministic state replication, not semantic safety.

Method: Proposals are represented as declarative execution contracts with cryptographic evidence chains, routed to a diverse panel of sandboxed validator agents. A risk-adaptive quorum predicate enforces model/archetype diversity, adjusts weights via calibrated assurance scores, and respects vetoes. Admitted proposals pass through a sovereign execution gate.

Result: On 500 infrastructure mutation scenarios, SQA reduces unsafe approval from 18.5% (single-agent) to 0.3%, with median validation latency of 1.45-4.12 seconds across risk buckets.

Conclusion: Semantic Quorum Assurance effectively addresses the semantic reliability gap in agentic infrastructure operations by providing a formal, diversity-enforcing validation mechanism with strong safety guarantees and acceptable latency overhead.

Abstract: As large language model (LLM) agents are integrated into autonomous cloud operations, distributed systems face a semantic reliability problem: proposer agents can generate production mutations, such as modifying IAM policies, opening firewall security groups, or executing data exports, that are syntactically valid and statically authorized but operationally unsafe. Classical distributed consensus protocols replicate deterministic state transitions but do not evaluate the safety of the proposed intent. To address this gap, we introduce Semantic Quorum Assurance (SQA), a control-plane primitive for governing non-deterministic agentic infrastructure. SQA represents proposals as declarative execution contracts bound to cryptographic evidence chains and routes them to a diverse panel of read-only, sandboxed validator agents. SQA aggregates their judgments under a risk-adaptive quorum predicate that enforces model and archetype diversity, adjusts weights based on calibrated assurance scores, and respects archetype-specific vetoes. Admitted proposals execute only through a sovereign execution gate. We instantiate SQA in a cloud-native control plane and formalize a correlated cognitive failure model for non-deterministic validators. On 500 infrastructure-inspired mutation scenarios, with safety results reported on held-out safe/unsafe trials excluding ambiguous scenarios, SQA reduces unsafe approval from 18.5% for single-agent validation to 0.3% while adding median validation latency of 1.45--4.12 seconds across the studied risk buckets.

</details>


### [338] [CausShield: Sample Reconstruction-Resilient Vertical FL via Causal Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08027)
*Yongqi Jiang,Yansong Gao,Siguang Chen,Anmin Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausShield uses causal decomposition to split VFL representations into task-relevant and task-irrelevant components, providing strong privacy protection against reconstruction attacks while maintaining model utility and convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VFL defenses fail to balance model utility and privacy protection — they either suppress task-relevant information alongside privacy features or rely on supervised training that leaves early epochs vulnerable to reconstruction attacks.

Method: CausShield adopts a structural causal model (SCM) insight, theoretically proving that causal features are task-relevant while non-causal features encode sample-specific private info. It decomposes shared representations into these two components via unsupervised representation learning, formulated as a carefully designed optimization problem.

Result: Outperforms 7 SOTA methods (including InvL at USENIX Security'25 and URVFL at NDSS'25) across privacy protection, model utility, and computational efficiency. Theoretically proven to preserve VFL convergence behavior.

Conclusion: CausShield achieves full-cycle privacy protection in VFL by leveraging causal decomposition, achieving a superior trade-off between utility and privacy without sacrificing convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Vertical federated learning (VFL) is a distributed learning paradigm that leverages vertically partitioned features across isolated parties without sharing raw samples; however, it remains vulnerable to active sample reconstruction attacks. Existing defenses fail to achieve a satisfactory trade-off between model utility and privacy protection, due to either suppressing task-relevant information alongside privacy-sensitive features or relying on end-to-end supervised training to converge the defense module, which exposes the model to early-epoch vulnerability. To address this challenge, we adopt a structural causal model (SCM) insight and construct CausShield. From a task-learning standpoint, causal features within a raw sample are those that are directly relevant and contributory to the learning objective, whereas non-causal features are task-irrelevant but often encode sample-specific private information, thereby facilitating reconstruction. Importantly, we lay a theoretical foundation to prove this insight. CausShield thus decomposes the shared representations between the client and the coordinating server in VFL into task-relevant and task-irrelevant components to ensure full-cycle privacy protection. Nonetheless, the decomposition is inherently challenging due to the dual objectives of preserving model utility while mitigating privacy leakage. We address this via a carefully formulated optimization problem, which is solved through unsupervised representation learning. We further theoretically prove that CausShield preserves the convergence behavior of standard VFL. Extensive experiments compare CausShield against seven SOTAs, including InvL (USENIX Security'25), and evaluate robustness against advanced reconstruction attacks such as URVFL (NDSS'25). Results demonstrate that CausShield consistently outperforms in privacy protection, model utility, and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [339] [Noise-Adaptive High-Probability Regret Bounds for Online Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08028)
*Wentao Zhang,Yutong Zhang,Wentao Mo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper resolves open questions in strongly convex online optimization by proving: (1) noise-adaptive high-probability regret bounds for full-information settings using a novel exponential supermartingale argument, (2) a minimax lower bound showing bandit feedback incurs linearly worse confidence cost than full information, and (3) simultaneous high-probability guarantees for constrained OCO with stochastic constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve open questions at the intersection of noise adaptivity, feedback structure (full-information vs. bandit), and constraint satisfaction in strongly convex online optimization, particularly regarding high-probability regret bounds.

Method: The authors develop an exponential supermartingale argument that bypasses the bounded-difference requirement of Freedman's inequality, enabling direct treatment of unbounded sub-Gaussian noise. They also provide minimax lower bound analysis for bandit feedback and derive simultaneous high-probability guarantees for constrained OCO.

Result: (1) Noise-adaptive regret improving by G/σ over Azuma-Hoeffding baseline for full-information. (2) Minimax lower bound: bandit regret scales as log(1/δ) vs √log(1/δ) for full information, showing formal separation. (3) Constrained OCO: O(√T log(m/δ)) regret and O(√T/(ζδ) + m√T log(m/δ)) violation.

Conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental limits and optimal algorithms for high-probability regret in strongly convex OCO across different feedback structures, with synthetic experiments confirming theoretical predictions.

Abstract: We study high-probability regret bounds for online convex optimization (OCO) with strongly convex losses and establish three results that resolve open questions at the intersection of noise adaptivity, feedback structure, and constraint satisfaction. For the full-information setting with sub-Gaussian stochastic gradients, we prove a noise-adaptive high-probability regret bound in which the martingale deviation term scales with the noise level $σ$ rather than the gradient bound $G$, yielding a multiplicative improvement of $G/σ$ over the classical Azuma-Hoeffding baseline. Our analysis introduces an exponential supermartingale argument that bypasses the bounded-difference requirement of Freedman's inequality, enabling direct treatment of unbounded sub-Gaussian noise without truncation artifacts. For bandit feedback, we prove a minimax lower bound: the high-probability regret scales linearly in $\log(1/δ)$, in contrast to the $\sqrt{\log(1/δ)}$ confidence cost under full information. This constitutes a formal separation in the confidence cost of strongly convex OCO across feedback models. Regarding constrained OCO with stochastic constraints satisfying a Slater condition, we provide simultaneous high-probability guarantees for both cumulative regret and long-run constraint violation, achieving $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T\log(m/δ)})$ regret and $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T}/(ζδ) + m\sqrt{T\log(m/δ)})$ violation. Synthetic experiments corroborate all theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [340] [SafeECGMatch: Calibration-Aware Joint Frequency and Time Space Semi-Supervised Learning for Open-Set ECG Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08037)
*Hongkyu Koh,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SafeECGMatch is a calibration-aware semi-supervised learning framework for ECG classification that handles out-of-distribution anomalies in unlabeled data through dual-branch architecture, adaptive label smoothing, and temperature scaling to achieve reliable OOD rejection and state-of-the-art accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ECG classification faces severe label scarcity, and standard SSL methods fail when unlabeled data contains out-of-distribution anomalies or unseen diagnostic classes, producing overconfident and incorrect pseudo-labels.

Method: Dual-branch architecture extracting time-frequency latent representations via ECG-specific augmentations; adaptive label smoothing and temperature scaling to dynamically align confidence with empirical accuracy; joint optimization of multiclass classifier and OOD detector across temporal and spectral domains.

Result: Evaluated on PTB-XL and PhysioNet/CinC Challenge benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and calibration.

Conclusion: SafeECGMatch advances reliable knowledge discovery in physiological time-series by enabling trustworthy OOD rejection and reliable pseudo-labeling under label distribution mismatch.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) classification models often suffer from severe label scarcity, making semi-supervised learning (SSL) an attractive strategy for reducing annotation costs. In clinical settings, however, unlabeled pools frequently contain out-of-distribution (OOD) anomalies or diagnostic groups absent from the labeled set. Standard SSL forces incorrect pseudo-labels onto these unseen classes, producing overconfident predictions. To address this, we propose SafeECGMatch, a calibration-aware safe SSL framework for single-label ECG classification under label distribution mismatch. Methodologically, SafeECGMatch employs a dual-branch architecture extracting time-frequency latent representations via ECG-specific augmentations. Crucially, it dynamically aligns confidence with empirical accuracy through adaptive label smoothing and temperature scaling, calibrating both the multiclass classifier and the OOD detector across temporal and spectral domains. This joint optimization allows trustworthy OOD rejection and reliable pseudo-labeling. Evaluated on the PTB-XL and PhysioNet/CinC Challenge benchmarks, SafeECGMatch achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and calibration, advancing reliable knowledge discovery in physiological time-series. Code is available at https://github.com/labhai/SafeECGMatch.

</details>


### [341] [When Behavioral Safety Evaluation Fails: A Representation-Level Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08044)
*Enyi Jiang,Anders Gjølbye,Yibo Jacky Zhang,Sanmi Koyejo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies the "audit gap" between behavioral safety and latent robustness in LLMs, proposes a Latent Vulnerability Score (LVS) to measure representation-level vulnerability under soft interventions, and demonstrates that models with safe surface behavior can still be internally vulnerable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM safety evaluations focus on behavioral outputs, ignoring representation-level robustness. The authors formalize this oversight as the "audit gap" and argue that safety assessments must go beyond observable behavior to capture latent vulnerabilities.

Method: The authors construct dissociated models that maintain safe outputs but remain vulnerable in latent space. They introduce an intervention-based evaluation framework using soft interventions (harmful fine-tuning and layer-wise latent perturbations) and propose the Latent Vulnerability Score (LVS) to quantify how easily harmful behavior can be elicited by bounded latent perturbations.

Result: Behavioral safety metrics are insufficient indicators of representation-level robustness. Dissociated models show significantly elevated LVSs despite maintaining safe refusal behavior under harmful intervention, with intermediate representations being the most sensitive to intervention.

Conclusion: Behavioral safety evaluation alone provides an incomplete picture of model robustness. The findings motivate representation-aware audits that assess both latent vulnerability and observable behavior for a more holistic safety assessment.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) safety has often been evaluated at the behavior level, which provides limited evidence of internal robustness, as these evaluations target outputs rather than representation-level vulnerability under intervention. We formalize this discrepancy as the audit gap: the difference between behavioral safety and robustness under intervention. To study this gap, we construct dissociated models that preserve safe outward behavior while remaining vulnerable in the latent space. We introduce an intervention-based evaluation framework to test model robustness through soft interventions in parameter and latent spaces, including harmful fine-tuning and layer-wise latent perturbations. To formalize the evaluation, we propose the Latent Vulnerability Score (LVS) to measure how easily harmful behavior can be elicited by bounded latent perturbations. Using this evaluation framework, we show that behavioral safety metrics are insufficient measures of representation-level robustness across multiple safely and unsafely aligned state-of-the-art models. Notably, dissociated models show substantially elevated LVSs despite comparable refusal behavior under harmful intervention, with intermediate representations being the most sensitive to intervention. Our results suggest that behavioral safety evaluation alone provides an incomplete picture of model robustness, motivating representation-aware audits of latent vulnerability and observable behavior.

</details>


### [342] [Beyond Homophily: Towards Generalized Graph Reconstruction Attack and Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08067)
*Zhanke Zhou,Bo Han,Xuan Li,Jiangchao Yao,Sanmi Koyejo,Michael K. Ng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies graph reconstruction attacks (GRA) on GNNs, analyzing when training adjacency can be leaked through various outputs, and proposes both improved attack (MC-GRA+) and defense (MC-GPB+) methods based on a Markov chain approximation view of GNN inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs can leak sensitive training graph adjacency information (e.g., social ties, transactions). The paper aims to systematically characterize when and why adjacency becomes recoverable from a trained GNN, and to develop both effective attacks and defenses.

Method: The authors propose a Markov chain approximation lens for GNN inference, treating layered forward computation as a chain of topology-dependent representations. They develop MC-GRA+ attack (optimizing a surrogate adjacency to match representations at each layer) and MC-GPB+ defense (suppressing adjacency-dependent information throughout the chain while preserving accuracy).

Result: Experiments on homophilic/heterophilic graph benchmarks and various GNNs show that MC-GRA+ improves reconstruction fidelity over prior methods, and MC-GPB+ defense reduces reconstruction success with only minor accuracy loss.

Conclusion: The paper provides systematic understanding of adjacency leakage in GNNs modulated by graph homophily, heterophily, and model inductive bias, and offers effective attack/defense tools for graph privacy.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely deployed on relational data, yet they can leak sensitive or proprietary information about the training graph adjacency, e.g., social ties, transactions, and interactions. This work studies graph reconstruction attacks (GRA), a form of model inversion that reconstructs the training adjacency from a trained GNN, given different levels of attacker-side information. We first provide a systematic characterization of when and why adjacency becomes recoverable through features, labels, embeddings, and predictions, with leakage modulated by graph homophily, heterophily, and the model's inductive bias. Motivated by these findings, we view GNN inference through a Markov chain approximation lens, treating the layered forward computation as a chain of topology-dependent representations. Building on this view, we develop complementary attack and defense methods. On the attack side, we propose MC-GRA (+), which reconstructs the adjacency by optimizing a surrogate adjacency whose GNN-induced representations align with those of the target model at each layer. On the defense side, we propose MC-GPB (+), which suppresses adjacency-dependent information throughout the representation chain while aiming to preserve classification accuracy under a privacy-utility trade-off. Experiments across homophilic/heterophilic graph benchmarks and GNNs show that our attacks improve reconstruction fidelity over prior methods, while our defenses reduce reconstruction success with only minor accuracy loss.

</details>


### [343] [DICE: Entropy-Regularized Equilibrium Selection for Stable Multi-Agent LLM Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08068)
*Yi Xie,Zhanke Zhou,Chentao Cao,Bo Liu,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies equilibrium selection problems in multi-agent LLM systems as a source of instability, formalizes them as Markov games, proposes Heterogeneous Quantal Response Equilibrium (HQRE) for well-posed coordination, and introduces DICE algorithms (DICE-PC and DICE-FT) that achieve significant accuracy improvements across 11 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems often fail to reliably outperform a single strong model with best-of-N sampling. The authors argue this instability stems from ill-posed equilibrium selection — current systems specify information sharing but not coordination conventions, leading to oscillation and drift between competing conventions.

Method: The authors formalize multi-agent LLM systems as discounted incomplete-information Markov games. They introduce Heterogeneous Quantal Response Equilibrium (HQRE), an entropy-regularized equilibrium with agent- and state-dependent temperatures. Under monotonicity, HQRE is unique and yields convergent mirror updates. Two algorithms are instantiated: DICE-PC (coordinating frozen models via prompt-control) and DICE-FT (parameter-efficient mirror fine-tuning).

Result: Across 11 benchmarks in 4 domains, DICE improves accuracy-cost trade-offs over strong baselines. DICE-PC improves by 4.3 percentage points and DICE-FT by 8.5 percentage points on reasoning and planning tasks on average.

Conclusion: The proposed HQRE framework and DICE algorithms provide a theoretically grounded solution to equilibrium selection instability in multi-agent LLM systems, yielding measurable improvements in coordination and performance.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems often fail to reliably outperform a single strong model equipped with best-of-N sampling. We argue that a core source of this instability is ill-posed equilibrium selection: current systems specify what information agents share, but not which coordination convention should be selected. We formalize a broad class of such systems as discounted incomplete-information Markov games and show that two common pathologies, oscillation between competing conventions and drift across them, can both induce unstable learning and linear Bayesian regret. To obtain a well-posed target, we introduce the Heterogeneous Quantal Response Equilibrium (HQRE), an entropy-regularized equilibrium concept with agent- and state-dependent temperatures. Under a monotonicity condition, HQRE is unique, admits linearly convergent mirror updates, and yields bounded Bayesian regret; the same condition yields rollout-measurable stability diagnostics. We instantiate this objective in two algorithms: DICE-PC, which coordinates frozen models through prompt-control actions, and DICE-FT, which performs parameter-efficient mirror fine-tuning. Across eleven benchmarks in four domains, DICE improves accuracy-cost trade-offs over strong within-class baselines; on reasoning and planning tasks, DICE-PC improves by 4.3 percentage points on average and DICE-FT by 8.5 points.

</details>


### [344] [ConSteer-RL: Steering Reasoning Capabilities in Large Language Models via Confidence-Aware Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08088)
*Qing Miao,Yiming Zhao,Jing Yang,Chenxi Liu,Yuehai Chen,Yuewen Liu,Shaoyi Du,Badong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ConSteer-RL enhances RLVR for LLMs by incorporating token-level confidence signals from log-probabilities into GRPO, using confidence-aware rewards to penalize overconfident errors and reward correct confident reasoning, achieving 2.3%-4.0% improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR for improving LLM reasoning is limited by sparse binary rewards and ignorance of model-internal uncertainty.

Method: Integrates token-level confidence signals from model log-probabilities into GRPO by aggregating per-token probabilities into a scalar confidence score, then using awareness-based reward shaping that penalizes overconfident errors while reinforcing correct and confident reasoning.

Result: ConSteer-RL consistently outperforms strong GRPO baselines with average improvements of 2.3%-4.0% across different model scales.

Conclusion: Confidence-aware reward shaping effectively addresses the limitations of sparse binary rewards in RLVR for improving LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has recently become a key paradigm for improving the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it remains limited by sparse binary rewards and its ignorance of model-internal uncertainty. In this paper, we propose ConSteer-RL, a simple yet effective framework that integrates token-level confidence signals derived from model log-probabilities into RLVR training. Specifically, building upon the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework, we construct a confidence-aware reward by aggregating per-token probabilities into a scalar confidence score and incorporating it into an awareness-based reward shaping mechanism that penalizes overconfident errors while reinforcing correct and confident reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that ConSteer-RL consistently outperforms strong GRPO baselines, achieving average improvements of 2.3%-4.0% across different model scales.

</details>


### [345] [Constraint-Aware Optimization for Robust Protein Stability Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08100)
*A Shivram,Aneesh S. Chivukula,Manik Gupta,Sourav Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A constraint-aware optimization framework (with Balanced MSE, Siamese anti-symmetric regularizer, and OOD-margin consistency loss) improves multimodal ΔΔG predictors on the SPURS backbone without architectural changes, boosting Spearman correlations on S669 (0.486→0.540) and S461 (0.653→0.711), though anti-symmetric training alone doesn't eliminate forward-reverse bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal ΔΔG predictors achieve strong in-distribution accuracy but suffer from limited robustness on OOD proteins, forward-reverse bias on paired mutations, and under-representation of rare stabilizing mutations. Prior work addresses these via architectural modifications; optimization-level intervention remains underexplored.

Method: A constraint-aware optimization framework combining: (1) Balanced Mean Squared Error, (2) Siamese anti-symmetric regularizer, and (3) OOD-margin consistency loss on per-position features. Applied to the SPURS backbone without architectural changes. Tested across 11 benchmarks with 3 random seeds.

Result: Spearman correlation improved on S669 (0.486→0.540, σ=0.002) matching the published SPURS baseline (0.50), and S461 (0.653→0.711). Consistent smaller gains on five additional OOD datasets. Diagnostic on Ssym showed anti-symmetric training doesn't eliminate forward-reverse bias; gains come from implicit regularization.

Conclusion: Optimization-level intervention can effectively improve multimodal ΔΔG predictor robustness without architectural changes, though the anti-symmetric regularizer acts via implicit regularization rather than exact thermodynamic constraint enforcement.

Abstract: Multimodal $ΔΔG$ predictors integrating protein language models with inverse-folding representations achieve strong in-distribution accuracy on the Megascale dataset but exhibit limited robustness on out-of-distribution (OOD) proteins, persistent forward-reverse bias on paired-mutation benchmarks, and under-representation of rare stabilizing mutations. Existing approaches address these limitations primarily through additional architectural components, leaving optimization-level intervention comparatively underexplored. We introduce a constraint-aware optimization framework combining Balanced Mean Squared Error, a Siamese anti-symmetric regularizer, and a novel OOD-margin consistency loss on the per-position feature representation, requiring no architectural changes to the SPURS backbone. Across eleven benchmarks and three random seeds, the framework improves Spearman correlation on S669 from 0.486 to 0.540 ($σ=0.002$ across seeds), matching the published SPURS baseline (0.50) without architectural modification, and on S461 from 0.653 to 0.711, with consistent smaller gains on five additional OOD datasets. A controlled diagnostic on Ssym reveals that anti-symmetric training does not eliminate systematic forward-reverse bias, indicating that gains arise through implicit regularization rather than exact thermodynamic constraint enforcement.

</details>


### [346] [A Unifying View of Attention Sinks: Two Algorithms, Two Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08105)
*Lukas Fesser,Mozes Jacobs,Thomas Fel,Andy Keller,Sham Kakade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attention sinks can reflect two distinct mechanisms: adaptive nop (suppressing updates) and broadcast (aggregating/redistributing information). These require different interventions (gating vs. registers), and combining both yields better results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand that visually similar attention sink patterns can hide fundamentally different algorithms, and to clarify what the model is actually computing when attention concentrates on a single token.

Method: The authors formalize two distinct sink mechanisms (nop and broadcast) on synthetic tasks, derive practical diagnostics (e.g., nop sinks have negligible value norms; broadcast sinks induce low-rank outputs), and apply these diagnostics to pretrained vision transformers.

Result: Both mechanisms exist at scale in vision transformers: sinks transition from CLS in early layers to patches in deeper layers. Register tokens designed for broadcast are repurposed for nop, showing neither intervention alone suffices; combining gating with registers yields complementary gains.

Conclusion: Effective intervention requires first diagnosing what the model is actually computing, as the same attention pattern can reflect different computations.

Abstract: When attention concentrates on a single token, a sink, what is the model actually computing? Attention sinks are ubiquitous in softmax transformers, yet this shared visual signature can hide fundamentally different algorithms. We show that visually similar sink patterns can reflect two distinct mechanisms: {i} adaptive nop, where a head suppresses its update by routing to a null token, and {ii} broadcast, where a sink aggregates and redistributes global information. In that case, sinks serve an analogous role: a safe destination when there is nothing useful to compute. Proposed interventions like gating or registers work because they implicitly target one or the other, revealing a duality between method and assumed mechanism: gating implicitly assumes nop; registers implicitly assume broadcast. Each mechanism leaves distinct traces (nop sinks exhibit negligible value norms; broadcast sinks induce low-rank outputs) which we formalize on synthetic tasks and use to derive practical diagnostics. Applied to pretrained vision transformers, these diagnostics reveal that both mechanisms exist at scale: sinks transition from CLS in early layers to patches in deeper layers, and concentrate in specialized heads. Strikingly, register tokens, designed for broadcast, are repurposed to also serve nop, confirming that neither intervention alone suffices. Combining gating with registers yields complementary gains in stability and performance. Overall, we find that the same attention pattern can reflect two very different computations and effective intervention requires first asking what the model is actually computing.

</details>


### [347] [Conditional Random Ordered Transport Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08113)
*Lei Luo,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Conditional Random Ordered Transport Spaces (CROTS) for distributional learning that accounts not just for distance between probability laws but also for whether transformations respect directional constraints (causal, physical, semantic, etc.). It establishes foundational theory including duality, convergence, measurability, and stability, showing that Wasserstein convergence can coexist with separate order-risk recursion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Wasserstein distance fails to capture whether mass transport respects admissible directions (e.g., causality, physical laws, monotonicity). In evidence-constrained learning, one must certify not only proximity but admissibility under available information.

Method: CROTS: a class of L^0-valued spaces of random probability measures with a Wasserstein ambient metric, closed stochastic order, hard/soft ordered transport discrepancies, and a conditional risk functional for order violation under an evidence sigma-field.

Result: Well-posedness and duality for hard/soft ordered transport, soft-to-hard variational convergence, measurability/completeness, reductions to classical geometries, ordered geodesics, constrained barycenters, conditional risk-transport duality, and separation of order-violating distributions.

Conclusion: The stability theorem shows learning dynamics can converge in Wasserstein while admissibility leakage follows a separate order-risk recursion, providing a language for evidence overreach, ordered distribution shift, robustness failure, and admissible distributional dynamics.

Abstract: A small Wasserstein distance does not certify that a transformation is admissible. In evidence-constrained, semantic, causal, physical, monotone, or risk-sensitive learning, one must ask not only how far two probability laws are, but whether mass has moved in a direction allowed by available information. We introduce conditional random ordered transport spaces (CROTS), a class of \(L^0\)-valued spaces of random probability measures equipped with a Wasserstein ambient metric, a closed stochastic order, hard and soft ordered transport discrepancies, and a conditional risk functional for evaluating order violation under an evidence sigma-field. The central object is an order-admissible transport geometry for random measure-valued dynamics, distinct from cone-valued metrics, ordered Kantorovich constructions, random Wasserstein spaces alone, and model-specific residuals for generative paths. We develop the foundations of CROTS as a space theory for reliable distributional learning. The results include well-posedness and duality for hard and soft ordered transport, soft-to-hard variational convergence, measurability and completeness of the random lifted space, reductions to classical Wasserstein and ordered geometries, ordered geodesics, constrained barycenters and projections, conditional risk-transport duality, and separation of order-violating distributions. The main stability theorem shows that random learning dynamics may converge in the ambient Wasserstein metric while its local admissibility leakage follows a separate conditional order-risk recursion. The resulting asymptotic order-risk floor provides a mathematical language for evidence overreach, ordered distribution shift, robustness failure, and admissible distributional dynamics.

</details>


### [348] [TRUST-SCF: Transformer-based Risk Understanding and Scoring for Transactional Supply Chain Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08140)
*Mohammadamin Davoodabadi,Amirabbas Shakeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRUST-SCF is a transformer-based framework for dynamic credit scoring in Supply Chain Finance that predicts repayment delays from transaction sequences and generates credit scores without explicit credit score labels, showing strong performance on real transaction data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing credit scoring systems in SCF and LendTech fail to adapt to evolving transaction behavior, repayment delays, and active exposure changes, necessitating a more dynamic approach.

Method: The framework uses (1) a transformer with financially aligned attention bias combining utilization similarity and recency, (2) log-transformed continuous repayment-delay prediction, and (3) a label-efficient pipeline deriving credit scores from predicted delay, simulated utilization risk, unpaid exposure, and nonlinear calibration without external credit labels.

Result: Experiments on 300,000+ real transactions show improved delay prediction over sequential baselines, and generated scores strongly associate with future repayment behavior.

Conclusion: TRUST-SCF is a practical framework for adaptive credit scoring and transaction-level risk mitigation in SCF and LendTech environments.

Abstract: Supply Chain Finance (SCF) and LendTech platforms need credit scoring systems that respond to evolving transaction behavior, repayment delays, and active exposure. We propose TRUST-SCF, a transformer-based framework for transaction-level risk prediction and dynamic credit scoring. Each user history is represented as a sequence of transaction tokens containing utilization, repayment delay and transaction position.
