The main contributions are: (1) a financially aligned attention bias that combines utilization similarity and recency, enabling the model to compare repayment behavior under comparable exposure conditions; (2) continuous repayment-delay prediction in a log-transformed target space, reducing the influence of extreme delays while improving sensitivity to short-delay behavior and (3) a label-efficient credit-scoring pipeline in which the final credit score is not trained using any explicit external credit-score label, but is instead derived from predicted delay, potential risk over simulated utilization, actual unpaid exposure, and nonlinear calibration. Experiments on real transaction data from more than 300,000 transactions show that TRUST-SCF improves delay prediction over sequential baselines and produces scores that are strongly associated with future repayment behavior. These results suggest that TRUST-SCF is a practical framework for adaptive credit scoring and transaction-level risk mitigation in SCF and LendTech environments.

</details>


### [349] [LogNEO: A GPT-Neo Reinforcement Learning Framework for Accurate Real-Time Log Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08153)
*David Eje,Tanmay Sharma,Khush Patel,Manuel Mazzara,Leonard Johard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LogNEO is a log anomaly detector using GPT-Neo (1.3B) fine-tuned with PPO and a novel position-aware reward scheme, achieving state-of-the-art F1-scores on HDFS (0.927), BGL (0.913), and Thunderbird (0.984) with 45ms latency at 15K events/sec.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting anomalies in large-scale system logs is critical for infrastructure reliability and security; prior methods like LogGPT have limitations in recall.

Method: Fine-tunes GPT-Neo (1.3B) using PPO with a partial-credit, exponentially decaying position-aware reward scheme and cross-entropy regularization.

Result: F1-scores of 0.927 (HDFS), 0.913 (BGL), 0.984 (Thunderbird); recall improved by up to 6pp over LogGPT; 45ms latency at 15K events/sec.

Conclusion: LogNEO achieves state-of-the-art anomaly detection with practical latency for production deployment via Kafka, Redis, and TensorRT acceleration.

Abstract: Detecting anomalies in large-scale system logs is critical for the reliability and security of modern computing infrastructure. We present LogNEO, a log anomaly detector built on EleutherAI's GPT-Neo (1.3B parameters) and fine-tuned with a novel partial-credit, exponentially decaying position-aware reward scheme combined with cross-entropy regularisation via Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO). The position-aware reward explicitly models prediction difficulty: early positions receive higher rewards for correct predictions, while later positions incur stronger penalties for errors. LogNEO attains F1-scores of 0.927, 0.913, and 0.984 on the HDFS, BGL, and Thunderbird benchmarks, improving recall by up to 6 percentage points over the prior state-of-the-art LogGPT while maintaining comparable precision. A production microservice deployment over Apache Kafka, Redis, and TensorRT-accelerated inference demonstrates 45 ms end-to-end latency at 15,000 events per second.

</details>


### [350] [Have I Solved This Before? Retrieving Similar Segmentation Problems for Evolutionary Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08155)
*Andreas Margraf,Henning Cui,Jörg Hähner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes shifting monitoring-system development from algorithm design to deeper problem analysis, using an abstract system model to collect knowledge incrementally and enable reuse of filter pipelines across similar image segmentation tasks, reducing costly retraining and improving design efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional monitoring-system development requires early design decisions under high uncertainty and expensive model training from scratch. The authors aim to reduce costs and risks by reusing knowledge across similar problems.

Method: The authors propose a knowledge-driven approach that gradually collects and stores knowledge in an abstract system model, enabling retrieval of similar solutions for new use cases. They analyze cross-domain transferability of filter pipelines for image segmentation problems.

Result: The study statistically analyzes the benefits of this transfer learning variant for image segmentation, showing that reusing previously generated pipelines reduces the risk of late revisions and that simple models help balance complexity, technical requirements, and reliability.

Conclusion: Shifting focus from algorithm design to problem analysis, combined with incremental knowledge collection and pipeline reuse, offers a promising direction for more efficient monitoring-system development, particularly for image segmentation tasks.

Abstract: Reliable integration and solid configuration of monitoring systems constitute a fundamental prerequisites for achieving high efficiency and productivity in contemporary manufacturing environments. Design decisions on sensor type and system architecture have to be made at an early stage and under comparably high uncertainty. This work investigates a research direction that deviates from the traditional monitoring-system development process by shifting the attention from algorithm design to a deeper analysis of the inspection problem. In contrast to traditional design cycles, this paper proposes to gradually collect knowledge and store it in an abstract system model. This enables the retrieval of similar solutions for future use cases, preventing the need for expensive model training from scratch and allowing instead for the incremental refinement of existing base configurations. Reuse of previously generated pipelines reduces the risk of late and costly revisions. As there is little knowledge on cross-domain transferability of filter pipelines, this study analyzes the potential of retrieving filter pipelines to transfer them to different but similar segmentation problems. Finally, we statistically analyze the benefits of this `transfer learning' variant which is predominantly applied to image segmentation problems. In addition, we discuss how simple models help balancing the trade-off between complexity, technical requirements, and reliability in the design process.

</details>


### [351] [AttentionCap: Transformer Based Capacitance Matrix Learning Toward Full-Chip Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08161)
*Jiechen Huang,Hector R. Rodriguez,Dingcheng Yang,Zuochang Ye,Yibo Lin,Wenjian Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AttentionCap is a Transformer-based model for 2D capacitance extraction that uses a Gram representation, symmetric-attention output, normalized Laplacian loss, and process-node embedding, achieving 0.67%/3.99% error on unseen real designs with 192× faster inference and strong transferability to new process nodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rule-based pattern matching for capacitance extraction struggles at advanced nodes, and existing MLP/CNN-based deep learning models are limited to fixed metal-layer combinations in a single process node, lacking flexibility and practical usability.

Method: Proposes AttentionCap, a customized Transformer with: (1) a Gram representation framework, (2) a physics-aligned symmetric-attention output layer, (3) a normalized Laplacian loss, and (4) a process-node embedding for multi-node learning.

Result: 0.67%/3.99% self/coupling-capacitance error on unseen real designs under multi-layer and multi-node settings; 4.6×/5.7× lower error than CNN-Cap; 192× faster inference; accurate transfer to unseen node with only 5K samples and 4K finetuning steps.

Conclusion: AttentionCap offers high accuracy on unseen real designs and strong transferability to new process nodes, providing significant practical value for modern EDA workflows.

Abstract: As capacitance extraction accuracy of rule-based pattern matching becomes difficult to sustain at advanced nodes, a growing trend emerges to develop deep-learning-based 2D capacitance models. However, existing MLP- and CNN-based methods constrain their input to fixed metal-layer combinations in a specific process node, limiting their usability in practice. Recognizing the inherent similarity between capacitance matrix and the prevailing attention mechanism, we propose AttentionCap, a customized Transformer for capacitance matrix learning, with a Gram representation framework, a physics-aligned symmetric-attention output layer, and a novel normalized Laplacian loss. We also introduce a process-node embedding to enable multi-node learning. Trained on synthetic data, AttentionCap attains 0.67\%/3.99\% self/coupling-capacitance error on unseen real designs under a multi-layer and multi-node setting, surpassing the CNN-Cap baseline with 4.6$\times$/5.7$\times$ lower self/coupling error and 192$\times$ faster inference speed. A pretrained AttentionCap accurately transfers to an unseen node with only 5K samples and 4K finetuning steps. With sufficient accuracy on unseen real designs and strong transferability to new process nodes, AttentionCap offers highly practical value for modern EDA workflows. Code and data are available at https://github.com/THU-numbda/AttentionCap.

</details>


### [352] [Explaining Data Mixing Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08167)
*Rui Dai,Shuran Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a theoretical framework explaining data mixing scaling laws by modeling domain overlap on shared skills and identifying Capacity Competition and Noise Reduction as key factors, outperforming empirical baselines with fewer parameters and successfully extrapolating to unseen scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing empirical scaling laws can predict model performance on multi-domain data mixtures, but a theoretical understanding of these loss behaviors is absent.

Method: Extends theoretical perspectives from standard neural scaling laws (Kaplan, Chinchilla) to multi-domain settings, assuming domains overlap on fundamental skills but diverge on specialized skills. Identifies Capacity Competition (finite model capacity couples domain losses globally) and Noise Reduction (optimal weights shift toward harder-to-learn domains).

Result: Outperforms existing baselines with lower Mean Relative Error, identifies higher-performing training mixtures, successfully extrapolates to large unseen scales using parameters fitted on smaller ones, and achieves this with significantly fewer parameters.

Conclusion: The proposed theoretical framework effectively explains and predicts data mixing loss behaviors, offering a more parsimonious and scalable alternative to empirical scaling laws.

Abstract: Recent research has established empirical scaling laws to predict model performance on multi-domain data mixtures. However, a theoretical understanding of these model loss behaviors remains absent. In this work, we propose a unified framework to explain the underlying mechanics of data mixing. Our approach extends theoretical perspectives originally developed for standard neural scaling laws (e.g., Kaplan and Chinchilla) to the multi-domain setting. Based on the distributional assumption that domains overlap on fundamental skills while diverging on specialized skills, we identify two key factors that govern the domain losses of models trained on different data mixtures: \textit{Capacity Competition}, where the allocation of finite model capacity couples domain losses globally, and \textit{Noise Reduction}, where optimal weights shift toward harder-to-learn domains to minimize overall noise. Empirical evaluations show that our framework outperforms existing baselines by fitting the loss landscape with a lower Mean Relative Error and identifying higher-performing training mixtures. Most importantly, our model successfully extrapolates across scales, predicting highly effective mixtures for large, unseen scales using parameters fitted on smaller ones. In addition, our model achieves these results using significantly fewer parameters compared to previous empirical laws. Our code is available at https://github.com/meiqwq/Explaining-Data-Mixing-Scaling-Laws.

</details>


### [353] [Frequency-Domain Latent Attention Gating for Cross-Domain Token Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08191)
*Kewei Li,Rongying Zhang,Xueli Wang,Xiwen Gong,Zhongjian Wang,Lan Huang,Ruochi Zhang,Fengfeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLaG is a plug-in aggregation module using FFT-based spectral transformation with learnable latent queries and gating, showing strong gains on AMP prediction and CIFAR-100 while remaining competitive on text tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token aggregation is a bottleneck in mapping token representations to sample-level predictions; existing pooling methods are limited to the original token domain and lack spectral awareness.

Method: FLaG transforms token representations using real FFT, summarizes spectral components with learnable latent queries, applies channel-wise gating, and reconstructs enhanced time-domain tokens for final pooling.

Result: FLaG achieves strongest gains on ESM2-8M AMP tasks and CIFAR-100, and remains competitive on IMDB and GLUE text benchmarks. Low-frequency bands contribute most overall; higher-band patterns are more sample-specific.

Conclusion: FLaG is an effective plug-in pooling module that leverages spectral information to improve sample-level predictions, with meaningful frequency-band behaviors and cross-attention patterns.

Abstract: Token aggregation is a common bottleneck in models that map token representations to sample-level predictions, yet most pooling methods operate only in the original token domain. We propose FLaG, a plug-in aggregation module that transforms token representations with the real FFT, summarizes spectral components with learnable latent queries, applies a channel-wise gate, and reconstructs enhanced time-domain tokens for final pooling. We evaluate FLaG on antimicrobial peptide (AMP) activity prediction with ESM2, image classification with ResNet18 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, and text classification with RoBERTa on IMDB and GLUE. FLaG achieves its clearest gains on the ESM2-8M antimicrobial peptide tasks and on CIFAR-100, while remaining competitive with strong text baselines on IMDB and GLUE. Then we probe its behavior on the AMP setting with band knockouts, gate summaries, residue perturbations, latent-query readouts, and structure-proxy stratification. We find that low-frequency bands contribute the most overall, and the remaining higher-band pattern is more sample-specific. The gate acts as a broadly shared spectral reweighting stage and the cross-attention patterns are sample-specific with mild query-wise differentiation, and higher-helix peptides exhibit stronger average spectral sensitivity in both bacteria. The supplementary materials, source code and data are released at https://www.healthinformaticslab.org/supp/ and https://github.com/Kewei2023/AMPCliff/tree/FLaG.

</details>


### [354] [Autonomous Aerial Manipulation via Contextual Contrastive Meta Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08533)
*Lixuan Jin,Bingxuan Lan,Xinyi Bao,Xiangyuan Xie,Chunjie Zhang,Zheng Chen,Tianshuo Liu,Ruijie Tian,Jinyu Ru,Gang Wang,Lei Yuan,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents Aco2, a meta-reinforcement learning framework enabling autonomous aerial delivery of diverse payloads via a quadrotor with a hook, using a contextual encoder and contrastive learning to adapt online without manual calibration or system identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing aerial delivery methods assume pre-attached payloads or specialized grippers, and cannot handle the highly variable flight dynamics induced by different payloads without manual calibration or explicit system identification, leaving versatile end-to-end aerial delivery largely unresolved.

Method: Aco2 uses (1) a contextual observation encoder that infers a compact latent context from recent interaction history for online adaptation, and (2) a contrastive objective that structures the context embedding around task-relevant variations to improve generalization without explicit system identification. The policy is trained entirely in simulation with domain randomization.

Result: Trained entirely in simulation with extensive domain randomization, Aco2 can be directly deployed on a physical quadrotor without real-world fine-tuning, successfully performing autonomous pickup, transport, and delivery of diverse handle-equipped objects.

Conclusion: The contextual contrastive meta-RL approach enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for autonomous aerial manipulation with diverse payloads, eliminating the need for manual calibration or system identification.

Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly being deployed in logistics, service robotics, and other real-world applications, creating a growing demand for autonomous payload acquisition and delivery. Existing approaches typically assume pre-attached payloads or rely on specialized grippers, leaving versatile end-to-end aerial delivery largely unresolved, where different payloads induce highly variable flight dynamics, requiring a single policy to adapt online without manual calibration or explicit system identification. To this end, we study \textbf{A}utonomous \textbf{A}erial Manipulation via \textbf{Co}ntextual \textbf{Co}ntrastive Meta Reinforcement Learning (\textbf{\textit{Aco2}}), a fully autonomous aerial delivery setting in which a quadrotor equipped with a lightweight hook continuously picks up, transports, and delivers diverse handle-equipped objects between randomized locations, all without human intervention. First, we design a contextual observation encoder that infers a compact latent context from recent interaction history, enabling the policy to adapt online to payload-dependent dynamics. To further improve the quality of this context, we introduce a contrastive objective that structures the context embedding around task-relevant variations, improving generalization across diverse payloads without requiring explicit system identification. Trained entirely in simulation with extensive domain randomization, \textit{Aco2} can be directly deployed on a physical quadrotor without real-world fine-tuning.

</details>


### [355] [Neural Field Tokenizations with Hierarchy and Spatial Locality Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08204)
*Alonso Urbano,David W. Romero,Max Zimmer,Sebastian Pokutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LH-NeF proposes a locality-preserving hierarchical encoder for neural fields that replaces memory-intensive meta-learning inner loops with a single forward pass, using 42× less memory and enabling 133× larger batches while matching or exceeding baselines across images, 3D shapes, and climate fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural field approaches are dominated by per-sample meta-learning, which scales poorly due to memory-intensive inner-loop optimization. Feed-forward alternatives typically introduce modality-specific assumptions, sacrificing the generality that makes neural fields attractive.

Method: LH-NeF uses a locality-preserving hierarchical encoder that maps raw coordinate-value field observations to structured tokens, from which the field is reconstructed during training, replacing meta-learning's inner loop with a single forward pass.

Result: Uses 42× less memory and supports 133× larger batches than the strongest modality-agnostic baseline. Matches or exceeds performance of modality-agnostic, modality-specific, and specialized generative neural field baselines on both reconstruction and downstream tasks across images, 3D shapes, and climate fields.

Conclusion: Locality and hierarchy are useful priors for learning field representations that can be injected without compromising modality-agnosticism, enabling efficient and general-purpose tokenized representations of continuous signals.

Abstract: Neural fields parameterize data as functions from coordinates to values, providing a unified framework for representation learning across modalities. Existing approaches are dominated by per-sample meta-learning, which scales poorly due to memory-intensive inner-loop optimization. The natural alternative -- feed-forward encoding -- typically introduces modality-specific assumptions, sacrificing the generality that makes learning with neural fields attractive. We argue that locality and hierarchy are useful priors for learning field representations that can be injected without compromising modality-agnosticism. We propose LH-NeF, a framework to learn general-purpose tokenized representations of continuous signals. A locality-preserving hierarchical encoder maps raw coordinate-value field observations to structured tokens, from which the field is reconstructed during training. By replacing meta-learning's inner loop with a single forward pass, LH-NeF uses 42$\times$ less memory and supports 133$\times$ larger batches than the strongest modality-agnostic baseline. Across images, 3D shapes, and climate fields, our learned representations match or exceed performance of modality-agnostic, modality-specific, and specialized generative neural field baselines on both reconstruction and downstream tasks.

</details>


### [356] [Public Machine Learning Solver Framework for Novices in the Machine Learning Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08212)
*Lokman Saleh,Hafedh Mili,Mounir Boukadoum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new platform that combines AutoML cheat sheets and decision-support systems to help non-experts solve ML problems by recommending complete, tailored pipelines rather than single algorithms, using expert-defined criteria, transfer learning, and first-order logic reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solving ML problems is complex and typically reserved for experts; existing systems for non-experts (fully automated AutoML, cheat sheets, decision-support systems) have limitations, such as recommending only single algorithms instead of complete pipelines.

Method: The platform combines categories 2 (expert cheat sheets) and 3 (decision-support systems), uses first-order logic to reason over a knowledge base, integrates expert-defined criteria with transfer learning, automatically extracts data characteristics (e.g., class imbalance, missing values), recommends ranked pipelines, and connects to a crowdsourcing platform for continuous expert updates.

Result: The platform is the first free, publicly accessible online framework that systematically captures and operationalizes expert knowledge to guide non-experts in solving ML problems in a structured, transparent manner. It is built incrementally, allowing seamless integration of new algorithms, criteria, and domain knowledge.

Conclusion: The proposed platform effectively bridges the gap between fully automated systems and static cheat sheets, providing semi-automated, transparent, and pipeline-level recommendations for non-expert ML users.

Abstract: Solving machine learning problems is complex and typically reserved for experts. Over the past two decades, systems have emerged to support non-experts. Based on our review, we identify three categories: (1) fully automated AutoML systems, (2) expert cheat sheets for algorithm selection, and (3) decision-support systems using selection criteria (accuracy, transparency, data requirements). We propose a new platform combining categories 2 and 3 to deliver semi-automated, intelligent solution recommendations for non-experts. Unlike existing approaches that recommend a single algorithm, our platform suggests a complete pipeline tailored to the user's problem. It integrates expert-defined selection criteria with transfer learning and automatically extracts data characteristics (e.g., class imbalance, missing values) from user-provided datasets. The platform uses first-order logic to reason over its knowledge base and recommends suitable algorithms ranked by relevance. It features a user-friendly interface and connects to a crowdsourcing platform for ML experts, ensuring continuous updates. The platform is built incrementally, allowing seamless integration of new algorithms, criteria, and domain knowledge. To our knowledge, this is the first free, publicly accessible online framework that systematically captures and operationalizes expert knowledge to guide non-experts in solving ML problems in a structured, transparent manner.

</details>


### [357] [How Deep Are Deep GPs, Really? A Sharp Threshold and a Non-Gaussian Limit for Compositional GPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08218)
*Mark Kozdoba,Shie Mannor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a sharp bandwidth threshold $r_c(d) = Θ(\sqrt{d})$ for deep Gaussian process priors, below which the prior converges to non-degenerate, non-Gaussian limit distributions with complex multimodal behavior, and above which it degenerates to constant functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the limiting behavior of deep Gaussian process priors as depth grows, particularly identifying whether non-trivial (non-degenerate) limits exist beyond the known degenerate regime.

Method: Theoretical analysis establishing a sharp bandwidth threshold $r_c(d) = Θ(\sqrt{d})$ for deep GP priors, combined with empirical verification across dimensions and visualization of the limit distributions.

Result: Identified a sharp threshold $r_c(d) = Θ(\sqrt{d})$; for $r < r_c(d)$ the prior converges to non-degenerate, non-Gaussian limit distributions with non-vanishing inter-coordinate dependence; for $r > r_c(d)$ it degenerates to constant functions.

Conclusion: Deep Gaussian process priors can admit non-trivial limits when bandwidth is below the threshold, contrasting with previously known degenerate regime, though the non-degenerate regime becomes increasingly narrow in high dimensions.

Abstract: Compositional priors describe the generic properties of layered functions in deep Bayesian models, where deep neural networks with random weights are a canonical example.In the wide-network limit, the prior is a Gaussian process with a depth-dependent kernel, and its behaviour as depth grows has been extensively studied through this kernel. Here, we study another case, where each layer itself is a vector valued Gaussian process, and our aim is similarly to understand the limiting behaviour of the prior as depth grows.
  Previous GP work has established that for the RBF kernel and a certain range of bandwidths $r$, the prior degenerates in the limit, converging to the set of constant functions -- which is not useful as a probabilistic model. In this paper we establish several new results. First, we identify a sharp bandwidth threshold $r_c(d) = Θ(\sqrt{d})$ above which the limit is degenerate, strengthening the earlier bounds. Second, and more importantly, we show that for $r$ below the threshold $r_c(d)$ the prior converges to a limit distribution $π_{\bar{Z}}$. We also prove that these distributions are non-degenerate and non-Gaussian, with non-vanishing dependence between coordinates. In contrast to the previously known degenerate regime, deep Gaussian process priors can therefore admit non-trivial limits.
  Empirically, we verify the threshold across a range of dimensions $d$, and demonstrate a complex multimodal behaviour of the limit distributions $π_{\bar{Z}}$ -- a regime that becomes increasingly narrow with $d$ and would be hard to identify without knowing the threshold.

</details>


### [358] [De novo molecular generation with optical property preconditioning at the token level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08221)
*Haozhe Huang,Manuel Gonzalez Lastre,Hyun Suk Park,Jorge A. Campos-Gonzalez-Angulo,Xinjian Liu,Alán Aspuru-Guzik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks a token-conditioned GPT-2 model for generating OLED molecules with targeted optical properties in a low-data regime, finding that while the model reproduces training distributions and shows directional control, controllability varies significantly across chemical motifs—with aryl nitriles exhibiting systematic red-shifting and reduced reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing OLED molecules with targeted optical properties is challenging due to limited high-quality data and unreliable conditional control in generative models across different chemical motifs.

Method: A GPT-2 model is pretrained on large chemical corpora, augmented with discrete property tokens (vertical absorption energy, oscillator strength, HOMO-LUMO gap), and fine-tuned via multi-task optimization in a low-data regime. Generated molecules are evaluated at the TDDFT level.

Result: The model reproduces dominant optical properties of training data while shifting toward lower molecular weight. Token-level control is directionally consistent but not fully orthogonal. Controllability varies by chemotype—conjugated aromatic-carbon motifs show better target satisfaction, while aryl nitriles exhibit systematic red-shifting and reduced control.

Conclusion: Quantitative benchmarks for conditional OLED generation must assess reliability in chemically meaningful subspaces rather than aggregate property distributions alone.

Abstract: Designing OLED molecules with targeted optical properties remains challenging due to the scarcity of high-quality data and the limited reliability of conditional control in generative models across chemical motifs. Here, we benchmark a token-conditioned autoregressive language model for OLED molecular generation in a realistic low-data regime. A GPT2 model is pretrained on large chemical corpora, augmented with discrete property tokens, and fine-tuned using multi-task optimisation. Conditioning targets vertical absorption energy and oscillator strength, with the HOMO-LUMO gap included as an auxiliary electronic descriptor.
  Generated molecules are evaluated at the TDDFT level to assess distributional fidelity and controllability. The generated library reproduces the dominant optical-property support of the training distribution while shifting towards lower molecular weight and fewer heavy atoms. Token-level control is consistently directional across conditioning bins, but is not fully orthogonal and exhibits local calibration irregularities. A chemotype-resolved analysis further shows that controllability depends strongly on local electronic environments: moderately conjugated aromatic-carbon motifs are associated with improved joint target satisfaction, whereas electron-withdrawing motifs, particularly aryl nitriles, show systematic red-shifting and reduced controllability.
  These results establish a quantitative benchmark for conditional OLED molecular generation and show that model reliability must be assessed in chemically meaningful subspaces rather than from aggregate property distributions alone.

</details>


### [359] [C$^3$ache: Accelerating World Action Models with Cross Inference Chunk Cache](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08962)
*Weisen Zhao,Lam Nguyen,Zhicong Lu,Yuzhang Shang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: C^3ache accelerates World Action Models by caching and reusing residuals across inference chunks (not just within a chunk), achieving up to 2.5× speedup with negligible performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WAMs generalize better than standard VLA policies but are computationally expensive due to multi-chunk denoising. Existing acceleration methods only reduce redundancy within a single chunk, overlooking redundancy across chunks.

Method: C^3ache is a training-free method that caches and reuses residuals computed at the same denoising step across consecutive inference chunks, exploiting the strong correlation between residuals during smooth robot behavior.

Result: Up to 2.5× speedup in total wall-clock inference time on benchmarks with Fast-WAM backbone, with negligible degradation in task success rate.

Conclusion: Cross-chunk redundancy is a significant and previously overlooked source of computation waste in WAMs. C^3ache effectively reduces this waste without training or sacrificing performance.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) generalize better than standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies to novel motions and environments, because a video-modeling objective lets them learn from abundant unlabeled video rather than scarce labeled robot demonstrations. This generalization is computationally expensive. To complete a task, a WAM runs over multiple inference chunks, and each chunk requires a costly denoising process. Existing acceleration methods reduce this cost by caching and reusing computation within a single chunk's denoising trajectory. Our empirical analysis reveals a substantial source of redundancy they overlook: redundancy across chunks. When a robot executes a smooth behavior, the residuals computed at a given denoising step are strongly correlated from one chunk to the next. We introduce C$^3$ache, a training-free method that caches and reuses these residuals across inference chunks at the same denoising step. Experiments on benchmarks with a Fast-WAM backbone show that C$^3$ache achieves up to a $2.5\times$ speedup in total wall-clock inference time, with negligible degradation in task success rate.

</details>


### [360] [GPT-Micro: A large language paradigm for accelerated, inexpensive, and thermodynamics-consistent discovery of constitutive models in manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08238)
*Soumik Dutta,Kiarash Naghavi Khanghah,Sania Shree,Logan McNeil,Thomas Feldhausen,Hongyi Xu,Rajiv Malhotra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces GPT-Micro, an autonomous framework that combines Large Language Models with thermodynamics constraints and sparse data to discover de-novo constitutive material models. It achieves over 70% data reduction versus ML, 400X faster discovery than human-driven modeling, and produces physically trustworthy, interpretable models validated on a printed electronics testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current constitutive model discovery relies on human intuition (slow, incremental) or conventional ML (high data costs), while existing LLM approaches often ignore fundamental thermodynamics laws. The authors aim to create an autonomous, data-sparse, and thermodynamics-compliant method.

Method: GPT-Micro integrates semantic knowledge extraction from literature, thermodynamics-based conservation law enforcement, sparse datasets, and LLM-driven hypothesis generation and refinement to discover de-novo constitutive models without subjective human starting hypotheses.

Result: Validation on a printed electronics constitutive modeling problem shows: (a) >70% data reduction vs. ML with no accuracy loss; (b) 400X faster discovery (months→hours) vs. human-driven modeling; (c) novel functional forms without human bias; (d) compact, conservation-compliant, interpretable models.

Conclusion: GPT-Micro demonstrates potential for rapid, low-cost, physically trustworthy, and interpretable microstructure modeling across manufacturing, overcoming key limitations of both human-driven and conventional ML approaches.

Abstract: Constitutive modeling of the relationship between process-imposed material states and fundamental material properties is critical to control of material microstructure in manufacturing processes. The limited accuracy resulting from the typical reliance on fallible human expertise and intuition for postulation and revision of the models functional form results in incremental and time consuming model discovery. Conventional Machine Learning (ML) incurs significant cost and time of data generation. Model discovery using Large Language Models (LLMs) suffers from the above issues and/or ignores the inviolability of fundamental thermodynamics laws. This work creates a novel GPT-Micro paradigm for autonomous, data sparse, and thermodynamics-compliant discovery of de-novo constitutive models. This framework seamlessly integrates semantic knowledge extraction from literature, enforcement of thermodynamics-based conservation laws, and sparse datasets, with LLM-driven generation and refinement of model hypotheses. Validation is performed for a long-intractable constitutive modeling problem in a printed electronics process testbed. This reveals significant and simultaneous advantages over the state-of-the-art including: (a) More than 70 percent reduction in data burden relative to ML-based modeling without loss in accuracy; (b) 400X reduction in discovery time after data generation, from months to hours, relative to human-driven modeling; (c) Discovery of models with novel functional forms without subjective human choice of a starting hypothesis; (d) Enhanced physics-rooted trustworthiness, human interpretability, and mechanistic insight via synthesis of compact, conservation-compliant, and physically complete analytical models. The potential of GPT-Micro to realize rapid, low-cost, physically trustworthy, and interpretable microstructure modeling across the manufacturing landscape is discussed.

</details>


### [361] [Differentially Private Synthetic Data via APIs 4: Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08259)
*Toan Tran,Arturs Backurs,Zinan Lin,Victor Reis,Li Xiong,Sergey Yekhanin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tab-PE extends the Private Evolution framework to generate differentially private synthetic tabular data, using lightweight heuristic operators (instead of expensive foundation models) to handle high-order correlations, achieving up to 10% better accuracy than AIM while running 28x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DP synthetic tabular data methods focus on low-order marginal queries and struggle with high-order correlations; the authors aim to address this gap by adapting the PE framework for tabular data.

Method: Tab-PE iteratively evolves candidate datasets using tabular-specialized heuristic operators (variation, private scoring, selection), replacing the large foundation models used in original PE with computationally efficient operators.

Result: Tab-PE significantly outperforms prior baselines on datasets with high-order correlations; compared to AIM (best baseline), it improves classification accuracy by up to 10% and runs 28 times faster.

Conclusion: Tab-PE makes the PE framework practical and scalable for tabular data, effectively handling high-order correlations under DP constraints.

Abstract: This paper investigates the problem of generating synthetic tabular data with differential privacy (DP) guarantees, enabling data sharing in sensitive domains. Despite extensive study, state-of-the-art methods often focus on minimizing low-order marginal query errors and overlook the challenges posed by high-order correlations. To address this gap, we extend the Private Evolution (PE) framework, originally developed for DP-compliant image and text synthesis, to tabular data. We introduce Tab-PE -- an algorithm for synthetic tabular data generation under DP constraints. Tab-PE iteratively improves a candidate dataset via an evolutionary process that leverages tabular-specialized operators to produce variations, privately scores them, and selects the highest-quality samples to retain and propagate. In contrast to the original PE, which relies on large foundation models, Tab-PE employs heuristic operators with significantly lower computational costs, making PE more practical and scalable for tabular data. Through extensive experiments on real-world and simulation datasets, we demonstrate that Tab-PE substantially outperforms prior baselines on datasets exhibiting high-order correlations. Compared to the best baseline -- AIM, Tab-PE improves classification accuracy by up to 10% while running 28 times faster.

</details>


### [362] [Causal Semantic Alignment for LLM-based Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08262)
*Kexuan Zhang,Xiaobei Zou,Cesare Alippi,Gary G. Yen,Yang Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CVAformer is a variable-level alignment framework for LLM-based time series forecasting that disentangles each variable into invariant and dynamic components and applies causal intervention to remove spurious correlations caused by dynamic confounders, replacing standard causal attention with non-causal attention for better cross-variable interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based time series methods overlook the heterogeneous nature of time series where dynamic fluctuations and invariant semantics are entangled, causing spurious correlations during alignment as dynamic components act as confounders.

Method: CVAformer disentangles each variable into invariant and dynamic components before alignment, applies causal intervention to mitigate confounding, and replaces standard causal attention with non-causal attention to capture variable-level interactions at each time step.

Result: Matches or exceeds state-of-the-art performance on long-term, short-term, few-shot, and zero-shot forecasting; achieves notably better accuracy in some cases.

Conclusion: Variable-level alignment and dynamic disentanglement are effective, offering a new perspective for LLM-based time series tasks.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have opened new possibilities for time series forecasting by enabling alignment between temporal patterns and pretrained word embeddings. However, most LLM-based methods overlook the heterogeneous nature of time series, where dynamic fluctuations and invariant semantics are entangled. This entanglement introduces spurious correlations during the alignment, as dynamic components act as confounders by simultaneously influencing invariant components and the resulting aligned embeddings. To address this issue, a variable-level alignment framework CVAformer is proposed. CVAformer explicitly disentangles each variable into invariant and dynamic components just before alignment, and applies causal intervention to mitigate the confounding effect of the dynamics. To better support variable-level alignment, CVAformer replaces the standard causal attention in LLMs with a non-causal attention mechanism that captures interactions among variables at each time step. Extensive experiments across long-term, short-term, few-shot, and zero-shot forecasting settings indicate that CVAformer matches or exceeds state-of-the-art performance on most datasets, and in some cases achieves notably better accuracy. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of variable-level alignment and dynamic disentanglement in CVAformer, offering a new perspective for LLM-based time series tasks.

</details>


### [363] [Safe-RULE: Safe Reinforcement UnLEarning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09559)
*Shixiong Jiang,Taozheng Zhu,Fanxin Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Safe-RULE, a defense framework using reinforcement unlearning to remove poisoned data influence in offline safe RL without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline safe RL is vulnerable to data poisoning attacks where adversaries inject malicious samples, compromising safety and inducing unsafe behaviors in safety-critical systems like robotics.

Method: Introduces Safe-RULE (Safe Reinforcement Unlearning), a defense framework that removes poisoned data influence without retraining from scratch or requiring access to the original training environment, while accounting for both task performance and safety constraints.

Result: Experiments on benchmark Safe RL tasks show the approach effectively enhances safety performance against data poisoning attacks.

Conclusion: Reinforcement unlearning is a viable defense mechanism for offline safe RL against data poisoning, improving safety without full retraining.

Abstract: Offline safe reinforcement learning (Safe RL) enables policy learning without online interactions, making it suitable for safety-critical systems such as robotics systems. However, its reliance on static datasets exposes offline Safe RL to data poisoning attacks, where adversaries inject malicious samples that compromise safety and induce unsafe policy behavior. In this work, we propose a new learning paradigm, named safe reinforcement unlearning (Safe-RULE), used as a defense framework to remove the influence of poisoned data without retraining from scratch or requiring access to the original training environment. We further extend reinforcement unlearning to offline Safe RL by explicitly accounting for both task performance and safety constraints during the unlearning process. Experiments across benchmark Safe RL tasks demonstrate that our approach effectively enhances safety performance against data poisoning attacks.

</details>


### [364] [Causal Agent Replay: Counterfactual Attribution for LLM-Agent Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08275)
*Jaineet Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAR identifies which step caused an LLM agent failure by modeling the run as a causal model, intervening on steps, and re-executing to measure outcome changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tools only show what happened (observability) or whether it passed (evaluation), but cannot pinpoint which step caused a failure. Heuristics fail because the harmful action step often differs from the decision step, and LLM-judge attribution is unreliable (only ~14% accuracy on Who&When).

Method: CAR models an agent run as a structural causal model, applies do-operations to individual steps, re-executes trajectories forward under the same stochastic policy, and measures distribution shifts. It introduces a contrastive estimator with a point-of-commitment rule to resolve confounds and a budget-bounded Monte-Carlo Shapley estimator for interacting steps.

Result: Contrastive estimator recovers the pivotal step in synthetic SCMs; Shapley recovers two-step interactions (0.44, 0.45, ~0; efficiency sum 0.909 vs analytic 0.91).

Conclusion: CAR provides principled, intervenable causal attribution with confidence intervals for LLM agent failures, and is open-source.

Abstract: When an LLM agent fails -- issues a refund it should not have, calls the wrong tool, leaks data -- existing tooling answers what happened (observability) or whether it passed (evaluation), but not which step caused the failure. The obvious heuristics are wrong: the step that executes the harmful action is usually not the step that decided on it, and LLM-judge attribution is correlational and unreliable (state-of-the-art step-level accuracy on the Who&When benchmark is about 14%). We present Causal Agent Replay (CAR), which answers the question by intervention: it models an agent run as a structural causal model, applies a do-operation to a step, and re-executes the trajectory forward under the same stochastic policy, measuring the shift in the outcome distribution. We define an intervention algebra over agent steps, a single-step contrastive estimator whose point-of-commitment rule resolves a confound specific to stochastic run-forward, and a budget-bounded Monte-Carlo Shapley estimator that splits credit across interacting steps. Every effect is reported with confidence intervals. We validate against synthetic structural causal models with planted ground truth: the contrastive estimator recovers the pivotal step, and Shapley recovers a two-step interaction (0.44, 0.45, ~0; efficiency sum 0.909 versus the analytic 0.91). CAR is open source and runs on hosted or free local models.

</details>


### [365] [Mesh Graph Neural Network Framework for Accelerating Finite Element Simulation for Arbitrary Geometries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08287)
*Josiah D. Kunz,Kamal Choudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mesh graph network (MGN) with rotation- and translation-invariant features predicts von Mises stress fields in 2D structures with varying hole geometries, outperforming conventional ML models (R² ≥ 0.97 vs. 0.01–0.86) and enabling generalization to unseen geometries without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite element analysis (FEA) is computationally expensive for multiple design iterations or load scenarios. Existing ML surrogate models struggle to generalize across varying geometries, limiting their practical utility in structural design.

Method: The authors propose a mesh graph network (MGN) that uses node types (fixed boundary, free surface, hole edge), relative edge features (distance between neighbors), and global features (applied load) instead of absolute coordinates, achieving translation and rotation invariance. The model extends Pfaff et al.'s mesh-based simulation framework to structural mechanics. Trained on 11 plate geometries under 20 load conditions, tested on 7 unseen geometries and 3 unseen loads.

Result: In the best case, MGN achieves R² ≥ 0.97 on unseen geometries and loads, versus R² ≈ 0.01–0.86 for Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and K-Nearest Neighbors. MGN consistently outperformed conventional models even in less favorable cases.

Conclusion: Graph neural networks with appropriate invariant features can serve as efficient, generalizable surrogates for finite element analysis across varying geometries, significantly reducing computational cost in structural design workflows.

Abstract: Finite element analysis (FEA) is essential for structural design but remains computationally expensive, particularly when evaluating multiple design iterations or load scenarios. Machine learning surrogate models offer a promising alternative, yet most approaches struggle with a critical limitation: generalizing across varying geometries. This work presents a mesh graph network (MGN) for predicting von Mises stress fields in 2D structural components with arbitrary hole geometries. Unlike traditional machine learning approaches that use absolute node coordinates as features, the proposed model builds on existing MGN frameworks that encode node types (e.g., fixed boundary, free surface, hole edge), relative edge features (distance between neighbors), and global features (applied load). This architecture is inherently translation- and rotation-invariant, enabling generalization to unseen geometries without retraining. The MGN was trained on 11 plate geometries under 20 load conditions and evaluated on 7 unseen geometries and 3 unseen loads. In the most favorable case, the model achieves $R^2 \geq 0.97$ on an unseen geometry and unseen load, compared to $R^2 \approx 0.01$--$0.86$ for conventional models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting , K-Nearest Neighbors) trained on identical data. However, even in less favorable cases, the MGN model still outperforms conventional models. This work extends the mesh-based simulation framework of Pfaff et al. (arXiv:2010.03409) to structural mechanics, demonstrating that graph neural networks can serve as efficient surrogates for finite element analysis across varying geometries.

</details>


### [366] [On solving symmetric multi-type orthogonal non-negative matrix tri-factorization problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08291)
*Rok Hribar,Gregor Papa,Janez Povh,Andrej Kastrin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes and evaluates two heuristic algorithms for symmetric multi-type orthogonal non-negative matrix tri-factorization, demonstrating their effectiveness on synthetic data and citation networks for tasks like link prediction and clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop interpretable and structured factorization methods for multi-type network data, where non-negativity improves interpretability and orthogonality provides natural assignment-type latent factors, while addressing the challenge of non-convex optimization.

Method: Two heuristic algorithms: (1) a fixed point method derived from KKT conditions with a penalty term for orthogonality; (2) a three-stage ADAM-based method combining non-negativity-preserving optimization, orthogonalization, and restricted ADAM refinement.

Result: Both algorithms recover near-optimal factorizations on synthetic data and remain stable under noise. On citation networks, learned embeddings are competitive with or outperform SVD, node2vec, and classical link prediction baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed heuristic methods effectively solve the symmetric multi-type orthogonal NMF problem, yielding interpretable and practically useful embeddings for network analysis tasks.

Abstract: We study the symmetric multi-type orthogonal non-negative matrix tri-factorization problem, where several symmetric non-negative matrices are simultaneously approximated by factors of the form $GS_{i}G^{\top}$, with a shared non-negative and orthogonal factor $G$. This model is motivated by clustering and network analysis, where non-negativity improves interpretability and orthogonality gives a natural assignment-type structure to the latent factor. Since the resulting optimization problem is highly non-convex, we develop two heuristic algorithms for computing high-quality local solutions. The first one is a fixed point method derived from the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions after adding a penalty term for the orthogonality constraint. The second one is a three-stage ADAM-based method that combines non-negativity-preserving optimization, orthogonalization, and restricted ADAM refinement on the feasible set. We evaluate both methods on synthetic data, including noisy instances, and on citation network benchmarks. The synthetic experiments show that both algorithms recover factorizations close to the optimum and remain stable under noise. On real networks, the learned embeddings are competitive with or better than standard baselines such as SVD, node2vec, and classical link prediction heuristics in link prediction, node clustering, and node classification tasks.

</details>


### [367] [QueryWeaver: Reliable Multi-Tool Query Execution Planning via LLM-Based Graph Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08300)
*Aishwarya Chakravarthy,Vidhi Kulkarni,Duen Horng Chau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A system that converts natural language queries into structured graphs and executes them via deterministic depth-first search planning, enabling reliable multi-step cross-tool queries even with smaller LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world queries over personal data span multiple applications and require structured planning, but individual tools expose only partial information. LLMs struggle with reliably executing multi-step, cross-tool queries.

Method: Converts natural language queries into structured graphs, then uses a deterministic planner with depth-first search to resolve dependencies and combine results across tools.

Result: High accuracy even with smaller or locally hosted LLMs; improved reliability over traditional keyword-based search and standard LLM tool use.

Conclusion: The structured graph + deterministic planner approach enables reliable execution of complex cross-tool queries, making it practical for personal data scenarios and deployable even with resource-constrained LLMs.

Abstract: Many real-world queries over personal data span multiple applications and require structured planning, as individual tools expose only partial information. While LLMs show strong reasoning and tool use, reliably executing multi-step, cross-tool queries remains challenging. We introduce a system that converts natural language queries into structured graphs and executes them via a deterministic planner. Our approach uses depth-first search to resolve dependencies and combine results across tools, improving reliability and enabling queries beyond traditional keyword-based search. We demonstrate high accuracy even with smaller or locally hosted LLMs.

</details>


### [368] [GeoGNN: Time Series Geo-Localization using Two-Tower Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08303)
*Toan Tran,Waqwoya Abebe,Abhishek Potnis,Supriya Chinthavali,Cyrus Shahabi,Li Xiong,Dalton Lunga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces GeoGNN, a two-tower architecture for time series geolocalization that infers geographic origins from raw time series data. By combining spatial graph embeddings with temporal representations, it achieves ~27% improvement over baselines on large-scale electricity consumption datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide spatial context to time series data, enabling location-aware downstream applications — a capability currently lacking in time series analysis.

Method: GeoGNN uses a two-tower architecture: a spatial tower learns embeddings of geographic cells via adjacency graph (GNN), and a temporal tower extracts representations from time series. At inference, dot-product similarity plus an auxiliary classification head predicts geographic origin.

Result: 27% average improvement in both fine- and coarse-grained geolocalization accuracy on large-scale electricity consumption datasets, outperforming all baselines.

Conclusion: GeoGNN effectively solves time series geolocalization, with significant accuracy gains, suggesting potential for broader location-aware time series applications.

Abstract: This paper investigates a novel concept of time series geolocalization, where the goal is to infer the geographic origin of each raw time series. Successful geolocalization can provide spatial context to time series, enabling downstream location-aware applications. We formalize the problem, adapt core ideas from image geolocalization to establish strong baselines, and propose GeoGNN, a two-tower architecture. During training, GeoGNN's spatial tower learns embeddings of geographic cell candidates by leveraging the geographic adjacency graph, while the temporal tower extracts informative representations from time series. During inference, each temporal representation is matched against candidate geographic embeddings using dot-product similarity, combined with an auxiliary classification head, to predict the time series' associated geographic origin. Experiments on large-scale, countrywide electricity-consumption datasets demonstrate that GeoGNN achieves the best performance across datasets and enhances both fine- and coarse-grained geolocalization accuracy by ~27% on average.

</details>


### [369] [Towards Graph Foundation Models for Dynamics in Complex Networked Systems: Lessons from Super-Spreader Identification in Multilayer Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08306)
*Michał Czuba,Mateusz Stolarski,Adam Piróg,Piotr Bielak,Piotr Bródka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues for inductive cross-network generalization in network dynamics, proposes four design properties, demonstrates ts-net (a model trained on synthetic networks) that achieves zero-shot generalization to real-world networks, and outlines five open challenges for Graph Foundation Models in this domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current network dynamics models follow a transductive paradigm (trained on one network, cannot be reused on others). The authors argue that inductive cross-network generalization is necessary for Graph Foundation Models in this domain.

Method: The authors propose four design properties for inductive generalization and present ts-net (TopSpreadersNetwork), trained solely on synthetic multilayer networks, as a proof of concept.

Result: ts-net achieves zero-shot generalization to real-world multilayer networks of varying size and layer count, outperforming classical heuristics and transductive baselines on three out of four metrics.

Conclusion: Based on ts-net's performance, the authors outline five open challenges: scale, many-layer generalization, self-supervised pretraining, cross-task transfer, and node-attribute integration.

Abstract: Network dynamics - including spreading, influence maximisation, and epidemic modelling - remain largely confined to the transductive paradigm, where models are trained on a single network and cannot be reused on unseen graphs without retraining. We argue that inductive cross-network generalisation is a necessary prerequisite for Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) in this domain and propose four design properties towards this goal. As a proof of concept, ts-net (TopSpreadersNetwork), trained solely on synthetic multilayer networks (MLNs), demonstrates zero-shot generalisation to real-world MLNs of varying size and layer count, outperforming classical heuristics and transductive baselines on three of four metrics. Based on ts-net's performance, we further outline five open challenges towards building GFMs for network dynamics: scale, many-layer generalisation, self-supervised pretraining, cross-task transfer, and node-attribute integration.

</details>


### [370] [Fourier fractal dimension to predict the generalization of deep neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08308)
*Joao B. Florindo,Davi Wanderley Misturini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Fourier fractal dimension-based generalization measure for deep neural networks, derived from analyzing Lévy-driven SGD dynamics in the frequency domain, which correlates strongly with actual generalization gaps and outperforms existing norm-based, margin-based, and PAC-Bayesian measures on CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting generalization performance of deep neural networks without hold-out validation is a fundamental challenge, and existing measures (norm-based, margin-based, PAC-Bayesian) have limitations. The heavy-tailed, non-Gaussian dynamics of SGD induce complex, scale-invariant trajectories that have not been fully exploited for generalization prediction.

Method: The authors propose a generalization measure based on the Fourier fractal dimension of network weight variations. They analyze the characteristic function of Lévy-driven stochastic differential equations in the frequency domain to extract a metric capturing geometric complexity. They also introduce a Fourier-based optimizer to actively regularize this fractal dimension during training.

Result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST show strong correlation between the proposed Fourier generalization measure and actual generalization gap. The method achieves state-of-the-art Kendall rank correlation coefficients, outperforming existing norm-based, margin-based, and PAC-Bayesian measures.

Conclusion: Frequency-domain fractal analysis is a powerful predictor for model generalizability and provides a principled foundation for developing more stable optimization algorithms.

Abstract: Predicting the generalization performance of deep neural networks without relying on hold-out validation data is a fundamental challenge in machine learning. While Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) drives the optimization of these highly parameterized models, its heavy-tailed, non-Gaussian dynamics induce complex, scale-invariant trajectories in the parameter space. In this paper, we propose a novel generalization measure based on the Fourier fractal dimension of the network's weight variations. By analyzing the characteristic function of the Lévy-driven stochastic differential equations in the frequency domain, we extract a metric that robustly captures the geometric complexity of the learning process. Furthermore, we introduce a customized Fourier-based optimizer designed to actively regularize this fractal dimension during training. Extensive empirical evaluations on the CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST datasets demonstrate that our proposed Fourier generalization measure exhibits a strong correlation with the actual generalization gap. Our method achieves state-of-the-art Kendall rank correlation coefficients, outperforming a wide array of existing norm-based, margin-based, and PAC-Bayesian measures. Ultimately, this work highlights the potential of frequency-domain fractal analysis as both a powerful predictor for model generalizability and a principled foundation for developing more stable optimization algorithms.

</details>


### [371] [Where the Score Lives: A Wavelet View of Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08309)
*Emma Finn,Binxu Wang,T. Anderson Keller,Demba E. Ba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an analytically solvable score function parameterization using 2D orthogonal wavelet basis to understand how architectural choices (CNNs, U-Nets, Transformers) impact generative behavior in diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Little is known about how different architectural choices (CNNs, U-Nets, Transformers) in score-approximation networks impact generative behavior in diffusion models. The authors aim to provide insight into this area.

Method: The authors propose an analytically solvable parameterization of the score function using an expansion in a 2D orthogonal wavelet basis. They derive interpretable optimal score functions in terms of moments of the data distribution, and use this to provide architecture-agnostic, moment-based analysis.

Result: The score machine can partially mimic the inductive biases of multiple architectures (U-Nets, CNNs), revealing which attributes of the data distribution matter most for denoising. The solvable formulation in terms of data moments helps understand how data distribution interacts with the score network.

Conclusion: The proposed approach takes a step toward understanding why different score architectures exhibit distinct generative behavior in diffusion models, by providing an interpretable framework that bridges data distribution moments and architectural inductive biases.

Abstract: Score-based generative models have had remarkable success over the last decade in generating a diverse set of visually plausible images. A variety of architectures including CNNs, U-Nets, and Transformers have been used as the score-approximation network in such diffusion modeling; however, to date, relatively little is known about how these architectural choices impact generative behavior. In this work, to provide insight into this area, we propose an analytically solvable parameterization of the score function using an expansion in a 2D orthogonal wavelet basis. In particular, we derive interpretable optimal score functions in terms of the moments of the data distribution. We use this parametrization to provide an architecture-agnostic, moment-based analysis that reveals which attributes of the data distribution tend to matter most for denoising. Our score machine is flexible enough to partially mimic the relevant inductive biases of multiple architectures, including U-Nets, and CNNs, taking a step towards understanding why different score architectures can exhibit distinct generative behavior. Since our score is solvable in terms of the moments of the data, we can begin to understand how the data distribution interacts with the score network to produce the behavior we observe in diffusion models.

</details>


### [372] [Orthogonality and Dimensionality in Airline Cluster Analysis using PCA and Kernel PCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08322)
*Andreas Schlapbach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper replicates and extends Renold et al. (2023)'s airline profit cycle clustering, finding their 6-cluster taxonomy is geometrically robust across raw and PCA spaces, but kernel PCA reveals a linear manifold with no hidden curvature. The silhouette criterion shows the data structurally supports only 3 clusters, though collinearity in raw 7D space suppresses this signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To verify the robustness of Renold et al. (2023)'s six-cluster taxonomy of US airline profit cycles and check for nonlinear manifold structure that might invalidate their results.

Method: Replicated k-means clustering in 7D raw space, 3D PC space, and 4D PC space; applied kernel PCA with six kernels (three families plus linear baseline) as a nonlinearity check; used silhouette criterion to assess structural cluster support.

Result: Six-cluster taxonomy is geometrically robust (bit-identical assignments between 3-PC and 7D spaces). All six kernels preserve six clusters in 2D. In 1D diagnostic, linear kernel conflates COVID year with peak-profit cluster, while nonlinear kernels shift it to post-financial-crisis cluster. Silhouette criterion shows data supports only 3 clusters, but collinearity masks this in raw 7D space.

Conclusion: The underlying manifold is intrinsically linear with no hidden curvature. The appropriate number of clusters for this data is 3, not 6, though collinearity obscures this structural signal.

Abstract: To characterize the US airline profit cycles from 1995 to 2020, the authors of Renold et al. (2023) combine k-means clustering, principal component analysis, and system dynamic modelling. We replicate their clustering experiment in three spaces -- the original 7-dimensional raw-variable space, a 3-dimensional PC score space, and a 4-dimensional PC score space using their dataset gratefully included in the paper. We show that the six-cluster taxonomy is geometrically robust: k-means in 3-PC space produces bit-for-bit identical cluster assignments relative to 7D raw space. As a nonlinearity check we apply kernel PCA under six kernels spanning three families plus a linear baseline. All six kernels preserve the six-cluster assignment in 2D. A 1D diagnostic tightens this: the linear kernel conflates the COVID year C_3 with the peak-profit cluster C_0, whereas all five non-baseline kernels shift C_3 to overlap only the post-financial-crisis cluster C_5. Agreement across the kernel families confirms an intrinsically linear manifold with no hidden curvature. The silhouette criterion reveals that the dataset structurally supports only three clusters, not six. Collinearity in the raw 7D space suppresses the silhouette signal that would otherwise identify k=3 as the structurally motivated choice.

</details>


### [373] [GENERIC-FNO: Embedding Energy Conservation and Entropy Production into Fourier Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08343)
*Jason Sulskis,Sathya Ravi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GENERIC-FNO is the first neural operator that preserves the full GENERIC thermodynamic structure (reversible + irreversible dynamics) exactly in function space, achieving machine-precision conservation laws without penalties, and performs competitively across multiple PDEs and backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing structure-preserving neural operators only enforce single conservation laws or Hamiltonian structure, and thermodynamically consistent learning is limited to finite-dimensional systems. There is a gap in learning neural operators that respect the full GENERIC (metriplectic) structure of nonequilibrium thermodynamics in function space.

Method: GENERIC-FNO learns energy (E) and entropy (S) functionals as neural operators, parameterizes Poisson (L) and friction (M) operators as diagonal Fourier multipliers with rank-one projections to enforce degeneracy conditions exactly by construction (no penalty terms needed). It also introduces a gauge-invariant dissipation diagnostic.

Result: Degeneracy conditions hold to machine precision (~10^-13), time stepping adds only O(dt^2) drift (~10^-6). The method works across 1D/2D FNOs and DeepONet, four PDEs, preserves structure under 4x super-resolution (64→256), and is competitive with or outperforms baselines on dissipative/mixed problems.

Conclusion: GENERIC-FNO successfully brings full thermodynamic consistency to neural operators, achieving exact structural guarantees for general PDEs spanning reversible, dissipative, and mixed regimes, while being resolution-agnostic and competitive in performance.

Abstract: We introduce GENERIC-FNO, the first neural operator to embed the full GENERIC (metriplectic) structure of nonequilibrium thermodynamics -- reversible, energy-conserving dynamics and irreversible, entropy-producing dynamics coupled through the degeneracy conditions -- directly in function space. Existing structure-preserving neural operators enforce at most a single conservation law or reversible (Hamiltonian) structure, while thermodynamically consistent learning has been confined to finite-dimensional, graph, or particle systems. GENERIC-FNO closes this gap: it learns the energy and entropy functionals as neural operators and parameterizes the Poisson and friction operators as diagonal Fourier multipliers sandwiched between rank-one projections that enforce the degeneracy conditions exactly, by construction, with no penalty term, update projection, or residual. The degeneracy identities hold to machine precision (residuals ~10^-13) for any initialization, dimension, or resolution, so the continuous-time dynamics conserve the learned energy and produce entropy exactly; the explicit time stepping adds only a small O(dt^2) drift (per-step residual ~10^-6). We further note that the (E,S,L,M) decomposition of a given flow is not unique, and introduce a gauge-invariant dissipation diagnostic separating reversible from dissipative dynamics independently of the learned functionals. Across three operator backbones (1D/2D FNOs and DeepONet) and four PDEs spanning reversible, dissipative, and mixed regimes, GENERIC-FNO preserves its exact structural guarantees zero-shot across a 4x super-resolution range (64 to 256), recovers the ground-truth ordering of physical dissipation, and is competitive with strong unconstrained and energy-penalized baselines, outperforming them on several dissipative and mixed problems at comparable or fewer parameters.

</details>


### [374] [Generative Frontier Planning for Adaptive Peer-Referral Recruitment under Covariate-Dependent Arrivals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08360)
*Lingkai Kong,Hezi Jiang,Andrew Ma,Keyu Wang,Akseli Kangaslahti,Milind Tambe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Generative Frontier Planning (GFP), a model-based method for adaptively allocating peer-referral recruitment resources in hidden populations, which handles realistic non-i.i.d. referral patterns with homophily and achieves a (1-1/e)-approximation guarantee, outperforming baselines on calibrated simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work assumes referrals are drawn i.i.d. from a homogeneous population, ignoring the homophily and shared context that drive real peer recruitment in hidden populations affected by infectious diseases. Public health agencies need to adaptively allocate limited referral resources across multiple rounds, where current decisions shape both the number and covariates of future recruits.

Method: GFP uses a censored count model and a conditional generative model to learn referral patterns conditioned on the referrer. It replaces per-step Monte-Carlo sampling with a deterministic backup over a latent covariate-coverage value surrogate. The surrogate is designed so the expected value depends on the offspring generative model through finite-dimensional summaries amortized offline, and the per-round objective is monotone with diminishing returns, enabling a marginal greedy allocation with (1-1/e)-approximation.

Result: On a simulation calibrated to real respondent-driven sampling data, GFP outperforms random, reinforcement-learning, and i.i.d. dynamic-programming baselines across four discount factors.

Conclusion: The two key properties (deterministic backup eliminating Monte-Carlo sampling and diminishing-returns structure enabling greedy approximation) make planning tractable for realistic peer-referral recruitment problems with homophily.

Abstract: Peer-referral recruitment systems such as respondent-driven sampling are critical for studying and intervening on hidden populations affected by infectious diseases. To accelerate recruitment, public health agencies must adaptively allocate limited referral resources across multiple rounds, where current decisions shape both the number and the covariates of future recruits. Prior work makes this problem tractable by assuming that referrals are drawn i.i.d.\ from a homogeneous population, an assumption that ignores the homophily and shared context that drive real peer recruitment. We instead consider a more realistic model in which both referral capacity and the covariates of newly referred individuals are conditioned on the referrer, learned from data with a censored count model and a conditional generative model. The resulting planning problem is challenging because each candidate allocation induces a different distribution over future recruits. We propose \emph{Generative Frontier Planning} (GFP), a model-based planner that replaces per-step Monte-Carlo sampling with a deterministic backup over a latent covariate-coverage value surrogate. The surrogate is designed so that the expected value of the next frontier depends on the offspring generative model only through finite-dimensional summaries that are amortized offline, and so that the resulting per-round objective is monotone with diminishing returns. Together, these two properties make planning tractable: the deterministic backup eliminates Monte-Carlo sampling, and the diminishing-returns structure lets a marginal greedy allocation achieve a \((1-1/e)\)-approximation for the per-round problem. On a simulation environment calibrated to a real respondent-driven sampling dataset, GFP outperforms random, reinforcement-learning, and i.i.d.\ dynamic-programming baselines across four discount factors.

</details>


### [375] [Pre-Intervention Prediction of Sparse Autoencoder Steering Side Effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08365)
*Evan Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that side effects of steering language models using sparse autoencoder (SAE) features can be predicted from feature statistics computed before intervention. Across multiple models and SAE variants, decoder geometry, activation statistics, co-activation structure, and direct-logit footprint predict steering modularity better than frequency-only baselines, though the best predictors vary by model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature steering with SAE features often has inconsistent side effects across contexts and perturbs unrelated features. The authors aim to predict these side effects in advance to enable cleaner steering without costly trial-and-error.

Method: The authors propose a pre-intervention screening framework that predicts steering modularity from feature statistics. They operationalize side effects along two axes: effect stability and collateral spread. They evaluate GPT-2-small, Pythia-70M-deduped, Gemma-2-2B, and Llama-3.1-8B across ReLU, JumpReLU, and TopK SAE dictionaries, using decoder geometry, activation statistics, co-activation structure, and direct-logit footprint as predictors.

Result: The proposed predictors outperform frequency-only and activation-magnitude baselines, especially in GPT-2-small, Pythia-70M, and Llama-3.1-8B. The successful axis of improvement varies by model: GPT-2 improves most cleanly, Pythia improves mainly on stability, Llama mainly on collateral, and Gemma only partially. The predictive signal persists under dictionary-width changes from 32K to 128K.
