PROBE-Web provides four key functionalities: (1) conventional evaluation toolkit, (2) flexible perspective-aware evaluation, (3) explainable case studies, and (4) evaluation landscape exploration. We believe that PROBE-Web can help users better understand KGC models aligning with their objectives.

</details>


### [428] [Backward Coherence and Hidden-State Stability in Recurrent Neural Networks: A Quasi-Reverse-Martingale Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08934)
*Yuan-chin Ivan Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces **backward coherence** as a principle for stabilizing hidden states in RNNs, showing that under contraction and summable backward drift, hidden states form quasi-reverse-martingales with almost-sure convergence, finite stopping times, and time-uniform confidence sequences. A backward-coherence regularization loss (equivalent to KL divergence in a Gaussian backward model) reduces empirical quasi-martingale total by 43–58%, reaches stability 28–44% earlier, and achieves geometric tracking-error recovery. Real-data experiments (ICU mortality, macroeconomic forecasting, activity recognition) confirm practical benefits including earlier stable representations and improved concept-drift handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The probabilistic meaning of the hidden state $h_t$ in recurrent neural networks is often unclear, and hidden-state instability can hinder interpretability, reliability, and theoretical guarantees for sequential inference.

Method: The paper formalizes **backward coherence**—the extent to which $h_t$ can be reconstructed from $h_{t+1}$ via a learned backward projector $g_φ$. Under contraction and summable backward drift, the hidden-state sequence becomes a **quasi-reverse-martingale**, enabling almost-sure convergence, rates under mixing, interpretable limiting representations, finite stopping times, and time-uniform confidence sequences. A backward-coherence regularizer is introduced, equivalent to minimizing KL divergence in a Gaussian backward model, linking to variational inference.

Result: Backward-coherence regularization reduces empirical quasi-martingale total $\hat Q$ by 43–58%, reaches stability 28–44% earlier than unregularized RNNs, and yields tracking-error recovery consistent with geometric bounds. The increment-sum tube $R_t$ achieves 100% simultaneous coverage (though conservative). On PhysioNet 2012, RMRNN matches AUC while achieving stable representations 13 hours earlier; on FRED-MD, one-month-ahead forecast error is reduced ~4× under concept drift; on UCI HAR, post-transition tracking error decays geometrically.

Conclusion: Backward coherence provides a principled framework for stabilizing RNN hidden states with theoretical guarantees. The practical regularizer offers measurable gains in stability, convergence speed, and tracking performance across diverse real-world tasks. Extensions cover φ-mixing inputs, change-point tracking, and finite-sample concentration; however, universality is not claimed.

Abstract: Recurrent neural networks maintain a hidden state $h_t$, but its probabilistic meaning is often unclear. We study hidden-state stability through \emph{backward coherence}: the extent to which $h_t$ can be reconstructed from $h_{t+1}$ by a learned backward projector $g_φ$. Under contraction and summable backward drift, the hidden-state sequence forms a quasi-reverse-martingale. This yields almost-sure convergence, rates under mixing, an interpretable limiting representation, finite pathwise stopping times, and a theoretical framework for time-uniform confidence sequences.
  Simulations support the theory. Backward-coherence regularisation reduces the empirical quasi-martingale total $\hat Q$ by $43$--$58%$, reaches stability $28$--$44%$ earlier than an unregularised RNN, and gives tracking-error recovery consistent with geometric bounds. Additional tests confirm echo-state forgetting rates bounded by $ρ$ and verify the increment-sum tube $R_t$ with $100%$ simultaneous coverage, although $R_t$ is conservative; in practice, the defect-tail proxy $\hat Q_t$ is the more useful monitor. The backward-coherence loss is also equivalent to minimising a Kullback--Leibler divergence in a Gaussian backward model, linking the method to variational inference. Extensions cover $φ$-mixing inputs, change-point tracking, and finite-sample concentration.
  Three real-data studies further validate the approach. On PhysioNet 2012 ICU data, the Reverse Martingale RNN (RMRNN) matches RNN mortality-prediction AUC while reaching stable representations 13 hours earlier. On FRED-MD, it reduces one-month-ahead forecast error by about fourfold under concept drift. On UCI Human Activity Recognition, it maintains lower post-transition tracking error with geometric decay. The guarantees apply under the stated assumptions; universality is not claimed.

</details>


### [429] [PAI: Preserving Amplitude Information in Representation-Based Time-Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08935)
*Kang Zhang,Wei Jian Lau,Shoushou Ren,Dong Lin,Joon Son Chung,Chuanhao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that representation-based time-series anomaly detection methods lose amplitude information in embeddings, harming performance on amplitude-related anomalies. The authors propose PAI, a scoring scheme with a diagnostic module and score augmentation function that explicitly retains amplitude information, achieving significant improvements across methods and datasets (up to 98.4% VUS-PR gain).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing representation-based time-series anomaly detection methods learn embeddings that are amplitude-agnostic, which degrades performance on amplitude-related anomalies. This failure is prevalent across all existing representation-based methods.

Method: PAI consists of two modules: (1) a diagnostic module that compares cosine and Euclidean scoring on the same representation bank to test if amplitude information is captured; (2) a final score augmentation function that computes point-wise median and MAD deviation score and a local mean-shift score, fused with the representation score to produce the final anomaly score.

Result: On TSB-AD-U-Eva and TAB UV datasets, PAI improves all four evaluated representation-based methods across every metric, achieving average VUS-PR gains of 98.4% and 36.8%. PaAno + PAI outperforms state-of-the-art by 15%.

Conclusion: Explicitly retaining amplitude information is crucial for representation-based time-series anomaly detection, which has been underemphasized in existing scoring schemes.

Abstract: Representation-based time-series anomaly detection algorithms significantly outperform other methods on diverse anomaly detection tasks. However, we notice that they suffer from a major limitation in our evaluation - their learned embeddings are often amplitude-agnostic. Losing amplitude information can degrade performance on amplitude related anomalies, and this failure is prevalent across all existing representation-based methods. To address aforementioned issues, we propose a new anomaly scoring scheme named PAI. PAI consists of two complementary modules, a diagnostic module and a final score augmentation function. The diagnostic module compares cosine and Euclidean scoring on the same representation bank to test whether amplitude information is already captured in the learned representation. Then in final score augmentation function, PAI computes a point-wise median and MAD deviation score and a local mean-shift score-which are fused with the representation score to produce the final anomaly score. On the TSB-AD-U-Eva and TAB UV datasets, PAI improves all four evaluated representation-based methods across every reported metric, achieving average VUS-PR gains of 98.4% and 36.8%, respectively. Among all evaluated combinations, PaAno + PAI achieves the best performance, outperforming the state-of-the-art method by 15%. Further evaluation on bootstrap confidence intervals, anomaly-type breakdowns, and a TS2Vec input-normalization ablation further support the proposed scheme. These results suggest that explicitly retaining amplitude information is important for representation-based time-series anomaly detection, which has been underemphasized in existing scoring schemes. Code is available at: https://github.com/pantheon5100/PAI

</details>


### [430] [From Hazard Functions to Language Space: Cox-Supervised Distillation of Survival Risk into a Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08945)
*Nicholas I-Hsien Kuo,Blanca Gallego,Louisa Jorm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The authors propose a pipeline that fine-tunes a large language model (Qwen-based) to predict survival risk from clinical text, using Cox proportional hazards model predictions as training targets, achieving competitive performance across three medical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether time-to-event risk information from a Cox model can be transferred into a generative large language model, enabling survival risk reasoning in language models.

Method: Clinical covariates are converted into text prompts; a Qwen-based LLM is fine-tuned to generate patient-specific survival risk using Cox model predictions as the training target, framed as a text-generation task.

Result: Across GBSG2, ACTG320, and WHAS500 datasets, the model achieves competitive held-out discrimination and calibration. t-SNE visualizations reveal smooth risk gradients in latent space.

Conclusion: Large language models can internalise survival-risk structure and support calibrated prediction, offering a pathway towards time-to-event reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: We investigate whether information about time-to-event risk estimated by a Cox proportional hazards model can be transferred into a generative large language model. We propose a text-based survival modelling pipeline in which structured clinical covariates are converted into text prompts and a Qwen-based large language model is fine-tuned to generate patient-specific survival risk using Cox model predictions as a training target. Across GBSG2, ACTG320, and WHAS500, the model achieves competitive held-out discrimination and calibration despite being trained as a text-generation task rather than with a conventional survival-analysis loss. We further analyse the geometry of the model's hidden states, where t-SNE visualisations reveal smooth risk gradients in latent space, suggesting that the model represents survival risk as a continuous structure rather than isolated risk categories. Together, these findings suggest that large language models can internalise survival-risk structure while supporting calibrated prediction, providing a route towards time-to-event reasoning in language models.

</details>


### [431] [Self-Consistent Generative Paths via Admissible Random Variational Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08953)
*Lei Luo,Yingzhen Zhang,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework for defining and testing self-consistency in generative probability paths, formalized via random fixed-point residuals (R-FPR), with theoretical guarantees and practical applications across diffusion, flow, one-step, VAE, GAN, and autoregressive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unifying views describe how generative paths are constructed (e.g., diffusion, flow matching, distillation), but lack a framework to answer when a generated probability path is self-consistent across diverse model families.

Method: Defines a self-consistent generative path as a random fixed point of admissible local variational transport corrections, using a random variational transport operator that combines divergence/geometry, energy, and structural constraints. Introduces the random fixed-point path residual (R-FPR) to measure the gap.

Result: Proves well-posedness, random fixed-point existence/attraction, non-contractive existence, residual-to-generation error bounds, empirical residual concentration, proxy perturbation bounds, continuous-time limits, and operator-level generalization with model-specific corollaries.

Conclusion: The theory transforms endpoint matching into path self-consistency testing and provides a residual-control principle for diagnosing failures, regularizing training, and guiding adaptive sampling across diffusion, flow, one-step, VAE, GAN/WGAN, and autoregressive generators.

Abstract: Modern generative models often define an entire probability path from a simple prior to the data law, rather than only an endpoint map. Diffusion models follow stochastic denoising paths, flow matching learns transport fields, consistency and distillation methods compress paths into one or a few steps, adversarial models match terminal distributions, and VAEs generate through latent kernels. Existing unifying views mainly describe how such paths are constructed. We study a complementary question: when is a generated probability path self-consistent? We define a self-consistent generative path as a random fixed point of admissible local variational transport corrections. In this framework, a local correction is specified by a random variational transport operator combining a divergence or geometry term, an energy term, and a structural constraint. The framework contains random regularized optimal-transport proximal steps as a structured instance, while also allowing non-OT divergences, latent kernels, adversarial constraints, causal discrete kernels, and terminal one-step maps. The theory yields a random fixed-point path residual (R-FPR), which measures the gap between the actual generated path and an admissible local correction. We prove well-posedness, random fixed-point existence and attraction, non-contractive existence, residual-to-generation error bounds, empirical residual concentration, proxy perturbation bounds, continuous-time limits, and operator-level generalization with model-specific corollaries. The resulting theory turns endpoint matching into path self-consistency testing and provides a residual-control principle for diagnosing failures, regularizing training, and guiding adaptive sampling across diffusion, flow, one-step, VAE, GAN/WGAN, and autoregressive generators.

</details>


### [432] [From inverse problems to neural operators: prediction, mechanism, and generalization of data-driven models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08956)
*Conor Rowan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper taxonomizes various Scientific Machine Learning modeling strategies (SINDy, Neural ODEs, neural operators, etc.) by showing they share a common structure differing only in the assumed model class of the input-output relation. Drawing on philosophy of science, the authors argue that only models based on parsimonious differential equations can achieve mechanism discovery and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify apparently disparate modeling strategies in Scientific Machine Learning (SINDy, Neural ODEs, neural operators) by revealing their common structure, and to determine which approaches are capable of genuine mechanism discovery and generalization.

Method: Philosophical analysis drawing from the literature on scientific models and mechanisms; conceptual comparison of modeling strategies (inverse problems, SINDy, Neural ODEs, neural operators) based on their assumed model class for input-output relations.

Result: All data-driven modeling strategies share a common structure and differ only in the model class of the input-output relation; only models based on parsimonious differential equations can discover mechanisms and generalize.

Conclusion: The proposed taxonomy unites different SciML approaches and provides insight into their appropriate use cases, with mechanism discovery requiring parsimonious differential equation models.

Abstract: Scientists have historically relied on mathematical models based on differential equations to relate system inputs -- forces, fluxes, or heat sources -- to outputs, such as displacement, velocity, concentration, and temperature. These models rely on deep domain knowledge to determine the form of the governing differential equation, which is then calibrated with data by solving an inverse problem. In recent years, the field of Scientific Machine Learning has introduced a variety of alternative modeling strategies for physical systems. A method called Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics learns the governing equation as a sparse linear combination of terms in a user-defined library. Neural Ordinary Differential Equations construct the governing equation by taking in the state and its derivatives at the input layer of a neural network. Entirely foregoing the modeling framework of differential equations, neural operators directly learn a non-linear mapping between the system inputs and outputs. From inverse problems to neural operators, all of these modeling strategies can be conceptualized as data-driven machinery to predict a system's response over a range of inputs. It is then natural to wonder how exactly these various strategies relate to each other, and whether they can be neatly taxonomized. Drawing from the philosophical literature on scientific models, we argue that many model types have a common structure, differing only in the assumed model class of the input-output relation they define. Connecting to philosophical ideas on mechanism, and arguing that data from physical systems arises from solutions to parsimonious differential equations, we propose that only certain models are capable of mechanism discovery, and thus generalization. Our analysis is intended to unite apparently disparate modeling strategies and provide insight into their appropriate use cases.

</details>


### [433] [Online Learning with Recency: Algorithms for Sliding-window Streaming Multi-armed Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08977)
*Vladimir Braverman,Chen Wang,Liudeng Wang,Samson Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies single-pass sliding-window streaming multi-armed bandits, providing theoretical analysis for pure exploration and regret minimization under memory constraints, with experiments validating trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the streaming MAB model to incorporate a sliding-window constraint (recency effect), where only the most recent W arms are valid, and to understand fundamental limits on memory, regret, and exploration in this realistic setting.

Method: The authors formulate a streaming MAB model with a sliding window of size W, requiring single-pass algorithms with limited memory. They analyze pure exploration (finding the best vs. approximate best arm) and regret minimization under this model, proving hardness results and trade-offs.

Result: Finding the best arm is hard with sublinear memory, but finding an approximate best arm is efficient. Sharp memory-regret trade-offs for single-pass algorithms are established.

Conclusion: The sliding-window streaming MAB setting introduces fundamental memory constraints that differ significantly from standard streaming MABs, with clear trade-offs validated experimentally.

Abstract: Motivated by the recency effect in online learning, we study algorithms for single-pass *sliding-window streaming multi-armed bandits (MABs)* in this paper. In this setting, we are given $n$ arms with unknown sub-Gaussian reward distributions and a parameter $W$. The arms arrive in a single-pass stream, and only the most recent $W$ arms are considered valid. The algorithm is required to perform pure exploration and regret minimization with limited memory, defined as the number of stored arms. The model is a natural extension of the streaming multi-armed bandits model (without the sliding window) that has been extensively studied in recent years. We provide a comprehensive analysis of both the pure exploration and regret minimization problems with the model. For pure exploration, we prove that finding the best arm is hard with sublinear memory while finding an approximate best arm admits an efficient algorithm. For regret minimization, we explore a new notion of regret and give sharp memory-regret trade-offs for any single-pass algorithm. We complement our theoretical results with experiments, demonstrating the trade-offs between sample, regret, and memory.

</details>


### [434] [Heterophily-Aware Adaptive Knowledge Distillation for Hypergraph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08978)
*Joohee Cho,David Yoon Suk Kang,Yunyong Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HADES is a heterophily-aware adaptive distillation method for hypergraph neural networks that quantifies node heterophily to modulate teacher knowledge transfer, enabling lightweight student models that often outperform teachers while achieving up to 12.3x faster inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HNNs show substantially lower prediction performance on heterophilic nodes (connected through semantically diverse hyperedges), indicating teacher knowledge reliability varies across nodes. This motivates the need for a distillation method that accounts for node-level heterophily.

Method: HADES quantifies node heterophily and uses it as an estimate of teacher reliability to adaptively modulate the transfer of teacher knowledge during distillation.

Result: HADES consistently improves student performance across different HNN teachers and distillation objectives. Student models often surpass teacher predictive performance while achieving up to 12.3x faster inference.

Conclusion: Heterophily-aware adaptive distillation is effective for hypergraph neural networks, producing lightweight yet high-performing student models.

Abstract: Hypergraph knowledge distillation aims to retain the predictive performance of a hypergraph neural network (HNN) teacher while reducing inference costs through a lightweight student model. In this work, we observe that HNNs exhibit substantially lower prediction performance on heterophilic nodes connected through semantically diverse hyperedges, indicating that the reliability of teacher knowledge varies across nodes. Motivated by this observation, we propose HADES, a heterophily-aware adaptive distillation method for hypergraph neural networks. HADES quantifies node heterophily and leverages it as an estimate of teacher reliability to modulate the transfer of teacher knowledge during distillation. Experimental results on real-world hypergraphs demonstrate that HADES consistently improves student performance across different HNN teachers and distillation objectives. In many cases, the resulting student models surpass the predictive performance of their teachers while achieving up to 12.3 times faster inference.

</details>


### [435] [Beyond Neural Collapse: Task-Intrinsic Geometry Governs Neural Representations in Modular Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08985)
*Hu Tan,Kuo Gai,Shihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explains why neural networks solving modular addition converge to a 2D cyclic geometry instead of the expected neural collapse simplex. The authors identify a layerwise training mechanism where classifier weights lock into a rank-2 plane first, then embeddings are constrained to that plane; interpret in-plane dynamics as entropy-regularized transport leading to phase alignment; and quantify the cyclic solution's advantage over neural collapse via a Θ(K) benefit in weight-decay surrogates vs. O(1) cross-entropy gain, yielding a critical threshold λ_crit = Θ(1/K).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the discrepancy between neural collapse theory (predicting simplex ETF geometry) and the observed 2D cyclic geometry in modular addition tasks, providing a mechanistic explanation for grokking behavior.

Method: Theoretical analysis combining layerwise training dynamics, entropy-regularized transport on S^1, and asymptotic complexity comparison between cyclic and simplex solutions under cross-entropy and weight-decay surrogates.

Result: Cyclic rank-2 solutions achieve Θ(K) advantage under Schatten/weight-decay surrogates, while simplex ETF gains only O(1) in cross-entropy, establishing a critical regularization threshold that explains the prevalence of cyclic geometry.

Conclusion: Grokking on modular arithmetic is governed by a task-structured trade-off between separation, symmetry, and complexity, not by maximal separation alone.

Abstract: While neural collapse (NC) predicts that a $K$-class-balanced classifier should organize terminal representations as a $(K-1)$-dimensional simplex equiangular tight frame (ETF), modular addition consistently enters a different regime: networks compress to a two-dimensional cyclic geometry in which both classifier weights and token embeddings lie on circles. We refine the explanation of this phenomenon in three directions. First, we formalize a layerwise non-uniform training mechanism: downstream classifier weights are driven by dense cross-entropy gradients into a rank-2 equiangular configuration before upstream embeddings fully reorganize, and once this classifier plane forms, backpropagated feature gradients constrain embedding motion to the same plane while weight decay suppresses orthogonal components. Second, after this subspace locking, the induced in-plane dynamics admit an entropy-regularized transport interpretation on $S^1$; combined with modular-addition labels, this reduces embedding formation to phase alignment, whose minimizers are single-frequency characters of $\mathbb{Z}/P\mathbb{Z}$ and hence equal-angle points on a circle. Third, we quantify why this solution prevails over NC: a simplex ETF gains only an $O(1)$ advantage in cross-entropy, whereas the cyclic rank-2 solution enjoys a $Θ(K)$ advantage under Schatten or weight-decay surrogates, yielding a critical threshold $λ_{\mathrm{crit}} = Θ(1/K)$. Our results explain both why classifier weights move first and why embeddings subsequently align with them, showing that grokking on modular arithmetic is governed not by maximal separation alone but by a task-structured trade-off between separation, symmetry, and complexity.

</details>


### [436] [LEAF: A Learning-Enabled ADMM Framework for Accelerated Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08993)
*Binh Nguyen,Trinh Tran,Truong X. Nghiem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEAF is a learning-enhanced ADMM framework for convex optimization that uses an Input Convex Neural Network to learn the Moreau envelope, achieving faster convergence with theoretical guarantees and up to 10x speedup over traditional solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To accelerate convex optimization by combining learning-based methods with ADMM while preserving convexity and convergence guarantees, avoiding the complexity and data inefficiency of learning high-dimensional operators directly.

Method: LEAF approximates the Moreau envelope of the objective using an Input Convex Neural Network (ICNN), then integrates it into ADMM (MEL-ADMM) and its splitting variant (sMEL-ADMM). The ICNN preserves convexity and smoothness while reducing model complexity by learning a scalar-valued function.

Result: Up to an order-of-magnitude speedup over state-of-the-art solvers with low optimality gaps, while achieving convergence rates comparable to classical ADMM but with reduced per-iteration cost.

Conclusion: LEAF demonstrates that learning a structured (scalar, convex) surrogate like the Moreau envelope via ICNN is an effective and efficient strategy for accelerating ADMM, with strong theoretical and empirical results.

Abstract: We propose LEAF, a learning-enabled ADMM framework for accelerated convex optimization. The key idea is to approximate the Moreau envelope of the objective function using an Input Convex Neural Network (ICNN), resulting in a learned model that preserves convexity and smoothness. This leads to the proposed Moreau Envelope Learning ADMM (MEL-ADMM) and its splitting variant sMEL-ADMM. Unlike existing approaches that learn high-dimensional operators directly, LEAF learns a scalar-valued Moreau envelope, significantly reducing model complexity and improving data efficiency. The framework accommodates a broad class of convex problems with smooth and non-smooth objectives. By embedding convexity explicitly through the ICNN architecture, the proposed approach maintains high approximation accuracy while preserving key structural properties of the optimization problem. Both MEL-ADMM and sMEL-ADMM are developed with theoretical guarantees of convergence and feasibility under the learned model. Rigorous analysis shows that the proposed methods achieve convergence rates comparable to classical ADMM while reducing per-iteration computational cost. Numerical experiments demonstrate up to an order-of-magnitude speedup over state-of-the-art solvers while maintaining low optimality gaps

</details>


### [437] [Understanding Quantization-Aware Training: Gradients at Quantized Weights Bias to the Low-Loss Basin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09012)
*Hanyang Li,Jianhao Ma,Ying Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a geometric "river and valley" framework explaining why PTQ fails at aggressive bitwidths (quantization grid exceeds basin width, causing jumps outside the low-loss basin) and how QAT recovers (STE-based gradients sense valley walls, steering weights back into the basin). Theoretical proofs and experiments across vision/language models validate the framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why post-training quantization (PTQ) fails at aggressive low-bitwidths while quantization-aware training (QAT) can recover accuracy, and to unify these phenomena under a single geometric theory.

Method: Proposes a geometric "river and valley" landscape model where full-precision training follows a low-loss river inside a valley. PTQ fails when the quantization grid exceeds the basin width, causing deployed quantized points to land outside the basin. QAT recovers because STE-based gradients sense the valley wall and steer quantized iterates back inward. The method includes a local landscape model, formal PTQ failure mode construction, and finite-time QAT recovery proof under local quantizer-compatibility assumptions.

Result: Experiments on vision and language models under multiple quantization schemes confirm: (1) PTQ exhibits basin-crossing failure at aggressive bitwidths as predicted, and (2) QAT successfully recovers accuracy via the proposed geometric steering mechanism.

Conclusion: The geometric framework successfully unifies PTQ failure and QAT recovery, providing theoretical insight into when and why each method works, and potentially guiding future quantization algorithm design.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) converts a trained full-precision model into low-bit weights without task-level retraining, while quantization-aware training (QAT) incorporates quantization into the training loop. Although PTQ is efficient and often accurate at moderate bitwidths, it can fail sharply at aggressive bitwidths; QAT is more expensive but can often recover the lost accuracy. We propose a unified geometric framework that explains both PTQ failure and QAT recovery. We model full-precision training as following a low-loss \emph{river} inside a wider \emph{valley}: a normal neighborhood of the river forms a nearly flat \emph{basin}, while leaving this basin incurs a sharp loss increase. When the quantization grid is comparable to the basin width, local PTQ objectives, including rounding and Hessian-based second-order reconstruction, can select a high-loss deployed quantized point outside the basin even when nearby low-loss quantized points exist. In this regime, straight-through-estimator-based QAT has a useful bias: it evaluates gradients at the deployed quantized weights while updating latent full-precision weights, causing the gradient to sense the valley wall and acquire an inward component that steers subsequent quantized iterates back into the basin. We formalize this mechanism through a local landscape model, construct a geometric PTQ failure mode, and prove finite-time QAT recovery under local quantizer-compatibility assumptions. Experiments across vision and language models under multiple neural-network quantization schemes corroborate the predicted basin-crossing failure of PTQ and the corresponding recovery mechanism of QAT.

</details>


### [438] [Structural Grid Descriptors Predict Within-Task Solver Success on ARC-AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09026)
*Ayan Pendharkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Intermediate grid states predict ARC-AGI solver success (AUC=0.885), generalize across architectures, enable early stopping (33-65% compute savings), and reveal DSL coverage gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether structural properties of intermediate grid states can predict whether a symbolic ARC-AGI solver will succeed, framed as a test of conditional mutual information.

Method: 44,800 runs across two solvers (beam search and Stochastic DFS), 400 ARC tasks, 28 configurations per solver. Hand-crafted grid descriptors measured at 50% trajectory completion; within-task AUC, cross-solver transfer, pre-registered held-out validation, configuration-residualized analysis, early stopping experiments.

Result: Mean within-task best-feature AUC=0.885 (p<0.001); cross-solver AUC=0.747-0.762; pre-registered held-out AUC=0.765; configuration-residualized AUC=0.927/0.896; early stopping reduces compute 33.6% (beam) and 65.3% (SDFS) with minimal solve loss; 229/400 tasks have 0-step DSL coverage collapse.

Conclusion: Intermediate grid states contain predictive signals for solver success, generalizable across architectures and enabling compute savings, but DSL coverage limitations severely constrain performance on evaluation tasks.

Abstract: We ask whether structural properties of intermediate grid states predict whether a symbolic ARC-AGI solver will succeed, framed as a test of conditional mutual information I(X;Y|task) > 0. Across 44,800 runs spanning two architecturally distinct solvers (beam search and Stochastic DFS), 400 ARC tasks, 28 configurations per solver, and both training and evaluation splits, hand-crafted grid descriptors measured at 50% trajectory completion discriminate successful from failed runs within the same task (mean within-task best-feature AUC = 0.885, p < 0.001 under within-task label permutation). Most predictive content lies along a single grid-complexity axis. The result generalizes across solver architectures: a feature selected on one solver predicts success on the other with AUC 0.747-0.762 in all four transfer directions (p < 0.001, leakage controlled). On a pre-registered held-out set of 41 reliable tasks, the frozen feature n_components_final achieves AUC = 0.765 (95% CI [0.717, 0.810], p < 0.001), robust under task-clustered bootstrap resampling and cross-solver task collapsing. The signal is not explained by solver capacity (configuration-residualized AUC = 0.927 and 0.896 for beam search and SDFS, p < 0.001) and is only weakly coupled to score trajectories (R^2 approximately 0). Early stopping at 50% completion reduces beam-search compute by 33.6% while retaining 98.9% of solves; degenerate-trajectory detection reduces SDFS compute by 65.3% with no solve loss. Finally, on 229 of 400 evaluation tasks the DSL primitive library produces no valid transition from the input grid. This 0-step collapse is invariant to search budget and universally failed by beam search, indicating a DSL coverage limitation rather than a search-budget effect.

</details>


### [439] [TRIAGE: Dialectical Reasoning for Explainable Risk Prediction on Irregularly Sampled Medical Time Series with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09030)
*Hyeongwon Jang,Gyouk Chu,Changhun Kim,Joonhyung Park,Hangyul Yoon,Eunho Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRIAGE trains LLMs to generate dialectical reasoning over competing clinical outcomes, achieving 3.3% AUPRC improvement and 81% calibration error reduction while producing interpretable rationales for irregularly sampled medical time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs collapse graded clinical risk into overconfident binary predictions, undermining calibration and cross-patient comparability in clinical early warning systems.

Method: TRIAGE trains an LLM to generate dialectical reasoning over competing clinical outcomes by eliciting outcome-specific rationales, mitigating risk polarization while yielding continuous risk scores grounded in explicit clinical reasoning.

Result: Average AUPRC improvement of 3.3%, 81% reduction in calibration error, and 20% better clinical reasoning quality over post-hoc explanations.

Conclusion: Dialectical reasoning formulation effectively addresses risk polarization in LLM-based clinical early warning, improving both calibration and interpretability.

Abstract: Clinical early warning systems built on electronic health records, in which clinical observations are recorded as irregularly sampled medical time series (ISMTS), must deliver both calibrated risk scores for patient triage and interpretable rationales that clinicians can verify. Large Language Models (LLMs) have been explored for this task, yet they collapse graded clinical risk into overconfident binary predictions. This risk polarization undermines both calibration and cross-patient comparability. To address this, we propose TRIAGE, a framework that trains an LLM to generate dialectical reasoning over competing clinical outcomes by eliciting outcome-specific rationales. This dialectical formulation mitigates risk polarization, enabling a single LLM to yield continuous risk scores grounded in explicit clinical reasoning. Evaluated on three ISMTS benchmarks, TRIAGE achieves an average AUPRC improvement of 3.3% and reduces calibration error by 81% compared to the competitive baselines. An LLM-as-a-judge assessment further shows that our rationales surpass post-hoc explanations from the baseline by 20% in clinical reasoning quality. The source code is available at https://github.com/HyeongWon-Jang/TRIAGE .

</details>


### [440] [DynaCF: Mitigating Shortcut Learning in Reward Models via Dynamic Counterfactual Sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09043)
*Fengyuan Liu,Yongliang Miao,Zirui He,Yanguang Liu,Fei Sun,Mengnan Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynaCF is a dynamic reweighting framework that mitigates shortcut learning in reward model training by measuring shortcut sensitivity via counterfactual perturbations and downweighting shortcut-sensitive samples in the Bradley-Terry objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward models trained from pairwise preferences often exploit superficial shortcut cues instead of learning true response quality, leading to poor robustness.

Method: DynaCF measures shortcut sensitivity online by applying semantics-preserving counterfactual perturbations and tracking margin shifts and preference flips under the current model, then dynamically downweights high-sensitivity samples in the Bradley-Terry loss.

Result: Extensive experiments show DynaCF consistently improves robustness in preference modeling.

Conclusion: Dynamically reweighting training samples based on shortcut sensitivity helps reward models learn more robust and task-relevant preference signals.

Abstract: Reward models trained from pairwise preferences often exploit superficial shortcut cues rather than learning true response quality. We propose DynaCF, a dynamic reweighting framework for mitigating shortcut learning in reward model training. Unlike static shortcut heuristics, DynaCF measures shortcut sensitivity online during optimization by applying semantics-preserving counterfactual perturbations and tracking the resulting margin shifts and preference flips under the current model. Samples with higher shortcut sensitivity are dynamically downweighted in the Bradley-Terry objective, encouraging the model to rely less on superficial patterns and more on task-relevant preference signals. Extensive experiments show that DynaCF consistently improves robustness in preference modeling.

</details>


### [441] [Decoy-Calibrated Failure Audits for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09046)
*Vyzantinos Repantis,Ameya Gawde,Harshvardhan Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Janus is a statistical procedure for auditing AI model errors that confirms a failure explanation only if it beats a "decoy floor" of randomly assigned descriptors and replicates on held-out data, preventing false positives from selective reporting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditors risk false discoveries when testing many candidate explanations for model failures—the largest observed effect may just be the best among many tried. Rigorous safeguards are needed to distinguish real failure modes from statistical noise.

Method: Janus scores each candidate explanation (descriptor) by error-rate lift, compares real descriptors against fake ones with identical frequencies but random assignment to examples (decoy floor), and confirms a descriptor only if it beats decoys on discovery data and replicates on separate held-out data.

Result: Janus correctly identifies planted long-chain failures in controlled multi-table lookup tasks. On MuSiQue and LongBench v2, it rejects all plausible high-error pockets flagged by the SliceLine baseline. Ablations show that both safeguards (decoy floor and holdout check) are essential: uncalibrated threshold gave 20 findings, decoy floor left 1, holdout rejected the last.

Conclusion: The principle separates proposing explanations from reporting them—candidates can come from any source, but only those that beat decoys and replicate on fresh data become trustworthy audit findings.

Abstract: Useful audits reveal not only how often a model fails, but also where its failures concentrate. An auditor may test many candidate explanations: long inputs, indirect questions, distracting evidence, or combinations of these factors. The risk is selection. The largest observed effect may reflect a real failure mode, or it may simply be the best result among many tried. We introduce Janus, a procedure for deciding when a proposed error explanation is credible enough to report. The goal is not to generate new explanations, but to decide which ones hold up. The auditor starts with a fixed model, a labeled evaluation set, and a frozen list of candidate explanations, which we call descriptors. Janus scores each descriptor by its error-rate lift, then compares real descriptors with fake ones that have the same frequencies but are randomly assigned to examples. A descriptor is confirmed only if it beats this decoy floor on the data used for discovery and then repeats on separate held-out data. In a controlled audit of multi-table lookup tasks, Janus identifies the planted failure, confirming long-chain descriptors and their interactions. The LLM often stops partway through the lookup chain instead of reaching the final answer. On two public benchmarks, MuSiQue and LongBench v2, the SliceLine baseline flags plausible high-error pockets, but Janus confirms none of them. Ablations show why both safeguards matter. On LongBench v2, an uncalibrated fixed threshold reports 20 descriptors, the decoy floor leaves one, and the holdout check rejects the last one after its lift shrinks from 0.36 to 0.05. The resulting principle separates proposing explanations from reporting them. Candidates may come from any source, but only those that beat decoys and replicate on fresh data become audit findings.

</details>


### [442] [Beyond Convolution: Advancing Hypergraph Neural Networks with Hypergraph U-Nets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09051)
*Fuli Wang,Wei Qian,Daniel L. Lau,Gonzalo R. Arce*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper pioneers U-Net architectures for hypergraph data by proposing Parallel Hierarchical Pooling (PHPool) and Unpooling (PHUnpool) operators that preserve structural information through hierarchical clustering, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While convolutions have been extended to hypergraphs, the U-Net architecture (requiring pooling/unpooling) has remained unexplored for hypergraph data due to the lack of well-defined operations that retain structural information.

Method: The authors propose PHPool and PHUnpool operators that construct pooling and unpooling operations simultaneously by cutting a hierarchical clustering dendrogram at different granularities, enabling global, parallel, and structure-preserving down/up-sampling.

Result: The model demonstrates superior performance over state-of-the-art graph and hypergraph deep learning methods on hypergraph reconstruction simulation, hypergraph classification, and node-level anomaly detection tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical parallel pooling/unpooling approach effectively enables U-Net architectures for hypergraphs by preserving maximal structural information and achieving efficient computation.

Abstract: Convolutions have successfully transitioned from image processing to the complex realm of non-Euclidean higher-order domains, particularly in hypergraphs. Despite the success in convolution, the exploration of a popular architecture named U-Net remains largely unexplored for hypergraph data due to the lack of well-defined pooling and unpooling operations. This work pioneers the study of U-Net architectures for hypergraph data, addressing the critical challenge of designing effective pooling and unpooling operations that retain maximal structural information from the input hypergraph. Motivated by hierarchical clustering, we propose to construct the pooling and unpooling operators all at once by cutting the clustering dendrogram at different granularities, named the Parallel Hierarchical Pooling (PHPool) and Unpooling (PHUnpool) operators. Unlike existing pooling methods that risk local structural damage through a sequential learning procedure, our PHPool operators are designed in a global and parallel manner to ensure fidelity to the original hypergraph structure with efficient computation while the PHUnpool operators are tailored to perform inverse operations of the PHPools for hypergraph reconstruction. We validate our model through hypergraph reconstruction simulation, hypergraph classification, and node-level anomaly detection, where it demonstrates superior performance over existing state-of-the-art graph and hypergraph deep learning methods.

</details>


### [443] [INFUSER: Influence-Guided Self-Evolution Improves Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09052)
*Siyu Chen,Miao Lu,Beining Wu,Heejune Sheen,Fengzhuo Zhang,Shuangning Li,Zhiyuan Li,Jose Blanchet,Tianhao Wang,Zhuoran Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INFUSER is an iterative co-training framework where a Generator creates questions from unstructured documents and a Solver improves by training on them; the Generator is rewarded by an optimizer-aware influence score (via DuGRPO) measuring whether questions improve the Solver, creating an adaptive curriculum. It achieves over 20% relative improvement on Olympiad and SuperGPQA benchmarks over strong self-evolution baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolution methods either depend on heavily curated/teacher-generated training data, or use unsupervised generation rewarded by a difficulty heuristic that doesn't guarantee solver improvement. The authors aim to create a self-evolution framework that adaptively generates useful training data without external supervision.

Method: INFUSER uses a co-training loop: (1) a Generator drafts questions and golden answers from unstructured document pools; (2) a Solver trains on these with standard correctness rewards; (3) the Generator is rewarded via an influence score measuring each question's actual contribution to solver improvement; (4) a dual-normalized GRPO variant (DuGRPO) handles the noisy influence score for generator training.

Result: On Qwen3-8B-Base, INFUSER outperforms strong self-evolution baselines with over 20% relative improvement on Olympiad and SuperGPQA benchmarks. An 8B INFUSER co-evolving generator outperforms a frozen 32B thinking generator on math and coding tasks.

Conclusion: Each design choice (co-training, influence-based reward, DuGRPO) is necessary. INFUSER generalizes well to instruction-finetuned anchors and can be augmented with rule-verifiable RLVR data, demonstrating flexibility and broad applicability.

Abstract: Self-evolution offers a scalable path to stronger reasoning: a pretrained language model improves itself with only minimal external supervision. Yet existing methods either depend on extensively curated or teacher-generated training data, or, when the generator runs unsupervised, reward it by a difficulty heuristic that need not improve the solver. We introduce INFUSER, an iterative co-training framework with two co-evolving roles: a Generator that drafts questions and reference golden answers from a pool of unstructured, automatically collected documents, and a Solver that improves by training on them. The solver is trained with standard correctness rewards against the generator-provided answers, while the generator is rewarded by an optimizer-aware influence score that measures whether each proposed question would actually improve the solver on the target distribution. Because this continuous, noisy influence score is poorly served by standard GRPO, we propose DuGRPO, a dual-normalized variant of GRPO, for generator training. Together, these turn the document pool into an adaptive curriculum that favors questions useful to the current solver, not just hard ones. On Qwen3-8B-Base, INFUSER outperforms strong self-evolution baselines with over 20% relative improvement on Olympiad and SuperGPQA benchmarks, and an 8B INFUSER co-evolving generator outperforms a frozen 32B thinking generator on math and coding. Ablations confirm each design choice is necessary, and two extensions, applying INFUSER to an instruction-finetuned anchor and augmenting it with rule-verifiable RLVR data, further demonstrate the flexibility and generalizability of the framework. Code is available at https://github.com/FFishy-git/INFUSER.

</details>


### [444] [Stage-1 Controls the Entropy Regime, Not the Outcome](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09059)
*Jianxiong Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies two-stage post-training for VLMs (SFT/OPD warm-start followed by RL) using Qwen2.5-VL-7B, finding that Stage-1 primarily affects policy entropy regimes but has limited downstream impact on final performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what the Stage-1 warm-start (SFT vs OPD) actually controls in the two-stage post-training pipeline for VLMs, given that different warm-starting methods are used but their effects are not well-characterized.

Method: Used Qwen2.5-VL-7B with a same-modality 72B VLM teacher for OPD, compared three warm-start methods (SFT, OPD, early-stopped SFT), evaluated on Geometry3K (in-domain) and MathVista (out-of-domain), measuring accuracy, entropy, answer diversity, and pass@16.

Result: All warm-starts reach narrow 53-54% on Geometry3K; early-stopped SFT improves MathVista by +2.1 points; OPD shows substantially higher policy entropy and answer diversity (+2.0 to +5.2 pass@16) but these advantages disappear after RL training.

Conclusion: Stage-1 is strongly associated with entropy regime but downstream payoff is small and localized; there is no evidence that OPD is a better RL warm-start than SFT.

Abstract: Two-stage post-training -- a Stage-1 warm-start (supervised fine-tuning, SFT, or on-policy distillation, OPD) followed by Stage-2 reinforcement learning (RL) -- is increasingly used for vision-language models (VLMs). We ask what Stage-1 actually controls in a small-data study using Qwen2.5-VL-7B with a same-modality 72B VLM teacher for OPD. First, the three warm-starts reach a narrow $53$--$54\%$ band on Geometry3K internal validation, consistent with the narrow range reported by recent specialized methods; this setup provides little evidence that Stage-1 changes the in-domain endpoint. Second, a matched-recipe, early-stopped SFT improves out-of-domain MathVista by $+2.1$ points, reversing the $-9.5$-point drop of an over-trained variant. The clearest difference is the \emph{entropy regime}: OPD enters RL with substantially higher policy entropy than either SFT initialization, and the separation remains visible through the available trajectories. At the in-domain initialization, OPD also has higher answer diversity and pass@16 ($+2.0$ to $+5.2$ points over SFT), although problem-level bootstrap intervals show that the smaller contrast is uncertain. The advantage is absent after RL (endpoint pass@16 values within $1.1$ points) and on MathVista (six models within $1.2$ points). Our contribution is therefore a bounded empirical characterization: Stage-1 is strongly associated with the entropy regime in this setup, but the downstream payoff is small, localized, and not evidence that OPD is a better RL warm-start.

</details>


### [445] [OnlyDense: Reduced-Order Modeling for Lagrangian simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09065)
*Tu Do,Shannon Ryan,Santu Rana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a neural basis function framework for scalable representation and dynamics modeling of massive Lagrangian particle systems (like SPH/MPM) by treating the state as a function in Hilbert space, achieving high accuracy with as few as 32 basis functions on simulations with over 1 million particles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lagrangian simulation methods (SPH, MPM) are computationally expensive for multi-scale phenomena; existing graph-based or nonlinear latent manifold methods are inadequate for massive particle systems.

Method: Treats system state as a function in Hilbert space, approximates state space with a linear subspace spanned by learned neural basis functions, enabling direct projection to latent coefficients and explicit access to basis functions.

Result: Achieves R² > 0.99 with only 32 basis functions on large-scale SPH simulations with over 1 million particles, including extreme deformation and fragmentation events.

Conclusion: The framework unifies classical projection-based reduced-order modeling with deep learning, is invariant to discretization points, and accurately reconstructs and predicts complex dynamics.

Abstract: In science and engineering, Lagrangian simulation methods such as Smooth Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) or Material Point Method (MPM) are often employed to study the behavior of dynamic systems. However, these methods can be prohibitively computationally expensive, particularly when simulating multi-scale spatial or temporal phenomena, e.g., void growth and coalescence within macro-scale geometries, structural failure of spacecraft components resulting from hypervelocity impact of space debris particles, etc. In contrast to graph-based methods, where the state of the system is understood as a discrete set of particles, we propose a learning framework for scalable representation and dynamics modeling of massive particle systems by treating the system state as a function and its evolution as a trajectory in Hilbert space. Rather than representing the state as a discrete set of particles or embedding it in a nonlinear latent manifold, we approximate the state space with a linear subspace spanned by learned neural basis functions. This parameterization enables direct projection to obtain latent coefficients and explicit access to the basis functions, avoiding optimization over a nonlinear latent space. The resulting representation admits a natural interpretation: latent variables correspond to coefficients in Hilbert space, and basis functions correspond to spatial modes, analogous to Proper Orthogonal Decomposition. The framework thus unifies classical projection-based reduced-order modeling with modern deep learning, while remaining invariant to the number of discretization points. Experiments on large-scale SPH simulations with over one million particles, including dynamic events with extreme deformation and fragmentation, demonstrate that the proposed method accurately reconstructs and predicts dynamics, achieving an R$^2$ score above $0.99$ with as few as $32$ basis functions.

</details>


### [446] [A Unifying Lens on Reward Uncertainty in RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09073)
*Ely Hahami,Yoel Zimmermann,Ray Zhou,Jack Benarroch Jedlicki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using distributional reward models to address reward hacking in RLHF, deriving a closed-form effective reward that unifies existing pessimistic aggregation heuristics (mean, worst-case, uncertainty-weighted optimization) under a single theoretical framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF suffers from reward hacking due to proxy reward model errors, and standard scalar RMs lack principled uncertainty quantification for pessimistic mitigation.

Method: The authors argue for distributional reward models p(r|x,y) and derive a closed-form effective reward under KL-regularized RLHF via Bayesian inference or KL-DRO lens, showing that existing heuristic ensemble aggregation methods (mean, WCO, UWO) are special cases or truncations of this unified expression.

Result: A unified closed-form expression for pessimistic reward aggregation that subsumes mean, worst-case optimization, and uncertainty-weighted optimization as limits or truncations, clarifying implicit assumptions of each heuristic.

Conclusion: Distributional reward models provide a principled foundation for pessimism in RLHF, theoretically unifying and explaining prior heuristic aggregation methods.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is bottlenecked by \emph{reward hacking}, where the policy exploits errors in a proxy reward model (RM) and produces high RM scores without genuine quality gains. A natural mitigation is \emph{pessimism}: penalizing rewards in regions where the RM is uncertain. However, standard scalar RMs provide no principled notion of uncertainty. We argue that the right object is a \emph{distributional} reward model $p(r\mid x,y)$. Under either a Bayesian inference or a KL-distributionally robust optimization (KL-DRO) lens, the KL-regularized RLHF objective admits a closed-form effective reward $\tilde r(x,y) = \pmβ\log\mathbb{E}_p[e^{\pm r/β}]$. The pessimistic branch unifies the prior heuristics for RM ensemble aggregation: mean aggregation, worst-case optimization (WCO), and uncertainty-weighted optimization (UWO) all emerge as limits or truncations of this single expression. This also clarifies the implicit assumptions of each existing rule.

</details>


### [447] [Neural Legendre-Fenchel transform with Hessian Preconditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09077)
*Basile Plus-Gourdon,Frank Nielsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Hessian-based preconditioning strategy for the deep Legendre-Fenchel transform, leveraging affine invariance to improve convergence and accuracy for ill-conditioned convex functions in high dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The LF transform is fundamental in convex analysis and machine learning, but existing numerical methods (including deep learning approaches) struggle with ill-conditioned functions where closed-form conjugates are unavailable.

Method: The method reformulates LF transform as projective polarity with affine invariance. It applies an affine deformation around a minimizer so the second-order Taylor approximation matches the canonical paraboloid (identity conjugation map). A residual network learns this simplified mapping, and the original conjugation is recovered via inverse deformation. Preconditioning adds only one eigendecomposition at initialization and two matrix-vector multiplications per query.

Result: Experiments on diverse convex functions, including high-dimensional benchmarks, show improved convergence rates and enhanced numerical accuracy, with particularly significant gains for ill-conditioned problems.

Conclusion: The preconditioning strategy effectively addresses ill-conditioning in numerical LF transforms with modest overhead, though the paper also discusses limitations and scope of applicability.

Abstract: The Legendre-Fenchel (LF) transform is a fundamental tool in convex analysis and machine learning that maps lower semi-continuous functions to their convex conjugates. In practice, when closed-form formula are not available for expressing convex conjugates of given functions, one must approximate them using various techniques. One recent such versatile numerical method is the deep Legendre transform method which relies on neural networks although it remains challenging particularly for tackling ill-conditioned functions. This work builds on the reformulation of the LF transform as a projective polarity. A notable property of this framework is its affine invariance. We leverage this affine invariance to introduce a Hessian-based preconditioning strategy. Specifically, we apply an affine deformation around a minimizer so that the second-order Taylor approximation of the function coincides with the canonical paraboloid, whose conjugation map is the identity. A residual network initialized near the identity can then learn this simplified mapping, while the original conjugation map is recovered through the inverse deformation. The proposed preconditioning incurs only a modest computational overhead, consisting of a single eigendecomposition during initialization and two matrix-vector multiplications per query. Experiments on a diverse set of convex functions, including high-dimensional benchmarks, demonstrate improved convergence rates and enhanced numerical accuracy of the conjugation, with particularly significant gains for ill-conditioned problems. Finally, we discuss the scope of applicability of our proposed method and highlight several of its limitations.

</details>


### [448] [The Hidden Bias of Process Reward Models:PRISM for Rewarding the Right Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09078)
*Aakriti Agrawal,Souradip Chakraborty,Armin Saghafian,Nihal Sharma,Rizal Fathony,Nam H Nguyen,C. Bayan Bruss,Amrit Singh Bedi,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRMs suffer from step-level data imbalance causing false positives that steer reasoning astray. PRISM, a contrastive training framework with temporal lookahead and difficulty-aware curriculum, reduces false positives by 22% and improves downstream task accuracy by up to 33%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PRM training using cross-entropy on imbalanced step-level data produces high false-positive rates, where plausible but incorrect steps are overcredited. These false positives actively mislead downstream reasoning tasks (Best-of-N, guided decoding, policy optimization), unlike false negatives which mainly slow exploration.

Method: PRISM (Precision Ranking for Improved Step Modeling) is a policy-aware PRM training framework that uses: (1) contrastive step-level comparisons with hard negatives generated by temporal lookahead, (2) no additional human labels, and (3) a difficulty-aware curriculum to optimize the contrastive step margin.

Result: On PRMBench and ProcessBench, PRISM reduces false positives by 22% and improves macro F1 over strong discriminative PRMs. For downstream tasks: guided decoding accuracy improves up to 22%, Best-of-N selection accuracy improves up to 33%.

Conclusion: Trustworthy process supervision requires moving beyond pointwise label fitting to reliable relative comparisons; rewarding the right reasoning for the right reasons is more important than simply assigning high rewards.

Abstract: Process Reward Models (PRMs) improve credit assignment for reasoning by providing step-level feedback. However, we identify a hidden bias in PRMs caused by severe imbalance in step-level training data. Standard cross-entropy training amplifies this bias, causing PRMs to overcredit plausible but incorrect steps and produce high false-positive rates. We show that these false positives have an asymmetric downstream effect: false negatives mainly slow exploration, whereas false positives actively steer Best-of-N selection, guided decoding, and policy optimization toward flawed reasoning. This suggests that PRM training should shift from pointwise label fitting to reliable relative comparisons. To address this, we propose PRISM (Precision Ranking for Improved Step Modeling), a policy-aware PRM training framework that learns from contrastive step-level comparisons and hard negatives generated by a temporal lookahead strategy, requiring no new human labels. We further use a difficulty-aware curriculum to optimize the contrastive step margin. Across PRMBench and ProcessBench, PRISM substantially reduces false positives (22% on PRMBench) and improves macro F1 over strong discriminative PRMs. When applied to policy optimization and search tasks, including guided decoding and Best-of-N selection, it consistently improves accuracy (up to 22% for guided decoding and 33% for Best-of-N) and robustness. More broadly, trustworthy process supervision is not just about assigning high rewards, but about rewarding the right reasoning for the right reasons.

</details>


### [449] [FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4: Lightning Index Ultra-Long Context via Lookahead Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09079)
*Yan Wang,Qifan Zhang,Jiachen Yu,Tian Liang,Dongyang Ma,Xiang Hu,Zibo Lin,Chunyang Li,Zhichao Wang,Jia Li,Yujiu Yang,Haitao Mi,Dong Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LSA introduces a Neural Memory Indexer (based on DeepSeek-V4) that proactively predicts and caches only query-critical KV chunks, reducing GPU memory to 13.5% of full-context baseline while slightly improving accuracy (+0.6%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional LLMs suffer from severe GPU memory bottlenecks during ultra-long context serving because they keep the full KV cache loaded throughout decoding.

Method: Proposes Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA) with a Neural Memory Indexer using a backbone-free decoupled training strategy (dual-encoder architecture) trained independently via standard retrieval frameworks without loading the massive backbone model into GPU memory.

Result: Compresses KV cache footprint to 13.5% of full-context baseline, preserves/slightly elevates accuracy (+0.6%), and at 500K scales suppresses physical KV cache overhead by over 90% without destabilizing reasoning capacities.

Conclusion: The "less is more" paradigm maximizes serving efficiency while acting as an effective attention denoiser for long-term global memory tasks.

Abstract: Conventional LLMs keep the full KV cache loaded during decoding, causing a severe GPU memory bottleneck for ultra-long context serving. In this report, we propose Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA), a novel inference paradigm powered by a Neural Memory Indexer built upon the DeepSeek-V4 architecture. Rather than passively attending to all historical tokens, LSA proactively predicts future context demands and preserves only the query-critical KV chunks in the GPU memory. Crucially, we instantiate this architecture via a backbone-free decoupled training strategy. By formulating the indexer as a standard dual-encoder architecture, we train it independently using standard retrieval training frameworks without ever loading the massive backbone model into GPU memory.
  We demonstrate that this "less is more" paradigm significantly maximizes serving efficiency while acting as an effective attention denoiser in tasks that rely on long-term global memory. Across primary long-context evaluation suites (e.g., LongBench-v2, LongMemEval, and RULER), FM-DS-V4 compresses the average physical KV cache footprint down to merely 13.5% of the full-context baseline, while consistently preserving or slightly elevating downstream accuracy (+0.6% absolute margin on average). Crucially, at extreme 500K scales, FlashMemory suppresses the physical KV cache overhead by over 90% without destabilizing the backbone's core reasoning capacities.

</details>


### [450] [Beyond FLOPs: Benchmarking Real Inference Acceleration of LLM Pruning under a GEMM-Centric Taxonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09080)
*Haozhe Hu,Hao Wu,Anhao Zhao,Longwei Ding,Peiran Yin,Yunpu Ma,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a GEMM-centric taxonomy to unify comparison of LLM pruning methods and systematically evaluates the acceleration-quality Pareto frontier, finding static depth pruning is the strongest baseline across most settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite many pruning methods claiming acceleration, their realized speedups vary hugely due to hardware and kernel dependencies, making practical benefits poorly understood across different pruning families.

Method: Introduces a GEMM-centric taxonomy that reorganizes pruning methods by M, N, K dimensions of matrix multiplication, and builds a unified benchmarking framework for implementation-consistent comparison across the pruning design space.

Result: Static depth pruning is the strongest Pareto-optimal baseline. During prefill, the frontier shifts from static depth (0-4% loss) to dynamic depth (5-16% loss) to static width pruning (17-26% loss).

Conclusion: Provides the first unified view of practical limits of pruning-based LLM acceleration and offers guidance for future pruning research.

Abstract: Pruning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for accelerating large language model (LLM) inference, spanning a broad spectrum of methods that remove computation across tokens, layers, heads, dimensions, and attention patterns. Despite sharing the same objective, these pruning approaches induce fundamentally different execution behaviors, causing realized speedups to depend heavily on hardware and kernel implementations. Consequently, the practical acceleration benefits of different pruning families remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a GEMM-centric taxonomy that reorganizes existing pruning methods according to the logical \textbf{M}, \textbf{N}, and \textbf{K} dimensions of general matrix multiplication (GEMM). Leveraging this abstraction, we build a unified benchmarking framework that enables implementation-consistent comparison across the pruning design space and systematically characterizes the acceleration--quality Pareto frontier. Our results show that static depth pruning remains the strongest Pareto-optimal baseline and stays closest to its theoretical acceleration upper bound in memory-bounded scenarios. During prefill, the frontier transitions from static depth at low quality loss (0\%--4\%), to dynamic depth at moderate loss (5\%--16\%), and finally to static width pruning at higher loss levels (17\%--26\%). These findings establish the first unified view of the practical limits of pruning-based LLM acceleration and provide guidance for future pruning research.\footnote{Code is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/LLM-Pruning/tree/main/PruningInferSim}

</details>


### [451] [Stabilizing On-Policy Distillation for MLLM Reasoning with Global Normalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09091)
*Dongze Hao,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNDPO stabilizes on-policy distillation by replacing unstable token-level KL scores with batch-normalized relative advantages, improving robustness and performance in multimodal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) offers dense supervision advantages over RLVR, but naive token-level distillation suffers from gradient instability caused by magnitude misalignment in outlier states.

Method: Proposes Globally Normalized Distillation Policy Optimization (GNDPO), which transforms raw KL scores into batch-level relative advantages to stabilize optimization while retaining token-level guidance.

Result: GNDPO substantially improves training robustness and downstream performance across multimodal reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Batch-level normalization effectively mitigates gradient explosions in on-policy distillation, making it a practical solution for post-training with stronger teacher models.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has recently emerged as an important post-training paradigm. By using a stronger teacher model to provide dense, fine-grained supervision for sampled trajectories, OPD offers a clear advantage over reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which typically depends on sparse binary or outcome-based environmental feedback. However, naive token-level distillation can suffer from gradient instability, due to magnitude misalignment in outlier states. To address this issue, we propose Globally Normalized Distillation Policy Optimization (GNDPO), a practical method that stabilizes optimization by transforming raw KL scores into batch-level relative advantages. This normalization effectively mitigates gradient explosions while retaining the benefits of token-level guidance. Experimental results show that GNDPO substantially improves training robustness and downstream performance across multimodal reasoning tasks. The code is released at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/GNDPO.

</details>


### [452] [From Shortcuts to Reasoning: Robust Post-Training of Theory of Mind with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09092)
*Jike Zhong,Yuxiang Lai,Ming Li,Yuheng Li,Wuao Liu,Behzad Dariush,Konstantinos Psounis,Shao-Yuan Lo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that many Theory of Mind (ToM) evaluations suffer from "shortcut" issues (reaching 99% accuracy via spurious correlations), and proposes Thinking-RFT (Reinforcement Fine-Tuning with verifiable rewards + explicit reasoning chains) which outperforms SFT by 6% on average and up to 10% on complex reasoning, with better generalization and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior ToM post-training progress is confounded by shortcut learning—models exploit spurious correlations rather than true ToM reasoning. The authors aim to systematically detect these shortcuts and develop more robust training methods.

Method: First, a framework is developed to analyze ToM datasets for shortcuts (finding "belief" tracking tasks especially vulnerable). Then, Thinking-RFT is proposed: Reinforcement Fine-Tuning with verifiable rewards combined with explicit reasoning chains. It is evaluated on four shortcut-free datasets across three ToM contexts, compared against SFT and non-thinking variants.

Result: Thinking-RFT improves ToM by 6% over SFT overall (10% on higher-order reasoning, 7% on multimodal cases). It generalizes better to unseen domains and counterfactuals. The joint effect of reasoning+RL is critical—Thinking-RFT outperforms Non-Thinking-RFT by 7%. RFT works by grounding reasoning on anchor cues (keywords, state changes) corresponding to causal factors.

Conclusion: The study provides guidance for creating shortcut-free ToM datasets and demonstrates that Thinking-RFT is an effective approach for advancing robust ToM capabilities in foundation models.

Abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) is a must-acquire skill for modern foundation model systems to operate effectively and safely in the real world. Recent works have explored honing ToM via post-training; however, we show that such progress is confounded by a pervasive "shortcut" issue: tasks can reach up to 99% accuracy by simply exploiting spurious causal correlations, leading to a false sense of ToM. Motivated by this, we first develop a framework to systematically examine ToM datasets for shortcuts and provide guidance for future development. We find that questions reducible to pure state tracking, such as "belief," are especially shortcut-prone compared to mind questions, such as "intention," where reasoning beyond tracking is required. Using four shortcut-free datasets across three ToM contexts, we then comprehensively study whether Reinforcement Fine-Tuning with verifiable rewards and explicit reasoning chains, called Thinking-RFT, elevates ToM beyond Supervised Fine-Tuning, or SFT. Our key findings are as follows. First, Thinking-RFT effectively improves ToM in all scenarios, with a 6% improvement over SFT, particularly in complex higher-order reasoning, with a 10% improvement over SFT, and multimodal cases, with a 7% improvement over SFT. It also generalizes notably better to unseen domains and higher-order queries while being more robust to counterfactuals. Second, ToM benefits specifically from the joint effect of reasoning and RL: Thinking-RFT outperforms Non-Thinking-RFT by 7% on average. Third, RFT works by learning to ground its reasoning on anchor cues, such as keywords and state changes, that correspond to causal factors. We believe our study is useful for developing effective and robust ToM post-training datasets and advancing critical ToM capabilities.

</details>


### [453] [Addressing Market Regime Changes and Heavy-Tailed Returns in Portfolio Optimization via Bayesian VAR and Elliptical Black-Litterman](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09104)
*Daniil Mikriukov,Ruoyu Sun,Angelos Stefanidis,Jionglong Su,Zhengyong Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BAVAR-BLED combines Bayesian VAR averaging and Black-Litterman with elliptical distributions in a TD3 architecture to handle fat-tailed returns and regime changes, achieving superior portfolio performance (Sharpe 1.72, Sortino 2.70, 57.26% returns) on DJIA constituents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DRL portfolio optimization frameworks ignore fat-tailed return distributions and treat historical data homogeneously, causing poor performance during regime changes and extreme market events.

Method: The BAVAR-BLED algorithm integrates BAVAR (multi-scale temporal features, regime-aware estimates) as prior inputs to BLED (Student's t-distributions for fat tails), within a TD3 architecture using transformer networks for view construction and CNNs for risk-aversion estimates.

Result: Evaluated on 29 DJIA constituents over a decade, BAVAR-BLED achieved Sharpe ratio 1.72, Sortino ratio 2.70, and total returns of 57.26%, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses fat-tailed returns and regime changes in portfolio optimization, demonstrating substantial practical improvements over existing DRL approaches.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) frameworks for portfolio optimization have shown promise for their ability to learn allocation rules dynamically from market data. However, these models fail to account for fat-tailed returns, which characterize actual market behavior with more frequent extreme events. Furthermore, historical data is treated homogeneously, without accounting for temporal importance, leading models to fail during regime changes. We propose a new BAVAR-BLED algorithm that combines methods derived from Bayesian-Averaging Vector Autoregressive (BAVAR) and the Black-Litterman model using Elliptical Distributions (BLED) within a TD3 architecture.
