Extensive experiments across multiple TFCL settings and benchmarks demonstrate that LargeMonitor achieves precise, robust detection and diagnosis of complex data streams while consistently improving the performance of existing online TFCL algorithms.

</details>


### [480] [Graph Mamba Operator: A Latent Simulator for Interacting Particle Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09432)
*Karn Tiwari,Niladri Dutta,N M Anoop Krishnan,Prathosh A P*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraMO is a latent-space simulator combining state-space models with graph neural networks for long-horizon prediction in interacting dynamical systems, achieving state-of-the-art results on N-body, motion capture, and robotics benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN-based simulators suffer from error accumulation over long horizons due to autoregressive rollouts, separate treatment of spatial and temporal dynamics, and limited ability to capture multi-hop dependencies and global structure.

Method: GraMO integrates state-space models with graph-based interaction learning, coupling graph interactions and temporal state updates within a single recurrence using a linear update with input-dependent coefficients.

Result: Achieves lowest error across N-body systems, motion capture, and robotics benchmarks, with the largest gains in long-horizon prediction.

Conclusion: The unified spatial-temporal recurrence in GraMO effectively reduces error accumulation and outperforms prior methods that treat spatial and temporal updates separately.

Abstract: Modeling interacting dynamical systems requires capturing spatial interactions alongside long-range temporal dependencies. Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a natural representation but typically rely on autoregressive rollouts and treat spatial and temporal dynamics separately, leading to error accumulation over long horizons. Existing approaches also focus on local interactions and short temporal contexts, limiting their ability to capture multi-hop dependencies and global structure. We introduce the Graph Mamba Operator (GraMO), a latent-space simulator that integrates state-space models with graph-based interaction learning. In contrast to prior work that sequences nodes or applies spatial and temporal updates in separate stages, GraMO couples graph-based interactions and temporal state updates within a single recurrence. The update is linear in the latent state, with input-dependent coefficients that adapt across regimes. We evaluate GraMO on N-body systems, motion capture, and robotics datasets, achieving the lowest error across benchmarks and the largest gains in long-horizon prediction.

</details>


### [481] [Operator learning for solving Fokker-Planck equations with various initial conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09434)
*Li Zeng,Xiaoliang Wan,Yaobin Wang,Fabio Nobile,Tao Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a conditional normalizing flow-based PINN framework to efficiently solve the Fokker-Planck equation across a range of initial conditions, using the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation and a linearized SDE-based base distribution to handle Dirac delta singularities, with a time-weighted loss function for numerical stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently approximate the solution operator of the Fokker-Planck equation for a whole range of initial conditions, addressing challenges with Dirac delta initial distributions and numerical instabilities at small times.

Method: (1) Reformulates the FPE problem using the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation to approximate a transition PDF from a Dirac mass. (2) Uses a linearized SDE's PDF as the base distribution for normalizing flow to avoid singularities. (3) Introduces a time-weighted loss function to balance causality and training difficulty.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method for solving the FPE across various initial conditions.

Conclusion: The conditional normalizing flow-based PINN framework successfully approximates the solution operator of the FPE, overcoming challenges related to Dirac delta initial conditions and small-time numerical instabilities.

Abstract: The Fokker-Planck equation (FPE) plays a pivotal role in describing the time evolution of probability density functions (PDFs) for systems governed by stochastic dynamics. In this work, we propose a conditional normalizing flow-based physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for efficiently approximating the solution operator of the FPE for a whole range of initial conditions. Leveraging the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation for Markovian stochastic processes, the problem is reformulated into approximating a transition PDF starting at initial time from a Dirac mass centered at an arbitrary point. The PDF of an associated linearized stochastic differential equation (SDE) is employed as the base distribution for the normalizing flow, providing a good approximation of the target PDF, especially for small times, and thereby avoiding the singularity of the map associated with the Dirac delta initial distribution. Furthermore, a time-weighted loss function is introduced to mitigate numerical instabilities arising at small times, achieving a balance between causality and training difficulty as time progresses. A variety of numerical experiments are presented to illustrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method.

</details>


### [482] [Breaking the Tokenizer Barrier: On-Policy Distillation across Model Families](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09456)
*Yifan Niu,Han Xiao,Dongyi Liu,Zelong Wang,Dihong Gong,Yasheng Wang,Jia Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a cross-tokenizer On-Policy Distillation method that enables knowledge transfer between LLMs with different tokenizers, using a precise token-mapping algorithm to propagate token-level signals, achieving better compute efficiency than SFT-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OPD methods require teacher and student models to share the same tokenizer, limiting applicability. Current cross-tokenizer practice relies on SFT of teacher responses, which fails to capture rich knowledge in the teacher's probability distribution.

Method: The authors enable standard on-policy distillation to work across model families by designing a precise token-mapping algorithm that allows high-fidelity token-level signals to propagate across different tokenizers.

Result: Cross-tokenizer OPD is significantly more compute-efficient than SFT-based baselines across various benchmarks, unlocking a broader range of teacher-student pairs.

Conclusion: This work expands the applicability of OPD to cross-tokenizer scenarios, opening new avenues for adapting and enhancing interactions between LLMs.

Abstract: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) has become a core technique in the post-training of Large Language Models (LLMs) for transferring knowledge from domain experts to student models. However, existing OPD distillation methods require teacher and student models to share the same tokenizer, restricting the applicability of OPD within the model series. Current mainstream practice typically employs Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on teacher-generated responses for cross-tokenizer distillation, which fails to capture the rich knowledge embedded in the teacher's probability distribution. In this work, we enable the standard on-policy distillation method to operate across model families, ensuring that high-fidelity token-level signals can propagate across different tokenizers with a precise token-mapping algorithm. Extensive experiments show that cross-tokenizer OPD is significantly more compute-efficient than baselines on various benchmarks. Our results unlock a broader range of teacher-student pairs for OPD, opening up new avenues for adapting and enhancing interactions between LLMs.

</details>


### [483] [Escaping the KL Agreement Trap in On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09471)
*Haoran Xin,Anhao Zhao,Ying Sun,Jin Li,Xiaoyu Shen,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a "low-KL agreement trap" in on-policy distillation where teacher models provide weak supervision when students drift into degenerate states, and proposes KAT, a termination rule that detects and filters such weak supervision, improving mathematical reasoning performance while significantly reducing computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation can suffer from a "low-KL agreement trap" where the teacher locally agrees with a degraded student state, producing low reverse KL divergence but minimal corrective training signal, thus hindering effective learning.

Method: KAT (KL Agreement Trap Termination) - an online termination rule for on-policy distillation that detects persistent low-KL agreement using a dynamic training-adaptive threshold to filter out weak supervision from degenerate agreement states.

Result: Improves avg@k accuracy by 2.66% and pass@k by 3.43% across four mathematical benchmarks, while reducing average rollout length by 59.73%.

Conclusion: Filtering weak supervision from degenerate agreement in on-policy distillation improves both model performance and computational efficiency, demonstrating that not all teacher-student agreement is beneficial for learning.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense token-level supervision by asking a teacher to score student-generated rollouts. However, when the student drifts into an unrecoverable prefix, the teacher may locally agree with the degraded state, producing low reverse KL but little corrective training signal. We identify this persistent regime as a low-KL agreement trap. Further analyses show that tokens during and after such traps produce less useful supervision signals. We propose KAT (KL Agreement Trap Termination), an online OPD termination rule that detects persistent low-KL agreement with a dynamic training-adaptive threshold. By filtering weak supervision from degenerate agreement, KAT improves avg@k accuracy by 2.66% and pass@k by 3.43% across four mathematical benchmarks, while reducing average rollout length by 59.73%.

</details>


### [484] [Loss-Guided Adaptive Scale Refinement for Molecular Force Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09480)
*Limin Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A loss-guided adaptive scale refinement framework is proposed for molecular force prediction, automatically discovering optimal modeling scales beyond predefined anchors, achieving significant MAE reductions (e.g., 399.65→381.23) in NaCl aqueous systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing molecular representation learning methods rely on manually predefined spatial scales, which may not align with task-optimal modeling scales due to the multi-scale nature of molecular interactions.

Method: A loss-guided adaptive scale refinement framework that starts from predefined scale anchors and optimizes through interpolation, routing, differentiable scale updates, and scale pool refinement.

Result: Oracle hard routing reduces force MAE from 399.65→382.67; continuous oracle interpolation further reduces to 380.96; close-contact MAE drops from 327.22→260.51; final updated scale pool {0,0.125,0.25,0.375,0.5,0.75,1} achieves 381.23 MAE.

Conclusion: Adaptive scale refinement is a promising direction for molecular representation learning, particularly when fixed-scale modeling is insufficient.

Abstract: Molecular systems involve interactions across multiple spatial scales, from local coordination and short-range perturbations to long-range electrostatic and solvent-mediated effects. However, most molecular representation learning methods rely on manually predefined scales, and the task-optimal modeling scale may not coincide with these fixed levels. This study introduces a loss-guided adaptive scale refinement framework for molecular force prediction, treating predefined scales as initial anchors and discovering task-effective resolutions through interpolation, routing, differentiable scale updates, and scale pool refinement.
  Using a NaCl aqueous ionic system as a minimal testbed, this study constructs short-scale and long-range force prediction branches and analyzes their complementarity. Oracle hard routing reduces the overall force MAE from 399.65 to 382.67, while continuous oracle interpolation further reduces it to 380.96. In close-contact regimes with nearest-ion distance below 0.6 nm, the close-contact MAE decreases from 327.22 to 260.51. A minimal scale pool update experiment shows that starting from endpoint anchors {0,1}, loss-guided updates automatically generate intermediate scales and recover most of the continuous oracle performance. The final updated scale pool {0,0.125,0.25,0.375,0.5,0.75,1} achieves an overall MAE of 381.23.
  These results support adaptive scale refinement as a promising direction for molecular representation learning, especially when fixed-scale modeling is insufficient.

</details>


### [485] [BUDDY: BUdget-Driven DYnamic Depth Routing for Adaptive Large Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09514)
*Yuhua Zhou,Shaoqi Yu,Shichao Weng,Changhai Zhou,Mingze Yin,Fei Yang,Aimin Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Buddy is a budget-driven dynamic depth routing framework for LLMs that selectively executes top-k Transformer layers based on input, uses first-layer KV cache for low-overhead context, supports decode-time rerouting, and optionally predicts compute budgets — achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs with strict budget control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have high inference cost due to depth and parameter scale. Existing depth pruning methods provide limited control under user-specific compute budgets and fail to adapt routing paths as context grows during decoding.

Method: Buddy uses a lightweight Decision Module to score intermediate layers conditioned on input and deterministically executes top-k layers. It reuses the first-layer KV cache as global context, pools it with the newest token representation before each routing decision, and includes an optional Budget Predictor when no explicit budget is given.

Result: Experiments on Llama-family and Qwen models show Buddy is competitive with strong static pruning baselines and often improves the accuracy-compute trade-off, while uniquely supporting strict budget control, decode-time rerouting, and multiple budgets within a single trained model.

Conclusion: Buddy enables flexible, budget-aware dynamic depth routing with superior adaptability during decoding, outperforming static pruning approaches in accuracy-efficiency balance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) incur high inference cost due to their depth and parameter scale. Depth pruning can reduce latency by skipping redundant Transformer blocks, but existing methods (i) provide limited control under user-specific compute budgets and (ii) typically fix the routing path, failing to adapt as the context grows during decoding. We propose Buddy, a budget-driven dynamic depth routing framework. Buddy uses a lightweight Decision Module to score intermediate layers conditioned on the input and deterministically executes the top-k layers to satisfy a given budget. To support decode-time adaptation, Buddy reuses the first-layer KV cache as a low-overhead global context source and pools it together with the newest token representation before each routing decision. When no explicit budget is provided, an optional Budget Predictor estimates an input-dependent compute level to balance quality and efficiency. Experiments on Llama-family and Qwen models show that Buddy is competitive with strong static pruning baselines and often improves the accuracy-compute trade-off, while uniquely supporting strict budget control, decode-time rerouting, and multiple budgets within a single trained model.

</details>


### [486] [Investigating Calibration Challenges in Probabilistic Electricity Price Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09517)
*Jan Niklas Lettner,Hadeer El Ashhab,Benjamin Schäfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a critical flaw in current probabilistic electricity price forecasting: proper scoring rules prioritize sharpness over calibration, leading to overconfident and unreliable forecasts. The authors argue for a shift toward calibration-aware approaches to ensure distributional integrity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As renewable energy integration increases market volatility, probabilistic electricity price forecasting is needed for risk management, but current scoring rules produce poorly calibrated, overconfident models.

Method: The paper highlights the gap between theoretical proper scoring rules and practical calibration, showing that models can become proxies for deterministic forecasts when calibration is neglected.

Result: Proper scoring rules prioritize sharpness over calibration, yielding statistically unreliable uncertainty estimates and overconfident forecasts in energy markets.

Conclusion: Future research must shift toward calibration-aware objectives and architectures to ensure distributional integrity of energy market forecasts.

Abstract: As renewable energy integration increases market volatility, probabilistic electricity price forecasting has become essential for effective risk management. However, current-proper-scoring rules often prioritize forecast sharpness at the expense of calibration, leading to overconfident and statistically unreliable uncertainty estimates. This work highlights the critical gap between theoretical scoring and practical calibration, demonstrating that models can become mere proxies for deterministic forecasts when reliability is neglected. We conclude that future research must shift toward calibration-aware objectives and architectures to ensure the distributional integrity of energy market forecasts.

</details>


### [487] [Efficient Traffic Prediction at Scale: A Systematic Study of STGCN Architectural Depth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09539)
*Soban Nasir Lone,Mohamed Abouelela,Taeyoung Yu,Jiwon Kim,Constantinos Antoniou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether STGCN architectures are over-parameterized for traffic prediction, finding that a single-block variant achieves near-optimal performance with significantly lower computational cost (61% lower CPU latency, 37% higher throughput) compared to the standard 2-block design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While STGNNs dominate traffic prediction, their computational demands hinder practical ITS deployment. Recent efficient alternatives exist, but the fundamental question of whether these architectures themselves are over-parameterized has been unexplored.

Method: Systematic comparison of 1-block, 2-block (standard), and 3-block STGCN variants across four diverse traffic datasets, measuring both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency (latency, throughput).

Result: 1-block STGCN achieves optimal short-term prediction (10 mins) on 3/4 datasets with only ≤1.8% relative error degradation at longer horizons. The 2-block variant incurs 61% higher CPU latency and 37% lower throughput. The 3-block offers <0.5% improvement at more than double computational cost.

Conclusion: The default 2-block STGCN may be over-parameterized for many traffic prediction applications, with significant implications for both practitioners deploying ITS systems and researchers benchmarking efficiency-focused methods.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) have become the dominant approach for traffic prediction, yet their computational requirements pose challenges for practical deployment in intelligent transportation systems (ITS). While recent work has proposed efficient alternatives to STGNNs, a fundamental question remains unexplored: are these architectures themselves over-parameterised? We examine this question using the Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (STGCN), one of the most widely adopted models in this domain. Through systematic experiments across four diverse traffic datasets, we compare 1-block, 2-block (standard), and 3-block STGCN variants. Our findings reveal that the single-block architecture achieves optimal performance for short-term prediction (10 mins) on three of four datasets, while incurring only marginal degradation ($\leq$1.8% relative error) at longer horizons. Crucially, the 2-block variant incurs 61% higher CPU inference latency and 37% lower throughput relative to 1-block -- substantial overhead for resource-constrained ITS deployment. The 3-block architecture offers no favourable tradeoff, more than doubling computational cost for $<$0.5% relative improvement. These results suggest that the default 2-block STGCN may be over-parameterised for many applications, with implications for both practitioners deploying traffic prediction systems and researchers benchmarking efficiency-focused methods.

</details>


### [488] [On Choosing the $μ$ Parameter in Gaussian Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09582)
*Bogdan Kulynych,Antti Honkela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides principled mappings from pure-DP ε to GDP μ by matching worst-case membership inference attack success, recommending μ≈ε/5 as a conservative conversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide principled, practical conversion rules between pure differential privacy (ε) and Gaussian differential privacy (μ) for reporting privacy guarantees in ML.

Method: Matches worst-case membership inference attack success across three metrics: multiplicative advantage at fixed FPR, precision at fixed recall, and standard privacy profile.

Result: Provides tabulated μ values across useful parameter ranges and recommends μ≈ε/5 as a conservative general-purpose conversion.

Conclusion: The conversion μ≈ε/5 offers a practical, conservative mapping from pure-DP to GDP for reporting privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Recent work argues for using Gaussian differential privacy (GDP) to report the privacy guarantees in privacy-preserving machine learning. We provide principled mappings from pure-DP $\varepsilon$ to GDP $μ$ by matching the worst-case success of a strong-adversary membership inference attack in terms of three metrics: multiplicative advantage at fixed FPR, precision at fixed recall, and the standard privacy profile. We tabulate $μ$ values across a useful range of parameters and recommend $μ\approx \varepsilon/5$ as a conservative general-purpose conversion.

</details>


### [489] [Assessing Sample Quality in Conditional Generation under Compositional Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09601)
*Berker Demirel,Valentino Maiorca,Marco Fumero,Theofanis Karaletsos,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a post-hoc trust score for evaluating conditional generative models in extrapolative regimes (where reference distributions are unavailable), combining global realism and attribute-wise faithfulness to enable filtering, ranking, and abstention of generated samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conditional generators are useful for exploring novel attribute combinations (e.g., in scientific domains), but standard evaluation metrics fail because they require a reference target distribution that doesn't exist in the extrapolative regime.

Method: The trust score combines two components: (1) global realism (compatibility with the real data manifold) and (2) attribute-wise faithfulness (whether a sample is closer to requested attributes than to alternatives). The score is post-hoc, per-sample, and requires only the training distribution under a mild coverage condition.

Result: The score enables meaningful comparisons across extrapolated generations, effective filtering/ranking/abstention. In biological imaging, selected samples preserve morphological structure better and improve downstream predictive performance. Similar gains on controlled vision benchmarks. The score can also be applied during generation before full decoding.

Conclusion: The proposed trust score addresses the evaluation circularity in extrapolative conditional generation, works with off-the-shelf pretrained models, and improves both sample quality and downstream task performance.

Abstract: Conditional generators provide a natural tool for controllable generation, including settings where the desired condition is a new composition of observed attributes or experimental factors. In many applications, especially in scientific domains, such models are attractive to explore conditions for which real samples are rare, expensive, or not yet observed. However, this creates a circularity for evaluation: standard conditional quality metrics require a reference target distribution, but in the extrapolative regime that distribution is unavailable by definition. We address this problem with a post-hoc, per-sample trust score for assessing conditional samples using only the training distribution. The score combines two estimable quantities: global realism, measuring compatibility with the real data manifold, and attribute-wise faithfulness, measuring whether a sample is closer to the requested attributes than to plausible alternatives. We show that the score can recover meaningful comparisons across extrapolated generations, under a mild coverage condition on the observed attributes. These comparisons enable effective filtering, ranking, and abstention of generations and can be used directly on off-the-shelf pretrained models. In biological imaging, selected samples preserve real morphological structure better and improve downstream predictive performance, while similar gains are observed on controlled vision benchmarks. Finally, we show how the score can be applied during generation, enabling abstention before full decoding. Code is available at https://github.com/berkerdemirel/faithful-cond-gen.

</details>


### [490] [Closure-Validated Circuit Discovery in Attention Heads: Co-activation Proposes, Ablation Disposes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09607)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Clustering attention heads by co-activation statistics can identify circuit proposals, but only causal closure testing (ablation validation) confirms whether they are true functional circuits; otherwise they may be spurious or even harmful to remove.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether cheap co-activation clustering signals can reliably identify attention-head circuits in transformer models, or whether more rigorous causal validation is needed.

Method: Adapt a sparse-autoencoder clustering recipe to attention heads, validate by causal ablation (closure test) rather than reconstruction, and compare against matched-random controls on Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B (dense), and OLMoE-1B-7B (MoE) models.

Result: Clustering passes closure for dense models (Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B) but fails for the MoE model (OLMoE-1B-7B) where ablation improves loss. Decoupling between clustering signals and functional importance is observed across training.

Conclusion: Cheap co-activation signals yield circuit proposals, not confirmed circuits; closure testing via causal ablation is necessary to separate real circuits from spurious correlations.

Abstract: Interpretability increasingly treats groups of components, not individual units, as the basic object, and proposes to find them by clustering co-activation statistics. We ask whether such a cheap signal actually identifies an attention-head circuit. Adapting a sparse-autoencoder clustering recipe to attention heads -- but validating by causal ablation rather than reconstruction -- we cluster heads and then run a closure test: ablate the discovered community and compare per-example damage to matched-random controls. Across two dense 1B-scale models (Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B) and two input distributions, the communities pass closure. In a Mixture-of-Experts model (OLMoE-1B-7B), route-conditional clustering recovers a statistically real signal that nonetheless does not survive closure -- ablation improves loss, the wrong direction. Extending closure across training, attention-target selectivity and participation ratio decouple from function in both directions. We conclude that a cheap signal is a circuit proposal, not a confirmed circuit; closure is what separates them.

</details>


### [491] [Constrained user-item allocation for e-commerce marketing campaigns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09623)
*Maja Lindström,Natalija Glisovic,Jan von Pichowski,Tommy Löfstedt,Martin Rosvall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper formalizes the "auto-targeting" problem for marketing campaigns (jointly selecting users and items to form disjoint campaign groups) and proposes three approaches: constrained spectral biclustering, greedy local search with swaps, and a multi-armed bandit framework for exploration. Biclustering achieves the highest quality, lift, and fairness, while bandit methods scale better on large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retailers need to decide both which products to promote and which users to target, but these decisions are coupled. Existing approaches either assume predefined campaign structures or decouple item selection from user assignment, failing to discover campaign groupings directly from joint user-item interaction patterns.

Method: The paper proposes three complementary strategies: (i) constrained spectral biclustering to find dense regions in the user-item affinity matrix, (ii) greedy local search with pairwise swaps for combinatorial refinement, and (iii) a multi-armed bandit framework for exploration to escape local optima.

Result: Evaluated on synthetic data, Amazon Reviews benchmarks, and large-scale proprietary commercial data. Biclustering consistently achieves the highest campaign quality, lift, and fairness scores. It runs efficiently on smaller datasets but its runtime increases substantially on very large datasets, where bandit-based methods offer a scalable alternative.

Conclusion: Biclustering is the best-performing method for campaign quality and fairness, while bandit-based methods provide better scalability for very large datasets, offering a practical trade-off depending on dataset size.

Abstract: When running marketing campaigns, retailers must decide which products to promote and which users to target. These decisions are inherently coupled: effective campaigns match users and items with strong mutual affinity into non-overlapping groups of predefined sizes. However, existing approaches assume predefined campaign structure or decouple item selection from user assignment, and cannot discover campaign groupings directly from joint interaction patterns. We therefore formalize this campaign problem as auto-targeting: jointly selecting users and items to construct multiple disjoint campaigns. To solve this combinatorial problem, we propose three complementary strategies: (i) constrained spectral biclustering to find dense regions in the user-item affinity matrix, (ii) greedy local search with pairwise swaps for combinatorial refinement, and (iii) a multi-armed bandit framework to escape local optima through exploration. We evaluate these methods on a synthetic dataset, the Amazon Reviews benchmarks, and large-scale proprietary commercial data, and compare the results to simulated annealing as a baseline. The results show that biclustering consistently achieves the highest campaign quality, lift, and fairness scores. While biclustering runs efficiently on smaller datasets, its runtime increases substantially on very large ones, where bandit-based methods instead offer a scalable alternative.

</details>


### [492] [Data-driven discovery of governing differential equations across physical systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09638)
*Siyu Lou,Hao Xu,Wenguan Wang,Lu Lu,Hao Sun,Yang Liu,Linfeng Zhang,Dongxiao Zhang,Yuntian Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This review proposes a problem-oriented perspective on data-driven differential equation discovery, introducing a two-dimensional phase diagram (structural vs. coefficient complexity) and the Representation-Evaluation-Optimization (REO) framework to unify diverse methodological approaches, while arguing the field's next challenge is using discovered equations to revise theories and form new scientific concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven differential equation discovery has expanded rapidly with diverse methodological directions, especially AI-based approaches, but still lacks a clear organizing perspective to understand why different methods succeed or fail across problem settings.

Method: The authors propose (1) a two-dimensional phase diagram organizing discovery problems by structural complexity and coefficient complexity, and (2) the Representation-Evaluation-Optimization (REO) framework as a fundamental abstraction of the discovery process.

Result: The phase diagram clarifies how the field has evolved from sparse equations with simple coefficients toward more complex governing laws, and explains why different methodological families succeed or fail. The REO framework shifts discussion from individual algorithms to fundamental principles determining discoverability.

Conclusion: The next challenge is not merely recovering equations, but using them to revise existing theories, distil mechanisms, and form new scientific concepts.

Abstract: Differential equations play a critical role in scientific discovery because they provide a mathematical framework to describe the behaviour of physical phenomena. As a promising alternative to traditional first principles, data-driven differential equation discovery has attracted increasing attention for its ability to infer governing laws directly from experimental or simulated data, especially when the underlying physics is unclear. However, the field has expanded rapidly along diverse methodological directions, particularly with the emergence of AI-based approaches, and still lacks a clear organizing perspective. In this Review, we propose a problem-oriented perspective on data-driven differential equation discovery. We first introduce a two-dimensional phase diagram of equation discoverability, where discovery problems are organized according to structural complexity and coefficient complexity. This phase diagram shows how the field has moved from the discovery of sparse equations with simple coefficients toward more complex governing laws with richer structures and more flexible parameterizations. It also clarifies why different methodological families succeed or fail in different problem settings. We then present the representation-evaluation-optimization (REO) framework as a fundamental abstraction of the discovery process. By identifying the core problems of equation discovery that persist across algorithmic variations, REO shifts the discussion from individual algorithms to the fundamental principles that determine discoverability. We connect these perspectives to applications across physics and adjacent sciences, and argue that the next challenge is not merely recovering equations, but using them to revise existing theories, distil mechanisms and form new scientific concepts.

</details>


### [493] [A Unifying Framework for Concept-Based Representational Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09653)
*Grégoire Dhimoïla,Victor Boutin,Agustin Martin Picard,Thomas Fel,Thomas Serre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified framework for defining concept alignment along two axes (representations vs. concepts, instance-wise vs. distributional), shows existing methods provide different guarantees than assumed, introduces an intervention-based benchmark (InterVenchA), and proposes CoSAE that jointly enforces complementary objectives where as little as 0.1% paired data suffices for strong alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept alignment across models and modalities is poorly defined; existing approaches use the same terminology while optimizing different objectives, obscuring what is actually being aligned.

Method: The paper decomposes alignment along two axes (representations vs. concepts, instance-wise vs. distributional), introduces InterVenchA benchmark, and proposes Coupled Sparse Autoencoder (CoSAE) to jointly enforce complementary alignment objectives.

Result: Commonly assumed equivalences between alignment objectives fail; optimizing one property does not recover others; purely unsupervised objectives fail at instance-level alignment; CoSAE with as little as 0.1% paired data recovers strong alignment.

Conclusion: Concept alignment is fundamentally multi-objective and must be defined, measured, and optimized as such.

Abstract: Learned representations across models and modalities often exhibit striking structural similarities, suggesting shared underlying concept decompositions. However, concept alignment remains poorly defined: existing approaches optimize different objectives under the same terminology, obscuring what is actually aligned.
  We propose a unifying framework that decomposes alignment along two axes: what is aligned (representations vs. concepts) and at what level (instance-wise vs. distributional). This induces four corresponding properties -- instance-wise and distributional variants of translation and concept consistency -- and reveals precisely which of these guarantees existing methods provide. We further introduce \InterVenchA, an intervention-based benchmark that separately measures extraction quality, translation quality, and concept consistency. Through theory and experiments, we show that commonly assumed equivalences between alignment objectives fail in practice: optimizing one property does not reliably recover the others, and purely unsupervised objectives fail to recover meaningful instance-level alignment. We then propose the Coupled Sparse Autoencoder (CoSAE), which jointly enforces complementary alignment objectives. Strong alignment emerges only in this regime. Surprisingly, as little as 0.1\% paired data is sufficient to recover instance-level alignment when anchoring distributional objectives.
  Overall, our results show that concept alignment is fundamentally multi-objective: it must be defined, measured, and optimized as such.

</details>


### [494] [Muon Learns More Robust and Transferable Features than Adam](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09658)
*Tianyu Ruan,Fengzhuo Zhang,Shuche Wang,Shihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Muon optimizer produces more robust and transferable features than Adam/SGD for LLMs and vision classifiers, supported by empirical evidence (larger logit margins, higher effective rank) and theoretical proof.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate Muon's unclear feature-learning advantages over Adam and SGD, focusing on robustness and transferability.

Method: Evaluated pretrained models on corrupted images/texts for robustness; used layer-wise probes and linear/full fine-tuning for transferability; analyzed effective rank; provided theoretical proof on a multi-component classification problem.

Result: Muon consistently outperforms Adam/SGD in robustness (larger logit margins across layers) and transferability (higher effective rank), across both transformers and CNNs.

Conclusion: Muon's feature-learning advantages are empirically and theoretically verified, with larger margins and higher effective rank as key factors.

Abstract: Muon has recently emerged as a state-of-the-art optimizer for pretraining Large Language Models (LLMs) and vision classifiers. Despite its efficiency advantage over Adam and SGD, the feature-learning advantage of Muon remains unclear. This paper investigates Muon's feature-learning advantage through the lens of robustness and transferability. First, by evaluating pretrained models on corrupted images and texts, we show that features learned by Muon are consistently more robust than those learned by Adam and SGD across different architectures, including transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Using trained layer-wise probes, we further show that this robustness advantage is reflected in larger logit margins across layers. Second, by training linear classifiers or fine-tuning full models from pretrained parameters on downstream tasks, we demonstrate that Muon-learned features transfer more effectively than those learned by Adam and SGD. This transferability advantage is further supported by the diversity of hidden states across layers, as measured by effective rank. Finally, in a representative classification problem with multi-component features, we prove that Muon attains larger margins and higher effective rank than Adam and SGD, providing theoretical support for our empirical findings.

</details>


### [495] [In-Context Learning for Latent Space Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09664)
*Tuan A. Vu,Harri Lähdesmäki,Julien Martinelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the mismatch between pretrained tabular foundation models and latent-space Bayesian optimization by continuing pretraining with synthetic optimization tasks on molecular VAE latent spaces, using a regularizer to preserve the original regression prior while improving LSBO performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular foundation models (e.g., TabPFN, TabICL) achieve SOTA regression but are pretrained on distributions that mismatch the latent-code-to-objective maps encountered in LSBO for molecular design.

Method: Continued pretraining of tabular foundation model surrogates with synthetic optimization tasks defined on the latent space of a molecular VAE, plus a regularizer that anchors the model to the original checkpoint to prevent overspecialization.

Result: The adapted model achieves strong performance on held-out molecular optimization benchmarks, demonstrating the relevance of LSBO-specific adaptation.

Conclusion: LSBO-specific continued pretraining of in-context surrogates effectively addresses the distribution mismatch and improves optimization performance for molecular design.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a central tool for sample-efficient design, and latent-space Bayesian optimization (LSBO) extends it to structured objects such as molecules and proteins. In parallel, tabular foundation models such as TabPFN and TabICL now achieve state-of-the-art regression performance and are increasingly used as BO surrogates. Because their Bayesian behavior is induced by large synthetic pretraining collections, the composition of this pretraining distribution is crucial. LSBO creates a distinctive mismatch: the induced map from latent code to objective value differs markedly from the regression tasks used to train current in-context models. We address this mismatch by complementing the pretraining stage of tabular foundation model surrogates with synthetic optimization tasks defined on the latent space of a molecular VAE. The continued-pretraining objective features a regularizer that anchors the model to the original checkpoint, preserving its broad regression prior while avoiding overspecialization to the adaptation tasks. On held-out molecular optimization benchmarks, the resulting model achieves strong performance, supporting the relevance of LSBO-specific adaptation for in-context surrogates.

</details>


### [496] [Algorithm for Contextual Queueing Bandits with Rate-Optimal Queue Length Regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09668)
*Seoungbin Bae,Dabeen Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves the queue length regret for contextual queueing bandits from $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/4})$ to $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$, matching a proven minimax lower bound of $\Omega(T^{-1/2})$, via a three-phase algorithm (CQB-$\eta$-2) that limits random exploration to an early cutoff round.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the existing suboptimal $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/4})$ queue length regret rate in contextual queueing bandits and establish the fundamental minimax limit.

Method: Proposes CQB-$\eta$-2, a three-phase algorithm: (i) pure random exploration for initial estimation, (ii) $\eta$-random exploration with UCB for continued learning under negative drift, and (iii) pure UCB after a cutoff round. Regret is decomposed at the cutoff boundary.

Result: Achieves $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$ queue length regret, matching the proven minimax lower bound of $\Omega(T^{-1/2})$ up to logarithmic factors.

Conclusion: The minimax dependence on horizon $T$ is characterized: $\widetilde{\Theta}(T^{-1/2})$ up to log factors. The improvement stems from concentrating random exploration only before a cutoff, not throughout the entire horizon.

Abstract: Contextual queueing bandits provide a framework for learning to schedule heterogeneous jobs under unknown context-dependent service rates. Under stochastic contexts, existing algorithms achieve $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/4})$ queue length regret, defined as the expected difference between the learner's and oracle's queue lengths at horizon $T$. In this paper, we improve this rate to $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$. The key observation is that random exploration is needed only up to a carefully chosen cutoff round, rather than throughout the entire horizon. We propose CQB-$η$-2, a three-phase algorithm: (i) pure random exploration to construct an initial estimator, (ii) $η$-random exploration combined with a UCB rule to continue learning while maintaining negative drift, and (iii) pure UCB after the exploration cutoff. Our proof decomposes the queue length regret at the cutoff round. Before the cutoff, negative drift suppresses queue length differences caused by suboptimal choices. After the cutoff, the first two phases provide sufficient random exploration samples, ensuring that UCB decisions incur small departure-rate gaps. Combining these two bounds yields queue length regret of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{-1/2})$. We further prove a minimax lower bound of order $Ω(T^{-1/2})$. The proof constructs two hard instances that are statistically indistinguishable up to the final service decision, and uses a queue-specific coupling argument to convert the resulting testing error into queue length regret. Together, our upper and lower bounds characterize the minimax dependence on the horizon $T$ up to logarithmic factors.

</details>


### [497] [Transition-Based Digital Twin Modelling for Alzheimer's Disease under Sparse Longitudinal Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09671)
*Yinyu Huang,Yilin Zhang,Sofia Michopoulou,Christopher Kipps,Rahman Attar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a personalized digital twin framework for Alzheimer's disease prediction using multimodal longitudinal data. It integrates transition-based and sequence-based modeling strategies to predict cognitive status and diagnosis, finding that local transition modeling is more data-efficient and robust for sparse, irregular clinical data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AD progression is highly heterogeneous and observed through sparse, irregular longitudinal data. Existing ML approaches focus on static classification or cohort-level risk estimation, lacking subject-specific modeling and uncertainty-aware reasoning.

Method: A personalized digital twin framework combining complementary modeling strategies (transition-based and sequence-based) using multimodal data from ADNI (cognitive assessments, clinical variables, MRI-derived phenotypes) to predict cognitive status and diagnostic categories with uncertainty quantification and what-if trajectory analysis.

Result: Transition-based modeling of adjacent visits achieved higher predictive accuracy than sequence-based modeling in the sparse, irregular ADNI setting, while sequence models remain valuable for uncertainty-aware trajectory forecasting.

Conclusion: Local transition modeling is more data-efficient and robust for personalized disease forecasting in neurodegenerative disorders. The choice of temporal modeling strategy should align with clinical data structure, and transition-based digital twin formulations offer a practical, interpretable approach.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression is highly heterogeneous and is typically observed through sparse and irregular longitudinal data, posing challenges for prediction and personalised monitoring. Existing machine learning approaches have improved AD prediction using multimodal data, yet often focus on static classification or cohort-level risk estimation, providing limited support for subject-specific modelling and uncertainty-aware reasoning. To address these limitations, we present a personalised digital twin framework for AD prediction and scenario-based analysis using multimodal longitudinal data. The proposed approach integrates complementary modelling strategies to capture clinical transitions and temporal dependencies across visits. Using data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), including cognitive assessments, clinical variables, and MRI-derived phenotypes, the framework predicts cognitive status and diagnostic categories while quantifying predictive uncertainty and enabling patient-specific what-if trajectory analysis. Evaluation on leak-free subject-level splits demonstrates strong performance in score forecasting and diagnosis classification. In this sparse and irregular ADNI setting, transition-based modelling of adjacent visits achieved higher predictive accuracy than the sequence-based branch, suggesting that local transition modelling may be more data-efficient. While sequence models remain valuable for uncertainty-aware trajectory forecasting, local transition modelling offers a more data-efficient and robust predictive strategy. These findings highlight the importance of aligning temporal modelling strategies with clinical data structure and suggest that transition-based digital twin formulations may provide a practical and interpretable approach for personalised disease forecasting in neurodegenerative disorders.

</details>


### [498] [AutoMegaKernel: A Statically-Checked Agent Harness for Self-Retargeting Megakernel Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09682)
*Jaber Jaber,Osama Jaber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoMegaKernel compiles Llama-family models into a single persistent CUDA kernel that runs the entire forward pass in one launch, with a static validator that certifies safety, auto-targets multiple GPU architectures, and achieves speedups over cuBLAS on inference-class GPUs (up to 1.33x) while trailing on training-class GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate manual CUDA kernel writing for transformer models by automatically compiling them into single persistent megakernels that are deadlock/race-free and portable across GPU architectures.

Method: A frozen schedule-IR validator statically certifies safety; the same source retargets multiple architectures (sm_80/90/120); uses an agent-drivable autoresearch loop to self-improve; supports precision-asymmetric W8A16 quantization.

Result: Zero false-accepts across 7,160 adversarial schedules (6,091 unsafe), accepted 360 real lowerings; reproduces HuggingFace greedy decode token-for-token (perplexity match 2.5e-7); beats cuBLAS on inference-class GPUs (L4 up to 1.33x, L40S 1.25-1.27x, A10G up to 1.08x, RTX 5090 1.19-1.23x) but trails on training-class A100/H100 due to cross-SM-sync bottleneck.

Conclusion: The system contribution is automatic, verified megakernel generation that benefits inference-class GPUs, with a clearly reported limitation on high-bandwidth training-class hardware. Code is open-sourced.

Abstract: AutoMegaKernel (AMK) compiles a HuggingFace Llama-family model into a single persistent cooperative CUDA kernel that runs the whole forward pass in one launch, with no per-model hand-written CUDA. The contribution is the system, not raw speed.
  A frozen schedule-IR validator statically certifies deadlock-freedom and race-freedom via static graph checks (not a mechanized proof), so an unsafe agent-proposed schedule is rejected before launch: across 7,160 adversarial schedules (6,091 unsafe) it had zero false-accepts and accepted all 360 real lowerings. The same source retargets sm_80/sm_90/sm_120 from one codebase, auto-generates correct megakernels for 10 of 10 supported models, and on a real SmolLM2-135M checkpoint reproduces HuggingFace greedy decode token-for-token (perplexity match 2.5e-7). An unattended, agent-drivable autoresearch loop self-improves the megakernel over its own baseline (1.25-1.72x).
  A search-found int8 (W8A16) megakernel beats CUDA-graphed cuBLAS bf16 at batch-1 decode across NVIDIA's datacenter inference fleet: L4 up to 1.33x, the current-gen L40S 1.25-1.27x, A10G up to 1.08x at scale, and the consumer RTX 5090 1.19-1.23x. The ordering is not a clean function of bandwidth (the 864 GB/s L40S beats the 600 GB/s A10G); the divide is inference-class vs training-class. AMK trails cuBLAS on the high-bandwidth training-class A100/H100, where the harness localizes the cross-SM-sync bottleneck; we report the gap plainly. This is a precision-asymmetric (W8A16 vs bf16) comparison at decode position 0; the largest real checkpoint is TinyLlama-1.1B. Code and the harness: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/AutoMegaKernel

</details>


### [499] [When Do Local Score Models Extrapolate Across Size? A Diagnostic Theory and Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09705)
*Wenjie Xi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that stable size extrapolation in generative models depends on the quasi-locality of Gaussian-smoothed scores, not just architectural locality, and introduces FDLF benchmark to validate this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why translation-invariant local architectures sometimes fail to generalize from small to large systems in scientific generative modeling.

Method: Formalizes the mechanism via Tweedie's formula and posterior covariance, proves a size-uniform comparison theorem for local marginals under reverse diffusion, and introduces FDLF (Finite-Depth Local Flow) benchmark with exact scores and controllable response ranges.

Result: Under spatial mixing, smoothed score remains quasi-local relative to receptive field, enabling stable extrapolation. When spatial mixing weakens, score locality degrades, causing size transfer failure.

Conclusion: Architectural locality alone is insufficient for size extrapolation; the quasi-locality of the Gaussian-smoothed score is the true governing factor.

Abstract: Scientific generative modeling often requires size transfer, where models trained on small systems are evaluated on larger ones. While translation-invariant architectures enable this evaluation, we show that architectural locality alone does not guarantee stable size extrapolation. Instead, stable extrapolation is governed by the quasi-locality of the Gaussian-smoothed score. Through Tweedie's formula, far-away perturbations can influence local score components via posterior covariance, meaning a local model succeeds only if its receptive field covers the smoothed score's response range. We formalize this mechanism, proving a size-uniform comparison theorem for local marginals under reverse diffusion. We also introduce Finite-Depth Local Flow (FDLF), a white-box diagnostic benchmark with exact scores, densities, and controllable response ranges. Empirically, we validate the interplay between spatial mixing, smoothed-score quasi-locality, and model receptive fields. Under spatial mixing, the smoothed score remains quasi-local relative to the receptive field, enabling stable extrapolation. Conversely, when spatial mixing weakens, the score's locality rapidly degrades, causing size transfer to fail.

</details>


### [500] [BrainSurgery: Reproducible and Reliable Declarative Weight Manipulations for Model Editing and Upcycling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09707)
*Gianluca Barmina,Annemette Broch Pirchert,Andrea Blasi Núñez,Lukas Galke Poech,Peter Schneider-Kamp*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BrainSurgery is a YAML-based tool for robust, reproducible manipulation of deep learning model checkpoints, supporting structural modifications, mathematical transformations, and built-in validation assertions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Managing large deep learning checkpoints is challenging, and current workflows rely on fragile ad-hoc Python scripts for tasks like layer restructuring and precision casting.

Method: BrainSurgery abstracts storage formats and memory management, executing complex transformations through declarative YAML plans with regex-based structural targeting and built-in shape/dtype/value assertions.

Result: Demonstrated through four examples and three case studies including model upcycling and LoRA extraction.

Conclusion: BrainSurgery provides a strong foundation for reproducible and validated tensor surgery operations in deep learning research.

Abstract: As deep learning models scale, managing, inspecting, and modifying large checkpoints has become increasingly challenging. Researchers often need to alter model weights for layer restructuring, precision casting, low-rank factorization, and architectural debugging, yet these workflows often rely on fragile ad-hoc Python scripts. Here, we introduce BrainSurgery, a tool for robust and reproducible "tensor surgery" on neural network checkpoints, and provide a system demonstration covering four examples and three case studies from model upcycling to LoRA extraction. By abstracting storage formats and memory management, BrainSurgery executes complex transformations through declarative YAML plans. It supports structural modifications, mathematical transformations, and tensor reshaping through expressive regex and structural targeting, while built-in assertions validate tensor shapes, data types, and values to prevent silent errors. We envision that BrainSurgery will provide a strong foundation for future research through its reproducible and validated operations.

</details>


### [501] [Evaluating the Representation Space of Diffusion Models via Self-Supervised Principles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09718)
*Xiao Li,Yixuan Jia,Zekai Zhang,Xiang Li,Lianghe Shi,Jinxin Zhou,Zhihui Zhu,Liyue Shen,Qing Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework (ICR metric based on Fisher analysis) to jointly evaluate representation and generation in diffusion models, showing that invariance peaks at intermediate noise levels for representation quality, and that ICR can detect memorization onset during training without external evaluators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between understanding diffusion models' generative capabilities and their emerging role as self-supervised representation learners, and to develop a unified framework for jointly analyzing both aspects.

Method: The authors decompose features into invariant and residual components, derive the Invariant Contamination Ratio (ICR) — a Fisher-based metric quantifying residual contamination of invariant signals — and apply this framework to analyze discriminative (representation) and generative behavior of diffusion models.

Result: (1) Invariance peaks at intermediate noise levels, correlating with best downstream classification performance. (2) ICR serves as a sensitive training-time indicator of early learning, where increasing residual energy along Fisher directions marks memorization onset, detectable from training features alone without test sets.

Conclusion: Diffusion models can be effectively monitored from a self-supervised perspective through the geometry of their learned representations, with ICR providing a practical tool for detecting memorization during training.

Abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable generative capabilities and have also emerged as powerful self-supervised representation learners, yet the connection between these two abilities remains less explored. Drawing inspiration from self-supervised learning (SSL), we introduce a framework for jointly evaluating the representation and generation capabilities of diffusion models. Specifically, we decompose features into invariant and residual components and derive the Invariant Contamination Ratio (ICR), a Fisher-based metric that quantifies how residual variation contaminates invariant signal in feature space. We use this framework to analyze both discriminative and generative behavior of diffusion models. On the representation side, we find that invariance peaks at intermediate noise levels, which also yield the best downstream classification performance. On the generative side, we study how training transitions from genuine generalization to memorization in data-limited regimes, and show that ICR serves as a sensitive training-time indicator of early learning: increasing residual energy along Fisher directions marks the onset of memorization, detectable from training features alone without external evaluators or held-out test sets. Overall, our results show that diffusion models can be monitored from a self-supervised perspective through the geometry of their learned representations.

</details>


### [502] [Disentanglement with Holographic Reduced Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09725)
*Jhonny J. Velasquez Olivera,Christo K. Thomas,Walid Saad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using holographic reduced representations (HRRs) for unsupervised disentanglement of factors of variation in data. The HRR unbinding operation provides an inductive bias that separates symbolic concepts, and the authors prove information-theoretic properties including approximate independence of symbol-value pairs and a per-slot capacity bound, demonstrating competitive results on disentanglement metrics and robustness to noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentanglement of factors of variation remains a long-standing challenge. Prior VAE/GAN-based methods rely on continuous representations, but the authors argue that disentangled representations are better treated as symbolic structures due to compositional relationships among concepts. Learning discrete symbolic structures with neural networks while maintaining differentiability is difficult and requires complex architectures.

Method: The authors introduce an unsupervised learning algorithm that uses holographic reduced representations (HRRs) for neural disentanglement. The HRR unbinding operation serves as an inductive bias for separating factors of variation. They also provide an information-theoretic analysis of the HRR unbinding channel, proving that unbinding induces approximately independent symbol-value pairs and deriving a per-slot capacity bound.

Result: The method yields competitive results against baselines as measured by latent traversals and disentanglement metrics. The HRR representation is more robust to noise than other disentangled representations and maintains reconstruction quality across a range of signal-to-noise ratios.

Conclusion: The HRR-based approach offers a novel symbolic treatment of disentanglement with theoretical grounding (information-theoretic capacity bounds) and practical advantages (noise robustness), differentiating it from standard autoencoder-based models where latent units are scalar dimensions rather than summed vectors.

Abstract: Disentanglement, the separation of factors of variation in data using neural networks, remains a long-standing challenge in machine learning. Prior work has addressed this problem with variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks that incorporate ideas from variational inference and information-theoretic constraints. In contrast to methods that rely on continuous representations, we propose a design that treats disentangled representations as symbolic structures, motivated by the compositional relationships among the concepts that make up samples from a distribution. However, learning discrete symbolic structures with neural networks while maintaining differentiability is difficult and often requires complex architectures. To address this, we introduce an unsupervised learning algorithm that uses holographic reduced representations (HRR) for neural disentanglement. We show that the HRR unbinding operation provides an inductive bias for separating factors and yields competitive results against baselines, as measured by latent traversals and disentanglement metrics. We complement these empirical findings with an information-theoretic analysis of the HRR unbinding channel. We prove that unbinding induces approximately independent symbol-value pairs and derive a per-slot capacity bound that quantifies how many distinct symbolic concepts can be reliably encoded, giving a quantitative account of the inductive bias toward disentanglement. The resulting representations differ from standard autoencoder-based models, in that their latent units are vectors that are summed together, rather than scalar dimensions of a low-dimensional latent vector. We show that this HRR representation is more robust to noise than other disentangled representations and maintains reconstruction quality across a range of SNRs.

</details>


### [503] [Tight Sample Complexity of Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09731)
*Chenxiao Yang,Nathan Srebro,Zhiyuan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the VC dimension of depth-L Transformers with W parameters, showing it is Θ(LW log(TW)), and provides tight bounds on the sample complexity of chain-of-thought learning with such Transformers, establishing both upper and lower bounds for teacher forcing and general learning rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide tight theoretical guarantees on the generalization capacity of Transformers, specifically characterizing their VC dimension and the sample complexity required for chain-of-thought learning.

Method: The paper derives upper and lower bounds on the VC dimension of depth-L Transformers with W parameters, and analyzes the sample complexity of chain-of-thought learning under teacher forcing, establishing matching bounds.

Result: VC dimension upper bound: O(LW log(TW)), lower bound: Ω(LW log(TW/L)). Sample complexity for chain-of-thought learning: upper bound O(LW log((T+T')W)), lower bound Ω(LW log((T+T')W/L)).

Conclusion: The VC dimension and chain-of-thought sample complexity of Transformers are nearly matching, providing tight theoretical understanding of their generalization properties.

Abstract: We tightly characterize the VC dimension of depth-$L$ Transformers with a total of $W$ parameters, mapping an input sequence of length $T$ to a single output, establishing an upper bound of $O(L W \log (T W))$ and a nearly matching lower bound of $Ω(L W \log (T W / L))$. We further tightly characterize the sample complexity of chain-of-thought learning using such a Transformer, showing teacher forcing (i.e. selecting a predictor consistent with the entire chain-of-thought on training data) learns with sample complexity $O\left(L W \log \left(\left(T+T^{\prime}\right) W\right)\right)$ and that any learning rule that uses chain-of-thought data requires at least $Ω\left(L W \log \left(\left(T+T^{\prime}\right) W / L\right)\right)$ examples, where $T$ is the input length and $T^{\prime}$ is the number of autoregressive steps.

</details>


### [504] [Learning Dynamics Reveal a Hierarchy of Weight-Induced Layerwise Gram Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09744)
*Claudio Nordio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout and quadratic loss, rewriting gradient descent as collective dynamics in terms of fields defined on the training-set space. For single hidden layers, weight variables can be eliminated, yielding closed residual equations with a factorized kernel. For deeper networks (depth 3+), closure requires a hierarchy of weight-induced Gram operators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reformulate gradient descent dynamics from weight-space to a collective dynamics framework defined on training-set space, enabling better understanding of neural network learning behavior.

Method: The approach analyzes feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout under quadratic loss, deriving closed-form equations for residual dynamics. For single hidden layers, weight elimination yields a factorized collective kernel. For deeper networks (depth 3+), a hierarchy of weight-induced Gram operators is required for closure.

Result: For single hidden layers, residuals follow a closed equation governed by a kernel that factorizes into an input-geometric matrix and a dynamical co-activation matrix. For deeper networks, residual dynamics maintains a clean layer-wise kernel structure, but depth 3+ requires Gram operators for information transport across layers.

Conclusion: Gradient descent in ReLU networks can be understood as collective dynamics in training-set space, with varying complexity depending on network depth. Single hidden layers allow full weight elimination, while deeper networks require hierarchical Gram operators for closure.

Abstract: We study feed-forward ReLU networks with fixed readout and quadratic loss. The aim is to rewrite gradient descent not primarily as a dynamics in weight space, but as a collective dynamics closed in terms of fields defined on the training-set space. For a single hidden layer, the weight variables can be eliminated from the activation dynamics, yielding a closed equation for the residuals governed by a collective kernel that factorizes into an input-geometric matrix and a dynamical co-activation matrix. For deeper networks, the residual dynamics retains a clean layer-wise kernel structure. However, from depth three onward, closure requires a hierarchy of weight-induced Gram operators that mediate information transport across layers.

</details>


### [505] [Perturbative Contrastive Physical Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09756)
*Kyungeun Kim,Amanuel Anteneh,Israel Klich,Olivier Pfister,J. M. Schwarz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCPL is a framework where physical systems learn by contrasting their responses under slightly different conditions, without needing external backpropagation. Demonstrated on spring networks and photonic circuits for classification and analog multiplication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand physical systems through responses to perturbations and enable learning from contrasts between physical states under different conditions, without centralized gradient computation or external processors.

Method: PCPL framework: learning emerges from measurable contrasts between physical states produced by controlled changes to inputs, boundary conditions, parameters, or interpreter functions. Unifies Equilibrium Propagation and Frequency Propagation. Demonstrated on spring networks (updating bond stiffness via displacement/force measurements) and continuous-variable photonic circuits (using x quadrature measurements and finite-difference Jacobian estimates).

Result: Both spring networks and photonic circuits successfully learn classification tasks. Photonic circuits also trained to implement analog multiplication, demonstrating progress toward autonomous physical learning.

Conclusion: Contrast-driven updates reflect local sensitivities or global inverse-problem structure without centralized gradient computation. Effective learning geometry emerges implicitly from the system's own physical response.

Abstract: Responses to perturbations are key to understanding physical systems. The ability to contrast such responses by comparing how a system reacts under slightly different conditions provides a mechanism for learning. Here, we introduce Perturbative Contrastive Physical Learning (PCPL), a general framework in which learning emerges from measurable contrasts between physical states produced by controlled changes to inputs, boundary conditions, parameters, or interpreter functions. PCPL unifies and extends prior approaches: Equilibrium Propagation is rooted in contrasts between free and nudged equilibria in energy-based systems, while Frequency Propagation corresponds to contrasts extracted from sinusoidally driven, frequency-demodulated responses. We show that contrast-driven updates can reflect either local sensitivities or global inverse-problem structure, yet do not require centralized gradient computation. Instead, effective learning geometry emerges implicitly from the system's own physical response, allowing learning behavior to arise without an external processor or explicit backpropagation. We demonstrate PCPL in two platforms: (i) spring networks that update bond stiffness using measured displacements and forces, and (ii) continuous-variable photonic circuits trained via x quadrature measurements and finite-difference estimates of the Jacobian. Both platforms successfully learn classification tasks. We further show that a continuous-variable photonic circuit can be trained to implement analog multiplication, illustrating a step toward more autonomous physical learning systems.

</details>


### [506] [Preserving Plasticity in Continual Learning via Dynamical Isometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09762)
*Andries Rosseau,Robert Müller,Ann Nowé*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies dynamical isometry (layer-wise Jacobian singular values near one) as a key mechanism for preserving neural network plasticity during continual learning, proposes an isometry-promoting regularization scheme and the AdamO optimizer, and demonstrates improved performance across continual learning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual training of deep neural networks under non-stationarity leads to progressive loss of plasticity, limiting further learning. The paper aims to understand and mitigate this plasticity loss.

Method: The authors (1) relate plasticity to the empirical Neural Tangent Kernel, (2) identify dynamical isometry as a key mechanism, (3) revisit almost-everywhere isometric networks that are universal Lipschitz approximators, (4) propose an isometry-promoting regularization scheme that reactivates dormant ReLU units, and (5) introduce AdamO, an adaptive optimizer decoupling isometry regularization from gradient updates.

Result: The proposed methods consistently match or outperform existing approaches across supervised and reinforcement-learning continual-learning benchmarks designed to induce plasticity loss.

Conclusion: Dynamical isometry is a key mechanism for preserving plasticity in continual learning. The proposed regularization and AdamO optimizer effectively preserve plasticity, and prior approaches can be reinterpreted through this lens as targeting only a partial measure of isometry.

Abstract: Continual training of deep neural networks under non-stationarity often leads to a progressive loss of plasticity, eventually limiting further learning. We relate plasticity to the empirical Neural Tangent Kernel, and identify dynamical isometry (the condition that layer-wise Jacobian singular values remain close to one) as a key mechanism for preserving plasticity in continual learning. We revisit a class of networks that are almost-everywhere isometric while remaining universal Lipschitz function approximators, demonstrating that near-dynamical isometry is compatible with expressive nonlinear representations. For general architectures, we propose an efficient isometry-promoting regularization scheme and identify a novel mechanism by which it can reactivate dormant ReLU units. Building on this, we introduce AdamO, an Adam-style adaptive optimizer that decouples isometry regularization from gradient updates, analogous to AdamW. We further reinterpret prior plasticity-preserving approaches through the lens of dynamical isometry, showing that they target only a partial measure of isometry. Across supervised and reinforcement-learning continual-learning benchmarks designed to induce plasticity loss, our methods consistently match or outperform existing approaches.

</details>


### [507] [iOSWorld: A Benchmark for Personally Intelligent Phone Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09764)
*Lawrence Keunho Jang,Mareks Woodside,Geronimo Carom,Andrew Keunwoo Jang,Jing Yu Koh,Ruslan Salakhutdinov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: iOSWorld is a benchmark for personalized phone agents, featuring 26 iOS apps, 133 tasks across single-app, multi-app, and memory/personalization categories. Best models achieve 52% overall, with privileged XML inputs boosting frontier models by 26 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mobile agent benchmarks lack personalized, device-level intelligence that reasons over user identity, history, and preferences; they only handle isolated instructions in impersonal sandboxes.

Method: The authors built iOSWorld, an interactive native iOS simulator with 26 apps containing connected personal data, and 133 tasks across three difficulty levels (single-app, multi-app, memory/personalization). They evaluated vision-only and vision+XML models.

Result: Best model achieves 52% overall accuracy (37% on multi-app tasks). Privileged XML access improves frontier models by 26 percentage points, while smaller models see no benefit.

Conclusion: iOSWorld reveals significant room for improvement in personalized agent reasoning, especially for multi-app and memory-based tasks. The benchmark is open-sourced to drive further research.

Abstract: A useful phone agent needs to be personally intelligent. It should reason over a user's identity, history, and preferences as they exist on the device, not just follow isolated instructions in an impersonal sandbox. Existing mobile agent benchmarks lack this kind of personalization. We introduce iOSWorld, the first interactive native iOS simulator benchmark built around a persistent user identity spanning 26 newly built iOS apps. These apps contain connected data such as transactions, messages, travel records, social relationships, and financial activity. iOSWorld includes 133 tasks across three increasingly difficult categories. Single-app tasks (27) test one app, multi-app tasks (60) span 2 to 8 apps, and memory and personalization tasks (46) require agents to infer patterns from personal data. We evaluate frontier and open-source computer-use models in both vision-only and privileged vision+XML settings. The best configuration reaches 52\% overall but only 37\% on multi-app tasks. Privileged vision+XML access improves frontier models by up to 26 percentage points, while smaller models do not benefit from added accessibility-tree input. We release iOSWorld as an open-source benchmark with all apps, seeded data, tasks, rubrics, and evaluation code.

</details>


### [508] [Zero Touch Predictive Orchestration: Automating Time-Series Models for the Cloud-Edge Continuum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09787)
*Abd Elghani Meliani,Arora Sagar,Adlen Ksentini,Raymond Knopp*

Main category: cs.LG
