TL;DR: A novel automated time-series prediction architecture using data-mixing (local sparse data + public TimeTrack dataset) and Neural Architecture Search to solve the cold start problem in Cloud-Edge Continuum orchestration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud-Edge Continuum faces a severe "cold start" problem: newly discovered edge nodes lack historical data to train localized predictive models, while generalized models fail to capture unique hardware and microservice behaviors, making proactive Zero Touch Management difficult.

Method: Proposes a fully automated architecture with: (1) a lightweight Resource Exposer (RE) for dynamic node discovery and telemetry collection; (2) a data-mixing methodology merging sparse local samples with TimeTrack (public high-resolution dataset); (3) a Neural Architecture Search (NAS) engine to auto-generate accurate baseline models.

Result: Data-mixing with TimeTrack significantly improves forecasting accuracy (MSE, MAE, MAPE) and accelerates convergence compared to training on sparse local data alone, generic datasets only, or mixing with standard alternative datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed data-mixing approach effectively mitigates the cold start problem in CEC, establishing a robust foundation for continuous MLOps deployment.

Abstract: The Cloud-Edge Continuum (CEC) enables latency-critical applications by distributing resources to the far edge, but its extreme volatility makes proactive Zero Touch Management via time-series forecasting essential. However, orchestrators face a severe "cold start" problem: newly discovered nodes lack the historical data required to train localized predictive models, while generalized models fail to capture unique hardware and microservice behaviors. To solve this, we propose a fully automated time-series prediction architecture driven by a novel data-mixing methodology. At the infrastructure level, we introduce a lightweight, technology-agnostic Resource Exposer (RE) that dynamically discovers nodes and continuously collects customizable telemetry (e.g., compute, network, energy). To overcome the sparsity of these initial local samples, our framework automatically merges them with TimeTrack, our publicly available, high-resolution dataset collected at 45-second intervals. This synergizes TimeTrack's foundational, high-frequency temporal patterns with the precise calibration of the local node data. Processed through a Neural Architecture Search (NAS) engine, the system automatically generates highly accurate baseline models. Experimental results demonstrate that merging the target data with TimeTrack effectively mitigates the cold start challenge. This integration significantly improves forecasting accuracy measured in Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) and accelerates convergence compared to training on the sparse local samples alone, training solely on generic datasets, or mixing the target data with standard alternative datasets, establishing a robust foundation for continuous MLOps deployment.

</details>


### [509] [Bandits for Efficient Experimentation: Adapting to Control Group, Preferences, and Context Drifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09802)
*Udvas Das,Waris Radji,Debabrota Basu,Odalric-Ambrym Maillard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies linear contextual bandits with drifting context distributions and user-specific preferences, proposing Dri-MED that handles non-stationary heteroskedastic noise while ensuring baseline constraint satisfaction, achieving instance-dependent regret of $\tilde{\mathcal O}\left(\frac{\kappa}{\tilde{\Delta}}d^2\log(T)\right)$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ expected constraint violations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the practical scenario where a learner must provide recommendations to users with personalized preferences under time-varying context distributions, while simultaneously ensuring each decision's mean reward exceeds a baseline policy—a setting not adequately handled by existing methods that ignore drift or preference structure.

Method: The authors reduce the problem to a linear bandit with stationary mean but heteroskedastic and non-stationary noise, then adapt the MED (Minimax Exploration-Driven) strategy to handle this noise structure using heteroskedastic regression, resulting in the Dri-MED algorithm.

Result: Dri-MED achieves $\tilde{\mathcal O}\left(\frac{\kappa}{\tilde{\Delta}}d^2\log(T)\right)$ instance-dependent regret (where $\tilde{\Delta}$ is the constraint-aware sub-optimality gap and $\kappa$ is a variance-aware term) and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ expected constraint violations. Numerical results show significant improvement over conservative baselines.

Conclusion: Dri-MED effectively handles drifting contexts and user preferences under baseline constraints, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: We consider a variant of the linear contextual stochastic multi-armed bandits, where the learner must provide recommendations to a group of users, each having its personalized preference vector, and in the presence of context distributions that are drifting over time. Under practitioner-friendly assumptions, we reduce this setting to linear bandit with stationary mean but heteroskedastic and non-stationary noise. We further study the case when the learner must ensure the mean reward of each decision must exceed that of a baseline strategy $\boldsymbolπ_0$ at each decision step. We introduce Dri-MED, an algorithm inspired from the linear version of the MED strategy, and carefully adapted to handle the non-stationary heteroskedastic noise. We show that the instance-dependent regret scales as $\tilde{\mathcal O}\left(\fracκ{\tildeΔ}d^2(\log(T)\right)$, where $\tildeΔ$ is the constraint-aware sub-optimality gap subject to policy $π_0$, with variance-aware multiplicative term $κ$ that we carefully handle using heteroskedastic regression. We further show Dri-MED enjoys $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ expected constraint violations. Our numerical results suggest that Dri-MED significantly outperforms conservative baselines that ignores the drift and preference structure.

</details>


### [510] [Topological Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09806)
*Lennart Bastian,Samuel Leventhal,Mustafa Hajij,Tolga Birdal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Topological Neural Operators (TNOs) extend neural operators to cell complexes, using Discrete Exterior Calculus for cross-dimensional coupling, with Hierarchical TNOs (HTNOs) adding learned coarse complexes for long-range information, achieving improved accuracy on PDE benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend neural operators from functions on points/edges to topological domains (cell complexes) to better respect geometric support and conservation structure of physical quantities.

Method: TNOs represent data on cells of varying dimension, use Discrete Exterior Calculus for gradient/curl/divergence-type coupling, and decouple fixed topological information flow from learned transformations. HTNOs add learned coarse complexes for long-range propagation.

Result: TNOs and HTNOs improve accuracy on PDE benchmarks including irregular-geometry flow problems; controlled studies show benefits of higher-rank and topological structure.

Conclusion: The framework subsumes existing NOs, provides a unified perspective across discretizations, and demonstrates that explicit topological structure improves operator learning.

Abstract: We introduce Topological Neural Operators (TNOs), a principled framework for operator learning on cell complexes that lifts neural operators (NOs) from functions on points and/or edges to topological domains. TNOs represent data as features defined on cells of varying dimension and model their interactions through Discrete Exterior Calculus, enabling explicit cross-dimensional coupling via gradient-, curl-, and divergence-type operators. The key design principle is to decouple where information flows, as governed by fixed topological operators, from how it is transformed (which is learned), yielding models that respect the geometric support of physical quantities and expose conservation and compatibility structure. We further propose Hierarchical TNOs (HTNOs), which incorporate learned coarse complexes to propagate long-range and topology-dependent information. Our framework subsumes existing NOs as a special case, providing a unified perspective on operator learning across discretizations. Across a range of PDE benchmarks, including irregular-geometry flow problems, TNOs and HTNOs improve accuracy; controlled studies further isolate the benefits of native higher-rank and topological structure. Project page: https://circle-group.github.io/research/TNO

</details>


### [511] [Rethinking the Divergence Regularization in LLM RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09821)
*Jiarui Yao,Xiangxin Zhou,Penghui Qi,Wee Sun Lee,Liefeng Bo,Tianyu Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRPO replaces the hard divergence mask in DPPO with a smooth quadratic regularizer, providing continuous gradient attenuation for more stable and efficient LLM RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like PPO/GRPO use ratio-clipping which poorly proxies distributional shift in long-tailed vocabularies. DPPO uses a divergence-based hard mask but discards gradients beyond the boundary instead of correcting them.

Method: DRPO replaces the hard mask in DPPO with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer on policy shift, preserving trust-region geometry while providing bounded, continuous gradient weights and corrective signals beyond the boundary.

Result: Across model scales, architectures, and precision settings, DRPO improves the stability and efficiency of LLM RL training.

Conclusion: A smooth regularizer outperforms hard masking for trust-region control in off-policy LLM RL, enabling more stable optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key component of post-training large language models (LLMs). In practice, LLM RL is often off-policy because of training-inference mismatch and policy staleness, making trust-region control essential for stable optimization. Mainstream methods such as PPO and GRPO approximate this control with a ratio-clipping mechanism, but the importance ratio can be a poor proxy for distributional shift in long-tailed vocabularies. Recent work such as DPPO addresses this mismatch by replacing ratio-based clipping with a divergence-based mask, yielding a trust region defined by the sampled token's absolute probability shift. However, DPPO still relies on a hard mask: once a token crosses the trust-region boundary in a harmful direction, its gradient is discarded rather than corrected. To address this, we propose Divergence Regularized Policy Optimization (DRPO), which replaces the hard mask with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer on policy shift. DRPO preserves the same trust-region geometry as DPPO while inducing bounded, continuous gradient weights that attenuate diverging updates and provide corrective signals beyond the boundary. Experiments across model scales, architectures, and precision settings show that DRPO improves the stability and efficiency of LLM RL training.

</details>


### [512] [An Agency-Transferring Model-Free Policy Enhancement Technique](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09825)
*Anton Bolychev,Georgiy Malaniya,Sinan Ibrahim,Pavel Osinenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method to embed a functional (but suboptimal) baseline policy into RL training via an arbitration mechanism that gradually transfers control from the baseline to a learnable policy, improving both training efficiency and goal-reaching rates throughout training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training RL policies from scratch is costly and inefficient, yet many control problems already have a functional baseline policy available. The goal is to leverage such baselines to improve training efficiency and produce a superior final policy.

Method: An arbitration mechanism that selects between a baseline policy and a trainable learning policy at each step. It initially relies heavily on the baseline and progressively transfers agency to the learning policy. The baseline is formalized as functional: reaching and staying in a goal set with high probability.

Result: On continuous-control benchmarks, the proposed method achieves returns matching or exceeding competitive approaches while maintaining the highest goal-reaching rates throughout training, including the final stage where the learning policy operates without baseline support.

Conclusion: Embedding a functional baseline via progressive arbitration improves training efficiency and goal-reaching rates, yielding a standalone learning policy that outperforms the baseline and matches/exceeds competitive methods.

Abstract: Training reinforcement learning (RL) policies from scratch is
  costly: it requires careful reward and environment design,
  extensive tuning, and substantial computation.
  Yet many control problems already have a functional but
  suboptimal policy available as a baseline.
  This paper proposes a method for embedding such a baseline into
  the RL training process, simultaneously improving training
  efficiency relative to from-scratch methods and producing a
  learning policy that outperforms the baseline.
  At each step, the method arbitrates between the baseline policy
  and a trainable learning policy, initially relying strongly on
  the baseline policy and then progressively transferring agency to
  the learning policy.
  By the end of training, the learning policy is a standalone
  neural network that operates without baseline policy support.
  The paper formalizes what it means for the baseline policy to be
  functional: under this policy, the agent reaches a goal set and
  remains there with high probability.
  The proposed arbitration mechanism is designed to exploit this
  property during training, yielding high goal-reaching rates right
  from the beginning of training.
  A theoretical analysis provides a formal interpretation of this
  behavior under stated assumptions and extends it to the final
  baseline-free regime, where explicit lower bounds are derived for
  the goal-reaching probability of the standalone learning policy.
  Empirical results on continuous-control benchmarks show that the
  proposed method achieves returns that match or exceed those of
  competitive approaches, while maintaining the highest
  goal-reaching rates throughout training among the compared
  methods -- including in the final stage, where the learning policy
  operates without any baseline support.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [513] [Finite-Blocklength Lossy Joint Source-Channel Coding over Unknown Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07933)
*Adeel Mahmood,Harish Viswanathan,Jinfeng Du*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies finite-blocklength performance of lossy joint source-channel codes when the channel is unknown but the source distribution is known, establishing achievability results for mismatched-design JSCC with applications to block erasure channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of lossy joint source-channel coding when there is a mismatch between the designed channel and the actual channel, particularly in the finite-blocklength regime.

Method: The paper develops mismatched-design achievability bounds using rate-distortion and rate-dispersion functions along with two new quantities: mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion. It uses Poisson functional representations and Gibbs posteriors parameterized by a family of decoder-side kernels.

Result: For block erasure channels, channel mismatch incurs no penalty. The paper demonstrates a second-order universal family of source-channel codes over block erasure channels. The Gibbs posterior envelope recovers the generalized mutual information.

Conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding mismatched JSCC performance at finite blocklengths, showing universality for block erasure channels and establishing fundamental bounds via new dispersion quantities.

Abstract: We analyze the finite-blocklength performance of lossy joint source-channel codes (JSCC) in an unknown-channel framework, where the true channel is unknown but the source distribution is known. We establish achievability results for mismatched-design JSCC, where the code design is based on a channel $Q_{Y|X}$ but deployed over a different channel $P_{Y|X}$. Our mismatched-design achievability result allows nonstationary channel laws and arbitrary standard Borel alphabets for the source, reproduction, channel input and channel output. The achievability bound is given in terms of the rate-distortion and rate-dispersion functions, as well as two channel-dependent quantities that we call the mismatched-design rate and mismatched-design rate-dispersion. For block erasure channels, our result shows that channel mismatch incurs no penalty. We then show a second-order universal family of source-channel codes over the set of block erasure channels. Our code construction uses Poisson functional representations of suitable conditional probability measures to produce the encoder and decoder outputs. We use a parameterized family of Gibbs posteriors as the decoder-side kernels, whose envelope recovers the generalized mutual information.

</details>


### [514] [Fluid Antenna System-Enabled Mitigation of Asynchronous Reception in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08017)
*Jun Qian,Zan Li,Junhui Rao,Ross Murch,Khaled B. Letaief*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes integrating fluid antenna systems (FASs) into distributed cell-free massive MIMO to mitigate asynchronous reception effects. It derives spectral efficiency performance with MR precoding, proposes a nonmonotone accelerated projected gradient ascent algorithm to jointly optimize FAS positions and power control, and shows FAS spatial DoFs can counteract delay phases, outperforming fixed-position antennas in both coherent and non-coherent transmissions for 6G systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical distributed deployments suffer from asynchronous signal arrivals, which exacerbate multi-user interference and degrade system performance, especially for coherent transmission. The paper aims to natively mitigate this asynchronous reception effect.

Method: Integrates fluid antenna systems (FASs) into distributed cell-free massive MIMO systems, exploiting reconfigurable spatial positions to release additional spatial degrees of freedom (DoFs). Establishes FAS-enabled data transmission model with asynchronous reception (delay phases). Derives analytical downlink SE performance under coherent/non-coherent transmissions using low-complexity Maximum Ratio (MR) precoding. Proposes a nonmonotone accelerated projected gradient ascent algorithm to jointly optimize FAS positions and power control coefficients for maximizing downlink sum SE.

Result: Asynchronous reception severely degrades coherent transmission performance, but optimized FAS positions (spatial DoFs) with efficient power control significantly counteract unknown delay phases and outperform traditional fixed-position antennas. For non-coherent transmission (which bypasses asynchronous reception), FAS leverages spatial reconfigurability to maximize signal strength and achieve more pronounced SE gains.

Conclusion: The proposed FAS-enabled system with efficient power control mitigates performance degradation due to asynchronous reception and outperforms traditional fixed-position antennas, paving the way for practical FAS deployment in robust, highly efficient 6G cell-free massive MIMO systems.

Abstract: Practical distributed deployments inherently suffer from asynchronous signal arrivals, which exacerbate multi-user interference and degrade system performance, especially for coherent transmission. To natively mitigate the asynchronous reception effect, this paper proposes integrating fluid antenna systems (FASs) into distributed cell-free massive MIMO systems, exploiting their reconfigurable spatial positions to release additional spatial degrees of freedom (DoFs). We establish the FAS-enabled data transmission model with asynchronous reception, i.e., delay phases. We also derive the analytical downlink spectral efficiency (SE) performance of the proposed system under coherent and non-coherent transmissions, using low-complexity Maximum Ratio (MR) precoding to provide fundamental theoretical bounds. Specifically, we propose a novel nonmonotone accelerated projected gradient ascent algorithm to jointly optimize FAS positions and power control coefficients, maximizing the downlink sum SE. Numerical results demonstrate that while asynchronous reception severely degrades system performance for coherent transmission, the spatial DoFs unlocked by optimized FAS positions, along with efficient power control, can significantly counteract the effects of unknown delay phases and outperform traditional fixed-position antennas. For non-coherent transmission, which inherently bypasses asynchronous reception, the application of FAS leverages spatial reconfigurability to natively maximize signal strength and achieve more pronounced SE gains. Ultimately, our proposed FAS-enabled system, coupled with efficient power control, mitigates performance degradation due to asynchronous reception and outperforms traditional fixed-position antennas, paving the way for the practical deployment of FASs in robust, highly efficient 6G cell-free massive MIMO systems.

</details>


### [515] [Soft Covering via Hypothesis Testing: Typical-Code Exponents and Mismatched Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08124)
*Neri Merhav*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the quenched (typical-code) behavior of false-alarm and missed-detection error exponents in Neyman-Pearson testing with soft covering, proving self-averaging of error exponents for matched and mismatched likelihood ratio tests, and characterizing the coding rate threshold for simultaneous positivity of both error exponents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the typical-code (quenched) behavior of error exponents in Neyman-Pearson testing with soft covering, complementing the average-code (annealed) analysis from previous work, and to study the effects of model mismatch on these exponents.

Method: The authors analyze the quenched regime of error exponents for Neyman-Pearson tests using randomly selected fixed-composition codebooks, proving convergence of normalized log-error probabilities to their average-code exponents. They extend this to mismatched likelihood ratio tests using wrong channel models and characterize the degradation.

Result: For almost every randomly selected fixed-composition codebook, the negative normalized logarithms of both FA and MD error probabilities converge to their average-code exponents (self-averaging). This property persists under model mismatch. The coding rate threshold beyond which both error exponents cannot be simultaneously positive is characterized; in the matched case, this equals the channel input-output mutual information rate.

Conclusion: The paper establishes that error exponents for Neyman-Pearson testing are self-averaging under soft covering, both for matched and mismatched scenarios, and identifies the fundamental rate limit for simultaneously achieving positive FA and MD exponents.

Abstract: We study the typical-code (quenched) behavior of the false-alarm (FA) and missed-detection (MD) error exponents of the Neyman-Pearson test associated with soft covering, complementing the average-code (annealed) analysis that has been carried out in a companion paper [1]. We prove that, as the block-length tends to infinity, for almost every randomly selected fixed-composition codebook, the negative normalized logarithms of both error probabilities converge to their respective average-code exponents. In other words, the error exponents are self-averaging. We then extend the scope and study a mismatched likelihood ratio test that assumes the wrong channel model. Here, we derive the mismatched error exponents, show that self-averaging persists under mismatch, and characterize the degradation. In particular, we characterize the coding rate beyond which the two kinds of error exponents cannot be positive at the same time, which in the matched case, is given by the channel input-output mutual information rate.

</details>


### [516] [Superdirectivity as a Spectral-Collision RKHS Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08174)
*Hong Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper reinterprets array superdirectivity using reproducing-kernel Hilbert space theory, showing that the M^2 endfire gain law arises naturally from polynomial jet geometry and Christoffel-Darboux kernel asymptotics as array spacing approaches zero, separating fundamental gain limits from numerical conditioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous, geometric interpretation of array superdirectivity that separates the fundamental gain limits from numerical conditioning issues, unlike classical approaches relying on near-singular optimization.

Method: Develops an RKHS framework where the exponential family from a linear array converges to a polynomial jet space as spacing → 0. Array gain is linked to the diagonal of the reproducing kernel, and asymptotic analysis of the Christoffel-Darboux kernel yields the M² endfire law. Christoffel function collapse rates distinguish boundary from interior behavior.

Result: The M² endfire gain law emerges from endpoint asymptotics of the Christoffel-Darboux kernel. The Christoffel function collapses M times faster at the hard edge than in the interior, identifying superdirectivity as a boundary concentration phenomenon. The quadratic scaling is specific to L²([-1,1]) geometry; other RKHS geometries yield different scalings.

Conclusion: Superdirectivity is fundamentally a geometric boundary concentration phenomenon explained by RKHS and polynomial jet theory, not merely a numerical artifact. The approach cleanly separates gain limits from conditioning, and the M² scaling is geometry-dependent.

Abstract: We develop a reproducing-kernel Hilbert space interpretation of array superdirectivity based on spectral-collision limits and polynomial jet geometry. As the spacing of an $M$-element linear array tends to zero, the exponential family generated by a linear array undergoes a spectral collision, and the associated finite-dimensional subspaces converge in reproducing kernel to a polynomial jet space. Array gain equals the diagonal evaluation of the reproducing kernel, and the $M^2$ endfire law emerges from endpoint asymptotics of the Christoffel-Darboux kernel. Unlike classical derivations that rely on near-singular optimization, the present approach separates array gain limits from numerical conditioning, and identifies superdirectivity as a geometric boundary concentration phenomenon: Christoffel function collapse at the hard edge is a factor of $M$ faster than in the interior. The quadratic scaling is tied specifically to the flat $L^2([-1,1])$ geometry; alternative RKHS geometries admit different concentration scalings.

</details>


### [517] [Prime Event Languages: An Information-Theoretic Investigation of Twin-Prime Event Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08395)
*Jinhua Liao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces prime event languages — representing prime phenomena (twin primes, prime gaps) as symbolic sequences — and analyzes them using information theory and Markov models on data up to N=5×10^9. Results show weak but real first-order statistical structure: first-order Markov improves cross-entropy slightly, and mutual information drops by ~11,700-fold from lag 1 to lag 2, indicating a short information horizon of about one event.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether representing arithmetic phenomena (primes) as symbolic event sequences can reveal information-theoretic structure that is less apparent in traditional numerical, probabilistic, or analytic frameworks.

Method: Construct prime event languages from twin-prime occurrences and prime-gap records up to N=5×10^9 (234,954,223 primes). Apply finite-order Markov models with train/test validation, mutual-information analysis, and information-horizon measurements to quantify statistical dependencies.

Result: First-order Markov modeling reduces test-set cross-entropy from 0.325350 to 0.319949 bits (~0.0054 bit gain), validated out-of-sample. Mutual information drops from ~5.96×10⁻³ bits (lag 1) to ~5.07×10⁻⁷ bits (lag 2), an ~11,700-fold (~4 orders of magnitude) reduction. Beyond lag 2, information fluctuates near noise floor.

Conclusion: Prime event languages exhibit weak but reproducible short-range statistical structure with first-order dependence and an effective information horizon of about one event — not perfectly memoryless, but not strongly predictable either. This demonstrates that alternative symbolic representations can uncover information-theoretic organization in arithmetic phenomena.

Abstract: Prime numbers are traditionally studied through numerical, probabilistic, and analytic frameworks. In this work, we introduce the concept of a prime event language, in which arithmetic phenomena are represented as symbolic event sequences and analyzed using tools from information theory and stochastic processes. Using all primes up to $N = 5 \times 10^9$ (234,954,223 primes), we construct event languages based on twin-prime occurrences and record prime-gap events. We investigate their statistical properties through finite-order Markov models, train/test validation, mutual-information analysis, and information-horizon measurements. For the Twin Prime Event Language, first-order Markov modeling reduces test-set cross entropy from 0.325350 bits to 0.319949 bits, corresponding to an information gain of approximately 0.0054 bits. This gain survives out-of-sample validation and therefore reflects genuine statistical structure rather than overfitting. Mutual-information analysis independently confirms the Markov results and shows that measurable dependence is concentrated almost entirely at lag 1. The mutual information decreases from approximately $5.96 \times 10^{-3}$ bits at lag 1 to approximately $5.07 \times 10^{-7}$ bits at lag 2 (approximately 11,700-fold reduction), representing a reduction of more than four orders of magnitude. Beyond lag 2, residual information fluctuates near the statistical noise floor. These results indicate that prime event languages are neither perfectly memoryless nor strongly predictable. Instead, they exhibit weak but reproducible short-range statistical structure characterized by first-order dependence and an effective information horizon of approximately one event. More broadly, this work illustrates how alternative representations can reveal information-theoretic organization that remains less apparent in conventional numerical descriptions of arithmetic phenomena.

</details>


### [518] [Simplest Nontrivial Maxwellian Random Field Models for Stochastic LoS MIMO Using the Dyadic Green's Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08463)
*Lumeng Xu,Said Mikki*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel stochastic dyadic Green's function (SDGF) framework for modeling EM MIMO channels under wavenumber uncertainty, providing Maxwell-consistent random field models of LoS propagation that serve as fundamental baselines for stochastic channel characterization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop physically consistent stochastic models for EM MIMO channels that, unlike conventional phenomenological fading models, are exact solutions of Maxwell's equations and preserve the vectorial structure and dispersion relation.

Method: The authors propose a full-wave, physics-compliant stochastic dyadic Green's function (SDGF) framework with two models: an analytically tractable wavenumber Gaussian model and a more general stochastic plane wave (SPW) model, both preserving Maxwell's equations and naturally incorporating propagating and evanescent modes.

Result: The complex SPW model's key results can be reproduced by the simpler Gaussian model with limited variance. The Maxwell-consistent stochasticity explains observed increases in channel capacity and DoF over deterministic MIMO baseline in 2D continuous MIMO systems.

Conclusion: These idealized Maxwellian random field theoretic models provide a physically grounded reference point for understanding fundamental limits in stochastic LoS propagation environments.

Abstract: This letter introduces a novel, full-wave, physics-compliant stochastic dyadic Green's function (SDGF) framework for modeling electromagnetic (EM) multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) channels under wavenumber uncertainty. Unlike conventional phenomenological fading models, the proposed approach provides what appear to be the simplest exact random field models of electromagnetic line-of-sight (LoS) propagation that are also exact solutions of Maxwell's equations. Hence, we dub them Maxwellian random field theoretic models. These physically consistent stochastic models, including an analytically tractable wavenumber Gaussian model and a more general stochastic plane wave (SPW) model, serve as fundamental baseline models for stochastic LoS channel characterization. By preserving the vectorial structure of Maxwell's equations and the dispersion relation, the framework naturally incorporates both propagating and evanescent modes. Our analysis of ergodic capacity and degrees of freedom (DoF) reveals that the key results of the complex SPW model can be reproduced by the simpler Gaussian model with limited variance. Furthermore, we provide examples using 2D continuous MIMO systems, illustrating how the model's Maxwell-consistent stochasticity explains observed increases in channel capacity and DoF over the deterministic MIMO capacity baseline. These idealized Maxwellian random field theoretic models offer a physically grounded reference point for understanding fundamental limits in stochastic LoS propagation environments.

</details>


### [519] [New Codes from Cyclic and Negacyclic Codes of Even Length over $\mathbb{Z}_4$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08750)
*Nuh Aydin,Mohamed O. Belghith,Godwin Idowu,Trang T. T. Nguyen,Long B. Tran*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses theoretical results to design search algorithms that find 2500 new cyclic codes and 730 new negacyclic codes over ℤ₄ with improved parameters, and obtains good binary quantum codes from them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To discover new linear codes over ℤ₄ with better parameters than previously known codes, leveraging existing theoretical foundations.

Method: Designs search algorithms based on established theoretical results to find cyclic and negacyclic codes of even length over ℤ₄.

Result: Found 2500 new cyclic codes and 730 new negacyclic codes with improved parameters; derived good binary quantum codes from these ℤ₄ codes.

Conclusion: The search algorithms successfully produced many new codes with enhanced parameters, and the derived binary quantum codes also show good performance.

Abstract: This paper uses theoretical results previously established in the literature to design search algorithms to find new linear codes over $\mathbb{Z}_4$ from cyclic and negacyclic codes of even length. As a result of these searches, we have found 2500 new cyclic codes and 730 negacyclic codes. These new codes exhibit improved parameters compared to previously known codes. Additionally, we have obtained binary quantum codes with good parameters from such $\mathbb{Z}_4$ codes.

</details>


### [520] [Non-Uniform Codebook Design for Optical IRS-Assisted VLC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08774)
*Rashid Iqbal,Dimitrios Bozanis,Dimitrios Tyrovolas,Christos K. Liaskos,Muhammad Ali Imran,George K. Karagiannidis,Hanaa Abumarshoud*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a geometric-optics-based non-uniform codebook design for OIRS-assisted VLC systems to achieve more uniform coverage on the user plane, outperforming uniform codebooks with fewer codewords.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical OIRS deployment requires a finite offline codebook to avoid repeated real-time optimization of mirror orientations, but uniform codebooks with fixed angular steps provide non-uniform coverage due to nonlinear mapping from steering angles to reflection locations on the user plane.

Method: A geometric-optics-based non-uniform codebook design is proposed, constructing an individual codebook for each OIRS element based on its geometric position to distribute reflected beams more uniformly over the user plane.

Result: The proposed design provides more uniform spatial mapping with fewer codewords than the uniform codebook; sweep-angle resolution has a stronger effect on codebook accuracy than tilt-angle resolution.

Conclusion: The non-uniform codebook design effectively addresses the nonlinear mapping issue, enabling more efficient OIRS-assisted VLC deployment with improved coverage uniformity.

Abstract: Optical intelligent reflecting surfaces (OIRS) can improve the coverage of indoor visible light communication (VLC) systems, however, practical deployment requires a finite offline codebook to avoid repeated real-time optimisation of mirror orientations. A uniform codebook with fixed angular steps does not provide uniform coverage on the user plane, because the mapping from steering angles to reflection locations on the user plane is nonlinear. To address this problem, this paper proposes a geometric-optics-based non-uniform codebook design for OIRS-assisted VLC systems. The proposed method constructs an individual codebook for each IRS element according to its geometric position, so that the reflected beams are distributed more uniformly over the user plane. The codebook accuracy is evaluated using the Frobenius norm of the channel error matrix. Simulation results show that the proposed design provides more uniform spatial mapping with fewer codewords than the uniform codebook, and that the sweep-angle resolution has a stronger effect on the codebook accuracy than the tilt-angle resolution.

</details>


### [521] [Flexible Coupler Antenna Enhanced Wireless Communication: Modeling and Coupler Position Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08829)
*Xiaodan Shao,Chuangye Shan,Yunlong Du,Junling Li,Rui Zhang,Cheng-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a flexible coupler antenna (FCA) system that achieves mechanical beamforming by moving passive coupling elements around a fixed active antenna, significantly reducing active antennas and RF chains while maintaining high performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the high cost and complexity of conventional active array beamforming systems by replacing many active antennas and RF chains with a single active antenna and movable passive couplers.

Method: The authors model the FCA system using multi-port circuit theory for both LoS and multipath channels, derive mechanical beamforming weights as functions of coupler positions, and formulate an SNR maximization problem solved via a block-coordinate conditional gradient method.

Result: Simulation results show the proposed FCA system significantly outperforms benchmark schemes in achievable rate while using far fewer active antennas and RF chains.

Conclusion: Mechanical beamforming via movable passive couplers offers a cost-effective and energy-efficient alternative to traditional active array beamforming for wireless communication systems.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel flexible coupler antenna (FCA) that translates passive coupling elements around a fixed-position active antenna to reshape the induced currents on the passive elements for radiation. A new form of mechanical beamforming is achieved by moving only the passive coupling elements while keeping the active antenna stationary. The proposed design significantly reduces the antenna and radio-frequency (RF) chain costs of conventional active array beamforming with low mechanical control complexity and energy consumption. For the purpose of exposition, we consider a point-to-point communication system with one FCA at the transmitter and one fixed antenna at the receiver. Specifically, based on multi-port circuit theory, we establish both the line-of-sight (LoS) and multipath channel models and derive the mechanical beamforming weights of the passive couplers as functions of their positions. Then, we formulate a new problem to maximize the received signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by optimizing the positions of passive couplers at the transmitter, subject to coupler movement and transmit power constraints. Solving the resulting problem is inherently difficult because coupled channel and mechanical beamforming create non-linearity in the objective function.To tackle this problem, we propose an efficient block-coordinate conditional gradient method to search for the best positions of all passive couplers by sequentially optimizing the position of each coupler with those of the other couplers fixed in an iterative manner.Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed system significantly outperforms benchmark schemes in terms of achievable rate, but
  with significantly reduced active antennas and RF chains.

</details>


### [522] [Optimal Regret Exponents for Bayesian Statistical Decision Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08895)
*Hyun-Young Park,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the exact exponential decay rate (error exponent) of optimal Bayes regret in finite-state finite-action Bayesian statistical decision problems, showing it equals the minimum multivariate Chernoff information over minimal incompatible subsets of states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While exact error-exponent characterizations exist for special cases like hypothesis testing and exclusion, the asymptotic behavior of optimal Bayes regret for general decision problems was largely unknown.

Method: The authors derive the exact exponent for arbitrary loss functions by identifying minimal incompatible subsets of states (collections of states where no single action is optimal for all), and computing the minimum multivariate Chernoff information over these subsets.

Result: The optimal regret decays exponentially fast with an exponent given by the minimum multivariate Chernoff information over minimal incompatible subsets. This recovers classical results for symmetric multiple hypothesis testing and hypothesis exclusion, and provides the first exact exponent for list hypothesis testing.

Conclusion: A unified exact error-exponent characterization for general Bayesian decision problems is established, generalizing known special cases and yielding new results for list hypothesis testing.

Abstract: We study finite-state finite-action Bayesian statistical decision problems. While exact error-exponent characterizations are known for several special cases, including hypothesis testing and hypothesis exclusion, the asymptotic behavior of the optimal Bayes regret is largely unknown for general decision problems. In this paper, we show that the optimal regret always decays exponentially fast and characterize its exact exponent for arbitrary loss functions. The exponent is given by the minimum multivariate Chernoff information over the minimal incompatible subsets of states, where an incompatible subset is a collection of states for which no single action is optimal for all states in the subset. Our result recovers the classical pairwise-minimum Chernoff exponent for symmetric multiple hypothesis testing and the multivariate Chernoff exponent for hypothesis exclusion, while also yielding, to the best of our knowledge, the first exact exponent characterization for list hypothesis testing.

</details>


### [523] [Embedding linear codes over Z4 into self-orthogonal codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08964)
*Junmin An,Jon-Lark Kim,San Ling*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper investigates self-orthogonal embedding problems for linear codes over Z4, establishes tight bounds and exact lengths, classifies binary cases, and presents an algorithmic construction that yields new codes with improved minimum Lee distances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the self-orthogonal embedding problem for linear codes over Z4, a topic not previously fully explored, and to improve upon existing code constructions.

Method: Theoretical analysis establishing bounds and exact lengths for self-orthogonal embeddings over Z4, complete classification of doubly even self-orthogonal embeddings for binary codes, and an algorithmic construction for shortest embeddings under specific conditions.

Result: Tight bounds and exact shortest self-orthogonal embedding lengths under specific conditions (e.g., for quaternary Preparata codes), complete classification of doubly even embedding lengths for binary codes, and twelve new Z4-linear codes with higher minimum Lee distances than previously known.

Conclusion: The paper provides a systematic framework for self-orthogonal embeddings over Z4, and demonstrates practical improvements in code construction, yielding better codes than those in existing databases.

Abstract: The purpose of this paper is to investigate the self-orthogonal embedding problem for linear codes over Z4. We propose several tight bounds on the length of the shortest self-orthogonal embedding over Z4, and determine the exact shortest self-orthogonal embedding length under specific conditions. As an example satisfying these conditions, we establish the exact length of the shortest self-orthogonal embedding for the quaternary Preparata codes. Furthermore, to establish these results, we completely classify the exact length of the shortest doubly even self-orthogonal embedding for binary linear codes in every possible case. Finally, when the shortest self-orthogonal embedding length of a given free code over Z4 is equal to the shortest doubly even self-orthogonal embedding length of its residue code, we present an algorithm to construct all possible shortest self-orthogonal embeddings. With our algorithm, we found twelve linear codes over Z4 whose minimum Lee distances are higher than those of the Z4-linear codes in Aydins database.

</details>


### [524] [Deterministic versus Stochastic Optimization for Joint Path Planning and Dynamic Time Splitting in Multiple-UAV-Cached IoT Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09014)
*Trinh Van Chien,Dinh Thanh Tung,Waqas Khalid,Ngo Cong Dung,Banh Thi Quynh Mai,Symeon Chatzinotas*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper optimizes throughput in wireless-powered IoT networks with multiple UAVs using backscatter/caching, dynamic time splitting, and a BCD/GA alternating algorithm, achieving ≥31% improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maximize total throughput in UAV-aided IoT networks by jointly optimizing DTS ratio, trajectory, and transmission power while leveraging caching, backscatter, and EH capabilities under non-convex constraints.

Method: Proposes an alternating algorithm using BCD to jointly optimize DTS ratio (closed-form via KKT conditions), trajectory, and transmission power; also introduces a GA (one-point crossover, value mutation, rank-based selection) for comparison.

Result: BCD and GA achieve at least 31% throughput improvement over benchmarks with reduced computational time.

Conclusion: The proposed solutions demonstrate significant performance gains and practical feasibility in caching-enabled UAV-aided IoT networks.

Abstract: This paper examines wireless-powered Internet of Things (IoT) networks involving multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with backscatter and caching technologies to relay and transmit signals. For data communication and energy harvesting (EH), the source transmits information and power to UAVs using the dynamic time splitting (DTS) method. UAVs use harvested energy for passive communication (backscatter) and for active communication (transmitting information) to the destination. The primary objective is to maximize the total throughput by jointly optimizing the DTS ratio, trajectory, and transmission power, leveraging the UAVs' caching capability. This optimization problem is challenging due to its non-convexity. Therefore, an efficient alternating algorithm using the block coordinate descent (BCD) method is proposed to optimize each variable given the fixed values of the other parameters. By applying the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions, we derive a closed-form expression for the optimal DTS ratio, significantly reducing computation time. The optimal values for the other two parameters are determined using the BCD. In order to thoroughly assess the effectiveness of various solutions for the original problem, this paper introduces an approach leveraging a genetic algorithm (GA). The GA in this context employs a one-point crossover method, value mutation, and rank-based selection based on fitness values. Numerical results show that the BCD and GA achieve at least 31% throughput improvement over the benchmarks, with reduced computational time. These findings demonstrate the performance gain and practical feasibility of our solutions in caching-enabled UAV-aided IoT networks.

</details>


### [525] [Extreme Points of the $(0,δ)$-LDP Polytope with Small Input Size and Arbitrary Output Sizes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09161)
*Supriya Rawat,Myna Vajha,Gowtham R. Kurri,Anand Sarwate*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the extreme points of the (ε,δ)-LDP privacy polytope, completing the characterization for small input alphabets (k=2,3) and identifying new extreme mechanisms (star configuration types) for larger alphabets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While (ε,0)-LDP polytopes are well understood, the (ε,δ)-LDP polytope with δ>0 remains largely unexplored. Recent work showed fundamental differences even for ε=0, motivating the need for a complete characterization.

Method: The authors provide complete characterizations of extreme points for low-input-alphabet regimes k=2 and k=3 with arbitrary output alphabet size m, and identify new star configuration type extreme mechanisms for larger k.

Result: Complete extreme point characterizations for k=2 and k=3 input alphabets (any output size m). Discovery of new star configuration extreme mechanisms for larger input alphabets.

Conclusion: The (ε,δ)-LDP polytope is fundamentally different from (ε,0)-LDP and requires distinct analysis. The authors advance understanding by fully characterizing small-input regimes and identifying novel extreme mechanisms for larger settings.

Abstract: The structure of locally differentially private (LDP) mechanisms can be understood through the geometry of the corresponding privacy polytope. While the extreme points of the \( (ε,0)\)-LDP polytope are well characterized (Kairouz \emph{et al.}, 2014; Holohan \emph{et al.}, 2017; Pensia \emph{et al.}, 2017), comparatively little is known for the \((ε,δ)\)-LDP polytope with \(δ>0\). Recent work (Elangovan and Jog, 2024) has shown that even in the special case \(ε=0\), the \( (0,δ) \)-LDP privacy polytope exhibits fundamentally different behaviour. In this work, we provide complete characterizations of the extreme points for the low-input-alphabet regime \(k=2\) and \(k=3\) and with arbitrary output alphabet size \(m \). We also identify new extreme mechanisms for larger input alphabet sizes $k$, of the star configuration type, as introduced by Elangovan and Jog (2024).

</details>


### [526] [Consecutive Support Matching Induced Parameter Tuning Accelerates Momentum Iterative Hard Thresholding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09382)
*Samrat Mukhopadhyay,Debasmita Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: CosMIHT introduces an adaptive momentum mechanism for iterative hard thresholding that automatically switches between conservative and accelerated parameters based on support stability, achieving near-optimal convergence with theoretical guarantees and superior empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Momentum-based acceleration of IHT can speed up sparse signal recovery but requires careful parameter tuning complicated by frequent support changes. Existing methods lack automatic adaptation between conservative and aggressive regimes.

Method: CosMIHT uses a Consecutive Support Matching rule: when two consecutive iterates share the same support, it estimates extreme eigenvalues via a lightweight power method and switches to optimal heavy-ball parameters; otherwise, it stays conservative.

Result: Two-phase convergence theory: (1) Wandering phase — linear contraction with explicit bound on iterations to identify correct support; (2) Lock-in phase — with high probability, near-optimal accelerated convergence. Extensive experiments show faster convergence than state-of-the-art without compromising recovery quality.

Conclusion: CosMIHT automatically interpolates between cautious and accelerated behavior, resolving the parameter tuning difficulty in momentum-based IHT while providing strong theoretical guarantees and practical superiority.

Abstract: Momentum-based acceleration of iterative hard thresholding (IHT) can dramatically speed up sparse signal recovery from linear measurements, but its effectiveness hinges on careful parameter tuning -- a task complicated by the frequent support changes inherent to hard thresholding. We propose CosMIHT(Consecutive Support Matching Induced Momentum IHT), which resolves this difficulty through a simple adaptive rule: start with the conservative parameters and whenever two consecutive iterates share the same support, estimate the extreme eigenvalues of the support restricted Gram matrix via a lightweight power method and switch to the corresponding optimal heavy-ball parameters. This mechanism allows CosMIHT to automatically interpolate between cautious MIHT-like behavior during support discovery and near-optimal accelerated convergence after support identification.
  Under standard restricted isometry assumptions, we develop a two-phase convergence theory. In the \emph{wandering phase}, we establish a linear contraction of the recovery error up to a noise floor and derive an explicit upper bound on the number of iterations required to identify the correct support. In the \emph{lock-in} phase, we establish that, with a randomly initialized power method based eigenvalue estimates that depend on the number of power iterations, the algorithm enjoys, with high probability, a near-optimal accelerated convergence rate akin to the heavy ball method. We corroborate the theoretical findings with extensive numerical experiments on both noiseless and noisy measurements demonstrating that CosMIHT achieves faster convergence than state-of-the-art iterative sparse recovery techniques without compromising the recovery performance.

</details>


### [527] [Explicit and asymptotically good constructions of Algebraic Geometry codes in the sum-rank metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09448)
*Peter Beelen,Elena Berardini,Anina Gruica,Maria Montanucci*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper extends recent work on Linearized Algebraic Geometry codes, which are the sum-rank metric analog of classical AG codes, providing explicit optimal and asymptotic constructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To further develop the recently introduced Linearized AG codes in the sum-rank metric by providing explicit, optimal, and asymptotic constructions that were missing or incomplete in prior work.

Method: The paper builds upon prior work [9] on Linearized AG codes, using quotients of Ore polynomial rings with coefficients in algebraic function fields to construct and analyze codes in the sum-rank metric.

Result: Explicit, optimal, and asymptotic constructions of Linearized Algebraic Geometry codes are provided.

Conclusion: The paper advances the theory of Linearized AG codes by providing concrete constructions that are optimal and asymptotically good, extending the classical AG code paradigm to the sum-rank metric setting.

Abstract: Algebraic Geometry (AG) codes (i.e. linear codes from algebraic function fields) in the Hamming metric were proposed by Goppa in 1980 and have been intensively studied ever since. Linearized Algebraic Geometry codes, the analogue of AG codes in the sum-rank metric, were instead introduced more recently [9], using quotients of the ring of Ore polynomials with coefficients in an algebraic function field. In this paper, we further investigate the results in [9], providing explicit, optimal and asymptotic constructions.

</details>


### [528] [Constructions of Quantum $(r,δ)$-LRCs from cyclic codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09522)
*Rajendra Prasad Rajpurohit,Maheshanand Bhaintwal*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper constructs quantum locally recoverable codes (qLRCs) from classical cyclic $(r,δ)$-LRCs using a dual-containing condition, presenting three explicit families—two of which are optimal with respect to the quantum Singleton-like bound when pure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical LRCs are essential for efficient erasure recovery in distributed storage, and this work extends their benefits to quantum data storage by constructing quantum LRCs.

Method: The authors identify cyclic LRCs whose defining sets satisfy a dual-containing condition, enabling them to serve as valid CSS (Calderbank-Shor-Steane) ingredients for constructing qLRCs.

Result: Three explicit families of $(r,δ)$-qLRCs are presented; two are optimal with respect to the quantum Singleton-like bound (when pure), and Constructions 2 and 3 have no length restriction relative to field size.

Conclusion: The work provides optimal qLRC examples and demonstrates that cyclic LRCs can be effectively leveraged for quantum error correction without field size limitations on code length.

Abstract: Classical $(r,δ)$ locally recoverable codes (LRCs) play a central role in distributed data storage systems as they enable an efficient recovery from erasures by accessing a small number of surviving symbols. Motivated by their prospective use in future quantum data storage and by recent theoretical progress on quantum locally recoverable codes (qLRCs), we investigate the construction of qLRCs from classical cyclic $(r,δ)$-LRCs. Our approach identifies cyclic LRCs whose defining sets satisfy a dual-containing condition, allowing them to serve as valid CSS ingredients. We present three explicit families of $(r,δ)$-qLRCs, two of which are optimal with respect to the quantum Singleton-like bound, whenever the codes are pure, thereby providing optimal examples. Additionally, the codes presented in Constructions 2 and 3 have no bound on their lengths with respect to the field size required to obtain these codes.

</details>


### [529] [Formal Foundations and Proof-Carrying Certificates for q-ary Covering Codes in Lean 4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09600)
*Andreas Florath*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A Lean 4 formalization of covering-code theory in finite Hamming spaces, providing machine-checked upper/lower bounds and exact covering numbers via proof-carrying certificates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a reusable, auditable foundation for machine-checked covering-code certificates, ensuring correctness through formal verification rather than establishing new bounds.

Method: Formalizes q-ary Hamming-ball volume formula, sphere-covering lower bound, exact cases, product/relation rules, and selected certificates in Lean 4; includes proof-carrying database where bounds replay to verified proofs.

Result: Provides a formally verified framework with proof-carrying certificates for known covering-code bounds from van Laarhoven et al. (1989), not new record bounds.

Conclusion: Delivers an auditable, reusable formal foundation for covering-code verification, enabling machine-checked confidence in published bounds.

Abstract: Covering codes in finite Hamming spaces ask for small sets of words whose Hamming balls cover the whole space. This paper presents a Lean 4 formalization of the elementary theory of q-ary covering codes, centered on certificate predicates for upper bounds, lower bounds, and exact covering numbers $K_q(n,r)$. The formalization proves the q-ary Hamming-ball volume formula, the sphere-covering lower bound, elementary exact cases, product and relation rules, and selected small exact certificates. It also demonstrates an end-to-end workflow for checking explicit upper bounds transcribed from van Laarhoven et al. (1989). The accompanying database is proof-carrying: stored bounds have traces that replay to Lean proofs of the corresponding upper- or lower-bound predicates. The contribution is not new record bounds or a reproduction of known tables, but a reusable, auditable foundation for machine-checked covering-code certificates.

</details>


### [530] [Optimal Feedback Communication with Information Maximization and Distortion Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09698)
*Aolin Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper studies feedback communication over channels, deriving conditions for maximal mutual information between source and channel outputs, and showing that posterior matching is optimal for simultaneously maximizing information and minimizing distortion in symmetric discrete channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To optimally send a real-valued source through multiple uses of a channel with feedback, focusing on the trade-off between information maximization and distortion minimization.

Method: The authors derive sufficient (and under input-identifiability, necessary) conditions for maximal mutual information. They then solve the joint information maximization-distortion minimization problem for symmetric discrete channels (e.g., k-ary symmetric/erasure channels), showing posterior matching is essentially necessary.

Result: Posterior matching, while not necessary for information maximization alone, is both sufficient and essentially necessary for achieving both information maximization and distortion minimization in symmetric channels.

Conclusion: The work provides a new regularization perspective for distortion-minimizing feedback communication via information maximization, enabling tractable optimal solutions.

Abstract: We study the problem of optimally sending a real-valued source through multiple uses of a channel with feedback. First, we state a set of conditions that are sufficient for an encoder to achieve maximal mutual information between the source and all the channel outputs. This set of conditions are also necessary when the channel is input-identifiable, a condition widely satisfied by common channel models. More notably, we further study the information maximization-distortion minimization problem, where the mutual information between the source and all channel outputs still needs to be maximized, while at each step, the MMSE of estimating the source from the channel outputs so far also needs to be minimized. We derive a solution to this problem for discrete channels with certain symmetries, e.g. $k$-ary symmetric or $k$-ary erasure channels. We show that for such channels the famous posterior matching scheme, while not necessary for information maximization alone, is sufficient and essentially necessary for achieving both information maximization and distortion minimization. This work also provides a new perspective of regularizing distortion-minimizing feedback communication through information maximization, which enables us to find the optimal solution that otherwise would be intractable.

</details>


### [531] [XAInomaly: Explainable and Interpretable Deep Contractive Autoencoder for O-RAN Traffic Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09194)
*Osman Tugay Basaran,Falko Dressler*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces XAInomaly, an explainable semi-supervised Deep Contractive Autoencoder framework for anomaly detection in O-RAN, enhanced by a reactive XAI technique called fastshap-C for interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for anomaly detection in O-RAN that is not only accurate but also interpretable, scalable, and low-complexity, addressing the black-box nature of deep learning models.

Method: A Semi-supervised Deep Contractive Autoencoder (SS-DeepCAE) for robust representation learning of normal network behavior, plus a reactive XAI technique called fastshap-C for interpretability.

Result: The SS-DeepCAE model effectively learns compressed, robust representations of normal behavior, enabling accurate anomaly identification. The fastshap-C technique provides interpretability.

Conclusion: The XAInomaly framework offers an interpretable and scalable solution for anomaly detection in O-RAN, balancing accuracy with explainability.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques have become integral part in advancing next generation wireless communication systems by enabling sophisticated data modeling and feature extraction for enhanced network performance. In the realm of open radio access networks (O-RAN), characterized by their disaggregated architecture and heterogeneous components from multiple vendors, the deployment of generative models offers significant advantages for network management such as traffic analysis, traffic forecasting and anomaly detection. However, the complex and dynamic nature of O-RAN introduces challenges that necessitate not only accurate detection mechanisms but also reduced complexity, scalability, and most importantly interpretability to facilitate effective network management. In this study, we introduce the XAInomaly framework, an explainable and interpretable Semi-supervised (SS) Deep Contractive Autoencoder (DeepCAE) design for anomaly detection in O-RAN. Our approach leverages the generative modeling capabilities of our SS-DeepCAE model to learn compressed, robust representations of normal network behavior, which captures essential features, enabling the identification of deviations indicative of anomalies. To address the black-box nature of deep learning models, we propose reactive Explainable AI (XAI) technique called fastshap-C.

</details>


### [532] [BRAIN: Bayesian Reasoning via Active Inference for Agentic and Embodied Intelligence in Mobile Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.14033)
*Osman Tugay Basaran,Martin Maier,Falko Dressler*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes BRAIN, a Bayesian Active Inference agent for 6G networks that outperforms DRL baselines in adaptability (28.3% higher robustness), causal reasoning, and interpretability for dynamic radio resource allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DRL-based AI agents in 6G networks lack explainability, suffer from catastrophic forgetting, and struggle with dynamic adaptation—critical gaps for real-time, transparent decision-making in future mobile networks.

Method: BRAIN uses a deep generative model of the network environment and minimizes variational free energy to unify perception and action in a closed-loop paradigm, implemented as an O-RAN xApp on GPU-accelerated testbed.

Result: BRAIN achieved up to 28.3% higher robustness to sudden traffic shifts without retraining, robust causal reasoning for slice-specific QoS targets, and real-time interpretability through belief state diagnostics.

Conclusion: Bayesian reasoning via Active Inference offers a viable, superior alternative to DRL for building transparent, adaptable, and robust AI agents in 6G networks.

Abstract: Future sixth-generation (6G) mobile networks will demand artificial intelligence (AI) agents that are not only autonomous and efficient, but also capable of real-time adaptation in dynamic environments and transparent in their decisionmaking. However, prevailing agentic AI approaches in networking, exhibit significant shortcomings in this regard. Conventional deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based agents lack explainability and often suffer from brittle adaptation, including catastrophic forgetting of past knowledge under non-stationary conditions. In this paper, we propose an alternative solution for these challenges: Bayesian reasoning via Active Inference (BRAIN) agent. BRAIN harnesses a deep generative model of the network environment and minimizes variational free energy to unify perception and action in a single closed-loop paradigm. We implement BRAIN as O-RAN eXtended application (xApp) on GPU-accelerated testbed and demonstrate its advantages over standard DRL baselines. In our experiments, BRAIN exhibits (i) robust causal reasoning for dynamic radio resource allocation, maintaining slice-specific quality of service (QoS) targets (throughput, latency, reliability) under varying traffic loads, (ii) superior adaptability with up to 28.3% higher robustness to sudden traffic shifts versus benchmarks (achieved without any retraining), and (iii) real-time interpretability of its decisions through human-interpretable belief state diagnostics.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [533] [PathoSage: Towards Multi-Source Evidence Adjudication in Pathology via Experience-Aware Agentic Workflow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07549)
*Chengyang Zhang,Wenchuan Zhang,Bo Li,Mengran Li,Bob Zhang,Yuhao Yi,Hong Bu,Jiancheng Lv*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PathoSage is a three-stage framework for pathology multimodal reasoning that separates knowledge retrieval, evidence collection, and adjudication, using Structured Evidence Deliberation and a Beta-Bernoulli experience system to reduce hallucinations and improve tool reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs hallucinate morphological features in pathology, and current agentic systems merge tool outputs into shared contexts, leading to conflicting evidence and context contamination.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) knowledge retrieval, (2) evidence collection, (3) evidence adjudication via Structured Evidence Deliberation. Includes a Beta-Bernoulli experience system for training-free reliability modeling with continuous credit assignment.

Result: PathoSage mitigates VQA hallucinations and classifier disagreement, outperforming strong pathology MLLM and agentic baselines.

Conclusion: Explicit evidence adjudication and reliability-aware tool modeling are key for robust pathology agents.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and agent workflows have shown strong promise for computational pathology, yet reliable patch-level reasoning remains challenging. End-to-end pathology MLLMs often hallucinate morphological features, while recent agentic systems usually merge tool outputs and retrieved knowledge into a shared context, making decisions vulnerable to conflicting evidence and context contamination. We propose PathoSage, a three-stage framework that explicitly separates knowledge retrieval, evidence collection, and evidence adjudication for patch-level pathology multimodal reasoning. Its core component, Structured Evidence Deliberation, independently evaluates heterogeneous evidence from tools, performs conflict analysis, and generates the final judgment in a fresh context to reduce anchoring bias. We further introduce a training-free Beta-Bernoulli experience system with continuous credit assignment to model long-term tool reliability and construct similarity-weighted priors for future tool use. Experiments show that PathoSage effectively mitigates VQA hallucinations and classifier disagreement, outperforming strong pathology MLLM and agentic baselines. Our results highlight explicit evidence adjudication and reliability-aware tool modeling as key ingredients for robust pathology agents.

</details>


### [534] [OmniMem: Perturbation-aware Memory Compression for Streaming Audio-Visual LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07577)
*Guangzhi Sun,Yixuan Li,Yudong Yang,Chao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniMem is a memory-efficient streaming framework for audio-visual LLMs that uses modality-aware memory allocation, perturbation-aware selection, and budget-aware fine-tuning to improve long-video understanding with 2-4% accuracy gains under limited memory budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-form video understanding with audio-visual LLMs is limited by linearly growing video tokens and KV caches; existing compression methods treat all tokens uniformly, ignoring the severe token imbalance between visual and audio modalities.

Method: OmniMem introduces (1) modality-aware memory allocation to separately manage visual and audio contexts, (2) perturbation-aware memory selection to preserve informative and non-redundant KV states, and (3) budget-aware fine-tuning to consolidate useful information into retained memory.

Result: OmniMem achieves 2-4% absolute accuracy improvement over training-free compression baselines under the same memory budgets on VideoMME Long, LVBench, and LVOmniBench, with an additional 1-2% gain after fine-tuning.

Conclusion: OmniMem effectively addresses the memory bottleneck in audio-visual LLMs for long-video understanding by leveraging modality-aware strategies and achieves consistent gains across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Audio-visual large language models (LLMs) hold strong promise for long-form video understanding, yet their long-video inference is fundamentally limited by the linear growth of video tokens and key-value (KV) caches. We present OmniMem, a memory-efficient streaming framework designed specifically for audio-visual LLMs. Unlike existing compression methods that treat all tokens uniformly, OmniMem introduces a modality-aware memory allocation strategy that separately manages visual and audio contexts, addressing the severe token imbalance between the two modalities. OmniMem further preserves informative and non-redundant KV states through perturbation-aware memory selection, enabling compact memory without sacrificing long-range understanding. To strengthen compression under realistic deployment constraints, we also explore budget-aware fine-tuning, which encourages the model to consolidate useful information into retained memory. Experiments on VideoMME Long, LVBench, and LVOmniBench with video-SALMONN 2+ and Qwen-2.5-Omni show that OmniMem consistently improves over strong training-free compression baselines by 2-4% absolute accuracy under the same memory budgets, with an additional 1-2% gain after fine-tuning.

</details>


### [535] [Syll: Open-Source Personal Automation with Cross-Surface Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07594)
*Bo Zhang,Borui Zhang,Chenghao Jiang,Minglei Shi,Xiaofeng Wang,Zheng Zhu,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Syll is an open-source, self-hosted multimodal agent harness that unifies MCP/API tools, CLI, and GUI control, enabling teachable, auditable personal automation across heterogeneous interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personal AI agents need to operate across diverse interfaces (APIs, shells, web, desktop GUIs), but existing systems are tied to single interfaces and lack support for user teaching and auditability.

Method: Syll provides a modular runtime unifying MCP/API tools, CLI, and visual GUI control, with a bidirectional user-agent interaction layer: users demonstrate procedures to compile reusable skills, and agent actions are translated into multimodal evidence (logs, keyframes, approval checkpoints). Memory, skills, routines, and governance are externalized as editable local artifacts.

Result: Validated on production desktop applications (Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Audition, Stardew Valley, macOS Finder). Mechanism-oriented studies confirm multimodal routing, teachable GUI replay, and persistent local artifacts.

Conclusion: Syll can serve as a practical open-source foundation for personal automation that users can teach, inspect, and continuously extend.

Abstract: Personal AI agents must increasingly operate across APIs, shells, web surfaces, and desktop GUIs, yet many systems remain tuned to a single interface and offer limited support for user teaching and auditability. We present Syll, an open-source, self-hosted multimodal agent harness that unifies MCP/API tools, CLI execution, and visual GUI control in a modular runtime, enabling agents to coordinate computer use across heterogeneous interfaces while streamlining how users and agents exchange information. At the core of Syll is a bidirectional user-agent interaction layer: users teach procedures through direct demonstration, which Syll compiles into reusable skills; agent execution is translated back into multimodal evidence -- logs, keyframes, and approval checkpoints -- for inspection and control. Syll further externalizes memory, skills, routines, and governance as editable local artifacts, supporting straightforward inspection, extension, and downstream development. Our implementation has been validated on production desktop applications including Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Audition, Stardew Valley, macOS Finder and others. We report mechanism-oriented studies that validate multimodal routing, teachable GUI replay, and persistent local artifacts. We hope Syll can serve as a practical open-source foundation for personal automation that users can teach, inspect, and continuously extend.

</details>


### [536] [A case study of evaluating AI agents on a neuroscience data-to-discovery pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07718)
*Kai A. Horstmann,Ethan Lin,Alice A. Robie,Jennifer J. Sun,Kristin Branson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies general-purpose coding agents on a large-scale fly optogenetics data-to-discovery pipeline, finding that agents can solve individual stages but struggle with scientific judgment, visual self-evaluation, and end-to-end integration, while identifying open challenges like resource management and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate software development bottlenecks in scientific research pipelines that take domain experts days to months to build, with a focus on correctness and robustness rather than implementation details.

Method: Empirical study of general-purpose coding agents on a fly optogenetics data-to-discovery pipeline, using tasks substantially larger than existing benchmarks, datasets orders of magnitude bigger, and evaluation criteria grounded in domain expert standards.

Result: Agents solve several individual pipeline stages (stage-level automation is tractable), but struggle when no pre-defined criterion exists requiring scientific judgment; they attempt visual inspection for self-evaluation but largely fail to interpret outputs; end-to-end pipeline solving is beyond current agent abilities.
