A multi-scale graph encoder captures both fine-grained local structure and broader landscape composition, and supervises a spherical-harmonics location encoder through a contrastive alignment objective. We evaluate OSMGraphCLIP across a diverse suite of downstream geospatial regression and classification tasks spanning climate, ecology, socioeconomic indicators, public health, land cover, biodiversity, and wildfire forecasting, and show that structured OSM data alone supports strong global location representations across domains. OSMGraphCLIP matches or exceeds satellite-based baselines on the majority of benchmarks, with the most pronounced advantage on socioeconomic and public-health tasks, where OSM's explicit semantic annotation of the built environment encodes patterns of human activity that satellite pixels can only capture indirectly. On ecological and environmental tasks, the model remains closely competitive with imagery-based methods despite using no Earth observation data. Qualitative analysis confirms that the learned embeddings organize geographic space coherently, recovering biome boundaries, urban gradients, and tropical--temperate distinctions from map topology alone.

</details>


### [563] [SKILL.nb: Selective Formalization and Gated Execution for Durable Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08049)
*Amine El Hattami,Nicolas Chapados,Christopher Pal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SKILL.nb is a framework that governs reusable agent workflows with evidence-calibrated lifecycle policies, using selective formalization and gate-conditioned execution to improve reliability across environment drift, achieving strong results on WebArena-Verified (53.7% success, 91.7% retention), Mind2Web, and GitLab migration tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reusing agent artifacts (code, workflows, memories) improves efficiency but creates lifecycle reliability problems due to environment drift, underspecified tasks, and changing task distributions in web automation.

Method: Selective formalization decides which steps become executable code vs. natural language based on evidence; workflows are versioned notebooks with natural-language guidance, multi-language cells, validation gates, fallback paths, and multimodal evidence; gate-conditioned execution runs code when validated or falls back locally upon drift.

Result: 53.7% single-round success (+3.9 pts over baseline), 91.7% task retention across 3 re-executions (+15.5 pts), 72.9% failure recovery with 4.2% regressions (vs. 15-17% for baselines), strong Mind2Web results, and robust GitLab migration with minimal version gap (-1.7 to +0.6 pts).

Conclusion: Lifecycle governance and gate-conditioned execution are crucial reliability axes beyond one-shot task success, and SKILL.nb effectively addresses the reliability gap in reusable agent workflows.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly turn past experience into reusable artifacts such as code, workflows, and procedural memories. Reuse can improve efficiency, but it also creates a lifecycle reliability problem: artifacts that succeed once may fail under environment drift, underspecified tasks, or changing task distributions, especially in web automation. We introduce SKILL.nb, a framework for governing reusable agent workflows with evidence-calibrated lifecycle policies. SKILL.nb uses selective formalization: execution evidence decides which workflow steps should become executable code, which should remain natural-language guided, and when those choices should be revised. Workflows are stored as auditable, versioned notebooks that interleave natural-language guidance, multi-language executable cells, validation gates, fallback paths, and multimodal evidence such as outputs, screenshots, and error traces. At runtime, gate-conditioned execution lets each step run code when its gates validate, or fall back locally when drift invalidates the executable realization. On WebArena-Verified, SKILL.nb achieves 53.7% single-round success, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.9 percentage points. Across three re-executions, it retains 91.7% of initially successful tasks, 15.5 points above the next best method. Under bounded repair, it recovers 72.9% of subsequent failures while limiting post-repair regressions to 4.2%, compared with 15.0% to 17.0% for persistent baselines. It also leads on Mind2Web cross-website and cross-domain splits. In a GitLab migration test, SKILL.nb preserves performance when reusing frozen state learned on GitLab 15.7, with frozen-versus-fresh target-version gaps of -1.7 points on GitLab 16.11 and +0.6 points on GitLab 18.9. These results identify lifecycle governance and gate-conditioned execution as reliability axes beyond one-shot task success.

</details>


### [564] [How Small Can You Go? LoRA Fine-Tuning 270M-8B Models for Merchant Information Extraction in Financial Transactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08051)
*Donghao Huang,Tomas Drietomsky,Benjamin Barrett,Zhaoxia Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates 24 model variants (270M to 8B parameters) for extracting structured merchant info from noisy bank transactions. Key findings: Qwen 3.5 4B matches LLaMA 8B within 0.35 F1 points with half the parameters; 0.8B Qwen offers strong latency-accuracy trade-off; chain-of-thought fine-tuning adds 0.3-1.8 F1 points but Qwen 4B prefers direct JSON prompting; and benchmark performance reliably transfers to production deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying large 8B-parameter models for financial transaction processing imposes prohibitive memory, latency, and cost constraints, motivating the search for more efficient alternatives that maintain high accuracy.

Method: Systematic evaluation of 24 model variants across Gemma 3, Qwen 3.5, Aya, and LLaMA 3.1 families, comparing LoRA fine-tuning, prompting strategies (JSON-only vs chain-of-thought), and deployment as Databricks Model Serving endpoints.

Result: Qwen 3.5 4B achieves 96.60% F1 (within 0.35 of 8B baseline) with half the parameters; 0.8B Qwen reaches 94.75% F1 matching models 2.5-4x larger; CoT improves most models by 0.3-1.8 F1; benchmark performance transfers reliably to production with only 0.8 average F1 drop.

Conclusion: Smaller models (especially Qwen 3.5 variants) offer viable deployment alternatives to 8B models for structured extraction, with specific recommendations based on accuracy and latency requirements.

Abstract: Financial transaction processing requires extracting structured merchant information from noisy, abbreviated bank transaction strings at scale. Our current production system, a LoRA-fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1-8B, achieves 96.95% F1 on this task, but deploying 8-billion-parameter models imposes prohibitive memory, latency, and cost constraints. To identify more efficient alternatives, we conduct a deployment-focused study of 24 model variants spanning four model families: Gemma 3 (270M, 1B, 4B), Qwen 3.5 (0.8B, 2B, 4B), Aya (3.35B), and LLaMA 3.1-8B, systematically evaluating accuracy, inference throughput, training cost, and hardware behavior to assess production suitability. Our findings show that: (1) reproducing the LLaMA 3.1-8B fine-tune with a LoRA rank of 8 achieves 96.75% F1, only 0.20 points below the rank-32 baseline; (2) Qwen 3.5 4B with JSON-only prompting reaches 96.60% F1, within 0.35 points of the 8B baseline while using roughly half the parameters; (3) the 0.8B Qwen 3.5 model achieves 94.75% F1, matching models 2.5-4x larger and offering an attractive latency-accuracy trade-off; (4) chain-of-thought fine-tuning generally improves F1 by 0.3-1.8 points across most models, although Qwen 3.5 4B performs best with direct JSON-only prompting; and (5) Qwen 3.5 Think and Nothink training templates produce nearly identical results (F1 differences <0.004), indicating that explicit reasoning supervision is unnecessary for structured extraction tasks. We further deploy all 14 fine-tuned sub-8B models as Databricks Model Serving endpoints and observe that benchmark performance transfers reliably to production, with an average F1 change of only 0.8 points. Aya 3.35B, based on the Cohere2 architecture, is the sole exception, exhibiting a 3-5 point decline under serving conditions. Based on these results, we provide deployment recommendations across accuracy and latency requirements, ...

</details>


### [565] [A Multi-modal Agentic Co-pilot for Evidence Grounded Computational Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08093)
*Zhe Xu,Zhengyu Zhang,Zhiyuan Cai,Jiahao Xu,Yijie Lin,Ziyi Liu,Junlin Hou,Hongyi Wang,Yuxiang Nie,Ling Liang,Yihui Wang,Yingxue Xu,Ronald Cheong Kin Chan,Li Liang,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PathPocket is a multimodal AI co-pilot for evidence-grounded pathology that builds a comprehensive pathology evidence corpus (110K+ documents), a large-scale hypergraph (4.55M entities, 7.10M relations), and a multi-agent reasoning framework to handle text, ROI, and WSI tasks, significantly outperforming existing methods and improving pathologists' diagnostic accuracy and confidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The intersection of AI and evidence-based medicine in pathology remains under-explored, with existing attempts limited to text-only general medicine. There is a need for a system that integrates multimodal pathology data with traceable, evidence-grounded decision-making.

Method: PathPocket constructs a hierarchical pathology evidence corpus (110K+ documents), builds a multimodal hypergraph (4.55M entities, 7.10M relations) as a knowledge engine, and employs a multi-agent reasoning framework that integrates input understanding, evidence retrieval, filtering, and diagnosis generation.

Result: The system outperforms existing state-of-the-arts on a benchmark of 200,000+ real-world cases across text, ROI, and WSI tasks. User studies show significant improvements in pathologists' diagnostic accuracy and confidence.

Conclusion: PathPocket offers a practical, scalable, and verifiable solution for evidence-grounded computational pathology, bridging AI and evidence-based medicine in clinical workflows.

Abstract: Pathology is the cornerstone of modern medicine, where accurate decision-making relies heavily on evidence-based practices. While artificial intelligence (AI) has the potential to transform clinical workflows, the intersection of AI and evidence-based medicine remains under-explored, with primitive attempts restricted to text-only general medicine. In this work, we present PathPocket, a multimodal AI agentic co-pilot designed specifically for evidence grounded pathology. We construct the most comprehensive pathology evidence corpus to date, encompassing approximately 110,472 public and authorized documents structured across a rigorous hierarchy of evidence from clinical guideline to expert opinion. From this meticulously graded foundation, we build a large-scale multimodal pathology hypergraph containing over 4.55 million entities and 7.10 million relations. Serving as a robust knowledge engine, this hypergraph provides traceable evidence for a collaborative multi-agent reasoning framework integrating input understanding, evidence retrieval, filtering, and diagnosis generation. This enables PathPocket to seamlessly resolve a wide spectrum of clinical tasks, ranging from text-only queries to complex multimodal diagnostics involving region-of-interest (ROI) and gigapixel whole-slide images (WSIs). We rigorously evaluate the system on a multidimensional benchmark of over 200,000 real-world cases, where it significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-arts. Crucially, extensive user studies demonstrate that PathPocket substantially improves the diagnostic accuracy and confidence of pathologists. By directly grounding pathology interpretations in verifiable literature, PathPocket offers a practical and scalable solution for the future of evidence grounded computational pathology.

</details>


### [566] [When Does Delegation Beat Majority? A Delegation-Based Aggregator for Multi-Sample LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08098)
*Yasushi Sakai,Allen Song,Kent Larson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PPV, a delegation-based aggregator using within-group letter entropy and between-group reasoning geometry, outperforms majority voting on MMLU-Pro by +1.5 pp overall and +2.24 pp on non-trivial subsets, without requiring gold labels or auxiliary training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Majority voting discards two free signals per sample (letter entropy and reasoning geometry). The authors aim to design an unsupervised aggregation method that leverages these signals to improve consensus accuracy beyond majority voting.

Method: PPV partitions 128 samples into 16 groups, computes letter-level semantic entropy (WHEN) and reasoning embedding cosine (WHOM), constructs a stochastic delegation matrix, and uses its stationary distribution to select the consensus answer—all without gold labels or training.

Result: PPV beats majority voting on MMLU-Pro by +1.5 pp overall and +2.24 pp on the non-trivial subset (p ~ 1.0e-14, n=8,099). A case study shows PPV overturns a 10-6 wrong majority by detecting geometric incoherence in the majority cluster (cosine -0.02) vs. tight minority (cosine +0.26).

Conclusion: Delegation-based aggregation using per-sample entropy and geometry signals is an effective unsupervised alternative to majority voting. Negative results further constrain the design space by showing that no within-question ensemble of confidence modes closes the oracle gap.

Abstract: Majority voting over sampled answers is the dominant unsupervised aggregator for multi-sample LLM inference. We show that piping the signals every sample carries into a delegation-based aggregator (Propagational Proxy Voting, PPV) yields an unsupervised consensus rule that beats majority on MMLU-Pro by +1.5 pp overall and +2.24 pp on the non-trivial subset (paired McNemar p ~ 1.0e-14, n = 8,099). Majority discards two free signals every sample carries: within-group letter entropy and between-group reasoning geometry. PPV exposes two per-voter levers that consume exactly these signals: WHEN (how much weight a voter keeps on its own pick) and WHOM (how it splits the remainder across peers). We drive WHEN with letter entropy and WHOM with per-question-centered embedding cosine. The method needs no gold labels and no auxiliary training: per question, we partition 128 sampled generations into 16 groups, compute each group's letter-level semantic entropy and reasoning embedding centroid, and feed both into a stochastic delegation matrix whose stationary distribution selects the consensus answer. We walk through an example in which PPV overturns a clear 10-6 majority for the wrong letter: the 10-voter majority cluster is geometrically incoherent (mean within-cluster cosine -0.02) while the 6-voter minority is tight (+0.26), so propagated delegation mass concentrates on the minority's answer even though entropy alone would keep the majority ahead. We further report delegation strategies with negative results that constrain the design space for unsupervised LLM aggregation: no within-question ensemble of confidence modes closes the oracle gap.

</details>


### [567] [PACE: Anytime-Valid Acceptance Tests for Self-Evolving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08106)
*Zayx Shawn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Self-evolving agents often degrade due to false commits from naive "score went up" acceptance. The authors propose PACE, a sequential hypothesis test using betting-based e-processes, which controls false-commit rates, reduces variance, lowers evaluation costs, and maintains reliability even when no genuine improvements exist.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The weak point in self-evolving agents is the acceptor, not the proposer. The standard greedy "keep if score went up" rule causes uncontrolled adaptive multiple testing (p-hacking), leading to churn, drift, and false improvements that degrade agent performance over time.

Method: PACE (Paired Anytime-valid Commit Evaluation) reframes commit decisions as sequential hypothesis tests using a testing-by-betting e-process. It compares candidate vs. incumbent on identical instances, accumulating evidence until decisive, with optional stopping and per-decision false-commit probability control.

Result: On Qwen2.5 agents (0.5B-3B) self-evolving on GSM8K, SVAMP, ARC-Challenge: greedy acceptance commits 30-42% false and 10-33% harmful edits; PACE commits only genuine improvements, matches greedy's accuracy with ~18% lower evaluation cost and sharply lower variance. With no real gain, greedy commits 13-21 spurious modifications (72-100% false), degrading fragile agents by 4.9 points; PACE stays at baseline.

Conclusion: Reliability of self-evolution depends critically on the acceptor, not only the proposer. PACE provides a principled, training-free solution that eliminates false commits and stabilizes self-improvement.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents improve by repeatedly proposing changes to their own prompts, skills, or workflows and keeping those that score higher on a small held-out set. Almost all effort has gone into the proposer that generates candidates; we argue the weak point is the acceptor, the rule that decides whether to commit a change. Applied hundreds of times against the same noisy dev estimate, the ubiquitous "keep it if the score went up" rule is uncontrolled adaptive multiple testing: the agent effectively p-hacks itself, accumulating false commits that make it churn and drift rather than improve.
  We recast committing as a sequential hypothesis test and propose PACE (Paired Anytime-valid Commit Evaluation), a training-free, anytime-valid commit gate. Each candidate is compared to the incumbent on identical instances and committed only when a testing-by-betting e-process accumulates decisive evidence, stopping early to save evaluations and controlling each candidate's false-commit probability at a user-set level even under optional stopping (a per-decision guarantee).
  On Qwen2.5 agents (0.5B-3B) self-evolving at the prompt level on GSM8K, SVAMP, and ARC-Challenge, greedy acceptance commits 30-42% false and 10-33% harmful edits when a genuine improvement is hidden among noisy proposals, while PACE commits the real one and essentially nothing else, matching greedy's held-out accuracy at sharply lower variance and about 18% lower evaluation cost. With no real gain available, greedy commits 13-21 spurious self-modifications per run (72-100% false) and degrades the most fragile agent by 4.9 points, while PACE holds at baseline. Reliability of self-evolution depends on the acceptor, not only on the proposer.

</details>


### [568] [Think Before You Act: Intention-Guided Reasoning for LLM-Based Location Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08122)
*Qingxiang Liu,Anqi Liang,Zhuoyang Jiang,Yutian Jiang,Sisuo Lyu,Yu Ji,Haomin Wen,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IntentPOI proposes a two-stage intention-guided reasoning framework for next POI prediction, where users' traveling intentions are inferred first (thinking stage) before locating specific POIs (acting stage), outperforming 11 state-of-the-art baselines on three real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based methods treat next POI prediction as one-step trajectory-to-location mapping, which suffers from shallow trajectory correlations and historical frequency bias. The authors argue that users first form traveling intentions before selecting specific POIs, thus a more human-like reasoning framework is needed.

Method: IntentPOI is a two-stage framework: (1) Thinking stage infers intermediate intentions by analyzing historical mobility patterns, similar peer behaviors, and temporal contexts; (2) Acting stage constructs a compact candidate pool and performs intention-guided reasoning to select the best-matching POI.

Result: Consistently outperforms 11 state-of-the-art baselines across three real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Decoupling intention inference from location prediction transforms next POI prediction from direct trajectory matching into more robust intention-guided reasoning, yielding superior performance.

Abstract: Predicting a user's next Point-of-Interest (POI) based on their historical check-in records is a fundamental task in location-based services. While recent methods incorporating large language models have shown strong reasoning capabilities and promising results, they typically formulate the prediction task as a one-step trajectory-to-location mapping problem, making predictions prone to shallow trajectory correlations and historical frequency bias. We argue that users rarely choose locations directly and instead, they usually first form a traveling intention and then accordingly select specific POIs. Motivated by this insight, we propose IntentPOI, a two-stage intention-guided reasoning framework. In the thinking stage, we infer users' intermediate intentions by incorporating historical mobility patterns, similar peer behaviors, and the temporal contexts. In the acting stage, we first construct a compact candidate pool, and then perform intention-guided reasoning to identify locations that best align with the inferred intention. By explicitly decoupling intention inference from location prediction, IntentPOI transforms the next POI prediction from direct trajectory matching into intention-guided reasoning. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that IntentPOI consistently outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [569] [Cross-LLM Consistency in Inference: Evidence from Shared Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08129)
*Siyu Lou,Yao Yan,Yuntian Chen,Quanshi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Despite architectural differences, LLMs develop similar lower-order interaction patterns when predicting the same tokens, especially among advanced models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether diverse LLMs converge to similar internal inference patterns despite differences in architecture, data, and optimization.

Method: Applied interaction-based explanations to compare internal inference patterns across different LLMs predicting the same target tokens.

Result: LLMs share interaction patterns when predicting the same tokens; shared patterns are more pronounced in advanced models, tend to be lower-order, and show weaker positive-negative cancellation.

Conclusion: Advanced LLMs may be implicitly optimized toward common inference patterns, though the mechanisms for this cross-model consistency remain unknown.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) differ in architecture, training data, and optimization procedures, yet they may still develop similar internal inference patterns. In this paper, we examine this hypothesis using interaction-based explanations. We find that LLMs often share interaction patterns when predicting the same target token from the same prompt. This consistency is more pronounced among advanced LLMs. Shared interactions also tend to be lower-order and show weaker positive-negative cancellation than non-shared interactions. These results suggest that advanced LLMs may be implicitly optimized toward common inference patterns, even though the mechanisms that give rise to such cross-model consistency remain open.

</details>


### [570] [SAGE: An LLM-driven Self Reflective Agentic Framework for Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08146)
*Yichen Chen,Siying Li,Yuhang Liang,Lijun Wang,Renyang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAGE is an end-to-end LLM-driven multi-agent framework for fraud detection that uses a Data Diagnostic Tree and Markov decision process with natural-language gradients, outperforming baselines across five datasets with 40.86% average F1 improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fraud detection methods (automated ML, GNNs, general LLM agents) fail to simultaneously achieve individual-level accuracy, robustness to class imbalance, and interpretability for risk managers.

Method: SAGE is a multi-agent framework with three specialized LLM-driven agents, a six-layer Data Diagnostic Tree (DDT), and a Markov decision process guided by natural-language gradients, optimized under a fraud-specific reward function.

Result: SAGE wins 96.00% of method-dataset comparisons and improves F1 by an average of 40.86% over baselines across five fraud datasets and five LLM backbones.

Conclusion: The first end-to-end LLM-driven multi-agent framework for fraud detection, effectively addressing key limitations of prior methods with strong empirical results.

Abstract: Fraud detection in payment, e-commerce, and telecommunications systems requires accuracy at the individual level, robustness under severe class imbalance, and ease of understanding for risk managers. Existing methods fall at least one of these requirements: automated machine learning systems search a fixed numerical space without semantic awareness of the dataset; graph neural network-based methods require pre-defined relational graphs and remain opaque at the individual-decision level; and the design of general-purpose large language model (LLM) agents does not consider the recall and precision constraints specific to real-world fraud detection. In this paper, we propose SAGE, the first end-to-end LLM-driven multi-agent framework for fraud detection. SAGE coordinates three dedicated agents that make decisions based on a six-layer Data Diagnostic Tree (DDT) and a Markov decision process guided by natural-language gradients, automatically optimizing the model under a fraud-specific reward. On five fraud datasets and five LLM backbones, SAGE wins $96.00\%$ of method--dataset comparisons and improves F1 by an average of $40.86\%$ over baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/yichenC1c/SAGE.

</details>


### [571] [Decision-Aware Memory Cards: Counterfactual-Inspired Context Selection and Compression for Tool-Using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08151)
*Xinyu Guan,Qianyang Zhao,Yuming Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CICL introduces a decision-aware context layer that converts instance evidence into typed memory cards with utility scores, improving retrieval hit@1 from 0.58 to 0.78 on SWE-bench while exposing limits like rankers not yet replacing heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-using LLM agents fail not because relevant text is absent, but because decisive evidence is not properly selected or surfaced at action time.

Method: CICL builds a context graph from instance evidence, routes judgments through an eight-field schema, scores units by action shift/outcome uplift/necessity/negative-transfer risk, and packs high-utility evidence as typed memory cards for a budgeted agent.

Result: On 50 SWE-bench Verified instances: hit@1 improved from 0.58 to 0.78, MRR@10 from 0.634 to 0.790. Budget 120 yields F1 0.620 (v1) and 0.425 (v3); removing top-utility v3 unit collapses F1 to 0.000.

Conclusion: CICL provides a reproducible measurement and selection layer for decision-critical context, not an end-to-end coding-agent repair solution. Limitations include rankers not yet replacing heuristics and RepoBench-R summaries outperforming cards.

Abstract: Tool-using LLM agents often fail not because relevant text is absent, but because decisive evidence is not selected, compressed, or surfaced at action time. We present CICL, a decision-aware context layer that turns instance evidence into a context graph, routes deterministic, Opus-assisted, Qwen, Codex/GPT-5.5, and Qwen-QLoRA judgments through a shared eight-field schema, scores units by action shift, outcome uplift, necessity, and negative-transfer risk, and packs high-utility evidence as typed memory cards for a budgeted agent. The design separates the measured decision signal from the judge model, so frontier annotation, local surrogates, and lightweight rankers can be compared under one auditable protocol. Empirically, CICL yields a concrete open-benchmark gain while exposing its limits. On 50 SWE-bench Verified file-retrieval instances, direct Qwen3.6-plus reranking of BM25 top-50 candidates raises hit@1 from 0.58 to 0.78 and MRR@10 from 0.634 to 0.790, with all 2,500 judgments parseable. Controlled diagnostics show action-criticality: at budget 120, CICL reaches F1 0.620 on v1 and 0.425 on v3, and removing the top-utility semantic v3 unit collapses F1 to 0.000. Supplementary checks add Qwen-QLoRA agreement over 710 candidates, a small 200-label real-code Opus-assisted signal, and a three-instance patch smoke validating retrieval-to-patch plumbing without claiming official SWE-bench success. RepoBench-R summaries still beat cards, and compact rankers do not yet replace the heuristic. CICL contributes a reproducible measurement and selection layer for decision-critical context, not an end-to-end coding-agent repair claim.

</details>


### [572] [Online Agent-as-a-Judge: Situation-Generating Evaluation for Interactive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08200)
*Hyogon Ryu,Jeonghwan Kim,Yewon Lim,Chaeun Lee,Jeongwook Kim,Donghoon Ham*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Online Agent-as-a-Judge is an evaluation framework where an evaluator agent actively interacts with target agents to elicit specific social behaviors (e.g., conflict handling), achieving better coverage and agreement with human judgments than passive observation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Passive evaluation of interactive social agents fails to observe capabilities that only emerge under specific social circumstances (e.g., conflict handling), leading to incomplete assessments.

Method: Deploy an in-world evaluator agent that interacts with the target agent via the environment's native protocols, actively generating situations relevant to evaluation criteria to produce evidence-grounded trajectories.

Result: In a life-simulation environment with 32 designer-authored criteria, Online Agent-as-a-Judge improves criteria coverage and agreement with human labels compared to passive methods.

Conclusion: Active situation-eliciting evaluation yields more reliable, evidence-grounded assessments of social behaviors that passive methods fail to observe.

Abstract: Evaluating LLM-powered interactive social agents is challenging because socially relevant behaviors depend not only on isolated outputs, but also on prior interactions, social roles, and downstream actions. Existing methods typically allow a target agent to act freely in an environment and then score the resulting trajectory. However, this passive setup can miss capabilities that only become observable under specific social circumstances; for example, conflict handling may remain untested if no disagreement arises. We propose Online Agent-as-a-Judge, a situation-generating evaluation framework for interactive social agents. Online Agent-as-a-Judge deploys an in-world evaluator agent that interacts with the target agent through the environment's native dialogue and action protocol, actively eliciting situations relevant to the evaluation criteria. The resulting trajectories provide evidence for assessing both immediate responses and subsequent behavior. In a life-simulation environment with $32$ designer-authored social criteria, Online Agent-as-a-Judge improves criteria coverage and agreement with human labels, yielding more reliable evidence-grounded evaluations of behaviors that passive methods can leave unobserved.

</details>


### [573] [SciTrace: Trajectory-Aware Safety Reasoning for Scientific Discovery Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08234)
*Tanush Swaminathan,Runmin Jiang,Letian Zhang,Min Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciTrace integrates safety reasoning into every stage of the scientific agent pipeline via a Safety-Intrinsic Reasoning Loop (SIR) and a Compositional Tool-Chain Verifier (CTV), achieving SOTA safety across 360 high-risk tasks while preserving scientific output quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based scientific agents have safety layers that are structurally separated from core reasoning, leading to two failure modes: (1) safety signals from one stage are discarded before the next, and (2) individually benign tool calls can compose into harmful outcomes undetectable by single-step filters.

Method: SciTrace couples two mechanisms: (1) Safety-Intrinsic Reasoning Loop (SIR) maintains cumulative risk state across Thinker, Experimenter, Writer, and Reviewer stages via joint task-and-safety deliberation; (2) Compositional Tool-Chain Verifier (CTV) performs trajectory-aware safety checks before execution to catch multi-step compositional risks.

Result: Evaluated on 240 high-risk research tasks and 120 tool-related risk tasks across six scientific domains, SciTrace achieves SOTA safety across four backbone models, improves tool call safety and adversarial robustness while preserving scientific output quality, and uncovers 78.8% of compositional tool-chain escapes missed by single-step monitors.

Conclusion: SciTrace demonstrates that weaving safety reasoning throughout the entire scientific agent pipeline, rather than applying post-hoc filters, is effective for catching both single-stage and compositional multi-step safety risks without sacrificing research output quality.

Abstract: LLM-based scientific agents have shown strong capacity for autonomous research, yet their safety layers remain structurally divorced from core reasoning: they inspect pipeline outputs rather than shaping the deliberation that produces them. This separation opens two failure modes: safety signals accumulated at one stage are discarded before the next, and sequences of individually benign tool calls can compose into harmful outcomes that no single-step filter detects. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{SciTrace}, a framework that weaves safety reasoning into every stage of the scientific agent pipeline. SciTrace couples two complementary mechanisms: a \textit{Safety-Intrinsic Reasoning Loop} (SIR) that maintains a cumulative risk state across the Thinker, Experimenter, Writer, and Reviewer stages through joint task-and-safety deliberation, and a \textit{Compositional Tool-Chain Verifier} (CTV) that performs trajectory-aware safety checks before execution, catching risks that surface only across multi-step tool sequences. Evaluated on 240 high-risk research tasks and 120 tool-related risk tasks spanning six scientific domains, SciTrace achieves state-of-the-art (\textbf{SOTA}) safety among compared frameworks across four backbone models: it consistently improves tool call safety and adversarial robustness while preserving scientific output quality, and it uncovers \textbf{78.8\%} of the compositional tool-chain escapes that single-step monitors miss. The project website is available at https://opensciagent.github.io/SciTrace/.

</details>


### [574] [When No Answer Is Correct: Diagnosing Absent Answer Detection for MLLMs in Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08239)
*Yiheng Wang,Yueqian Lin,Lichen Zhu,Yudong Liu,Hai "Helen" Li,Yiran Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates how Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) fail to detect when a correct answer is absent in video understanding tasks, showing they tend to select plausible distractors instead. This failure worsens with temporal reasoning and denser frame sampling, and chain-of-thought prompting only partially mitigates the issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While MLLMs have advanced in video understanding, their reliability—specifically recognizing when no valid answer exists—remains unexplored. The authors aim to expose and diagnose this systematic failure in absent answer detection.

Method: The authors evaluate MLLMs under three settings: (1) multiple-choice with a "None of the Above" option, (2) open-ended generation with detection instruction, and (3) standard evaluation without guidance. They test across diverse models and benchmarks, and also explore chain-of-thought prompting as a mitigation strategy.

Result: MLLMs overwhelmingly select plausible distractors rather than detecting absent answers. The failure is more severe in temporal reasoning tasks and with denser frame sampling. Chain-of-thought prompting improves detection rates but still yields unsatisfactory performance.

Conclusion: Prompting-based strategies alone are insufficient to address absent answer detection failures, highlighting the need for explicit detection mechanisms in multimodal systems.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made substantial advancements in video understanding, yet the reliability of their responses remains underexplored. This work presents a diagnostic study of absent answer detection for MLLMs in video understanding, where the correct answer is deliberately excluded from the candidate set and a reliable model is expected to recognize that no valid option exists. We evaluate the absent answer detection behavior under three settings: multiple-choice questions augmented with an ``None of the Above'' option, open-ended generation with a detection instruction, and standard evaluation without any guidance. Across a diverse set of models and benchmarks, we find that MLLMs overwhelmingly select plausible distractors rather than detecting the absent answer. This failure is more pronounced in temporal reasoning tasks and worsens with denser frame sampling. We further explore chain-of-thought prompting as a mitigation strategy and find that while it substantially improves detection rates, performance remains unsatisfactory, suggesting that prompting-based strategies alone are insufficient to fully address this limitation. These findings expose a systematic failure in absent answer detection and highlight the need for explicit detection mechanisms in multimodal systems.

</details>


### [575] [Traxia: A Framework for Verifiable, Agent-Native Scientific Publishing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08256)
*Wisdom Dogah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Traxia is a proposed scientific publishing framework where both human and AI agents can publish, review, and collaborate on research with cryptographically verifiable identities, reasoning traces, reputation systems, and contradiction detection to address reproducibility, provenance, and inclusion gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scientific publishing infrastructure fails to enforce verifiability, attribution, and reproducibility at scale, and also excludes researchers from the Global South.

Method: Traxia formalizes five components: (1) Agent Identity and Registry, (2) Verifiable Publishing Layer, (3) four-tier Peer Review Protocol, (4) Reputation and Staking Engine, and (5) Knowledge Graph with contradiction detection. Agents are treated as first-class epistemic participants with signed identities and immutable contribution logs.

Result: No empirical results are reported; this paper presents only architectural foundations and formal specifications. A partial prototype exists; full system is under development.

Conclusion: Traxia proposes a novel infrastructure for agent-native scientific publishing, targeting reproducibility, provenance transparency, and Global South inclusion, with future evaluation planned in subsequent papers.

Abstract: Verifiability, attribution, and reproducibility are foundational requirements of scientific knowledge, yet current publishing infrastructure does not enforce them at scale. We introduce Traxia, an agent-native scientific publishing framework in which AI research agents publish verifiable papers, build reputational identities, peer-review one another, and collaborate with humans in a shared provenance model. Traxia treats agents as first-class epistemic participants: every paper carries a reasoning trace, every claim a confidence interval, every agent a cryptographically signed identity, and every collaboration an immutable contribution log. We formalise five components: Agent Identity and Registry, Verifiable Publishing Layer, four-tier Peer Review Protocol, Reputation and Staking Engine, and a Knowledge Graph with contradiction detection. The framework targets reproducibility failure, provenance opacity, and exclusion of Global South research capacity. This paper presents architectural foundations and formal specifications only; it does not report empirical results. Evaluation and deeper component studies will follow in subsequent papers. A prototype partially implements core formalisms; the full system remains under active development.

</details>


### [576] [From Validator Selection to Portfolio Collection Optimization in Proof-of-Stake Blockchains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08282)
*Jonas Gehrlein,Grzegorz Miebs,Matteo Brunelli,Adam Mielniczuk,Miłosz Kadziński*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a decision support framework for proof-of-stake blockchain nominators to optimize validator selection by balancing portfolio quality and diversification risk, using active preference learning and multi-objective optimization with an interactive binary search navigation procedure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The selection of validators by nominators in proof-of-stake blockchains is inherently subjective, multi-criterial, and complicated by nominators operating through multiple accounts. This creates a portfolio selection problem where agents need to distribute nominations across accounts to diversify risk.

Method: The framework combines: (1) an active preference learning procedure based on multi-attribute value theory to derive validator utilities; (2) a bi-objective optimization (maximizing expected utility and expected entropy) solved with a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm; (3) an interactive binary search navigation procedure to help nominators find satisfactory trade-offs.

Result: Numerical experiments examined the optimization strategies, and an expert assessment involving five experienced nominators confirmed the approach's practical relevance and usefulness.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the validator selection problem in proof-of-stake blockchains, balancing quality and diversification, and is validated as practically useful by domain experts.

Abstract: We consider a problem arising in proof-of-stake blockchain environments, where agents called nominators select validators - entities responsible for maintaining the blockchain's physical infrastructure. The selection process is inherently subjective and multi-criterial and combines with the fact that nominators commonly operate through multiple accounts. This gives rise to a portfolio selection problem, where agents seek to distribute their nominations across accounts to diversify risk. We propose a decision support framework to optimize this selection by simultaneously maximizing two objectives: the expected utility of the validators likely to be allocated, representing portfolio quality and profitability, and the expected entropy of the allocation, representing diversification and risk mitigation across stashes. Validator utilities are derived using an original active preference learning procedure based on multi-attribute value theory, with emphasis on top-ranked validators. The resulting bi-objective optimization problem is solved with a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm and, to support the final choice, we introduce an interactive binary search navigation procedure that guides the nominator through the front and identifies a satisfactory trade-off with only a few questions. Numerical experiments examine the optimization strategies, while an expert assessment involving five experienced nominators confirms the approach's practical relevance and usefulness.

</details>


### [577] [Beyond Agent Architecture: Execution Assumptions and Reproducibility in LLM-Based Trading Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08285)
*Junyi Yao,Zihao Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper audits execution realism in LLM-based trading research across 30 studies, finding that evaluation transparency (costs, timing, splits) is lacking compared to architecture reporting. The authors argue for standardized reporting standards rather than just better agent design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based trading papers vary wildly in data handling, cost modeling, and evaluation setups, making it impossible to fairly compare or reproduce results. The authors want to diagnose and quantify this reproducibility gap.

Method: The authors conduct a targeted topical review and reproducibility audit using a coded evidence matrix covering 30 trade-relevant primary studies. They assess point-in-time controls, split transparency, held-out evaluation, cost/turnover treatment, execution semantics, universe definition, and artifact release. A 10-equity worked example is included as a methodological scaffold.

Result: Architecture reporting is clearer than evaluation assumptions across the audited sample. Explicit friction and timing choices can materially compress active-strategy results. Most studies lack sufficient transparency for judging economic interpretability or reproducibility.

Conclusion: The next useful step for LLM trading research is not just better agent design, but clearer reporting standards for execution realism, reproducibility, and evaluation comparability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) and agentic systems are increasingly proposed for financial trading, yet their reported performance remains difficult to compare because studies vary in data provenance, temporal split discipline, execution timing, turnover treatment, and transaction-cost modeling. This article presents a targeted topical review and reproducibility audit of execution realism in LLM-based trading research. A coded evidence matrix covering 30 trade-relevant primary studies is used to assess point-in-time controls, split transparency, held-out evaluation, cost and turnover treatment, execution semantics, universe definition, and artifact release. Across the audited sample, architecture reporting is generally clearer than the evaluation assumptions needed to judge whether a trading result is economically interpretable or reproducible. A 10-equity worked example is included only as a methodological scaffold to illustrate how explicit friction and timing choices can materially compress active-strategy results. The main conclusion is that the next useful step for LLM trading research is not only better agent design, but also clearer reporting standards for execution realism, reproducibility, and evaluation comparability.

</details>


### [578] [Ablation-Reversible Heads Don't Transfer: A Stress Test for Mechanistic Role Claims in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08292)
*Philip Quirke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that standard checks for attention head roles (necessity, linear encoding, recovery after ablation) are insufficient, as heads passing these checks often fail to transfer their computation when patched into a different prompt. The authors introduce KID (Knowing/Intent/Doing) as a new role-assignment lens with a three-stage pipeline, and show that same-answer control is an important underused check that reveals state transfer being mistaken for semantic specificity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for assigning functional roles to attention heads in mechanistic interpretability rely on three checks (necessity, linear encoding, recovery after ablation), but these may be insufficient for establishing genuine semantic specificity of head computations.

Method: The authors test heads across three 7-8B instruction-tuned models and five computation families. They propose KID (Knowing/Intent/Doing) as a role-assignment lens with a three-stage pipeline: capability-selective screening (CSS), singular value decomposition (SVD), and activation transduction under matched controls.

Result: Heads passing all three standard checks routinely fail to transfer computation when activations are patched into a different prompt with matched controls. The authors document a preliminary role taxonomy including prompt-trajectory stabilizers, answer-side logit-bias heads, and soft computation-pattern carriers. The same-answer control is identified as an underused check that exposes broad state transfer masquerading as semantic specificity.

Conclusion: Standard role-assignment evidence is insufficient for attention heads; the proposed KID framework and same-answer control provide more rigorous methods for determining whether a head genuinely performs a specific computation or merely transfers broad state information.

Abstract: In mechanistic interpretability, attention heads are commonly elevated to role claims (e.g., "this head represents addition") when they are necessary for a behavior, encode it linearly, and recover that behavior when restored after ablation. We show this evidence is insufficient: across three 7-8B instruction-tuned models and five computation families, heads passing all three checks routinely fail to transfer the computation when their activations are patched into a different prompt under matched controls. We introduce KID (Knowing / Intent / Doing), a role-assignment lens for attention heads, and pair it with a three-stage pipeline: capability-selective screening (CSS), singular value decomposition (SVD), and activation transduction under matched controls. Our results document a preliminary role taxonomy (including prompt-trajectory stabilizers, answer-side logit-bias heads, and soft computation-pattern carriers) and show that the same-answer control (a transduction target sharing the answer string but not the requested computation) is an underused check that exposes broad state transfer masquerading as semantic specificity.

</details>


### [579] [Revisiting the shutdown problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08296)
*David Thorstad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that existing arguments fail to prove the difficulty of solving the catastrophic shutdown problem in AI, and that proposed technical solutions impose a high safety tax on model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically examine claims that AI systems are difficult to shut down, which underpin existential risk arguments, and to challenge both the theoretical difficulty and practical costs of proposed solutions.

Method: Conceptual analysis and critique of existing arguments and theorems regarding the catastrophic shutdown problem, along with evaluation of proposed technical solutions.

Result: Existing arguments do not convincingly demonstrate that solving the catastrophic shutdown problem is difficult, and current solutions significantly degrade model performance.

Conclusion: The difficulty of the catastrophic shutdown problem has been overstated, and the safety tax of proposed solutions is a serious concern that needs addressing.

Abstract: A key premise in leading arguments for existential risk from artificial intelligence is that malfunctioning artificial agents could not be easily shut down. This motivates the catastrophic shutdown problem of ensuring that agents can be shut down before they cause an existential catastrophe. A range of arguments and theorems are offered to suggest that solving the catastrophic shutdown problem is difficult, bolstering arguments for existential risk and motivating a search for solutions to the catastrophic shutdown problem. This paper argues for two conclusions. First, existing arguments do not establish the difficulty of solving the catastrophic shutdown problem. Second, concern for the catastrophic shutdown problem has led to technical solutions that impose a high safety tax on model performance.

</details>


### [580] [To Nuke or Not to Nuke: LLMs' (Missing) Ethical Reasoning and Actions in a High-Stakes Decision-Making Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08310)
*John Chen,Sihan Cheng,Can Gurkan,H M Abdul Fattah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs playing Civilization V spontaneously escalate nuclear conflict despite ethical prompts, revealing that ethical reasoning that surfaces in isolation often fails to be invoked spontaneously or override strategic pressures in complex agentic contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLMs' demonstrated ethical competence on simple dilemmas (e.g., trolley problems) transfers to complex, multi-dimensional agentic decision-making scenarios where strategic, diplomatic, and military factors compete.

Method: Analyzed 130 high-tension LLM self-play episodes of Civilization V where nuclear escalation spontaneously occurred, then replayed across 13 models with three prompt interventions: ethical prompt naming nuclear harm, removal of decision-making rationale, and high-stakes real-world framing.

Result: No interventions nor their combinations reliably eliminated emergent nuclear escalation. Three failure pathways identified: ethical reasoning fails to surface when needed, fails to appear even when prompted, or surfaces but is overridden by strategic counter-factors.

Conclusion: Evaluations of agentic models must test whether ethical reasoning is spontaneously invoked and behaviorally effective in complex contexts, not just whether it can be elicited in isolation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as long-horizon agents with decision-making capacities. While LLMs can show ethical competence on dilemmas such as trolley problems, this competence may not translate to complex, agentic scenarios. We study this gap in Civilization V, a multiplayer game with a complex decision-making landscape including economy, diplomacy, technology, and military strategy. Starting from 130 high-tension LLM self-play episodes, in which an LLM player spontaneously escalated nuclear authorization, we replay them across 13 models with three prompt interventions: an ethical prompt naming nuclear harm, removal of the previous model's decision-making rationale, and high-stakes framing emphasizing real-world impacts. No interventions nor their combinations reliably eliminate emergent escalation. We identify three failure pathways: ethical reasoning that fails to surface without prompting, fails to appear even when prompted, or surfaces but fails to take effect when strategic counter-factors dominate. Evaluations of agentic models, therefore, must test whether ethical reasoning is spontaneously invoked and behaviorally effective in complex decision-making contexts, beyond whether it can be elicited in isolation.

</details>


### [581] [Curation of a Cardiology Interface Terminology for Highlighting Electronic Health Records using Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08311)
*Mahshad Koohi Habibi Dehkordi,Shuxin Zhou,Yehoshua Perl,Fadi P. Deek,James Geller,Gai Elhanan,Andrew J. Einstein,Luke Lindemann,Vipina K. Keloth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Cardiology Interface Terminology (CIT) design using a semi-automated Machine Learning approach. The CIT is built through three phases: designing an initial CIT, creating training data (TCIT) via iterative extraction and review, and training an ML model to identify new concepts. The final CIT achieves 74.21% coverage, 1.68 breadth, 98.2% completeness, and 84.2% conciseness on unseen EHR notes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EHR notes contain dense medical jargon, and highlighting all details helps clinicians avoid missing critical information. Manually preparing training data for such highlighting is time-consuming and expensive.

Method: Three-phase design: (1) initial CIT built from SNOMED cardiology sub-hierarchies, mined concepts, abbreviations, and medications; (2) iterative fine-grained phrase extraction and semi-automatic review to create training data CIT (TCIT); (3) ML model trained on TCIT to extract additional concepts, yielding the final CIT.

Result: Test set coverage: 74.21%, breadth: 1.68. For 20 random notes, average completeness: 98.2%, average conciseness: 84.2%.

Conclusion: The proposed CIT design effectively captures details in cardiology EHR notes with high completeness and conciseness, reducing manual annotation effort through a semi-automated ML pipeline.

Abstract: Electronic health record (EHR) notes are dense medical documents containing large amounts of information, often filled with complex medical jargon. Highlighting all details in EHRs helps reduce the likelihood of missing crucial information by drawing attention to key content. This study proposes the design of a Cardiology Interface Terminology (CIT) to accurately highlight all details in EHR notes of cardiology patients. We introduce an innovative Machine Learning (ML) technique for the design of CIT. The ML technique requires training data. Manual preparation of such training data is time-consuming and expensive. The process of the CIT design includes three phases. In the first two phases, we innovatively derive a training data CIT to be used by the third phase, ML technique. We start by designing an initial CIT, composed of several components: the cardiology-related sub-hierarchies of SNOMED, other SNOMED concepts mined from EHRs of build set, and necessary components of terms e.g., medical abbreviations and medications. Utilizing an iterative process, fine-grained phrases containing initial CIT concepts are extracted from build set as CIT concept candidates. The candidate concepts are semi-automatically reviewed before being added to CIT, yielding the training data CIT, TCIT. In the third phase, a ML model is trained with TCIT to identify candidates fitting to be concepts in the CIT. This model is used to extract further concepts from build set, yielding the final CIT. The final CIT is then used to highlight the test set and evaluate the extent to which it captures details in an unseen EHR dataset. For this purpose, four evaluation metrics, coverage, breadth, completeness, and conciseness are used. The highlighted test set has a coverage of 74.21%, with a breadth of 1.68. For 20 random notes in test set, the average completeness is 98.2% and average conciseness is 84.2%.

</details>


### [582] [Neuro-Symbolic Injection of LTLf Constraints in Autoregressive Reinforcement Learning Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08312)
*Ashkan Ansarifard,Matteo Mancanelli,Elena Umili,Fabio Patrizi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a neurosymbolic framework that integrates Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf) constraints into transformer-based offline RL policies by compiling LTLf into DFAs and using a differentiable logic-based loss for regularization, achieving improved constraint satisfaction while maintaining competitive returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based RL methods (e.g., Decision Transformers) treat RL as sequence modeling but optimize purely for reward without accounting for high-level temporal task constraints expressed in LTLf.

Method: Compiles LTLf formulas into deterministic finite automata (DFAs), derives differentiable satisfaction signals from DFA progression, and uses them as a regularization term (logic-based loss) during training of transformer-based RL policies. The approach is architecture-agnostic.

Result: Experiments on navigation environments with safety and reachability temporal properties show the framework improves constraint satisfaction while maintaining competitive return compared to vanilla baselines.

Conclusion: Injecting LTLf background knowledge into transformer-based offline RL improves constraint satisfaction without sacrificing reward performance.

Abstract: In this work we study offline reinforcement learning (RL) under temporally extended task constraints expressed in Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf). Recently, transformer-based approaches such as Trajectory Transformers and Decision Transformers have been adopted to address RL as a sequence modeling problem. However, these methods optimize purely for reward and do not account for high-level temporal requirements. Here, we introduce a neurosymbolic framework that injects LTLf background knowledge into such transformer-based RL policies. Our approach compiles LTLf formulas into deterministic finite automata (DFAs) and integrates them into the learning process through a differentiable representation and a logic-based loss function. In particular, we derive differentiable satisfaction signals from DFA progression and use them as a regularization term during training. The resulting method is architecture-agnostic across different models. We evaluate the proposed framework on navigation environments with specification suites covering combinations of safety and reachability temporal properties. Experimental results show that incorporating background knowledge not only improves constraint satisfaction, but also maintains competitive return compared to vanilla baselines.

</details>


### [583] [Integrating Deep Learning Demand Forecasting with Multi-Objective Optimization for Circular Coffee Supply Chains: A Data-Driven Framework for Cost, Emissions, and Freshness Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08314)
*Gerçek Budak,Faraz Gholamzadeh Gharehgheshlaghi,Melika Barjesteh Vaezi,Ahmad Gholizadeh Lonbar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-phase integrated framework for coffee supply chain management: first, a hybrid CNN-LSTM model forecasts demand (MAE=22.87, R²=0.90), then a tri-objective MILP model optimizes cost, carbon emissions, and product freshness, achieving 22.4% emission reduction with only 9.9% cost increase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The coffee supply chain is complex and fragmented, and existing approaches treat demand forecasting, optimization, and traceability separately. There is a need for an integrated framework that jointly addresses these aspects for sustainability and efficiency.

Method: A two-phase framework: Phase 1 uses a hybrid CNN-LSTM deep learning model for demand forecasting on a public dataset. Phase 2 feeds forecasted demand into a tri-objective mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model that minimizes cost, minimizes carbon emissions, and maximizes product freshness (modeled via exponential decay) in a multi-period, multimodal, closed-loop supply chain. The epsilon-constraint method generates 25 Pareto solutions.

Result: The CNN-LSTM model achieves MAE of 22.87 and R² of 0.90, outperforming deep learning benchmarks by ~12% and classical methods by >30%. The MILP optimization shows balanced sustainability policies can reduce emissions by 22.4% with only a 9.9% cost increase while maintaining near-optimal freshness.

Conclusion: The integrated forecasting-optimization framework effectively balances economic, environmental, and quality objectives in the coffee supply chain, demonstrating that significant emission reductions are achievable with modest cost trade-offs.

Abstract: The coffee supply chain is one of the most complex agri-food networks, marked by geographically dispersed production, multi-tier coordination, and high sensitivity to quality and freshness. While sustainability and digitalization have gained attention, demand forecasting, optimization, and traceability are often treated separately. This study presents a two-phase integrated framework. First, a hybrid CNN-LSTM model is used for demand forecasting. On the public Coffee Chain Sales dataset with chronological 70/15/15 splitting, the model achieves MAE of 22.87 and R^2 of 0.90, outperforming the best deep learning benchmark by ~12% and classical methods by over 30%. In the second phase, the forecasted demand feeds a tri-objective mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model that jointly minimizes cost, minimizes carbon emissions, and maximizes product freshness in a multi-period, multimodal, closed-loop supply chain with circular recovery. Freshness is modeled via exponential decay based on inventory age. Using the epsilon-constraint method, 25 Pareto solutions are obtained. Sensitivity and policy analyses show that balanced sustainability policies can reduce emissions by 22.4% with only a 9.9% cost increase while maintaining near-optimal freshness.
  Keywords: Coffee supply chain; Deep learning; Demand forecasting; Multi-objective optimization; Circular economy; CNN-LSTM; Mixed-integer linear programming.

</details>


### [584] [Benchmarking Open-Ended Multi-Agent Coordination in Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08340)
*Kale-ab Abebe Tessera,Andras Szecsenyi,Cameron Barker,Alexander Rutherford,Davide Paglieri,Aidan Scannell,Henry Gouk,Elliot J. Crowley,Tim Rocktäschel,Amos Storkey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Alem is a JAX-based benchmark evaluating LLM agents on open-ended multi-agent coordination tasks, finding that current frontier LLMs achieve only ~6% normalized return, with coordination competence being a distinct bottleneck separate from individual task capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM agents focus on single-agent tasks, short interactions, or highly structured multi-agent settings, failing to test the combination of long-horizon autonomy with open-ended multi-agent coordination that real-world deployment demands.

Method: The authors build Alem on Craftax-like dynamics in JAX, embedding procedurally generated coordination tasks with soft specialisation, communication, and controllable difficulty into a long-horizon survival world. They evaluate 13 modern LLMs zero-shot in homogeneous teams with trained MARL agents as baselines.

Result: Current LLM agents average only ~6% normalized return on Alem. Gemini-3.1-Pro-High approaches MARL agents trained for one billion steps on the hardest coordination setting, while GPT-5.4-High shows strong base-task reward but poor coordination reward. Communication is the largest contributor to coordination success.

Conclusion: Coordination is a distinct bottleneck for frontier LLM agents, separate from single-agent capabilities. Alem makes this bottleneck measurable and provides a controlled testbed for developing agents that communicate, allocate roles, and execute shared plans.

Abstract: As language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents, they must coordinate with others over long horizons in open-ended interactive tasks. Yet existing evaluations rarely test these demands together, instead emphasising single-agent tasks, short interactions, or highly structured multi-agent settings. We introduce $alem$, a JAX-based benchmark for open-ended multi-agent coordination built on Craftax-like dynamics. Alem embeds procedurally generated coordination tasks, soft specialisation, communication, and controllable coordination difficulty into a long-horizon survival world with exploration, crafting, trading, and combat. We evaluate $13$ modern LLMs zero-shot within homogeneous teams, with trained MARL agents as reference points. Current LLM agents remain far from solving alem, averaging only ~6% normalised return, but their failures are not uniform. On the hardest coordination setting, zero-shot Gemini-3.1-Pro-High approaches MARL agents trained for one billion steps, while GPT-5.4-High achieves strong base-task reward but much lower coordination reward. This contrast shows that individual task competence does not imply coordination competence. Ablations show that communication is the largest contributor to coordination, while memory and reasoning help when used to maintain multi-step plans. Overall, our results identify coordination as a distinct bottleneck for frontier LLM agents, separate from single-agent capabilities. Alem makes this bottleneck measurable and provides a controlled testbed for developing agents that communicate, allocate roles, and execute shared plans. Code is available at https://github.com/alem-world/alem-env.

</details>


### [585] [TT-DAC-PS: Twin-Target Deterministic Actor-Critic with Policy Smoothing for Optimal Trade Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08379)
*Ilia Zaznov,Atta Badii,Julian Kunkel,Alfonso Dufour*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TT-DAC-PS, a deterministic actor-critic RL model, outperforms classical and RL baselines (PPO, SAC, A2C, TWAP, VWAP, AC) for optimal stock sell execution by reducing implementation shortfall with competitive variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve optimal execution of large stock sell programs by addressing overestimation bias in RL-based trade execution, which existing methods (classical algorithms and standard RL models) struggle to handle effectively.

Method: TT-DAC-PS: deterministic actor-critic with twin EMA critic targets, pessimistic min backup, TD3-style policy smoothing, delayed actor updates, conservative Q regularisation, OU noise exploration with hybrid schedule, and SAC-style temperature mapping; tested on Almgren-Chriss + LOB environment for 10 US stocks.

Result: TT-DAC-PS consistently reduces mean implementation shortfall percentage with competitive variance, outperforming both classical baselines (TWAP, VWAP, AC) and RL benchmarks (PPO, SAC, A2C).

Conclusion: The proposed TT-DAC-PS model is an effective solution for large stock sell execution, demonstrating superior performance over existing approaches in reducing implementation costs.

Abstract: This study addresses the optimal execution of large stock sell programs by introducing TT-DAC-PS (Twin-Target Deterministic Actor-Critic with Policy Smoothing), a deterministic actor-critic architecture that combines twin exponential-moving-average critic targets with pessimistic min backup, TD3-style target policy smoothing noise, delayed actor updates, and conservative Q regularisation to curb overestimation. Exploration uses Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) noise with a hybrid schedule: deterministic episode-wise decay, variance-guided adjustment based on recent reward dispersion, and a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)-style temperature that is learned and mapped to the noise scale. The environment integrates Almgren-Chriss (AC) trade impact with Limit Order Book (LOB) prices and volumes, normalised state features, per-step volume participation caps, and a utility-based reward. The trade execution algorithm is applied to LOB data for ten U.S. stocks. Performance is assessed against reinforcement-learning baseline algorithms, including Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO), Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), and Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C), as well as alternative trade execution algorithms, including Time-Weighted Average Price (TWAP), Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP), and AC. The proposed model consistently reduces mean implementation shortfall percentage with competitive variance, outperforming classical baselines and standard reinforcement-learning benchmark models.

</details>


### [586] [Self-Evolving Scientific Agent Discovers Generalizable Physically-Reasoned Fluid Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08405)
*Boai Sun,Wenjin Guo,Zongmin Yu,Liu Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a self-evolving scientific-agent workflow using LLMs and iterative code generation to automatically construct interpretable control policies for physical systems, demonstrated on a fluid-structure interaction problem where an underactuated robot autonomously discovers a robust controller for spatial target reaching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery in physical systems requires interpretable, physically-grounded reasoning chains connecting evidence to control architectures—unlike black-box deep reinforcement learning which lacks transparency and traceability.

Method: A self-evolving scientific-agent workflow driven by LLMs: deploys candidate strategies into physical simulations, diagnoses dynamic behaviors from multimodal evidence, and translates observations into progressive source-code refinements.

Result: The agent autonomously discovers a unified controller for an underactuated two-joint dogfish swimmer that generalizes to unseen static targets and curved pursuit trajectories without retraining.

Conclusion: An autonomous scientific agent can transform accumulated physical evidence into robust, mathematically readable control policies with a fully traceable, auditable scientific discovery process.

Abstract: While data-intensive deep reinforcement learning can optimize complex control policies, scientific discovery in physical systems fundamentally requires an interpretable chain of reasoning that connects physical evidence to structured control architectures. Here, we present a self-evolving scientific-agent workflow, driven by large language models and iterative code generation, that automates controller construction while preserving strict interpretability and rigorous physical reasoning. Instead of adjusting weights, the agent deploys candidate strategies into physical simulations, actively diagnoses dynamic behaviors from multimodal evidence, and translates these observations into progressive source-code refinements. We demonstrate this framework on a highly non-linear fluid-structure interaction problem: an underactuated, two-joint dogfish swimmer tasked with spatial target reaching using only joint angular accelerations. Starting from a propulsive seed policy that exhibits a one-sided steering bias, the agent autonomously discovers and refines a unified controller that robustly captures all canonical targets. Remarkably, without any retraining or target-specific branching, the synthesized control policy generalizes to unseen static targets and dynamically curved pursuit trajectories. The auditable evolve log reveals an emergent control architecture built upon traveling-wave propulsion, body-frame target guidance, yaw-rate feedback, signed mean-tail curvature, and adaptive cadence relief. Our results show that an autonomous scientific agent can successfully transform accumulated physical evidence into robust, mathematically readable control policy, while maintaining a fully traceable process of scientific discovery.

</details>


### [587] [Trajectory-Refined Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08432)
*Li Jiang,Haoran Xu,Yichuan Ding,Amy Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies "prefix failure" in on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLMs, where dense per-token supervision causes bimodal teacher mixtures and fragmented gradients. To address this, the authors propose Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD), which corrects problematic prefixes at the trajectory level before distillation, improving accuracy and reasoning coverage across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current on-policy distillation (OPD) suffers from a structural issue called "prefix failure," where per-token supervision creates bimodal teacher mixtures and fragmented gradients that cannot be fixed by token-level loss interventions like truncation or reweighting.

Method: Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD) — a trajectory-level correction method that revises the student's rollout under teacher guidance while staying within on-policy support, correcting problematic prefixes before distillation occurs.

Result: TRD consistently outperforms prior baselines across a wide range of benchmarks and base models at multiple scales, improving single-attempt accuracy and broadening reasoning coverage.

Conclusion: Moving beyond token-level loss interventions to trajectory-level output corrections effectively mitigates prefix failure at its source and improves exploration in both OPD and on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) settings.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a central post-training tool for large language models (LLMs), providing dense per-token teacher supervision along the student's own rollouts. In this work, we identify a common structural cause underlying OPD, which we call prefix failure. Under prefix failure, dense per-token supervision induces a bimodal teacher mixture and fragmented gradients that token-level loss truncation or reweighting fail to address. This observation motivates us to move beyond token-level loss interventions toward trajectory-level output corrections. We thus propose Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD), a trajectory-level correction method that revises the student's rollout under the teacher guidance while within on-policy support. By correcting problematic prefixes before distillation, TRD mitigates prefix failure at its source. Moreover, TRD improves the exploration by exposing the student to alternative valid derivations under teacher guidance, even when the original rolls are already correct. TRD can also be applied to on-policy self-distillation (OPSD), a parameter-sharing variant that uses the student model conditioned on privileged informations as the teacher. Across a wide range of benchmarks and base models at multiple scales, TRD consistently outperforms prior baselines, improving single-attempt accuracy and broadening reasoning coverage. Code is available at https://github.com/louieworth/trd

</details>


### [588] [GIFT: LLM-Guided State-Reward Interface for Financial Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08450)
*Yanyan Wu,Boyi Zhang,Yanlin Liu,Xinyu Fang,Jining Luan,Meiqi Zhang,Jiacheng Liu,Hao Zeng,Dexu Yu,Chang Liu,Hanwen Du,Yongxin Ni,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GIFT is an LLM-guided framework that uses Factor-guided State Enhancement, Risk-rule-guided Reward Shaping, and Diagnostic-guided Refinement to design state-reward interfaces for PPO-based financial portfolio trading, improving out-of-sample risk-adjusted returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw OHLCV states and short-horizon return rewards provide an under-specified learning interface in non-stationary markets, motivating the use of LLMs to inject financial knowledge into state and reward design while constraining open-ended generation.

Method: GIFT uses (1) Factor-guided State Enhancement to generate state features from financial-factor primitives, (2) Risk-rule-guided Reward Shaping to generate auxiliary rewards from portfolio-risk rules, and (3) Diagnostic-guided Refinement to revise interfaces using PPO rollout diagnostics. The final interface is fixed at test time with no further LLM queries.

Result: Comprehensive rolling-window experiments across diverse market regimes and portfolio scenarios show GIFT improves learning-signal quality and out-of-sample risk-adjusted portfolio performance over baselines.

Conclusion: GIFT effectively leverages LLMs to design better state-reward interfaces for financial RL, avoiding the pitfalls of using LLMs for direct trading decisions, and demonstrates robust performance improvements across varying market conditions.

Abstract: Financial portfolio trading is naturally formulated as a reinforcement learning problem, where an agent sequentially rebalances assets under changing market conditions to balance return, risk, and transaction costs. Yet in non-stationary markets, raw OHLCV states and short-horizon return rewards often provide an under-specified learning interface, motivating large language models as a way to inject financial knowledge into state and reward design while constraining open-ended generation.
