However, masked reconstruction may be poorly suited to the stochastic nature of these signals, while contrastive approaches rely on positive-pair definitions despite the semantic invariances of physiological signals being poorly understood. In this work, we show that next-token prediction is a simple and scalable alternative. We develop Hypnos, a multi-modal sleep foundation model trained using eight different sensing modalities (e.g. EEG, ECG, respiratory signals) drawn from over 20,000 overnight polysomnography recordings. We tokenize each modality into streams of discrete tokens using residual vector quantization, then train a large auto-regressive RQ-Transformer to jointly predict the next token across all modalities in parallel. After training, Hypnos can be applied to continuous streams of sensor data from any subset of supported modalities, generating embeddings for downstream tasks. Across a range of benchmarks, Hypnos significantly outperforms existing foundation models. In sleep stage classification, we match the performance of strong supervised baselines on held-out test sets whilst using \(100\times\) less labelled data. Hypnos even generalises to daytime physiology, surpassing a dedicated ECG foundation model at detecting atrial fibrillation. Our results demonstrate that next-token prediction is a strong self-supervised objective for representation learning from multi-modal physiological signals.

</details>


### [667] [From 0-to-1 to 1-to-N: Reproducible Engineering Evidence for MetaAI Recursive Self-Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09663)
*Dun Li,Jiatao Li,Hongzhi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces recursive self-design for AI systems, proposes a four-criteria evidence framework, maps existing systems (DGM, STOP, Goedel Agent, ShinkaEvolve) against it, highlights DGM's improvement from 20% to 50% on SWE-bench, and presents MetaAI-Mini as a reproducible protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and provide evidence for "recursive self-design" as an emerging AI development paradigm where AI systems modify their own design mechanisms.

Method: Proposes an operational evidence framework with four criteria (inspectable target system, meta-level modifier, feedback-directed selection, recursive continuation), maps existing systems against these criteria, and develops MetaAI-Mini as a reproducible HumanEval-based protocol.

Result: DGM shows improvement from 20% to 50% on SWE-bench Verified and from 14.2% to 30.7% on full Polyglot after 80 iterations; ablations indicate both open-ended exploration and self-improvement contribute.

Conclusion: Recursive self-design is a working paradigm with partial evidence from existing systems; MetaAI-Mini is provided as a protocol (not experimental result) for future reproducible research.

Abstract: Recursive self-design refers to AI-assisted modification of the mechanisms by which an AI system is built, evaluated, and improved. This paper treats MetaAI not as a mature paradigm, but as a working term for a human-seeded, AI-expanded development pattern in which the design space itself becomes a target of modification. We propose an operational evidence framework with four criteria: inspectable target system, meta-level modifier, feedback-directed selection, and recursive continuation. We then map public systems, including Darwin Goedel Machine (DGM), STOP, Goedel Agent, and ShinkaEvolve, against these criteria. DGM provides the most direct currently reported evidence: its published results show improvement from 20% to 50% on SWE-bench Verified and from 14.2% to 30.7% on full Polyglot after 80 iterations, with ablations suggesting that both open-ended exploration and self-improvement contribute. Finally, we provide MetaAI-Mini, a reproducible HumanEval-based protocol and codebase. Because no completed model run is included in this build, MetaAI-Mini is reported as a protocol rather than as an experimental result.

</details>


### [668] [Frequency-based Constrained Sampling for Interval Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09666)
*Djawad Bekkoucha,Abdelkader Ouali,Bruno Crémilleux*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CFips samples interval patterns under user-defined syntactic constraints proportionally to frequency, enabling feasible mining where exhaustive methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exhaustive pattern mining struggles with large pattern spaces; output space pattern sampling offers an alternative but prior work lacked support for user-defined syntactic constraints on interval patterns.

Method: CFips uses a multi-step sampling framework that decomposes syntactic constraints into elementary predicates on interval bounds, preserving exact sampling guarantees for proportional frequency-based sampling.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that constraint-integrated sampling completes mining tasks that would otherwise time out with exhaustive approaches.

Conclusion: Incorporating syntactic constraints directly into the sampling procedure enables efficient and principled exploration of large interval pattern spaces.

Abstract: Output space pattern sampling is a powerful alternative to exhaustive pattern mining for exploring large pattern spaces, as it enables users to focus on representative patterns drawn according to a chosen interestingness measure. In this paper, we address the problem of sampling interval patterns under user-defined syntactic constraints. We introduce CFips, a sampling approach that incorporates constraints directly into the sampling procedure. The approach relies on a multi-step sampling framework and supports several syntactic constraints by decomposing them into elementary predicates on interval bounds while preserving exact sampling guarantees. We formally prove that CFips samples interval patterns proportionally to their frequency within the constrained pattern space. The experimental results show that integrating constraints into the sampling procedure enables to complete mining tasks that would otherwise fail within a given time out.

</details>


### [669] [SpatialWorld: Benchmarking Interactive Spatial Reasoning of Multimodal Agents in Real-World Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09669)
*Hongcheng Gao,Hailong Qu,Jingyi Tang,Jiahao Wang,Zihao Huang,Hengkang Qiao,Shihong Huang,Junming Yang,Yi Li,Hongyixuan Yuan,Wenjie Li,Bohan Zeng,Wenbo Li,Bo Wang,Jianhui Liu,Olive Huang,Haoyang Huang,Wentao Zhang,Guoqing Huang,Nan Duan,Yinpeng Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SpatialWorld is a benchmark for evaluating interactive spatial understanding in multimodal agents, using 760 tasks across 8 simulation backends. The best model (GPT-5) achieves only 17.4% success, highlighting major gaps in active exploration and long-horizon planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on passive evaluation (static VQA) or simulator-specific pipelines, failing to assess general interactive spatial understanding in real-world tasks.

Method: SpatialWorld integrates 8 heterogeneous simulation backends under a shared protocol, with 760 human-annotated tasks. Agents operate under vision-only partial observability, actively gather egocentric evidence, and use a text-based action interface. Each task includes a validated initial state, reference trajectory, and terminal-state verifier.

Result: GPT-5 achieves 17.4% task success rate (TSR), Qwen-3.5 reaches 14.1% as the best open-source model. There is a mismatch between task success and execution efficiency, plus substantial domain-specific variations.

Conclusion: Robust spatial task solving remains challenging; bottlenecks lie in active exploration and long-horizon planning. SpatialWorld serves as a rigorous testbed for future spatial agents.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning is a foundational capability for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to perceive and operate within the physical world. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on passive evaluation (e.g., static VQA) or simulator-specific pipelines, failing to assess general interactive spatial understanding. We introduce SpatialWorld, a unified benchmark designed specifically for evaluating the interactive spatial understanding of multimodal agents in complex real-world tasks. Integrating eight heterogeneous simulation backends under a shared, simulator-agnostic protocol, SpatialWorld features 760 human-annotated tasks across diverse domains (e.g., household routines, travel, social collaboration). Agents must solve tasks under vision-only partial observability, actively gathering egocentric visual evidence and expressing decisions via a unified, text-based action interface native to MLLMs. For reliable evaluation, each task includes a human-validated initial state, a reference trajectory, and a terminal-state verifier. Evaluating 15 advanced agents reveals that robust spatial task solving remains challenging: the strongest model, GPT-5, achieves an average task success rate (TSR) of only 17.4%, while the leading open-source model, Qwen-3.5, reaches 14.1%. Further analysis exposes a clear mismatch between task success and execution efficiency, alongside substantial domain-specific performance variations. These bottlenecks in active exploration and long-horizon planning position SpatialWorld as a rigorous testbed for future spatial agents.

</details>


### [670] [Correlation Is Not Enough: Embedding Human Metadata for Individual Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09672)
*Suraj Biswas,Saurabh Gupta,Pritam Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Biomedical language models produce falsely high similarity scores (0.76-0.92) for unrelated cross-domain pairs (e.g., cortisol vs. stock market), achieving 0% discrimination accuracy. This creates critical errors in Large Behavioural Models that treat embedding proximity as causal evidence. A two-stage contrastive fix (72K pairs + BODHI hard-negative mining) boosts within-vs-across-domain separation from 1.05x to 2.30x, and OpenVINO optimization achieves 133x latency reduction to 10ms/query.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biomedical encoders fail at cross-domain discrimination (0% accuracy) because they assign high similarity to completely unrelated concepts. This is catastrophic for Large Behavioural Models (LBMs) that reason over user life graphs and treat embedding proximity as causal evidence—false proximity creates false causal edges that propagate errors downstream.

Method: (1) First contrastive pass on 72,034 pairs improves PubMedBERT BIOSSES correlation from 0.633→0.828 and separation 1.05x→1.63x. (2) Second pass (BODHI) mines hard negatives from missing biomedical KG edges, lifting separation to 2.30x. (3) OpenVINO on Intel Xeon 6737P with AMX achieves 133x speedup; FP16 outperforms INT8 due to AMX architecture quirks.

Result: Accuracy on cross-domain discrimination improved from 0% to significant separation (2.30x). Single-query latency reduced from 1367ms to 10ms (133x), reaching 555 sentences/sec. FP16 beats INT8 at all batch sizes on AMX hardware. No-AMX runs 13-27x slower.

Conclusion: Contrastive fine-tuning with hard negatives (BODHI) effectively fixes the cross-domain similarity collapse in biomedical encoders, and OpenVINO inference optimization makes deployment practical. The authors release benchmark suites, training corpora, and scripts for reproducibility.

Abstract: Ask a pretrained biomedical language model whether "cortisol 28 ug/dL" and "stock-market volatility" are related, and it returns a cosine similarity of 0.83 on a scale where 1.0 means identical. The two share no mechanism. This is not a corner case: every off-the-shelf biomedical encoder we tested (BioBERT, PubMedBERT, BioM-ELECTRA) scores unrelated cross-domain pairs between 0.76 and 0.92 when the answer should be near zero. Accuracy on cross-domain discrimination is 0%.
  Retrieval systems survive this, because a language model downstream filters the noise. A Large Behavioural Model (LBM), a foundation model whose subject is a person rather than a sentence, does not: it reasons over a graph of a user's life and treats embedding proximity as evidence that two events are causally linked. False proximity writes a false causal edge, and everything downstream inherits the error. Here, embedding geometry is not a tuning knob; it is correctness.
  We report the fix. A contrastive pass over 72,034 pairs raises PubMedBERT BIOSSES correlation from 0.633 to 0.828 and within-vs-across-domain separation from 1.05x to 1.63x. A second pass, BODHI, mines hard negatives from edges absent in a biomedical knowledge graph and lifts separation to 2.30x and the discrimination gap to +0.392, at a 4.5% BIOSSES cost. On an Intel Xeon 6737P with AMX, OpenVINO cuts single-query latency from 1367 ms to 10 ms (133x) and reaches 555 sentences/sec. One finding contradicts standard advice: FP16 beats INT8 on this silicon at every serving batch size, and we explain why. The same model on a no-AMX Ice Lake instance runs 13-27x slower. We release the benchmark suite, training corpora, the BODHI generator, and the OpenVINO scripts.

</details>


### [671] [(Auto)formalization is supposed to be easy: Trellis process semantics for spelling out rigorous proofs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09674)
*Wesley Pegden*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trellis autoformalizes math proofs into Lean using LLM agents in a constrained workflow inspired by the mathematician's notion of rigorous proof, achieving reliable formalization with generalist agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve reliable autoformalization of mathematical proofs into Lean without requiring task-specific agent training, inspired by the mathematician's concept that a rigorous proof can be elaborated in further detail at any point.

Method: Uses LLM agents in a deterministically constrained workflow with iterative refinement of natural language proofs, where progress is enforced by process semantics rather than specialized agent training.

Result: Produces an end-to-end Lean formalization of a recent Ramsey theory breakthrough on a modest budget using generalist agents.

Conclusion: The meaning-of-rigor inspired workflow enables reliable autoformalization without task-specific training, demonstrating that process structure can substitute for specialized agent capabilities.

Abstract: We present Trellis: an autoformalization system that leverages LLM agents in a deterministically constrained workflow to enforce incremental progress in Lean autoformalization tasks through iterative refinement of natural language proofs. Our approach is motivated by the common mathematician's notion of what it means to have a rigorous proof in the first place: namely, that it would be routine to elaborate any part of the proof in further detail. The result is a system which aims to achieve reliable autoformalization on a modest budget and with generalist agents, with specialization to autoformalization coming not from any task-specific agent training but instead from a meaning-of-rigor inspired workflow enforced by process semantics. We link to an end-to-end Lean formalization of a recent Ramsey theory breakthrough produced by the process.

</details>


### [672] [Proxy Reward Internalization and Mechanistic Exploitation: A Learned Precursor to Reward Hacking and Its Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09711)
*Mohammad Beigi,Ming Jin,Lifu Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRIME is a learned capability that emerges before visible reward hacking, where models assess task correctness, predict proxy acceptance, and reason about gaps between proxy and gold rewards. It appears in stages, predicts future hacking severity, adapts to evaluator changes, and persists even when overt hacking is suppressed, serving as an early-warning signal for alignment risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research on reward hacking focuses on detecting it after it becomes visible (high proxy reward but task failure). The authors aim to study what happens **before** that failure point, seeking an early signal to predict and prevent reward hacking.

Method: The authors introduce PRIME, a learned capability measured through chain-of-thought monitoring, direct probes, and activation-level concept vectors in coding RL environments with exploitable pytest rewards.

Result: PRIME emerges in a staged sequence before sustained hacking; its direct-probe score forecasts later hack onset and severity even when visible hacking is low; it adapts to evaluator changes, persists under gold reward suppression, and ablating its activation directions reduces hacking. In-domain PRIME tracks out-of-domain misalignment.

Conclusion: Exploitable proxy RL amplifies a proxy-internalization capability upstream of visible hacking, making PRIME a candidate early-warning signal for broader alignment risk.

Abstract: Reward hacking is usually studied after it becomes visible, once a model earns high proxy reward while failing the intended task. We instead study what proxy RL teaches before that failure appears. We introduce Proxy Reward Internalization and Mechanistic Exploitation (PRIME), a learned capability to assess task correctness, predict proxy acceptance, and reason about exploitable proxy--gold gaps. In coding RL environments with exploitable pytest rewards, we measure PRIME through chain-of-thought monitoring, direct probes, and activation-level concept vectors. We find that PRIME emerges in a staged sequence before sustained reward hacking, and that its current direct-probe score forecasts later hack onset and severity even when the visible hack rate is still low. PRIME also adapts when the evaluator changes, retargeting to whichever proxy--gold gap remains rewarded and persisting when gold reward suppresses overt hacking, and ablating its activation directions reduces hacking. Across checkpoints, in-domain PRIME tracks out-of-domain misalignment. Together these results suggest that exploitable proxy RL amplifies a proxy-internalization capability upstream of visible hacking, making PRIME a candidate early-warning signal for broader alignment risk.

</details>


### [673] [Beyond Probabilistic Similarity: Structural, Temporal, and Causal Limitations of Retrieval-Augmented Generation in the Legal Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09724)
*Hudson de Martim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that RAG failures in legal AI stem from an architectural mismatch between probabilistic retrieval and the hierarchical, temporal, and institutional structure of legal knowledge, proposing four deterministic-by-design commitments (ontological primacy, event reification, bitemporal correctness, and deterministic interaction protocols) to address three identified pathologies: mereological blindness, diachronic blindness, and causal opacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-profile legal AI failures (fabricated citations, anachronistic content) persist despite RAG adoption, and the authors argue these are not residual confabulations fixable by scaling models but symptoms of a deeper architectural mismatch between probabilistic retrieval and legal knowledge structure.

Method: The paper develops its argument in three moves: (1) articulating legal knowledge's ontological triad (hierarchical/mereological structure, diachronic dynamism under operational closure, causal traceability of provenance); (2) identifying three retrieval pathologies (mereological blindness, diachronic blindness, causal opacity) with operational definitions, failure mechanisms, examples, and detection criteria; (3) reviewing SOTA approaches through this lens and deriving four architectural commitments for deterministic-by-design legal retrieval.

Result: Existing approaches address the three identified requirements unevenly and do not compose into a paradigm treating them as co-constitutive. The analysis yields four architectural commitments: ontological primacy, event reification, bitemporal correctness, and deterministic interaction protocols for legal retrieval.

Conclusion: The proposed framework concerns quaestio juris (which norms apply and in what state) rather than downstream tasks, addressing legislative and constitutional retrieval primarily with interpretive time as an explicit extension.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a standard architectural response to unreliability in legal AI, yet high-profile failures, including fabricated citations submitted to courts and anachronistic legal content presented as current, continue to appear across jurisdictions. We argue that these failures are not residual confabulations to be eliminated by scaling language models, but symptoms of an architectural mismatch between probabilistic retrieval and the hierarchical, temporal, and institutional structure of legal knowledge. We develop the argument in three moves. First, we articulate the ontological commitment of legal knowledge as a triad of properties derivable from classical legal theory: hierarchical and mereological structure, diachronic dynamism under operational closure, and causal traceability of institutional provenance grounded in the duty of justification. Second, we identify three corresponding pathologies of retrieval (mereological blindness, diachronic blindness, and causal opacity), each developed with an operational definition, a failure mechanism, a canonical example, and detection criteria for diagnostic use. Third, we review the state of the art through this lens, showing that existing approaches address these requirements unevenly and do not yet compose into a paradigm that treats them as co-constitutive. From this analysis we derive four architectural commitments that characterize the deterministic-by-design direction for legal retrieval: ontological primacy, event reification, bitemporal correctness, and deterministic interaction protocols. The framework concerns quaestio juris (which norms apply and in what state) rather than the downstream tasks that act on identified norms, and addresses legislative and constitutional retrieval primarily, with interpretive time as an explicit extension.

</details>


### [674] [SearchSwarm: Towards Delegation Intelligence in Agentic LLMs for Long-Horizon Deep Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09730)
*Pu Ning,Quan Chen,Kun Tao,Xinyu Tang,Tianshu Wang,Qianggang Cao,Xinyu Kong,Zujie Wen,Zhiqiang Zhang,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SearchSwarm-30B-A3B, a model trained via supervised fine-tuning on harness-guided trajectories to acquire delegation intelligence—the ability to decompose complex tasks, delegate subtasks to subagents, and integrate results—achieving state-of-the-art results on BrowseComp (68.1) and BrowseComp-ZH (73.3) among models of comparable scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models face finite context windows while real-world tasks demand unbounded context. The paper aims to bridge the gap by training models to intelligently delegate subtasks to subagents, a capability lacking in naturally occurring text and unexplored in open-source training data synthesis.

Method: The authors designed a harness that guides a model toward high-quality task decomposition and delegation while constraining subagents to return properly summarized results. Trajectories from this harness encode correct delegation decisions and are used as supervised fine-tuning data to internalize delegation intelligence into model weights.

Result: The resulting model, SearchSwarm-30B-A3B, achieves 68.1 on BrowseComp and 73.3 on BrowseComp-ZH, the best results among all models of comparable scale.

Conclusion: The harness-guided data synthesis approach effectively trains delegation intelligence, enabling state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon agent tasks. The authors will release the harness, model weights, and training data to facilitate future research.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly expected to handle complex, long-horizon real-world tasks whose context demands can grow without bound, yet model context windows remain inherently finite. Recent work explores a paradigm where a main agent decomposes tasks and dispatches subtasks to subagents, which execute and return only summarized results, conserving the main agent's context budget. However, performing this well requires delegation intelligence: the ability to decompose complex tasks, determine when and what to delegate, and integrate returned results into the ongoing workflow. Training data for this capability is scarce in naturally occurring text, and to our knowledge, how to synthesize such data and train models to acquire this capability remains largely unexplored in the open-source community. To bridge this gap, we present a preliminary exploration targeting deep research, a representative long-horizon agent task. Specifically, we design a harness that guides the model toward high-quality task decomposition and delegation, while constraining subagents to return results properly to support the main agent's workflow. The harness-guided trajectories naturally encode correct delegation decisions, which we use as supervised fine-tuning data to internalize delegation intelligence into model weights. Our resulting model, SearchSwarm-30B-A3B, achieves 68.1 on BrowseComp and 73.3 on BrowseComp-ZH, the best results among all models of comparable scale. We will release our harness, model weights, and training data to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [675] [Multi-Turn Evaluation of Deep Research Agents Under Process-Level Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09748)
*Rishabh Sabharwal,Hongru Wang,Amos Storkey,Jeff Z. Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates whether deep research agents (DRAs) can improve their reports via feedback, comparing self-reflection (no external signal) vs. process-level feedback using a novel Research Gap Inference (RGI) method. Self-reflection yields negligible improvement; one round of process-level feedback helps significantly (~8-15 points gain), but gains do not compound due to regression on previously satisfied criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DRA benchmarks only assess single-shot outputs, overlooking the critical question of whether agents can iteratively improve their reports when guided by feedback.

Method: Multi-turn evaluation under two settings: (1) self-reflection (no external signal), and (2) process-level feedback via Research Gap Inference (RGI), which analyzes patterns of satisfied/unsatisfied rubric criteria to infer research-process gaps and provide targeted guidance.

Result: (i) Self-reflection yields negligible net improvement due to equal rates of incorporation and regression; (ii) one round of process-level feedback raises normalized scores by ~8-15 points with ~35-40% incorporation; (iii) gains do not compound—agents regress on up to 24% of previously satisfied criteria during full report rewriting.

Conclusion: Even with targeted feedback, reliable multi-turn improvement remains unattainable for current DRA architectures, highlighting a fundamental limitation in iterative refinement capabilities.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks for deep research agents (DRAs) assess only single-shot outputs, ignoring a key question: can DRAs improve their reports when guided by feedback? To investigate this, we conduct a multi-turn evaluation of DRAs under two feedback settings: self-reflection, in which the agent revises its report without any external diagnostic signal, and process-level feedback, in which the agent receives guidance targeting gaps in its research strategy. To enable process-level feedback, we design Research Gap Inference (RGI), a method that analyzes patterns of satisfied and unsatisfied rubric criteria to infer research-process gaps. Our analysis reveals three key findings: (i) under self-reflection, agents incorporate and regress on rubric criteria at nearly equal rates, yielding negligible net improvement; (ii) a single round of process-level feedback yields substantial gains, raising the normalized score by approximately $8$-$15$ points and yielding a roughly $35$-$40\%$ incorporation rate; (iii) these gains do not compound over subsequent turns, as agents regress on up to $24\%$ of previously satisfied criteria when rewriting the full report to address remaining gaps. Even with targeted guidance, reliable multi-turn improvement remains out of reach for the DRA architectures we evaluate. Our code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/sabharwalrishabh/Multi-Turn-Evaluation-of-DRAs.

</details>


### [676] [Collaborative Human-Agent Protocol (CHAP)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09751)
*Arsalan Shahid,Gordon Suttie,Philip Black*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CHAP is a protocol standardizing the shared workspace for human-agent collaboration in production deployments, replacing ad-hoc record-keeping (chat threads, ticket comments) with structured, auditable events including diffs, rationales, content hashes, and non-repudiable signatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production AI deployments have evolved from single-human-single-model supervision to multi-human, multi-agent collaborations crossing teams and trust boundaries, yet the technical infrastructure for recording human judgement moments (edits, approvals, handoffs) remains weakly specified—lost in chat threads, tribal memory, or application code.

Method: CHAP defines a small Core consisting of workspaces, participants, tasks, artefacts, and an append-only evidence log, plus composable profiles for review, modes, routing, deliberation, handoff, identity, signatures, and transparency-backed audit.

Result: Human overrides become structured events (diff + rationale + content hash); shift handoffs become portable envelopes; approvals become non-repudiable signed decisions replayable years later.

Conclusion: Existing protocols (MCP for tools/data, A2A for agent-agent) do not cover the shared human-agent workspace; CHAP fills this gap with a minimal core and composable extensions.

Abstract: Foundation models are moving from response generation into operational roles. They plan across steps, call tools, request human input, coordinate with other agents, and increasingly carry responsibility for work that affects customers, claims, code, contracts, and clinical decisions. Production deployments are no longer one human supervising one model. They are multi-human, multi-agent collaborations that cross teams, time zones, and trust boundaries. The technical surface for this collaboration remains weakly specified. When an agent drafts a response and a human edits it before it ships, the moment of human judgement is the most valuable signal in the system. In current practice it is recorded, if at all, in application code, chat threads, ticket comments, and tribal memory. Two protocol standards address adjacent concerns: MCP standardises agent access to tools and data, and A2A standardises agent-to-agent interoperability. Neither defines the shared workspace in which humans and agents perform accountable work together. This paper presents CHAP, the Collaborative Human-Agent Protocol. Under CHAP, the override that used to vanish into a chat thread becomes a structured event carrying a diff, a rationale, and a content hash. The handoff between shifts becomes a portable envelope rather than a pinned message. The human approval of an agent's draft becomes a non-repudiable signed decision that can be replayed years later. The protocol achieves this through a small Core (workspaces, participants, tasks, artefacts, and an append-only evidence log) together with composable profiles that add review, modes, routing, deliberation, handoff, identity, signatures, and transparency-backed audit as deployments require them. Specification, reference implementation, conformance suite, and worked examples are available at: https://github.com/BrightbeamAI/chap

</details>


### [677] [SIGA: Self-Evolving Coding-Agent Adapters for Scientific Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09774)
*Matthew Ho,Brian Liu,Jixuan Chen,Audrey Wang,Lianhui Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SIGA is a lightweight adapter layer that bridges coding agents with scientific simulators by providing executable contracts (vocabulary, constraints, validation rules), enabling agents to produce complete simulation configurations ~36x faster than human experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific simulators use specialized input languages that take domain scientists hours to days to learn. The goal is to minimize simulator-specific adaptations needed for off-the-shelf coding agents to operate real scientific software.

Method: SIGA (Simulator-Interface Grounding Adapter) supplies the simulator's executable contract through four components: retrieval, procedural memory, in-trajectory validation, and validation-enforced termination. It also supports self-evolution by rewriting adapter contents from prior trajectories.

Result: SIGA produces a complete GEOS deck in ~5 minutes with TreeSim >0.90, matching a human expert who took ~3 hours (36x speedup). On held-out sets, grounding improves TreeSim from 0.720 to 0.789 (~10% relative gain) and reduces across-seed standard deviation by 16x. Self-evolution yields the highest held-out GEOS mean. Transfers to OpenFOAM and LAMMPS show different dominant mechanisms depending on the interface bottleneck.

Conclusion: Lightweight, self-improvable grounding layers can turn general coding agents into practical operators of scientific software, with the dominant adaptation mechanism shifting based on the simulator's interface characteristics.

Abstract: Advanced scientific simulators expose specialized input languages that turn simulation goals into executable configurations, but learning them can cost domain scientists hours to days. We study simulator setup as a problem of agent-tool interface grounding: what minimal simulator-specific adaptations are needed for an off-the-shelf coding agent to operate real scientific software? Our intuition is that coding agents already know how to navigate files, edit code, run commands, and repair outputs, but they lack the simulator's executable contract: its vocabulary, structural constraints, validation rules, and termination conditions. We introduce SIGA, a Simulator-Interface Grounding Adapter that supplies this contract through retrieval, procedural memory, in-trajectory validation, and validation-enforced termination. We primarily evaluate SIGA on GEOS, an open-source multiphysics simulator used in subsurface science. SIGA produces a complete GEOS deck in about five minutes with TreeSim above 0.90, matching an extended-budget human expert who took about three hours, a roughly 36x wall-clock speedup. On a harder held-out set, grounding raises TreeSim from 0.720 to 0.789, a roughly 10% relative gain over the bare agent, and can reduce the across-seed standard deviation by 16x. Self-evolution further improves SIGA by rewriting adapter contents from prior trajectories, yielding the highest held-out GEOS mean and matching or outperforming the strongest hand-designed configuration. Transfers to OpenFOAM and LAMMPS show that the dominant mechanism shifts by interface: validation matters most when structural completeness is the bottleneck, while memory and retrieval matter most when domain correctness is the bottleneck. These results suggest that lightweight, self-improvable grounding layers can turn general coding agents into practical operators of scientific software.

</details>


### [678] [Evaluation Cards: An Interpretive Layer for AI Evaluation Reporting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09809)
*Avijit Ghosh,Anka Reuel,Jenny Chim,Wm. Matthew Kennedy,Srishti Yadav,Jennifer Mickel,Yanan Long,Andrew Tran,Anastassia Kornilova,Damian Stachura,Kevin Klyman,Felix Friedrich,Jeba Sania,Max Lamparth,Jan Batzner,Anoop Mishra,Eliya Habba,Yixiong Hao,Nathan Heath,Shalaleh Rismani,Usman Gohar,Andrea Loehr,David Manheim,Ruchira Dhar,Sree Harsha Nelaturu,Aarush Sinha,Leshem Choshen,Drishti Sharma,Ishan Khire,Amit Saha,Subramanyam Sahoo,Michael Hardy,Michael Alexander Riegler,Kabir Manghnani,Michelle Lin,Yanan Jiang,Yilin Huang,Asaf Yehudai,Jessica Ji,Aris Hofmann,Mubashara Akhtar,Nuno Moniz,Yacine Jernite,Stella Biderman,Zeerak Talat,Sanmi Koyejo,Mykel Kochenderfer,Irene Solaiman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes EvalCards, an operational reporting layer that unifies benchmark metadata, evaluation run data, and model metadata into a single interpretable record, validated across thousands of models and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI evaluation results are reported inconsistently across sources, making it hard to compare, identify omissions, or trace aggregate claims to evidence. Existing efforts are narrow, static, and lack extraction infrastructure for scale.

Method: (1) Derived a reporting schema from 52 papers and 10 stakeholder interviews; (2) implemented four interpretive signals (reproducibility, documentation completeness, provenance/risk, score comparability) with reader modes for research and non-research audiences; (3) deployed a monitoring tool across 5,816 models, 635 benchmarks, and 101,843 results.

Result: The monitoring tool surfaces systematic gaps in current reporting practice across a large-scale corpus of evaluations.

Conclusion: EvalCards provides an operational, composable, and audience-aware solution to the fragmented evaluation reporting landscape, with evidence of its effectiveness at scale.

Abstract: AI evaluation results are produced at scale but reported inconsistently across leaderboards, model cards, benchmark papers, and company blogs. The cost is interpretive: readers cannot reliably compare results across sources, identify what a report omits, or trace an aggregate claim to its underlying evidence. Recent efforts address isolated components but leave three gaps: they cover only narrow slices of the evaluation lifecycle and do not compose into a single interpretable record; they specify static representations that do not differentiate the questions different stakeholders bring to the same evidence; and they remain proposals on paper, lacking the extraction infrastructure required for adoption at scale. We present \EvalCards{}, an operational reporting layer that composes benchmark metadata, evaluation run data, and model metadata into a unified record. We (1) derive a reporting schema from a structured review of 52 papers and 10 stakeholder interviews, (2) implement four interpretive signals (reproducibility, documentation completeness, provenance and risk, and score comparability), rendered through reader modes calibrated to research and non-research audiences, and (3) deploy a monitoring tool that applies \EvalCards{} across 5,816 models, 635 benchmarks, and 101,843 results, surfacing systematic gaps in current reporting practice.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [679] [VoLo: A Physical Orchestrator for Open-Vocabulary Long-Horizon Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07723)
*Siyi Chen,Hugo Hadfield,Alex Zook,Mikaela Angelina Uy,Chan Hee Song,Erwin Coumans,Xuning Yang,Faisal Ladhak,Qing Qu,Stan Birchfield,Jonathan Tremblay,Valts Blukis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VoLoAgent is a VLM-based system for open-vocabulary long-horizon robot manipulation that orchestrates heterogeneous tools (including interruptible VLAs/WAMs) in a closed loop, evaluated on a new RoboVoLo benchmark, showing significant improvements over existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation in physical environments requires robots to handle flexible instructions, complex scenes, and dynamic execution with failure recovery, which existing single-model or tool-based systems cannot adequately address.

Method: VoLoAgent uses a VLM as a closed agent loop that orchestrates interruptible tools (VLA/WAM, vision models, action primitives), enabling mid-rollout steering, planning, monitoring, and failure recovery in physical settings.

Result: VoLoAgent substantially outperforms single VLA/VLM or tool-based systems on the RoboVoLo benchmark, with validation on real-robot experiments.

Conclusion: Physical Orchestration with interruptible tools enables more effective open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation, and the RoboVoLo benchmark provides useful failure-mode diagnostics for future work.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation requires robots to reason over flexible instructions and complex multi-object scenes while adaptively planning, executing, monitoring, and recovering from failures. We address these demands with a closed agent loop in which a VLM orchestrates heterogeneous robot capabilities as interruptible tools. Unlike in virtual AI agents, the timing of decisions, actions and tool calls is important in a physical world that does not pause for reasoning. We refer to this setting as Physical Orchestration, and propose VoLoAgent, a VLM that plans, monitors, and recovers by treating a VLA/WAM as an interruptible tool it steers mid-rollout alongside vision models and action primitives. To evaluate these long-horizon capabilities, we introduce RoboVoLo, a high-fidelity benchmark for open-vocabulary long-horizon manipulation across common sense, memory/state tracking, complex references, and world knowledge, with both task-level success and failure-mode diagnostics. Experiments show VoLoAgent substantially outperforms single VLA/VLM or tool-based systems, with validation on real-robot experiments. Project page: https://chicychen.github.io/VoLo/

</details>


### [680] [MinNav: Minimalist Navigation Using Optical Flow For Active Tiny Aerial Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07813)
*Aniket Patil,Mandeep Singh,Uday Girish Maradana,Nitin J. Sanket*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MinNav is a monocular navigation stack using optical flow and uncertainty to enable tiny aerial robots to fly through static/dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps without prior knowledge, achieving 70% success rate with low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable autonomous navigation for tiny aerial robots using only a monocular camera, which offers ideal balance of versatility, cost, and accuracy, without requiring prior knowledge of scene components or depth sensors.

Method: Uses optical flow and its uncertainty for navigation, combined with active exploratory movement to detect and avoid obstacles. Operates without any prior knowledge of scene layout or obstacle locations.

Result: Achieved 70% overall success rate in real-world experiments with static/dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps. Performance on par with depth-based methods but requires significantly less computation.

Conclusion: First monocular-only solution to handle all these navigation cases without prior knowledge, suitable for onboard deployment on tiny aerial robots with minimal computational requirements.

Abstract: Navigation using a monocular camera is pivotal for autonomous operation on tiny aerial robots due to their perfect balance of versatility, cost and accuracy. In this paper, we introduce MinNav, a navigation stack based on optical flow and its uncertainty to fly through a scene with static and dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps without any prior knowledge of the scene components and/or their locations/ordering. We further improve success rate by using the activeness of the robot to move around in an exploratory way to find obstacles and navigate. We successfully evaluate and demonstrate the proposed approach in many real-world experiments in various environments with static and dynamic obstacles and unknown-shaped gaps with an overall success rate of 70%. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first solution to tackle all the aforementioned navigation cases without prior knowledge using a monocular camera. Our approach is on par in performance with depth based methods with factors of magnitude less computation required and can readily run onboard tiny aerial robots. The accompanying video, supplementary material, code and dataset can be found at https://pear.wpi.edu/research/minnav.html

</details>


### [681] [Path Planning Using Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient: A Reinforcement Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07855)
*Qiang Le,Yaguang Yang,Isaac E. Weintraub*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a DDPG-based path-planning method for autonomous vehicles avoiding circular threat zones, achieving faster real-time performance than traditional optimal control while identifying the feasible starting region for safe missions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optimal control methods for path-planning in threat-laden environments are too computationally slow for real-time decision-making due to the nonlinear and nonconvex nature of the problem.

Method: Uses Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) to train an agent in simulation, with state (position/heading) mapped to actions. Reward includes attractive field (destination), repulsive fields (circular obstacles), and energy penalty. Models threats as circular 'no-go' zones.

Result: The DDPG agent produces effective safe paths significantly faster than the pseudo-spectral optimal control method, making it suitable for real-time applications.

Conclusion: The learning-based approach enables real-time path-planning and pre-mission feasibility assessment by identifying the largest set of safe starting points, assisting mission planning.

Abstract: Path-planning for autonomous vehicles in threat-laden environments is a fundamental challenge because the problem is nonlinear and nonconvex even in simplest scenarios. While traditional optimal control methods can be used to find ideal paths, the computational time is often too slow for real-time decision-making. To solve this challenge, we propose a method based on Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) and model the threat as possibly multiple circular 'no-go' zones. A mission is regarded as a failure if the vehicle enters this restricted zone at any time or does not reach a neighborhood of the destination. The DDPG agent is trained through trial and error in a simulated environment, learning a direct mapping from its current state (position and heading) to a series of feasible actions that guide the agent to safely reach its destination. The reword function has three parts: (a) an attractive field centered at the final destination, (b) some repulsive fields centered at the origins of circular obstacles, and (c) a penalty of control energy consumption (the magnitude of heading change) that indirectly in favor for straight path. The DDPG trains the agent using these incentives to find the largest possible set of starting points wherein a safe path to the destination is guaranteed. This provides critical information for mission planning, showing beforehand whether a task is achievable from a given starting point, assisting pre-mission planning activities. The approach is validated in simulation. A comparison between the DDPG method and a traditional optimal control (pseudo-spectral) method is carried out. The results show that the learning-based agent produces effective paths while being significantly faster, making it a better fit for real-time applications.

</details>


### [682] [End-to-End Control of a Powered Knee-Ankle Prosthesis Towards Unified, Tuning-Free Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07902)
*John Shim,Christoph Nuesslein,Sixu Zhou,Hanjun kim,Kinsey Herrin,Aaron Young*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a real-time end-to-end prosthesis controller using Temporal Convolutional Networks trained on multi-terrain data from 18 individuals with transfemoral amputation. Deployed on 4 participants across 5 locomotion modes, the controller successfully reproduced key biomechanical scaling behaviors (e.g., ankle torque with walking speed, knee flexion with ramp grade) and enabled seamless stair transitions without explicit mode classification or subject-specific tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Powered prostheses conventionally rely on impedance controllers requiring extensive manual tuning and explicit mode classification. This work aims to eliminate the need for intent classifiers and subject-specific tuning by developing an end-to-end controller that estimates continuous actuator signals directly from onboard sensors.

Method: Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) were trained on a multi-terrain dataset from 18 individuals with transfemoral amputation. The controller was deployed in real time across five locomotion modes (level ground, ramp ascent/descent, stair ascent/descent) on four participants (three able-bodied, one with transfemoral amputation).

Result: During level walking, the controller reproduced training-data scaling of peak ankle torque with walking speed (deployed 0.85 vs training 0.96 Nm/kg per m/s). During ramp ascent, it scaled knee pre-flexion with grade (deployed 2.92 vs training 3.30 deg/deg). During ramp descent, it increased resistive knee torque (+0.16 Nm/kg in both conditions). Seamless stair transitions were generated for both limb-leading sequences despite training data containing only one sequence type.

Conclusion: These results provide initial evidence towards end-to-end control that can provide unified, mode-adaptive prosthetic assistance without subject-specific tuning.

Abstract: Powered prostheses conventionally rely on impedance controllers that require extensive manual tuning and explicit mode classification. In this work, we present real-time deployment of an end-to-end prosthesis controller that estimates continuous actuator signals from onboard sensors, eliminating the need for intent classifiers and subject-specific tuning. Temporal Convolutional Networks were trained on a multi-terrain dataset from 18 individuals with transfemoral amputation and deployed in real time across five locomotion modes. Four participants (three able-bodied, one with transfemoral amputation) ambulated across level ground, ramp ascent and descent, and stair ascent and descent. During level walking, the deployed controller reproduced the training-data scaling of peak ankle torque with walking speed (deployed 0.85 Nm/kg per m/s, p = 0.001; training 0.96 Nm/kg per m/s, 95% CI [0.42, 1.50], p = 0.002), after excluding one outlier traced to atypical prosthesis loading. During ramp ascent, the controller scaled knee pre-flexion with grade (deployed 2.92 deg/deg, p = 0.027; training 3.30 deg/deg, 95% CI [1.83, 4.77], p < 0.001). During ramp descent, the controller increased resistive knee torque relative to level walking (deployed +0.16 Nm/kg, p < 0.001; training +0.16 Nm/kg, p = 0.008). Seamless stair transitions were generated for both intact- and prosthetic-side-leading sequences in ascent and descent, despite the training data containing only one limb-leading sequence. These results provide initial evidence towards end-to-end control that can provide unified, mode-adaptive prosthetic assistance without subject-specific tuning.

</details>


### [683] [X-OP: Cross-Morphology Whole-Body Teleoperation via MPC Retargeting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07934)
*Jen-Wei Wang,Sarthak Kaingade,Andrea Tagliabue,Nicholas Morozovsky*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical whole-body teleoperation framework using a single XR device, with MPC-based motion retargeting that optimizes alignment with operator intent and dynamic feasibility across diverse robot morphologies without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing whole-body teleoperation approaches rely on expensive exoskeleton suits or multi-camera setups, while single XR methods require robot-specific retraining and suffer from out-of-distribution failures due to neglecting dynamic feasibility.

Method: A hierarchical framework using a single XR device with (1) MPC-based motion retargeter jointly optimizing operator intent and robot dynamic feasibility, (2) state synchronization resetting simulator state per MPC step, and (3) SLAM-based global pose feedback for drift mitigation.

Result: Humanoid: 30% lower completion time, 20% lower power consumption; Mobile manipulator: zero collisions in simulation. Real-world validation confirmed effectiveness on both platforms with user-adjustable behavior.

Conclusion: The proposed plug-and-play, morphology-agnostic framework enables scalable whole-body teleoperation with real-time behavioral customization, generalizing across diverse robot platforms without robot-specific retraining.

Abstract: Whole-body teleoperation is essential for scalable robot data collection in loco-manipulation tasks, yet existing approaches relying on exoskeleton suits or multi-camera setups impose prohibitive cost, complexity, and environmental constraints. Recent methods using a single extended reality (XR) device with end-to-end reinforcement learning policies partially address these limitations but require robot-specific retraining, suffer from out-of-distribution failures, and rely on motion retargeting that neglects dynamic feasibility. We propose a hierarchical whole-body teleoperation framework driven by a single XR device that generalizes across diverse robot morphologies without retraining robot-specific policies. A Model Predictive Control (MPC)-based motion retargeter jointly optimizes alignment with the operator's intent and the robot's dynamic feasibility, generating optimal commands for existing low-level controllers. To ensure robust online execution, we introduce a state synchronization method that resets the simulator state at each MPC step to handle noisy real-world measurements and contact sensitivity, and integrate SLAM-based global pose feedback to mitigate long-term drift. Simulation results show higher success rates on whole-body control tasks for both a humanoid (over 30% lower completion time and 20% lower power consumption) and a mobile manipulator (zero collisions) compared to baselines. Real-world experiments further validate the effectiveness and flexibility of our method, demonstrating the successful deployment of the proposed retargeter on both platforms for whole-body control tasks and the ease of allowing users to adjust teleoperation behavior based on their preferences. This plug-and-play framework offers a scalable, morphology-agnostic solution for whole-body robot teleoperation, enabling real-time behavioral customization and broad applicability across platforms.

</details>


### [684] [PRISM: PRior-guided Imagination Sampling in world Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07974)
*Yuhai Wang,Jiawei Xia,Rongxuan Zhou,Xiao Hu,Yongliang Shi,Jing Du,Yang Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PRISM extracts action priors from a frozen JEPA world model's encoder via a lightweight MLP, then uses precision-weighted Product-of-Gaussians to guide model-based planning, yielding +35% and +32% success rate gains on Cube and PushT tasks without architectural bloat or significant overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model-based planners either search arbitrarily or discard expert state-conditioned confidence; obtaining a robust action prior typically requires independent visual encoders or large VLMs, introducing unnecessary architectural complexity.

Method: PRISM attaches a lightweight MLP to a frozen JEPA-style latent world model's encoder to predict a state-conditioned Gaussian prior. At plan time, it fuses this prior into the planner's sampling distribution via a precision-weighted Product-of-Gaussians update (closed-form, parameter-free).

Result: PRISM improves success rates by 35 percentage points over vanilla world-model-based MPC on Cube and 32 percentage points on PushT, without significant inference overhead.

Conclusion: Architectural bloat is unnecessary for obtaining action priors—the same data and world model representations inherently encode the agent's action intuition, and a lightweight MLP suffices to extract and leverage it for planning.

Abstract: A learned world model provides a powerful physical intuition for evaluating future states. But its effectiveness in continuous control also depends critically on how candidate actions are generated for model-based planning. Rather than solely asking how accurately a model can simulate the future, we ask: which candidate actions are worth evaluating in the first place? Existing planners typically search arbitrarily or use expert demonstrations only to initialize a sampling mean, discarding the expert's state-conditioned confidence. Properly guiding this search requires a robust action prior, yet current approaches often rely on independent visual encoders or large-scale VLMs to obtain one. We argue that this architectural bloat is unnecessary: the exact same data - and the learned representations of the world model itself - inherently encode the agent's action intuition. We introduce PRISM, a task-agnostic framework that extracts both from a single dataset while maintaining strict architectural simplicity. Building on a standard JEPA-style latent world model, PRISM attaches a lightweight MLP directly to its frozen encoder to predict a state-conditioned Gaussian prior. At plan time, PRISM fuses this prior into the planner's sampling distribution via a precision-weighted Product-of-Gaussians update. This parameter-free, closed-form integration steers the sampling process, making the prior confident where it is and ceding control where it is not. PRISM improves success rates by 35 percentage points over vanilla world-model-based MPC on Cube and 32 percentage points on PushT, without introducing significant inference overhead.

</details>


### [685] [Q-VGM: Q-Guided Value-Gradient Matching for Flow-Matching VLA Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08015)
*Ziqian Wang,Jiayu Sun,Xingjian Mao,Minqian Wang,Yao Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Q-VGM is an off-policy RL method that fine-tunes flow-matching VLA policies by aligning value gradients with the velocity field, avoiding unstable backpropagation through denoising. It achieves substantial success rate improvements on LIBERO (75%→92.5%), RoboTwin (76.4%→87.2%), and real-robot tasks (40%→67.5%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for improving flow-matching VLA policies via learned Q-functions either suffer from numerical instability when backpropagating through the denoising chain, cannot compute tractable action likelihoods for policy gradients, or only use the critic at test time without policy improvement.

Method: Q-VGM transforms value gradients into a denoising-time value-gradient field via VGG-Flow, avoiding end-to-end backpropagation and action likelihoods. It uses a Cal-QL ensemble critic with per-layer action injection over compact RLT features, and trains on a fixed replay buffer without requiring online denoising chain differentiation.

Result: LIBERO: 75.0%→92.5%; RoboTwin 2.0: 76.4%→87.2%; Real-robot tabletop: 40.0%→67.5%. Outperforms all same-backbone, same-critic baselines across all settings.

Conclusion: Q-VGM enables practical few-shot initialization followed by learn-from-experience fine-tuning, substantially improving task performance without additional expert supervision.

Abstract: We propose Q-Guided Value-Gradient Matching (Q-VGM), an off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) method that tackles a long-standing challenge in fine-tuning flow-matching vision-language-action (VLA) policies: efficiently improving an expressive flow-matching action expert with respect to a learned Q-function. Effective improvement must exploit the first-order (gradient) information of the critic, but this is difficult for flow policies, because directly back-propagating the value through their multi-step denoising process is numerically unstable at VLA scale, while the tractable action likelihoods required by policy-gradient methods are unavailable under iterative denoising. Existing value-based methods either backpropagate through the full denoising chain, use the critic only at test time without updating the policy, or distill critic-improved actions as terminal labels without supervising the velocity field. Q-VGM sidesteps these issues by leveraging VGG-Flow, a value-gradient view of flow alignment in generative modeling that transforms value gradient into a denoising-time value-gradient field rather than an unstable end-to-end objective. This requires no action likelihoods and no backpropagation through the denoising chain, and operates on a fixed replay buffer. The critic is an action-sensitive Cal-QL ensemble over compact RLT features with per-layer action injection. Q-VGM enables a practical few-shot initialization then learn-from-experience paradigm: starting from a few-shot-SFT pi0.5 VLA, the method leverages self-generated rollout data to substantially improve task performance without additional expert supervision. On LIBERO, Q-VGM raises the average success rate from 75.0% to 92.5%; on RoboTwin 2.0, from 76.4% to 87.2%; and on two real-robot tabletop tasks, from 40.0% to 67.5%, outperforming all same-backbone, same-critic baselines across all three settings.

</details>


### [686] [IntentNav: Learning Spatial-Visual Object Navigation from Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08029)
*Yuxin Cai,Zongtai Li,Maonan Wang,Muyi Bao,Haokun Zhu,Ruofei Bai,Ding Zhao,Zirui Li,Wenshan Wang,Wei-Yun Yau,Ji Zhang,Chen Lv*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IntentNav is a spatial-visual imitation framework that learns human-like ObjectNav policies by modeling search intent from human demonstrations, achieving SOTA on multiple benchmarks and zero-shot transfer to various robot morphologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object navigation requires human-like exploration strategies (e.g., selectively probing promising frontiers while avoiding redundant revisits), but existing methods lack explicit modeling of high-level search intent from low-level human actions.

Method: Proposes Frontier-based Human-Intent Labeling to infer search intent, a spatial-visual candidate space combining BEV memory and egocentric visual cues, and a VLM policy trained with Intent-Aligned Objective.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on MP3D, HM3D-v1, and HM3D-v2 ObjectNav benchmarks.

Conclusion: IntentNav effectively learns human-like exploration policies and the candidate-level navigation interface transfers zero-shot to wheeled, quadruped, and humanoid robots without fine-tuning.

Abstract: Object navigation requires a robot to search for an unobserved target in an unknown environment by deciding where to explore next under partial observability. Effective search resembles human-like exploration: selectively probing visually promising frontiers while relying on spatial memory to avoid redundant revisits. We propose IntentNav, a spatial-visual imitation framework that learns human-like ObjectNav policies from human demonstrations. To infer high-level search intent from low-level human actions, we introduce Frontier-based Human-Intent Labeling, which looks ahead in human demonstrations and labels the frontier that best explains the demonstrator's future search direction. We construct a spatial-visual candidate space, where BEV memory tracks explored regions, unexplored frontiers, and trajectory history, while egocentric visual memory provides semantic cues for each candidate. A VLM policy is trained to select among these grounded candidates, using Intent-Aligned Objective to encourage consistent and human-like exploration. IntentNav achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MP3D, HM3D-v1 and HM3D-v2 ObjectNav benchmarks. The proposed candidate-level navigation interface transfers zero-shot to wheeled, quadruped, and humanoid robots without further VLM fine-tuning. \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/w/IntentNav/}{Project page}.

</details>


### [687] [MuJoCo-Drones-Gym: A GPU-Accelerated Multi-Drone Simulator for Control and Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08039)
*Manan Tayal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MuJoCo-Drones-Gym is an open-source, Gymnasium-compatible multi-drone simulation environment built on MuJoCo physics engine, supporting arbitrary numbers of Crazyflie nano-quadcopters with modular physics models, action interfaces, observation spaces, and PettingZoo-based multi-agent RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quadcopter learning environments face a trade-off between physical fidelity, multi-agent support, and the throughput required by modern deep RL pipelines.

Method: Built on MuJoCo physics engine; supports arbitrary Crazyflie 2.x drones; modular API for selecting physics models (rigid-body, Python dynamics, ground effect, blade drag, inter-drone downwash), action interfaces (RPMs, thrust, velocity, waypoints), observation spaces (kinematics, RGB/depth/seg cameras, adjacency); PettingZoo ParallelEnv wrapper for multi-agent RL; seven task environments.

Result: A simulation environment that leverages MuJoCo's improved contact handling, rendering, and parallelizability compared to gym-pybullet-drones, with a suite of seven diverse task environments.

Conclusion: MuJoCo-Drones-Gym provides a high-throughput, physically faithful, multi-drone RL environment that overcomes the fidelity-versus-throughput trade-off found in existing quadcopter simulators.

Abstract: Robotic simulators are a cornerstone of modern research in aerial robotics, serving both as a vehicle for the development of new control algorithms and as the data source for training reinforcement learning (RL) policies. Yet, existing quadcopter learning environments often face a trade-off between physical fidelity, multi-agent support, and the throughput required by modern deep RL pipelines. In this paper, we present MuJoCo-Drones-Gym, an open-source Gymnasium-compatible multi-drone environment built on top of the MuJoCo physics engine. MuJoCo-Drones-Gym supports an arbitrary number of Bitcraze Crazyflie 2.x nano-quadcopters and exposes a modular API for selecting (i)~the physics model (rigid-body MuJoCo, explicit Python dynamics, or any subset of ground effect, blade drag, and inter-drone downwash), (ii)~the action interface (per-motor RPMs, collective normalized thrust, velocity setpoints, or PID waypoint commands), and (iii)~the observation space (kinematic state vectors, RGB / depth / segmentation cameras, or neighbourhood adjacency information). A PettingZoo ParallelEnv wrapper enables drop-in multi-agent reinforcement learning, while a suite of seven task environments, hover, velocity tracking, multi-drone hover, waypoint navigation, formation flight, gate racing, and a generic multi-agent template, demonstrates the breadth of the interface. We describe the environment design, the underlying physics and quadcopter dynamics, and illustrate its use through control and learning examples that mirror those of the closely related gym-pybullet-drones project, while taking advantage of MuJoCo's improved contact handling, rendering, and parallelizability.

</details>


### [688] [EgoAERO: Learning Dexterous Manipulation from a Single Egocentric Video without Object Assets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08057)
*Yichen Niu,Haoran Lv,Xinrui Zhang,Xueyao Wan,Shiyu Gao,Ying Ai,Hui Xu,Yongqi Hu,Hengyi Zhang,Yang Xie,Zhaxizhuoma,Yue Zhao,Zhenshan Bing,Yan Ding,Jianxing Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EgoAERO learns dexterous manipulation from a single egocentric RGB-D video without pre-scanned object assets, using asset-free object tracking/reconstruction, ego motion compensation, and adaptive contact optimization, then converts trajectories into robot policies via two-stage residual learning, validated on a 4.3M-frame dataset (EgoDex-R) and achieving performance close to CAD-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Egocentric RGB-D videos provide natural human manipulation data, but prior methods rely on pre-scanned object assets (CAD models) and struggle to extract object pose, geometry, and contact information, limiting their applicability for robot learning in real-world settings.

Method: EgoAERO comprises: (1) asset-free object tracking and reconstruction to recover object geometry and pose from a single RGB-D video; (2) ego motion compensation to stabilize the hand-object trajectory; (3) adaptive contact optimization to enforce physically consistent contact between hand and object; (4) two-stage residual learning to convert reconstructed trajectories into executable robot policies; (5) an online quality assessment mechanism for robust deployment; and (6) construction of EgoDex-R, a large-scale (4.3M frame) egocentric dataset.

Result: EgoAERO enables single-demonstration dexterous manipulation learning without requiring pre-scanned object assets. On the HOI4D benchmark, it achieves downstream policy performance close to CAD-based reconstruction methods. Real-world experiments confirm its practical applicability.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that asset-free, contact-consistent hand-object trajectory recovery from a single egocentric RGB-D video is feasible for dexterous robot policy learning, significantly reducing the data and equipment requirements compared to prior CAD-dependent approaches.

Abstract: Egocentric RGB-D videos offer a natural source of human dexterous manipulation demonstrations, but existing data is difficult to use for robot learning because object pose, geometry, and contact information are often missing or require pre-scanned object assets. We present EgoAERO, the first framework that learns dexterous manipulation from a single egocentric RGB-D human demonstration without object assets. EgoAERO reconstructs contact-consistent hand-object trajectories through asset-free object tracking and reconstruction, ego motion compensation, and adaptive contact optimization, then converts them into robot policies using two-stage residual learning. We further introduce an online quality assessment mechanism and construct EgoDex-R, a large-scale egocentric dataset with 4.3M RGB-D frames for dexterous policy learning. Simulation and real-world experiments show that EgoAERO enables single-demonstration dexterous manipulation and achieves downstream performance close to CAD-based reconstructions on HOI4D.

</details>


### [689] [Perceptive Behavior Foundation Model: Adapting Human Motion Priors to Robot-Centric Terrain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08059)
*Zifan Wang,Yizhao Li,Teli Ma,Qiang Zhang,Yudong Fan,Hao Xu,Shuo Yang,Junwei Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Perceptive BFM is a terrain-aware humanoid control framework that adapts human motion priors to robot environments using terrain-conformal reference synthesis and a residual Transformer tracker.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing humanoid behavior foundation models assume reference motions are already physically compatible with the robot's surroundings, which fails when human demonstrators and robots inhabit different environments.

Method: 1) Terrain-conformal reference synthesis (TCRS) converts human motion clips into terrain-consistent references. 2) Blind adapted-reference teacher trained with TCRS data. 3) Raw-reference student with identity-gated Transformer tracker using residual pathways for terrain-aware corrections.

Result: A framework that grounds human motion priors in robot-centric perception, enabling terrain-aware whole-body control with preserved raw kinematic motion references.

Conclusion: Perceptive BFM enables humanoid robots to adapt human motion priors to varying terrain while maintaining the behavioral interface of raw kinematic references.

Abstract: Humanoid behavior foundation models aim to acquire reusable whole-body control policies from broad human motion priors, enabling a single controller to produce diverse and expressive behaviors. However, existing motion-centric foundation policies largely assume that the reference motion is already physically compatible with the robot's surroundings. This assumption breaks when the demonstrator, operator, and robot inhabit different environments: a human motion may specify the intended behavior, but not the footholds, clearance, body height, or contact timing required by the robot's local terrain. We introduce \emph{Perceptive Behavior Foundation Model} (Perceptive BFM), a terrain-aware humanoid control framework that grounds human motion priors in robot-centric perception. The model preserves raw kinematic motion references as the behavioral interface, while using local terrain observations to adapt contacts, posture, and timing. To provide scalable terrain supervision, we develop \emph{terrain-conformal reference synthesis} (TCRS), which converts locomotion-oriented human motion clips into terrain-consistent references through contact-aware foothold construction, foot-geometry-aware swing optimization, support-aware root reconstruction, collision repair, and multi-point inverse kinematics. We then train a blind adapted-reference teacher and transfer its terrain-conformal behavior to a deployed raw-reference student through target-frame action alignment. The student is an identity-gated Transformer tracker whose terrain features enter through residual pathways initialized to preserve the motion-tracking prior and trained to produce local corrections only when needed.

</details>


### [690] [Cooperative Long Rope Skipping via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08064)
*Zihao Wang,Shijie Peng,Kerui Wu,Yu Huang,Ruiqi Xue,Dong Liu,Tian Xu,Lei Yuan,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes Marope, a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that enables multiple humanoid robots to perform cooperative long rope skipping, achieving efficient rope manipulation and robust adaptation to different player styles in both simulation and real-world settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans excel at dynamic motor skills, but humanoid robots lack coordination in multi-participant sports scenarios. Long rope skipping requires precise cooperation between two rope turners and a player, making it a challenging yet meaningful task that remains unexplored in existing humanoid sports research.

Method: Marope uses a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework: lower-level MARL trains decentralized rope manipulation policies, upper-level centralized scheduling coordinates execution, and diverse jumping policies are incorporated for generalization across player styles.

Result: Evaluated on Unitree G1 humanoid robots in simulation and real-world settings, Marope outperforms baselines with more efficient/stable rope manipulation and more robust/adaptable cooperation with varied players.

Conclusion: The proposed MARL framework successfully enables cooperative long rope skipping for humanoid robots, demonstrating feasibility for multi-robot coordination tasks in dynamic sports scenarios.

Abstract: Humans exhibit remarkable motor agility, enabling a wide range of dynamic skills such as running and jumping, which highlights the great potential of humanoid robots for athletic locomotion. Among athletic sports, long rope skipping requires two rope turners to cooperatively swing the rope while adapting to a player under different jumping rhythms, making it a meaningful yet challenging task for humanoid robots. Although existing methods for humanoid sports have achieved success in single-agent and interaction-free settings, such as running, dancing, and parkour, task scenarios that require precise coordination among multiple participants remain largely unexplored. To this end, we propose Marope, a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for cooperative long rope skipping with multiple humanoid robots. Specifically, Marope adopts a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for policy training. At the lower level, it learns decentralized rope manipulation policies through MARL, while at the upper level, a centralized scheduling policy is trained to coordinate the execution of the lower-level policies. To improve generalization across different player behavioral styles, Marope further incorporates diverse jumping policies into cooperative game training. We evaluate our approach on Unitree G1 humanoid robots in both simulation and real-world settings. Experimental results demonstrate that Marope outperforms various baselines, achieving more efficient and stable rope manipulation as well as more robust and adaptable cooperation with varied players.

</details>


### [691] [vla.cpp: A Unified Inference Runtime for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.08094)
*Khanh D. Nguyen,Hung T. Ho,Chinh T. Nguyen,Thanh Q. Duong,Linh D. Le,Duy M. H. Nguyen,Vien A. Ngo,An T. Le*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: vla.cpp is a portable C++ inference runtime for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies that runs efficiently on robot hardware (including embedded devices), supporting multiple architectures, achieving near state-of-the-art performance with significant memory and latency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA policies rely on Python/PyTorch stacks requiring workstation-class GPUs, which are mismatched with the hardware actually deployed on robots. The authors aim to provide a lightweight, portable inference runtime suitable for resource-constrained robot hardware.

Method: The authors build vla.cpp on llama.cpp, a C++ inference engine. It natively supports flow-matching and diffusion VLA inference patterns with cached vision-language prefix and cross-attending action expert. They implement an IMMA ladder GEMM derived from roofline analysis to optimize performance across hardware tiers, from consumer GPUs to 8GB embedded modules.

Result: vla.cpp matches state-of-the-art on LIBERO-Object within 1 episode out of 200, runs BitVLA at 100% success in only 1.3 GiB of memory, and works unchanged across three hardware tiers. The IMMA ladder GEMM reduces BitVLA per-step latency by 4.5x.

Conclusion: Batch-1 VLA inference is compute-bound (not bandwidth-bound), meaning utilization is the key deployment lever. The runtime successfully bridges the gap between VLA research and practical robot deployment by enabling efficient, portable inference on real robot hardware.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies are typically shipped as Python/PyTorch stacks that assume a workstation-class GPU, a mismatch for the hardware on which robots actually run. We present vla.cpp, a portable C++ inference runtime built on llama.cpp. To our knowledge, it is the first ggml-class engine to natively serve the flow-matching and diffusion VLA inference pattern, in which a cached vision-language prefix is consumed by a cross-attending action expert integrated over several solver steps. A single runtime serves seven architectures spanning five backbone and four action-head families behind one request/response protocol, with each model packaged as a self-contained bundle. On LIBERO-Object, the engine matches a state-of-the-art checkpoint to within one episode out of 200, and runs BitVLA at 100% success in 1.3 GiB of memory.
